# PART TWO
# MINUTES, TWENTY-NINTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY

First Session - Tuesday Evening
June 19, 2001

## 29-1 Assembly Called to Order and Opening Worship

The Twenty-Ninth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America gathered for the opening worship service at 7:30 PM on Tuesday, June 19, 2001 in the Lonestar Ballroom of the Adam’s Mark Hotel in Dallas, Texas.

## Gathering of the Commissioners

“Partita on Crown Him with Many Crowns”
Charles Callahan
Dr. Lawrence C. Roff, Organist (Knox Theological Seminary)

## Call to Order

Dr. Morton H. Smith, Moderator
**Prelude**
“O Sacred Head, Now Wounded”
Johannes Brahms
“Man of Sorrows”
arr. S. Dubberly
North Texas Presbytery Choir
Dr. Stephen Dubberly, Director

## Call to Worship

The Rev. David Sherwood

**Minister:**
Worthy are You, our Lord and our God, to receive glory and honor and power; for You did create all things, and because of Your will they existed and were created! Revelation 4:11

**People:**
Of old You did found the earth, and the heavens are the work of Your hands. Psalm 102:25

**Minister:**
But You, O LORD, do abide forever and Your name to all generations. Psalm 102:12

**People:**
And every created thing which is in heaven and on the earth and under the earth and on the sea, and all things in them, I heard saying, “To Him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb, be blessing and honor and glory and dominion forever and ever.” Revelation 5:13

Hymn of Praise No.4 “All Praise to God, Who Reigns Above” MIT FREUDEN ZART

Prayer of Adoration and The Lord’s Prayer (sung)
Mr. Sherwood

## The Confession

Westminster Confession of Faith, Chapter XXIX.1

Our Lord Jesus, in the night wherein He was betrayed, instituted the sacrament of His body and blood, called the Lord’s Supper, to be observed in His church, unto the end of the world, for the perpetual remembrance of the sacrifice of Himself in His death; the sealing all benefits thereof unto true believers, their spiritual nourishment and growth in Him, their further engagement in and to all

<a id="ga29-p20"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=22 printed_page=20 -->

duties which they owe unto Him; and, to be a bond and pledge of their communion with Him, and with each other, as members of His mystical body.

Hymn No. 170
“Fairest Lord Jesus”
CRUSADER’S HYMN

## Prayer and Offering Musical Offering
“Christ Jesus Lay in Death’s Strong Bands”
*arr. S. Dubberly*
text: Martin Luther*

## The Scripture Reading
Old Testament Reading
New Testament Reading
Dr. Smith
II Chronicles 6:18-21
Philippians 3:1-16

## The Evening Sermon
“A Call to Spiritual Renewal--
Dr. Smith
To Know Christ and the Power of His Resurrection”
*”That I may know Him and the Power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings...”*
Philippians 3:10

Hymn of Preparation No. 461
“Not What My Hands Have Done”
LEOMINSTER

## The Decalogue
The Rev. Elliott Greene
*Privately consider the meaning of each Commandment as it is read.*
Then God spoke these words saying,
You shall have no other gods before Me.
You shall not make for yourself an idol.
You shall not take the name of the Lord your God in Vain.
Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.
Honor your father and your mother.
You shall not murder.
You shall not commit adultery.
You shall not steal.
You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor.
You shall not covet.

## Public Confession of Sin.
Minister: If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just, and will forgive our sins and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

People: O God of grace, You have imputed my sin to my substitute and have imputed His righteousness to my soul, clothing me with a bridegroom’s robe, decking me with jewels of holiness. But in my Christian walk I am still in rags; my best prayers are stained with sin; my penitential tears are so much impurity; my confessions of wrong are so many aggravations of sin; my receiving the Spirit is tinctured with selfishness. I need to repent of my repentance; I need my tears to be

<a id="ga29-p21"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=23 printed_page=21 -->

washed; I have no robe to bring to cover my sins, no loom to weave my own righteousness; I am always standing clothed in filthy garments, and by grace am always receiving change of raiment, for You always justify the ungodly. I am always going into the far country, and always returning home as a prodigal, always saying, “Father, forgive me,” and You are always bringing forth the best robe. Grant me never to lose sight of the exceeding sinfulness of sin, the exceeding righteousness of salvation, the exceeding glory of Christ, the exceeding beauty of holiness, the exceeding wonder of grace.

## Private Confession of Sin.

## Declaration of Forgiveness

Dr. Peter Deison

Hear what comforting words the Lord says to all who repent and come to Him: “Come to Me, all who are weary and heavy laden, and I will give you rest. This is a true statement and worthy of full acceptance: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. Praise the Lord, O my soul, and forget not all His Benefits: He forgives all your sins.” Lift up your hearts! We lift them up unto the Lord! Let us give thanks to our Lord God. It is good and right to do so. It is very good and right that we should at all times and in all places give thanks unto You, O Lord, Holy Father, Almighty, Everlasting God. Therefore, with Angels and Archangels, and with all the company of heaven, we laud and magnify Your most glorious Name; evermore praising You singing:

**ALL:** “Holy, holy, holy Lord, God of power and might, heaven and earth are full of you glory. Hosanna in the highest. Hosanna in the highest.”

## The Prayer of Thanksgiving

The Words of Institution and Distribution of the Elements

Please hold the bread and cup until all have been
Served so that we may partake together

During the serving of the bread, please sing No. 378
“Here, O My Lord, I See Thee Face to Face”
MORECAMBE

During the serving of the cup, please sing No. 427
“Amidst Us Our Beloved Stands”
HAMBURG

Closing Hymn No. 263
“Lift High the Cross”
CRUCIFER

## Benediction

Dr. Smith

<a id="ga29-p22"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=24 printed_page=22 -->

postlude “Lift High the Cross ” Donald Busarow
Dr. R off

Following worship, the Assembly recessed for 15 minutes at 9:15 PM.

29-3 adoption of the docket
the third draft of the docket was declared adopted with the following
amendments: (1) that amendments to the RAO be voted on at the time of the report of
the Stated Clerk, and (2) That the Theological Examining Committee be allowed to
give their report as soon as it is available, and (3) that the reports of the other
committees be allowed to be presented as they become available, and (4) That the
Local Arrangements Committee be permitted to bring greetings from local dignitaries
when appropriate.

<a id="ga29-p47"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=49 printed_page=47 -->

29-4 election of moderator
the moderator opened the floor for nominations for moderator of the 29th
General Assembly. RE Jack Williamson placed in nomination RE Steve Fox. TE John
Stoghill placed in nomination RE Howard Q. Davis. In accordance with RAO 1-4,
ballots were distributed. during the counting of the ballots, a season of prayer was
held.
RE Steve Fox was elected Moderator 492-401. TE Smith offered prayer for the
new Moderator.
the chairman of the administrative Committee, RE William “Bingy” Moore,
presented to the retiring moderator a plaque in token of thea ss em bly’s appreciation for
his year of service as Moderator.

29-5 Constitutional Inquiry #1 from ted on clements
The Assembly received the following Constitutional Inquiry from TE Don
Clements and referred it to Committee on Constitutional Business (see 29-28, III,
Reference 7. p. 137 and 143).

29-6 election of recording and assistant clerks and assistant parliamentarians
On nomination by the Stated Clerk, TEs David Dively, J. Robert Fiol, and
Steven Meyerhoff were elected recording clerks; RE Walter Lastovica was appointed
timekeeper: Ed Nalley and Frank M. Barker III were appointed Sound Engineers; Dale
Carroll was appointed A-V Engineer; TE James A. smith was appointed chairman of
the floor clerks and RE Ric Springer Vice Chairman; Steve Lawton was appointed
Photographer; Mr. Jeff Bone was appointed Webmaster; Dr. Larry R off was appointed
Assembly Organist; Mr. Bill Lloyd was appointed Com puter Technician; and Dr. Cub
Culbertson was appointed On-Site Physician.
RE Sam Duncan and RE John B. White, Jr. were appointed assistant
parliamentarians

29-7 Recess
The Assembly recessed at 10:25 PM with prayer by TE William Fox.

Second Session - wednesday morning
Ju ne 18,2 0 0 1

29-8 assembly reconvenes
The Assembly reconvened at 8:00 AM on June 20, 2001 with the singing of
Psalm 42 from the Trinity Psalter and prayer by TE Wallace Tinsley.

29-9 appointment of committee on thanks
the moderator appointed the following men to serve as the committee on
Thanks: TE Henry Lewis Smith (Convenor) and RE Dwight Allen.

<a id="ga29-p48"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=50 printed_page=48 -->

29-10 assembly directs potomac presbytery to meet
at the request of potomac presbytery (BCO 13-12), the Assembly directed
Potomac Presbytery to meet immediately following the worship service this morning
for the purposes of dismissing a candidate to another presbytery to accept a call there.

29-11 partial report of the stated clerk
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, led the Assembly in prayer and presented his
report (See Appendix A, p. 362). the stated clerk reviewed the statistical portions of
his report. He recognized the churches which were added to or lost from the
denomination since the last Assembly. Prayer was offered for these new churches by
TE Bill Lyle.

29-12 presbytery votes on BCO amendments
the assembly moved to consideration of amendments to the Book of Church Order proposed by the Tw enty-Eighth General Assembly.

IT EM 1: add anew clause a tb 12.5d., after the words “Bible study groups;” as
follows: M28GA, 28-72,111,4, p. 281

to encourage the women in the church to hold meetings and give instruction
appropriate to their calling (e.g. Titus 2:3-5), while prohibiting women from
expounding and preaching the word of god a san ordinance of public worship
(cf. BCO 4-4; 53-2), as this is clearly forbidden in Scripture (1 Tim. 2:11-12).

The entire section would read:
12.5d. To call congregational meetings when necessary; to establish and
control Sunday schools and Bible classes with special reference to the children
of the church; to establish and control all special groups in the church such as

<a id="ga29-p50"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=52 printed_page=50 -->

Men in the Church. Women in the Church and special Bible study groups; to
encourage the women in the church to hold meetings and give instruction
ap propriate to their calling (e.g. titus 2:3-5), while prohibiting women from
expounding and preaching the word of god a san ordinance of public
worship (cf. BCO 4-4; 53-2), as this is clearly forbidden in scripture (1 Tim .
2:11-12); to promote World missions; to promote obedience to the Great
Commission in its totality at home and abroad; to order collections for pious
uses;
Defeated

ITEM 2: Amend BCO 32-19 by deleting the current language (indicated by strikeouts) and replacing it with the following new language.

M28GA, 28-72, III,12, p. 289

That BCO 32-19 be amended

32-19. No professional counsel shall be permitted as such to appear and plead in cases of process in any court; but an accused person may, if he desires it, be represented before the Session by any communing member of the same particular church, or before any other court, by any member of that court. A member of the court so employed shall not be allowed to sit in judgment in the case.

32-19. The accused shall be entitled to be represented before the courts of this church by counsel, who shall have the right to be heard by oral and/or written argument. Any such counsel shall be a communing member of the Presbyterian Church in America. Any member of a court so engaged shall not be allowed to sit in judgment of the case. No person shall accept any fee or other remuneration for any service rendered as counsel, but reimbursement may be made to cover any personal expenses incurred or to be incurred by such counsel in the course of representation.

Deferred to the 30th General Assembly

To permit these presbyteries who had not yet voted to vote (BCO 26-6).

ITEM 3: Change *BCO 14-1, 12* to include Reformed University Ministries as a permanent Committee.
M28GA, 28-31, III, 12, p. 102

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative Committee of General Assembly, Committee on Christian Education and Publications, Committee on Mission to North America, and Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed University Ministries.

The Administrative Committee of General Assembly shall consist of nineteen (19) twenty (20) members:

- **a.** Eleven Members in classes elected through the standard nomination and election procedure,
- **b.** One member each from the following program committees or agencies:

1. Christian Education and Publication;
2. Covenant College;
3. Covenant Theological Seminary;
4. Insurance, Annuities and Relief;
5. Mission to North America;
6. Mission to the World;
7. PCA Foundation;
8. Ridge Haven Conference Center;
9. Reformed University Ministries

The eleven members at large shall serve a term of four years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be one of its members at large.

Each program committee and agency shall designate its member each year at the last meeting of the committee or board before the meeting of General Assembly. The chief administrative officers of the program committees and agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an executive session is called.

Committee on Christian Education and Publications, Committee on Mission to North America, and Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed University Ministries shall consist of fourteen (14) men divided into four classes of three men each on odd years and four men each on even years, elected to serve four-year terms. Committees on Christian Education and Publications, Mission to North America, and Mission to the World shall have one ruling and one teaching elder as alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the year.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly's permanent committees or agencies shall not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly committee until one year

<a id="ga29-p54"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=56 printed_page=54 -->

has elapsed. (Exceptions may be perm itted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).
Adopted

## COMMUNICATION 2 From the Reformed Church of the United States
“Greetings”

Dear Rev. Taylor:

Greetings in the name of our blessed Lord and Savior.

I am, this date, in receipt of your invitation to send an observer from the Reformed Church in the United States to the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America.

The RCUS will not be able to send an observer but would like to thank the PCA for its kind invitation. The RCUS will anticipate the report of your General Assembly when it is presented to NAPARC in November 2001.

<a id="ga29-p59"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=61 printed_page=59 -->

We. in the RCUS, do thank you for your fellowship in the gospel with us and do
pray that god will bless the deliberations of your judicatory to his honor and glory and
the furthering of hi strut hint hiss in laden and desperately needy world.

in the bonds of Christ, I remain,

/s/ Rev. Ron Potter
Chairman, Interchurch Relations Committee
Reformed Church in the United States

communications 3 From the United Reformed Churches
“Greetings"

Dear Dr. Taylor,

greetings in the name of our lord and savior jesus Christ.
the committee for ecumenical relations and church unity of the united
Reformed Churches was very pleased to receive the invitation to send an ecumenical
observer to the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America.
However, we are unable to do so duet oa variety of circumstances. We regret that we
are unable to be present at your Assembly.
It is our prayerful wish that the Holy Spirit should endow your delegates with
scriptural wisdom so that the considerations and decisions of the assembly in dallas
may please the king of the Church, and tend to the edification of his saints on earth and
the building up of the church for whom our lord jesus gave his precious blood.
Our committee is very interested, however, in pursuing further relations with
your Interchurch Committee, and we have communicated this desire recently. We
would be pleased to engage in an exchange of communication and look forward to your
committees response.

In the Lord’s service.

/s/ Richard Stienstra, Secretary
Committee for Ecumenical Relations and Church Unity
United Reformed Churches

29-15 committee of commissioners on inter church relations
RE Sam Duncan, Chairman, led in prayer and introduced TE Irfon Hughes.
chairman of the inter church relations Committee. He then introduced the following
fraternal delegates who addressed the Assembly: Dr. John Carson (ARP), TE Dan
Davenport (OPC), TE Bruce Parnell (RPCNA), and Dr. Duck Young Won (KAPC).
The following Corresponding Delegates addressed the Assembly: Dr. Don Fortson
(EPC), Dr. Bong Gee Lee (Presbyterian church of Korea, Reformed), Dr. Chang He
Hwang (Presbyterian church of Korea, Kosin), and Pastor Antoine Schluchlien
(Reformed Church in France).
RE Duncan led the Assembly in prayer, specifically remembering TE Bruce
Howes (Permanent Committee member who struggles with cancer) and presented the
Report (See Appendix H, p. 510). RE Duncan closed the report with prayer.

#### I. business referred to the committee

• the report of the permanent committee on inter church relations
• the minutes of the permanent committee meeting sheldon
September 19, 2000, December 11, 2000 and March 12, 2001

#### II. statement of major issues discussed

• NAE decision to allow N CC-affiliated denominations to join NAE,
and the status of PCA efforts to rescind this decision
• the status of PCA efforts to remove the cr c from na parc
membership
• the current irc policy of inviting fraternal and corresponding
delegates and ecclesiastical observers to address General Assembly,
which gives less access (every other year) to fraternal delegates, who

<a id="ga29-p61"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=63 printed_page=61 -->

are in closest relation to the PCA, than to corresponding delegates
and ecclesiastical observers (yearly, if they attend and request access)

#### III. recommendations

1. That Fraternal Delegates, Corresponding Delegates, and Ecclesiastical
Observers be welcomed and invited to address the General Assembly as
determined by the IRC, with fraternal delegates being given five minutes
to speak, and corresponding delegates and ecclesiastical observers being
given three minutes to speak. A dopted
2. That visiting ministers be introduced to the General Assembly (BCO 13-
13). A dopted
3. That the Assembly reaffirm its membership in the World Reformed
Fellowship (formerly called the world fellowship of reformed
Churches - the web site for the World Reformed Fellowship is
wrfnet.org) based on its Bylaws and doctrinal standards (See Appendix
H, Attachment C, p. 520ff). A dopted
4. That the Assembly direct the IRC Permanent Committee to review their
whole policy on invitations to denominational delegates to the General Assembly and on the time allotted for fraternal delegates, corresponding
delegates, and ecclesiastical observers to address the General Assembly,
and to bring a clearer policy to the next General Assembly. [NOTE: this
motion assumes that IRC will take into account the concerns expressed
under II. above] A dopted
5. that the minutes of the permanent committee be approved without
exception and with notations sentto the secretary of the permanent
committee for the meetings of september 19, 2000,December 11. 2000,
and March 12, 2001. A dopted

## 29-21 Theological Examining Committee

TE Ligon Duncan, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report, which was **received**.

The Theological Examining Committee [TEC] met twice from GA 2000 to May 2001. The charge of the TEC is: “to examine all first and second level administrative officers of committees, boards and agencies, and those acting temporarily in these positions who are being recommended for first time employment” (BCO 14.1.14). The committee members are TE J. Ligon Duncan III (Chairman), TE Chuck Anderson, RE Bill Cranford, RE James “Bebo” Elkin, TE Steve Meyerhoff, RE Barry Teague and alternates, TE Philip Hardin and RE David Kesler. Minutes of each meeting are filed with Stated Clerk’s office. Here is our summary:

### Examination:

1. On October 4, 2000, the TEC examined TE Mack Griffith for a second level position at Ridgehaven Conference Center. Present were TE Chuck Anderson, RE Bill Cranford, TE Ligon Duncan (Chair.), and TE Steve Meyerhoff. He was examined, sustained and approved in all areas specified in BCO 14.1.14: Christian Experience, Views, Bible and Theology, Sacraments and Church Government, Church History and the History of the PCA. He indicated no exceptions to the Westminster Confession and Catechisms, and stood a fine examination.

2. On February 23, 2001, the TEC examined Charlie Baxter for a second level position in Development at the PCA Foundation in Atlanta. Present were RE

<a id="ga29-p64"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=66 printed_page=64 -->

Bill Cranford, TE Ligon Duncan, RE James “Bebo” Elkin, TE Steve Meyerhoff and RE Barry Teague. Mr. Baxter, though unordained, was examined in all the areas prescribed by the BCO in 14.1.14: Christian Experience, Views, Bible and Theology, Sacraments and Church Government, Church History and the History of the PCA. Mr. Baxter had no exceptions to the Westminster Confession and Catechisms, and stood an excellent exam. After an extended period of examination the committee moved, seconded and unanimously passed its approval on Mr. Baxter.

3. On May 23, 2001, the TEC examined Tim McKeown for a second level position in MNA (Coordinator of Multi-Cultural Church Planting). Present were TE Churk Anderson, TE Ligon Duncan (Chair), RE James “Bebo” Elkin, and TE Steve Meyerhoff. Mr. McKeown, a veteran MTW missionary, was examined in all the areas prescribed by the BCO in 14.1.14; Christian Experience, Views, Bible and Theology, Sacraments and Church Government, Church History and the History of the PCA. Mr. McKeown expressed no exceptions to the Westminster Confession and Catechisms, and stood a good exam. After an extended period of examination the committee moved, seconded and unanimously passed its approval on Mr. McKeown, noting his views on tongues (he believes that tongues was a non-revelatory gift of a known human language for the purpose of sharing the gospel, and possesses it; he does not believe in continuing revelation), culture and worship (e.g., dance and drama may be acceptable cultural expressions in worship), and the Lord’s Day (holds to the so-called ‘continental’ view of the Sabbath).

## Committee business:

1. The TEC is thorough and not cursory in its approach to examinations, taking seriously the directives of GA and the words of BCO 14.1.14. The committee pursues no agenda, but does hear the views of administrative leaders regarding controverted issues. Many of the questions the committee asks are drawn from various sources, including the PCA Ministerial Data Form, Bryan Chapell and Jim Meek’s booklet Preparing for Licensure and Ordination Exams (Christian Education and Publications), as well as various presbytery examinations. Generally speaking, as you already know, in the PCA there is an allowed diversity on some of these questions and an expected unity on others. The Theological Examinations Committee will explore these kinds of issues in the oral examination, but will not press for a particular line of response. There is a range of opinion on the committee itself pertaining to some of these questions. Hence the committee’s vote of approval will be based upon a charitable hearing of the candidates’ positions, a faithful reading of our Standards, and the corporate conscience of the church. The TEC is diligent to inform the GA of any exceptions to the Standards taken by a nominee.

2. TE Ligon Duncan, the TEC Chairman, may be reached at 601 973 9104, at lduncan@fpcjackson.org, or at 1390 North State Street regarding matters relating to the business of TEC. His Assistant, Mary Ball, can also be contacted

<a id="ga29-p65"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=67 printed_page=65 -->

at maryb@fpcjackson.org to arrange for logistical matters in relation to examinations.

3. The TEC requests that the nominating committee of GA place only those men in nomination who have expressed their willingness and ability to travel and perform the duties of the committee and, ordinarily, those with experience in theological examination at Presbytery and/or General Assembly level.

4. The TEC reminds the GA, its Committees and Agencies that BCO 14.1.14 says “No person will begin work or move on the field without prior examination and approval by the GA’s TEC.”

Respectfully submitted,
/s/ J. Ligon Duncan, III, Chairman

## 29-22 Committee of Commissioners on Covenant College

TE Wallace Tinsley, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the Report (See Appendix E, p. 450). He introduced Frank Brock, President of Covenant College, who addressed the Assembly. The report was closed with prayer by TE Tinsley.

## Business Referred to the Committee

- Proposed operating budget for 2001-2002
- Covenant College Financial Report
- Minutes of the Board of Trustees for October 5-6, 2000 and March 22-23, 2001
- The 1999-2000 Institutional Effectiveness Report (Commissioners’ Handbook, pp. 1201-1238)

## Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

- The search for a new President for Covenant College currently underway, due to the fact that President Frank Brock will be stepping down as President in June, 2002.
- The Institutional Effectiveness Report for 1999-2000.
- The addition of a Reformed University Campus minister at Covenant College.

## RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. That the General Assembly approve the Budget for 2001-2002 as submitted through the Administration Committee. Deferred to AC Report, p. 254-255
2. That the General Assembly accept The Covenant College Financial Report dated June 30, 2000, and prepared by Hazlett, Lewis, and Bieter, PLLC. Adopted
3. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the Board of Trustees for October 5-6, 2000 and March 22-23, 2001, with notations. Adopted
4. That the General Assembly receive as information The 1999-2000 Institutional Effectiveness Report including the 3-year plan 2001-2003 and commend the

<a id="ga29-p66"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=68 printed_page=66 -->

college in its desire to continue to care for the students in their academic and spiritual growth and the college’s commitment to continue to evaluate its effectiveness in both.
Adopted

5. That the General Assembly praise God for the call of Jed Johnston as Reformed University Fellowship campus minister to Covenant College, and pray for the effectiveness of this unique opportunity
Adopted

6. That the General Assembly thank and praise God for the excellent work and faithfulness of the Board of Trustees, President Brock, faculty and staff of Covenant College in serving the Presbyterian Church in America in its mission to educate students for the Kingdom of God.
Adopted

7. That the General Assembly encourage the entire denomination to pray that the search committee may know God’s leading for the next President of Covenant College and that we intercede for President Frank Brock as he begins his transition into other areas of ministry.
Adopted

29-27 partial report of Standing Judicial Commission
RE John White, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report.
the proposed amendment oft hes jc manual section 13.8 and a new section 16
and 18 (for text, see page 126) was postponed until the 3 0 th General Assembly so
that concerned presbyteries and individuals might send suggestions and concerns to the
Standing Judicial Commission. The Assembly ad opted a motion to insert
communication 7 (see text, p. 130) at the end of the report oft hes jc in the Minutes.

REPORT OF THE STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION
TO THE TWENTY-NINTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

introduction
The Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) held its fall meeting on October 19
and 20, 2000, and its annual meeting by telephone conference call on March 3, 2001.
the annual meeting was held in an effort to be good stewards in view of a short,
uncomplicated agenda and insufficient business to justify the expense of a face-to-face
meeting.

II. judicial cases
the following is a list of judicial cases before the commission over the period
since the last General Assembly:
1. Case 99-1 The John Wood Matter (Investigative Report and Findings)
2. Case 99-3 Joseph Gatis vs. Northeast Presbytery (Appeal)
3. Case 99-5 Lawrence Rountree vs. Covenant Presbytery (Appeal)
4. Case 99-6 Robert M. Shive vs. Central Carolina Presbytery (Appeal)
5. Case 99-7 Jeffrey M. Black vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery (Complaint)
6. Case 00-1 Erwin Morrison, et al. vs. Philadelphia Presbytery (Complaint)
7. Case 00-2 Philip J. Adams vs. Northeast Presbytery (Complaint)
8. Case 00-3 memorial of south coast presbytery
9. Case 00-4 Thomas Clark Staley vs. North Texas Presbytery (Complaint)
10. Case 00-5 Robert H. King vs. Evangel Presbytery (Complaint)
11. Case 00-6 David Scott Price vs. Northern Illinois Presbytery (Complaint)
12. Case 00-7 Ed Stadick vs. Northern Illinois Presbytery (Complaint)
13. Case 00-8 session of korean presbyterian church of washington vs.
Korean Capital Presbytery (Complaint)
14. Case 00-9 Sung Keon Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Complaint)
15. Case 00-10 Roger Tinsley et al vs. Southeast Alabama Presbytery
(Complaint)
16. Case 01-1 Charles C. Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Appeal)
17. Case 01-2 Sang Soo Ryoo vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Appeal)
18. Case 01-3 Sung Keon Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Appeal)
19. Case 01 -4 Moon K. Ham vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Appeal)

<a id="ga29-p70"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=72 printed_page=70 -->

20. Case 01-5 Moon K. Ham &amp; Charles C. Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Complaint)
21. Case 01-6 Sang Bai Kim &amp; Chan Soo Kim vs. Korean Eastern Presbytery (Complaint)
22. Case 01-7 Andy C. Lee vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Complaint)
23. Case 01-8 Andy C. Lee vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Complaint)
24. Case 01-9 Charles C. Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Appeal)
25. Case 01-10 Charles C. Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery (Appeal)
26. Case 01-11 Byung Han Yoo vs. Korean Northwest Presbytery (Complaint)

Of these, Cases 99-3 and 00-3 were withdrawn and 00-5 was abandoned. Cases 00-9, 01-1, 01-2, 01-3, 01-4, 01-5, 01-7, 01-8, 01-9, and 01-10 are in process. Since these cases are complex and related, a panel of five members has been appointed to hear all these cases. Cases 00-6, 01-6, and 01-11 await final action by the full SJC in October. The Standing Judicial Commission has completed its work on Cases 99-1, 99-5, 99-6, 99-7, 00-1, 00-2, 00-4, 00-7, 00-8 and 00-10 and the report on these is as follows:

## III. REPORT OF THE CASES

### 99-1
### THE JOHN WOOD MATTER
### (Investigative Report and Findings)

The Officers of the Standing Judicial Commission of the Presbyterian Church in America, following the mandate from the 28th General Assembly to take original jurisdiction of “the John Wood Matter” under BCO 34-1 and directing SJC to investigate the matter under 31-2, appointed a committee or panel composed of three members of SJC (RE Mark Belz, RE John Lane, and TE Lawrence Lunceford) to investigate the matter and report back to SJC.

In fulfillment of its mandate, the committee collected and reviewed documents and exhibits, and upon notice to all parties directly concerned in the matter, heard testimony on September 16, 2000 at Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church in Knoxville, TN (CSPC). The committee then formulated a written report to the full Standing Judicial Commission, for consideration at its October, 2000 meeting in Atlanta. The sole purpose of the investigation and review is to determine whether or not sufficient cause exists to initiate judicial process against TE John Wood.

The Standing Judicial Commission, upon review of and deliberation on the panel’s Report and all relevant documents, finds as follows:

### 1. A Woman Speaking At The Evening Services In August, 1998.

The investigation has not resulted in a strong presumption of guilt (BCO 31-2) on the part of TE John Wood in connection with a woman speaking on August 16 and 23, 1998, at CSPC, and therefore judicial process should not be instituted against him.

<a id="ga29-p71"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=73 printed_page=71 -->

The current controversy was touched off when Dr. Linda Eure spoke at the evening service of Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church in Knoxville on August 16, 1998. Dr. Eure was then the director of Women’s Ministries at CSPC. Earlier in the summer, the Senior Staff Administrative Committee, upon the suggestion of RE Mack Sells, had recommended to the Session that she be asked to speak. Testimony was received by the panel that the Session had approved her speaking at that service as well as at the next week’s evening service (August 23, 1998) to explain her views as well as the work of Women’s Ministries at the church. It is not clear as to whether TE John Wood was directly involved in the decision to have her speak or not, but he states that he favored the decision. There were two student visitors at the evening service on August 16, at least one of whom was offended, being (in his words) shocked “at seeing a woman mount the pulpit at the 6 p.m. worship service.” His complaint was communicated to the CSPC Session and also to TE Joseph Pipa.

The panel heard testimony from RE Mack Sells, who is a member of the Senior Staff Administrative Committee, and who was present at the July 7, 1998 meeting of that committee when the need to plan for the remaining summer evening services was discussed. Evening services at CSPC had dwindled in size over the years, nearing the point of extinction. CSPC morning services usually had approximately 3,000 in attendance while evening services had as few as 25 and as many as 60. Evening services were not held in the sanctuary but in a smaller chapel that is part of the church complex. It was not uncommon to have short-term missions reports and musical presentations in the evening service, sometimes in lieu of a sermon. In the summer of 1998, TE Roy Zinn was completing a New Testament series and it was not his plan to preach on August 16 or 23. The Session approved the suggestion that Dr. Linda Eure speak those two evenings, on Women’s Ministries at CSPC, of which she was the director. The announcement in the bulletin read “Sunday Evening Service will take place at 6:00 p.m. in the Chapel. Dr. Linda Eure will be speaking.”

Linda Eure did not lead the service on August 16; RE Ray Elder did. RE Mack Sells led in prayer. There was no sermon by a teaching elder or by a ruling elder. Linda Eure spoke, as planned. Ruling elders and teaching elders who attended that service testified that when she was speaking she explained what the Word of God meant, and that she applied it to the lives of those in the congregation. Some said they felt “uncomfortable” or “different” and while none of the witnesses interviewed used the word “preaching” to describe her speaking, all witnesses who were actually in the evening service were clearly negative about the manner in which Linda Eure dynamically set forth her views in the context of the evening service. At least two of the elder witnesses stated that, considering the manner in which she spoke, they were sure that the Session would never approve her speaking again. This episode highlights the need for all sessions to avoid scheduling such events that may disturb the peace of the Church.

Based upon the evidence, the SJC believes that when the Session approved the plan for Linda Eure to speak in the evening service, the Session did not intend to have her preach, nor did the Session intend to violate PCA polity. However, the SJC concludes that what she said “crossed the line,” as evidenced from the testimony of the

<a id="ga29-p72"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=74 printed_page=72 -->

ruling and teaching elders who attended the service. But this “crossing of the line” does not require the institution of process against TE Wood.¹

## 2. TE John Wood’s Views Regarding Women And Preaching.

SJC concludes that judicial process should not be instituted against TE John Wood for his expressed views regarding women and preaching because: (1) the investigation did not produce evidence that raises a strong presumption of guilt on the part of TE John Wood in connection with any public scandal caused by agitation regarding or the promotion of the view that women should be ordained or that women should preach in PCA churches; (2) the investigation has not resulted in a strong presumption that TE Wood promoted his views on women and preaching in PCA churches; and (3) there is no clear evidence that TE Wood’s views are outside the bounds of our Standards.

TE John Wood testified before the panel on September 16. Based on his testimony as well as the testimony of other witnesses interviewed that day, the SJC does not believe that the Linda Eure event was an effort on his part to promote women preaching at CSPC or within the PCA. Nevertheless, legitimate questions were raised about TE Wood’s views on the subject through subsequent statements he made to others inquiring about the Linda Eure event.

Both TEs Morton Smith and Joseph Pipa testified before the panel on September 16. They testified that on July 17, 1999, they talked to TE John Wood by teleconference. In that telephone conversation TE Wood, in answer to a question put by TE Smith: “Do you believe that a woman can preach in the church?” answered in the affirmative. TE Wood admitted to this, before the panel.

TE Wood also stated to the panel that in the telephone conference call with TEs Smith and Pipa, he was using the word “preach” in a broad, but he believes Biblical, sense—the witness that each Christian bears before the church and the world, but he also stated that he should have been more careful to articulate his belief that women should not be ordained to the teaching or ruling eldership and that they should not be permitted to “preach” in the traditional sense of authoritative teaching from the word of God, as teaching elders are called and ordained to do.

TE Wood stated to the panel that he holds to a view that: (1) excludes women from ordination; (2) excludes women from preaching (authoritative teaching); (3) permits women to do basically whatever unordained men can do in the church. This view, he claims, is exactly the view held by many others in the PCA, including the late Dr. James Boice.

The investigation did not produce evidence that raised a strong presumption of guilt that TE Wood is agitating for or promoting a view that women be ordained nor that he is promoting women preaching the authoritative word of God in worship services. Neither TE Wood nor CSPC has trumpeted this event; others within the PCA have spread the report both in private communications and in the secular press. The

¹ There was insufficient evidence to recommend any possible charge against TE John Wood related to the Linda Eure incident, because it is unclear as to whether he played a significant role in making the decision to have her speak, and it was equally unclear as to whether he was in attendance on either occasion.

<a id="ga29-p73"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=75 printed_page=73 -->

SJC, therefore, concludes that judicial process should not be instituted. However, in making this determination, the SJC is not endorsing the view of TE Wood that “women may do basically whatever unordained men can do in the church,” and PCA Ministers and elders are cautioned, for the peace and unity of the church to take great care in the teaching and implementing of views that might give the appearance of promoting a position that women may be ordained or that women may preach the authoritative word of God in a worship service.

Roll call vote:

|  TE | Dominic A. Aquila concur | RE | Mark Belz concur  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|   | Robert M. Ferguson concur |  | Frank A. Brock absent  |
|   | William Harrell disqualified |  | Robert C. Cannada absent  |
|   | Ben W. Konopa concur |  | J. Howard Donahoe concur  |
|   | Paul D. Kooistra concur |  | Samuel J. Duncan concur  |
|   | Lawrence Lunceford concur |  | John W. Lane concur  |
|   | William Lyle concur |  | Collie W. Lehn disqualified  |
|   | John M. McArthur concur |  | Thomas F. Leopard concur  |
|   | Charles E. McGowan absent |  | Robert H. Miller concur  |
|   | David P. Peterson absent |  | J. Grant McCabe concur  |
|   | G. Dewey Roberts concur |  | John B. White, Jr. concur  |
|   | James L. Smith, Jr. absent |  | W. Jack Williamson concur  |

17 concur, 0 dissent, 0 abstain, 2 disqualified, 5 absent.

## 99-1 THE JOHN WOOD MATTER CONCURRING OPINION

The undersigned respectfully submit this concurring opinion in support of the majority decision in this case.

This case was the first case in the history of PCA in which the General Assembly assumed original jurisdiction under BCO 34-1 and assigned the matter to the SJC.

We respectfully call attention to a statement in our BCO Preliminary Principles (7), to-wit:

“All church courts may err through human frailty. . .”

We believe the 28th General Assembly did err in assuming original jurisdiction of this case. The BCO 34-1 gives the General Assembly such authority only “if the Presbytery refuses to act...” The Record of the Case clearly shows that Tennessee Valley Presbytery did act on this matter, and thus General Assembly did not have the constitutional authority to assume original jurisdiction.

Attached is a copy of a statement by RE Mark Wilson given to the SJC Panel investigating this matter. (ROC pp. 244-245). Mr. Mark Wilson was the Moderator of

<a id="ga29-p74"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=76 printed_page=74 -->

Tennessee Valley Presbytery for all of 1999 when this matter was before that Presbytery. We contend that this information from the Presbytery’s Moderator should convince any objective person that, in fact, the Presbytery acted promptly and carefully in many ways. It may not have acted like some would have liked; but it did act.

According to Webster’s Dictionary, the verb “act” may be defined as “a determination of the will producing an effect in the sensible world” or, in a judicial case:

“The essential elements of such an act are three, viz.:

(1) An exertion of the will
(2) An accompanying state of consciousness
(3) A manifestation of such will by taking action.”

Taking the condition precedent for the General Assembly to assume jurisdiction in BCO 34-1 that “the Presbytery refused to act”, in the light of the common usage of “acting”, and the accepted definition in the dictionary set out above, the issue is:

Did Tennessee Valley Presbytery (TVP) act on the matter?

RE Mark Wilson was the Moderator of TVP for the entire time this matter was before the Presbytery. He certainly is in position to know the “actions” taken by Presbytery. Let us then look at his statement (ROC 244-247) - a copy of which is attached as Exhibit A.

TVP Moderator lists these actions, to-wit:

1. He acted in his capacity as Moderator by having discussions with several members of Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church (CSPC) Session. This action convinced him “that it was not their intention of having Miss Eure be the ‘pulpit supply’ and preach on those two evenings, but for her to speak/teach in the way that is accepted in many PCA churches and agencies, and most recently considered acceptable by our General Assembly.”
2. TVP received a communication from Western Carolina Presbytery regarding this matter. At the Spring Stated Meeting of TVP in 1999 a discussion was held during the presbytery meeting. TVP acted on the matter by making a decision “that we should set aside a time to discuss the issue by having an ‘informal’ discussion . . . at our Summer Meeting.”
3. At the TVP Summer Meeting such a discussion was held. Moderator Wilson says “it was a lively discussion, but all of the views shared, even that of John Wood, were still in conformity to the standards and practice within the PCA.”
4. After a discussion at the TVP Summer Meeting, TVP Stated Clerk Render Caines, and Teaching Elders Cal Boroughs and Randy Nabors met with John Wood.

“It was their clear conclusion that CSPC had not intentionally gone beyond the standards of the PCA.”

<a id="ga29-p75"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=77 printed_page=75 -->

5. 3 Sessions of TVP then overtured TVP at its Fall Meeting on October 12, 1999 and the Overtures were approved by TVP “that TVP considered the CSPC Session to be in conformity to PCA Standard because it was not their intention to have her preach.”

6. Another Overture of Mountain View Presbytery Church was passed by TVP at its October 12, 1999 meeting declaring:

“. . . that it is not proper for women to preach and teach in the church’s corporate worship.”

7. Copies of these overtures passed at the Fall TVP October 1999 meeting were mailed by the Stated Clark to the Stated Clerks of Ascension, and Calvary and Western Carolina Presbyteries.

These are undisputed facts that make it clear to us that TVP had acted on the John Wood/CSPC Session matter. These facts were available to the Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners of the 28th General Assembly, if they had made proper inquiry. We believe the 28th General Assembly violated the PCA Constitution when it ruled that TVP had “refused to act” and assigned the case to the SJC under BCO 34-1.

To try to avoid such unconstitutional action by the 28th General Assembly, the undersigned, W. Jack Williamson, raised this as a constitutional inquiry to be assigned under RAO 7-2(2) to the Committee on Constitutional Business for its advice and counsel.

The undersigned W. Jack Williamson pointed out that TVP had not “refused to act” but had affirmatively “acted”; and that if PCA ignored its Constitution, the unity of the body would be destroyed.

We remind our brothers of past history. We believe this is a true saying:

“If we don’t learn from history, we will likely repeat it.”

Our former denomination ignored its Constitution for political expediency and human emotion. This was one of the major reasons, we separated and formed PCA. This is the first time in our 27 years that we have seen a General Assembly ignore the advice of its Constitutional Business Committee on a constitutional matter. It was for such issues that the Constitutional Business Committee was included in the RAO. This Committee is composed of some of the leaders and best minds of the denomination. This Committee was excused from the General Assembly to study the constitutionality of the General Assembly assuming original jurisdiction of this matter under BCO 34-1. This Committee, after study, returned to the floor to make a detailed and thorough report to the conclusion that it would be unconstitutional for the General Assembly to assume original jurisdictional of this matter under BCO 34-1 for TVP had “not refused to act”. The Commissioners ignored this advice and rushed to a decision to assume original jurisdiction of this matter.

<a id="ga29-p76"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=78 printed_page=76 -->

We know of no Teaching or Ruling Elder in PCA who does not hold to *BCO 7-2* that “these offices are open to men only”.

We agree that there are varying views in the PCA about what constitutes “preaching” or “teaching”. John Wood sets out his view which may be somewhat different from some in the PCA. But we believe this SJC decision is correct that John Wood’s views on women in the church are not outside of the boundaries of PCA doctrine.

We acknowledge that John Wood’s statement to Dr. Morton Smith and Dr. Joey Pipa was a careless and not a thoughtful statement of his position; and it could be misconstrued. John Wood’s actual position, as clearly explained to the SJC Panel, could and should have been learned through a more careful investigation. We regret that did not happen. There were 2 students of Greenville Seminary present on August 16 at CSPC the evening Mrs. Eure spoke. One student communicated his complaint to CSPC Session and to Dr. Joey Pipa, President of Greenville Seminary. Then there spread from Greenville Seminary across the denomination and through their connections and friends a movement to discipline this man and his church. The Presbyterian and Reformed News, an independent publication, then got the story and fanned the flames with misinformation and without thorough investigation. This publication then gave the story to the public press in Greenville, S.C., Knoxville, TN and Charlotte, NC.

The mood generated by all this publicity, much of which, we now learn, was not complete or fully accurate, was one of high emotion for the General Assembly Commissioners that felt that John Wood and CSPC had violated the Scriptures and the Constitution of the PCA. Unfortunately, those contributing to creating such an emotional mood had not made a sufficiently thorough investigation to justify their incitement of the Commissioners. We deplore such conduct even though it may have been made with good intentions. We suggest to our Brothers, who participated in such publicity, that a wiser course would have been to make a more thorough investigation of the facts. This the SJC has done. Based on this thorough investigation, we conclude that it does “not produce evidence that raised a strong presumption of guilt that TE Wood is agitating or promoting a view that women may be ordained nor that he is promoting women preaching the authoritative word of God in worship services”.

We admonish all Brothers to be careful not to permit an emotional issue to lead us to unconstitutional action in the future.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ W. Jack Williamson   /s/ Dominic Aquila   /s/ Charles McGowan
/s/ William (Bill) Lyle   /s/ John B. White, Jr.   /s/ Ben Konopa

<a id="ga29-p77"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=79 printed_page=77 -->

# 99-1 THE JOHN WOOD MATTER CONCURRING OPINION

In issuing this concurring opinion, the undersigned wishes to be clear in his concurrence with the majority opinion that there is insufficient evidence to warrant process against TE John Wood in this matter. That having been said, however, there are two areas where the writer wishes to differ from the majority opinion as to the implications of the investigation.

1. A Woman Speaking at the Evening Services in August, 1998. In the last paragraph of this section, the majority opinion states that "what she [Dr. Linda Eure] said 'crossed the line'". The investigating panel used more precise language in saying that this constituted an offense. That an offense did occur should be made clear. What is not clear from the evidence is that this offense should be charged to TE Wood. On this basis, the writer agrees with the majority that process should not be instituted against TE Wood.

2. TE John Wood's Views Regarding Women and Preaching. The majority states that TE Wood holds to a view that "...excludes women from preaching (authoritative teaching)...". However, this statement leaves much unsaid with respect to TE Wood's view, which essentially defines the word "preaching" so that it becomes impossible for this exclusion to apply to any woman in the PCA.

In his sermon of July 2, 2000, TE Wood stated that a woman can stand in the pulpit on a Sunday morning and read the very same text and say the very same words in the very same setting as a man, and there is no conflict with 1 Timothy 2:12. Nor, by his reasoning, would it conflict with WLC Question and Answer 158. This is because he defines preaching as "teaching with authority which is enforced by the discipline of the church". Since a woman cannot be ordained, she cannot have this authority, and therefore cannot be preaching, no matter what she says from the pulpit.

According to this sermon, if a woman speaks God's truth from the pulpit, it is self-attesting, to be heard, believed and obeyed "and God will hold us accountable for it, but this church will not." If a teaching elder teaches "something" (God's truth, presumably) and a church member does not believe and obey, he can be disciplined. This position seriously distorts our standards for discipline in two ways. First, the "authority which is enforced by the discipline of the church" does not reside in the Teaching Elder, as his definition of preaching implies. It resides in the Session. Second, if a church member refuses to obey God's truth, as we find it in Scripture, he can be disciplined for it, whether it was taught by the Teaching Elder or a Sunday School teacher or anyone else. BCO 29-1 is perfectly clear on this point: "An offense, the proper object of judicial process, is anything in the doctrines or practice of a Church member professing faith in Christ which is contrary to the Word of God."

Furthermore, the average layman, upon hearing a sermon(?) delivered by a non-ordained woman, is going to hear the message from the pulpit and accept it as authoritative. To this typical layman, the definition of preaching can be taken from Webster: "The act or practice of discoursing on a religious subject; hence, a sermon."

<a id="ga29-p78"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=80 printed_page=78 -->

The fact that a message is being delivered from the pulpit lends an authority to the average hearer.

Finally, WCF XXI 5 speaks of sound preaching as a part of religious worship. If a message brought by an unordained person is not preaching, but is some other thing, does this not violate this portion of our Confession?

The very fact that TE Wood preached on this subject, presenting his views, demonstrates his willingness to teach publicly on this subject. Yet this is not a matter for process, since he has not been instructed by his Presbytery not to do so. However, it does underscore the importance of broaching the subject and instructing his Presbytery to so instruct him, which the majority opinion does not do.

/s/ RE Robert H. Miller

## 99-1 CONCURRING OPINION THE JOHN WOOD MATTER

This concurring opinion agrees with the unanimous judgment of the SJC that no indictment of the minister is warranted. First, because the SJC investigation did not raise a strong presumption that the minister was culpable for the woman “crossing the line” in the two evening services in August 1998. Second, the minister’s practice has complied with PCA policy and he has not publicly agitated for anything contrary.

However, this concurring opinion is concerned with one statement in the final SJC report. In the first paragraph under part 2 of the SJC report, under the heading “TE John Wood’s Views Regarding Women and Preaching,” the SJC also reached the conclusion quoted below.

(3) There is no clear evidence that TE Wood’s views are outside the bounds of our Standards.

That statement could result in some confusion and should be more clear. If it simply means the minister’s views are not contrary to any “fundamental of the system of doctrine set forth in the Standards” (BCO 21-5, vow 2) or that his views do not “strike at the vitals of religion and are industriously spread,” (BCO 34-5) then it is clear and reasonable why judicial process should not be instituted.

However, if the statement means that the views are in accord with our Standards, then that conclusion is too generous a characterization. Furthermore, if the views are not outside the bounds of our Standards, then he should not be restricted from “promoting” those views. While the main intent of this opinion is not to critique those views, it will comment on some aspects. (For a comprehensive treatment, see *Women in the Church: A Fresh Analysis of 1 Timothy 2:9-15*, Kostenberger, Schreiner and Baldwin, editors, Grand Rapids: Baker Book House, 1995, 334 pages.)

<a id="ga29-p79"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=81 printed_page=79 -->

It is important to note that the minister candidly and clearly expressed his views to the SJC investigating panel. After the panel was appointed, the minister, on his own initiative, submitted a tape and transcript of a sermon he preached at a Sunday morning worship service at Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church on July 2, 2000. The sermon text was 1 Corinthians 11:1-16 and was titled “Christian Worship: Ministries of Women.” The minister has provided clear evidence of his views. In this regard he has acted commendably and, given the circumstances, courageously. Below is the most pertinent excerpt from the tape of that sermon, in which he explains his views on 1 Timothy 2:12:

Paul is not saying that a woman may not do two things, namely, teach men or have authority over men, it is rather, for those of you who are literary types, it is what’s called, “epexegetical.” It means that the second term is interpreting the first. He is saying a woman may not teach in this way: authoritatively. Namely, she may not do that teaching which is enforced by the discipline of the church, which is uniquely the role of the teaching elder or the pastor or the bishop depending on your polity...

It’s a view that is taught by John Stott, probably the greatest evangelical teacher and exegete, I believe, of the second half of this century. And it is a view that is held within our denomination by the late Jim Boice. Jim just died about two weeks ago of liver cancer. He’s the one that actually discipled me in this view and I put in the front of the bulletin, excerpt from a letter in which Jim claims that point of view. He says that 1 Timothy 2 is only restricting the authoritative teaching of the teaching office. What does that mean?

Can a woman stand and read the very same text and say the very same words in the very same setting as a man and say, “There’s no conflict there”? Yes, I believe so. I argued that before our Presbytery. As it turns out, I’m gonna have to argue it again.

But an analogy would be this: When my children were small, sometimes one of them would look at the other and say, “If you don’t finish your food, you can’t have your dessert.” That was true. And as self-attesting true, it should have been believed and it should have been obeyed, and if it wasn’t obeyed it had consequences. But the other child could look back and say, “You can’t make me,” and that was also true. But I could make him. And that’s what Paul is prohibiting, if I understand him correctly. That’s the view of this church. It is the view of our constitution, though it’s being debated again.

The view is that Paul is prohibiting only that teaching with authority which is enforced by the discipline of the church. So that if we have a woman stand in this pulpit and speak on Sunday morning and she’s speaking God’s truth that is self-attesting, it should be heard, it should be believed and it should be obeyed and God will hold us accountable for it. But this church will not.

Whereas, if one of your teaching elders stands and teaches something and a member of the church says, “Baloney, I don’t believe that. I’m not gonna do it,” you can be

<a id="ga29-p80"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=82 printed_page=80 -->

disciplined, even to the barring from the Lord’s Table, to being barred from the Lord’s Table. That’s the difference. It’s authoritative teaching. And so, as [another prominent PCA minister] has said: in our polity, in our church, a woman may do anything that an unordained man may do. And that means that the only thing that is closed to her in our particular government is serving as an officer or administering the sacraments, but there is nothing in the teaching that is closed.

While the SJC unanimously concluded that what the woman said on those two Sunday evenings “crossed the line,” there is nothing in the record or the sermon indicating the minister shares that conclusion. It is reasonable to conclude his view permits what the woman said on those two Sunday evenings. However, if a line was crossed, it is presumably a line drawn by our Standards. Thus, if the view in question does not draw that same line, how can one conclude the view is within the bounds of our Standards on this subject? If a certain view agrees with our Standards then presumably the practice of that view would not “cross the line.”

The SJC reports the minister stated he holds to a view that “excludes women from preaching (authoritative teaching).” However, the view defines “authoritative teaching” as that which is only done by elders. Therefore, it is not even possible, by that definition, to ever consider a woman to be “preaching/authoritatively teaching” – regardless of what, where, when, and how she speaks.

The SJC also concluded that the minister is not “promoting women preaching the authoritative word of God in worship services.” The minister has certainly not “promoted” or agitated for any particular view. The SJC rightly concludes that neither the minister nor his church has trumpeted this issue. However, by the reasoning in the sermon excerpt, a woman is apparently permitted to take a sermon manuscript from a minister who preached in one church’s worship service and deliver it verbatim in another church’s worship service. Or, apparently, a minister could preach in the first service and a woman could deliver his manuscript verbatim in the second service, in the same church. The message delivered in the first church/service would be backed by the discipline of the church, but the message in the second church/service would not have that same authority. We are told that while the minister would be preaching, she should not be considered to be preaching because she is not ordained. That seems outside the bounds of Larger Catechism 158:

The Word of God is to be preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office.

However, the view in question seems to interpret LC 158 as if it meant the Word of God cannot possibly be preached by any other than such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office. But LC 158 is not a declarative statement. It is an imperative one. It does not say the Word of God is preached only by such. Rather, it says the Word of God is to be preached only by such. The primary emphasis is on the restriction, not the verb.

<a id="ga29-p81"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=83 printed_page=81 -->

In conclusion, the SJC properly judged that no indictment was warranted. The minister does not appear culpable for the details of the occurrences in August 1998, his regular practice has complied with PCA policy, and he has not agitated for anything contrary. Frankly, given the views expressed in his July 2 sermon, he has been commendably restrained. This restraint is seen in his August 25 cover letter accompanying the tape of his July 2 sermon, where he wrote:

This is the only time I have addressed this issue in public, that is, outside of responding to questions in TN Valley Presbytery, but believed that we had reached a point where silence could no longer be sustained.

In a statement to the SJC investigating panel, the 1999 moderator of TN Valley Presbytery, RE Mark Wilson, wrote:

John’s views about the role of women in the church were not the basis for the Session’s decision to ask [the woman] to speak. John’s views have come up in subsequent discussions in presbytery. John was hoping this might turn out to be an encouragement for the PCA to broaden its view in this area. However, after it became clear that the TVP would not accept his position on the issue, John was and continues to be graciously willing to remain in submission to our standards.

Rev. Wood should not be indicted for these views. An indictment would only be warranted if the PCA judged such views as violating a “fundamental of the system of doctrine set forth in the Standards” or as “striking at the vitals of religion and industriously spread.” The SJC has not judged thusly.

But no one should thereby assume the Church endorses such views. Such an assumption would misunderstand the difference between joint and several powers. The paragraph below may clarify such misunderstanding:

Those confused about this distinction between several and joint power believe that a minister’s teaching and the confession of the church must always be the same. Thus, they either become plenary subscriptionists (requiring subscription to everything said in the church’s confessional standards) or they become “floating” confessionalists (believing that every time a court approves a man with exceptions, this exception now becomes part of the church’s confession). Both views are wrong, and both are wrong because they fail to distinguish several and joint power.

A better alternative is to recognize the distinction between joint power and several power. The church’s confession is jointly determined. The PCA confesses only what she has jointly declared or approved. There may be within her individuals who respectfully disagree with portions of that confession. Disagreements, however, are not part of her confession. What such individuals say severally is not to be equated with or confused with what the church says jointly.

<a id="ga29-p82"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=84 printed_page=82 -->

The distinction between plenary subscription and floating confessionalism is, in my judgment, much more helpful than the commonly-heard but unhelpful distinction between “strict” and “system” subscription, neither of which is sufficiently meaningful because both are terms which allow of degree. How strict is strict? How loose is loose? Is one man’s “loose” another man’s “strict”? Who determines what the “system” is? The reason there has been so much discussion of this question, with so little resolution, is that the categories of the discussion themselves virtually doom the discussion to fruitlessness from the outset. In permitting the discussion to take place on these terms, we disguise the reality that some (falsely, and contrary to both the provision for registering exceptions and the confessional statement that councils may err) actually are promoting plenary subscription, while others (equally falsely, and contrary to the entire idea of a written confession) are promoting floating confessionalism.

If we candidly and jointly recognize the ancient distinction between several and joint power, the discussion would end. We would recognize that there is always a potential distinction between what the church says corporately, assembled to settle issues of faith and life, and what an individual officer of the church says individually, speaking from his own conscience with the light given him. (The Church’s Power: Its Relation to Subscription, Dr. T. David Gordon in The Practice of Confessional Subscription, David W. Hall, editor, Lanham, MD: University Press of America, 1995, pages 297, 299.)

In conclusion, the unanimous SJC opinion should not be interpreted as the PCA broadening its view in this area, nor should it be interpreted as the PCA restricting a minister from holding a different view in conscience.

/s/ RE Howard Donahoe

99-3
APPEAL OF JOSEPH GATIS
VS.
NORTHEAST PRESBYTERY

Withdrawn

99-5
APPEAL OF LAWRENCE ROUNTREE
VS.
COVENANT PRESBYTERY

#### I. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS

In May, 1992, Covenant Presbytery licensed Lawrence Rountree and approved him as student supply at the First Presbyterian Church, Indianola, MS. He was approved for ordination and installation as pastor of First Church, Indianola, at presbytery’s October 1992 stated meeting.

<a id="ga29-p83"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=85 printed_page=83 -->

In May, 1993, Presbytery dissolved the pastoral relationship between TE Rountree and First Church.

At its October 1994 stated meeting Presbytery approved TE Rountree remaining on its rolls without call and outside its geographical bounds; TE Rountree having moved to Austin, TX.

In October, 1995, and February, 1997, TE Rountree reported to Presbytery on his general situation as minister without call. In April, 1997, TE Rountree communicated with Presbytery’s Church Care Committee explaining his need for “a letter … recognizing the fact that I am a presbyter in good standing and free to minister the gospel” and requesting permission to labor out of bounds in conducting a Bible study in Austin.

The Committee met on April 22, 1997, and voted to deny TE Rountree’s request. On April 28, 1997, the chairman of South Texas Presbytery Candidates and Credential Committee wrote the chairman of Covenant Presbytery Church Care Committee to request official notification that TE Rountree “is approved to labor outside of your bounds until such time as it is appropriate for us to issue a call to him.”

Covenant Presbytery at its May, 1997, stated meeting voted to delay action of the Church Care Committee’s recommendation until the Committee communicates with South Texas Presbytery and brings a recommendation based on that communication.

On September 24, 1997, Covenant Presbytery’s Church Care Committee sent a pastoral letter to TE Rountree urging him to allow Presbytery to divest him without censure. TE Rountree responded on October 1, 1997, advising that he would respond by the next meeting of Presbytery.

TE Rountree reports to Covenant Presbytery and the chairman of the Church Care Committee responds advising TE Rountree to request that he be dismissed to South Texas Presbytery.

At its stated meeting on February 3, 1998, Covenant Presbytery approved TE Rountree’s request to be dismissed to South Texas Presbytery pending his reception by that Presbytery.

Covenant Presbytery at its May stated meeting rescinded the transfer of TE Rountree and instructed its Church Care Committee to investigate the report of TE Rountree’s divorce. At the October 1998 stated meeting of Presbytery, the committee reported that it had not found the strong presumption of guilt necessary to bring charges against TE Rountree. A motion to transfer TE Rountree to South Texas Presbytery was made. Presbytery instead passed a substitute motion to begin the two meeting process of involuntary divestiture.

On January 11, 1999, TE Rountree submits a request to demit the ministry voluntarily. At its February 1999 stated meeting Covenant Presbytery begins the process of voluntary divestiture of TE Rountree.

On April 1, 1999, TE Rountree advises that his request to demit voluntarily was made under protest; and at its May 1999 stated meeting Presbytery again begins process of involuntary divestiture.

At its October 1999, stated meeting Covenant Presbytery rejects TE Rountree’s request to be granted emeritus and votes to divest him without censure. On October 26, 1999, TE Rountree appealed his divestiture.

<a id="ga29-p84"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=86 printed_page=84 -->

On November 27, 2000, a Standing Judicial Commission Panel heard the appeal.

## II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE

Did Covenant Presbytery err in its divestiture without censure of TE Lawrence Rountree (BCO 34-10)?

## III. JUDGMENT

No. Presbytery acted within its constitutional prerogatives in divesting without censure TE Rountree.

## IV. REASONING AND OPINION

Ministers are required by the Book of Church Order to hold “his membership in the Presbytery within whose geographical bounds he resides unless there are reasons which are satisfactory to his Presbytery why he should not do so.” (BCO 13-2) That same section also provides that when a minister continues on the rolls “without a call to a particular work for a prolonged period, not exceeding three years, the procedure as set forth in BCO 34-10 shall be followed.”

TE Rountree had remained on the rolls of Covenant Presbytery without call and residing out of the geographical bounds of that presbytery since May of 1993 when his pastoral relationship with First Church of Indianola was dissolved. Despite various attempts to start works in Austin, TX, where he now resided, he was unable to do so.

In April of 1997 efforts were initiated to transfer TE Rountree to South Texas Presbytery. Those efforts did not produce results, principally because there was no particular work to which TE Rountree could be called.

In an effort to assist TE Rountree, Covenant Presbytery suggested that he voluntarily demit the ministry and seek readmission to South Texas or another presbytery at such time as he had a valid call to a particular ministry. After several false starts, Covenant Presbytery began the process of involuntary divestiture. TE Rountree then agreed to demit the ministry voluntarily, but subsequently decided that he was doing so under protest. At that point Covenant Presbytery began the two-meeting process of divestiture without censure.

After the first vote, TE Rountree requested that he be allowed to retire. Presbytery denied that request and completed the process of divestiture without censure.

In total, TE Rountree has been on the rolls of Covenant Presbytery without call for over seven years, four year longer than provided for in the Book of Church Order. To date, he still has no prospects for a call to a particular ministry. Residing out of the bounds of Covenant Presbytery, it is difficult for that presbytery to provide oversight and maintain pastoral relations. Covenant Presbytery therefore acted in accordance with the Book of Church Order to fulfill its responsibilities in BCO 13-2 and 34-10.

This opinion was written by John B. White, Jr., with the full concurrence of TEs Ben W. Konopa, Jr. and Dominic A. Aquila.

/s/ TE Ben W. Konopa, Jr., /s/ TE Dominic A. Aquila, /s/ RE John B. White, Jr.

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=87 printed_page=85 -->
