## 99-6
## APPEAL OF ROBERT M. SHIVE
## VS.
## CENTRAL CAROLINA PRESBYTERY

### I. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS

There was a prior Judicial Case (97-9) involving these same parties. Both at the presbytery level (ROC 1) and at the Judicial Panel hearing of this case (99-6) the parties, both Appellant and Appellee, agreed that the Record of this Case (ROC) rightly begins with the General Assembly’s adoption of the decision in SJC Case 97-9 at its 26th General Assembly June 30-July 3, 1998 (26th GA Min. p.134-144).

Any person reading this decision or the decision in SJC Case 97-9 should remember the important fact that Dr. Robert M. Shive is blind. All written material in these cases has to be read to him. It is an unfortunate fact which makes it more difficult for him to grasp the meaning of all written information in these cases.

The Statement of the Issues and Judgment in that Case (97-9) were as follows:

#### Statement of the Issues

1. Was it reversible error, under the particular circumstances, for the Session of Christ Covenant Church to have instituted judicial process against Dr. Shive prior to allowing personal contact between Dr. Shive and the ladies against whom he sinned in an effort to seek the reconciliation of these parties?

2. Did the Session of Christ Covenant Church commit reversible error in not recusing several members of the court who had stated an opinion concerning the case and was there a reversible prejudice present in this case?

3. Was it reversible error, under these particular circumstances, for the prosecutor of the charges filed against Dr. Shive, who was also a member of the court, to be involved in the court’s deliberations?

<a id="ga29-p86"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=88 printed_page=86 -->

4. Were there procedural and substantive errors committed by Central Carolina Presbytery in this case which require a remand in this case?
5. Did Central Carolina Presbytery err by affirming the decision of Christ Covenant Church Session’s infliction upon Dr. Robert M. Shive the highest censure based on the Record of the Case?

## Judgment in the Case

1. No. The Session acted under its authority of BCO 31-7 when it instituted the judicial process without allowing a personal meeting between Dr. Shive and the ladies against whom he committed sin.
2. No. The record does not reflect that the actions mandating recusal under BCO 32-17 occurred. Furthermore, two of the three members of the court complained of by Dr. Shive voluntarily recused themselves from the court prior to the court’s final deliberations as to the judgment and censure to be imposed.
3. No. While we believe it was an error of judgment and an error of application of BCO 31-7, especially in light of BCO 32-19, for the prosecutor to have been a part of the court during its deliberations, we do not believe that this error requires a reversal of the judgment and a remand for a new trial in this particular case.
4. No. We do not find that errors were committed by Central Carolina Presbytery requiring reversal and remand.
5. Yes. Central Carolina Presbytery erred in affirming and the Session of Christ Covenant Church erred in going straight to the highest censure of excommunication in light of the lack of compelling evidence in the Record of the Case to reflect that Dr. Shive is contumacious or impenitent. See BCO 30-4 and 33-3. Pursuant to BCO 42-9, we reverse that portion of the judgment by the Session of Christ Covenant Church inflicting the censure of excommunication on Dr. Shive, and we remand the matter back to the Session of Christ Covenant Church for it to impose the censure of indefinite suspension from the Sacraments upon Dr. Robert M. Shive in accordance with BCO 36-5, and with the instruction that, until satisfactory evidence of repentance is given to the Session of Christ Covenant Church, to impose such conditions concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of Christ Covenant Church as the Session may find.”

Under BCO 14-7 this decision in SJC 97-9 was “binding and conclusive on the parties” who are the same parties as in this case. In SJC 97-9, the Appellant, Dr. Shive, alleged that the Session of Christ Covenant Church (Session) erred in 5 areas. These 5 areas are listed as “Issues” in the decision (supra). This SJC 97-9 decision sustained the verdicts of the Session and the Presbytery in the first four allegations and issues. In the 5th area, the decision in SJC 97-9 sustained in part Appellant’s specification of error. It reversed “that portion of the judgment by Session of Christ Covenant inflicting the censure of excommunication.” The decision did sustain all other portions of the 5th area, which included the verdict of guilty against Dr. Shive in 4 areas. (ROC 33 and 42-43)

<a id="ga29-p87"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=89 printed_page=87 -->

On January 9, 1998, the full Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) reviewed and affirmed the decision of the Panel in SJC 97-9, which was later in June-July 1998 adopted by the General Assembly (GA).

Less than one week after the Full SJC reviewed and affirmed the Panel’s decision, the Session began to implement the decision on January 16, 1998 by assigning a 4 man (later 2 added) Discipline Commission (DC) to work with Dr. Shive (ROC 26). The Session immediately followed the recommendation of the SJC by reducing the penalty on Dr. Shive from excommunication to “indefinite suspension from the Sacraments.” On March 6, 1998 the DC informed Dr. Shive (who is blind), in writing of this action by the Session, to-wit:

“Whereas you, Dr. Robert M. Shive, member of Christ Covenant Presbyterian Church, are convicted by sufficient proof of the sins of sexual immorality, abuse, licentiousness, premeditated sexual exploitation, lying and bearing false witness and scandalous living, we the Christ Covenant Session, in the name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus Christ, do now declare you suspended from the Sacraments of the Church, until you give satisfactory evidence of repentance. (ROC 53) (Emphasis added).

In the 16 months from January, 1998 to April, 1999 the DC met 11 times, had numerous communications with Dr. Shive, and met formally with him 4 times (ROC pages 42-77, summarized ROC pages 16-18, and ROC 104).

In the course of these meetings and communications with Dr. Shive, it became obvious early on (from such documents as the April 1998 letter of Dr. Shive to Mr. Bernie Lawrence, Chairman of the DC (ROC 56-57) in which Dr. Shive refers to his meeting with the DC on March 21, 1998) that there was a “fundamental disagreement between you and me and that obvious sticking point is in the interpretation and intent of the SJC final document.” (ROC 56).

In this letter to the DC, Dr. Shive refers to a telephone conversation with the Chairman of the DC in which the Chairman indicated he had telephone conversations with RE Tom Leopard and TE Dominic Aquila (SJC Panel Member in SJC 97-9) and with Dr. Paul Gilchrist, Stated Clerk of the GA. Because of this “fundamental difference” between the Session’s and DC’s interpretation of the SJC decision in Case 97-9 and Dr. Shive’s interpretation, the Chairman of the DC sought assistance from these individuals as to the proper interpretation of the decision. Dr. Shive interpreted the SJC decision in Case 97-9 as reversing the verdicts of guilt on all 4 charges (ROC 56-57).

Writing to Mr. Bernie Lawrence, Dr. Shrive stated:

“You clearly indicated to me that you believed the SJC’s document of January 1988 did in no way countermand Christ Covenant’s position on my guilt.” (ROC 56).

The DC in its letter to Dr. Shive of March 6, 1998 stated its interpretation of the decision in Case 97-9 as follows:

<a id="ga29-p90"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=90 printed_page=null -->

“We have prayerfully framed our approach to discipline and shepherd you in a
way that we hope will move you to repentance and protect, as well as, promote,
the peace and purity of Christ’s Church. In so doing, we are working with the
understanding that General Assembly, based on the record of the Case, has
altered the censure of excommunication to indefinite suspension. Neither
presbytery nor General Assembly have overturned any of the Session’s
judgments with respect to your guilt. Accordingly, this letter is intended to
impose the censure of indefinite suspension from the sacraments and establish
several conditions and expectations around the censure that we believe are
prudent and necessary.” (ROC 53).

because of these differences in interpretation of the decision in case 97-9 and
cause of the fact that Dr. Shive is blind. Dr. shive in his letter of april 1, 1998,
idressed to Mr. Bernie Lawrence, who was chairman of the DC, requested the
llowing:

“You did indicate tome in our meeting of march 21 that the three above
mentioned elders (Dr. Gilchrist, TE Aquila and RE Leopard) had been contacted
by telephone for their interpretation of that document (the decision in Case 97-
9). at the risk of sounding legalistic, since I was not privy to those
conversations, nor do I have any written documentation from these three
gentlemen. . . . I would like you to supply me with written documents from
elders Leopard, Aquila and Gilchrist.” (ROC 56-57).

After this request for “written documentation,” Mr. Bernie Lawrence, as
h airman of the DC, did forward Dr. Shive’s letter of april 1, 1998 (ROC 56-57) to Dr.
lul Gilchrist, stated clerk of the General Assembly. On April 16, 1998 Dr. Gilchrist
plied in writing in response to questions on Case 97-9, stating:

Let me put in writing what I said to you on the phone re: the import oft hes jc
decision. First, the heart of the decision is in II. statement of the issues and
III. judgment in th ec ase. The first four Issues were answered in the negative
in the Judgment. It upheld the Presbytery and the Session. Issue 5 was decided
against the presbytery only because it considered the penalty of
excommunication was too harsh for the sins of Dr. Shive as delineated in the
record of the Case. Hence that part was remanded back to the Session to
impose the censure of indefinite suspension. In doing so, the SJC did not
reverse the charges of sin and the finding of guilt against Dr. Shive. That the sin
and guilt were not overturned is evidenced by the recommendation of indefinite
suspension from the Lord’s Supper as well as by the further “instruction that,
until satisfactory evidence of repentance is given to the session of christ
Covenant Church” that the Session had a right to impose any further “conditions
concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of christ covenant church as the
Session may find.”

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=91 printed_page=null -->

00-5
complaint of robert H. king
V S.
EVANGEL PRESBYTERY

This case was abandoned by complainant.

<a id="ga29-p94"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=94 printed_page=null -->

that judgement. The minister then filed an appeal with the General Assembly (Case 99-3). In addition, the prosecutor in the original trial (Adams) filed a complaint with the Presbytery relating to the case. In January 2000, the Presbytery ruled the complaint out of order on the grounds of BCO 43-1. Adams then filed complaint with the General Assembly, and in May 2000, the SJC officers ruled the complaint administratively out of order on the same grounds. However, in July 2000, the appellant in Case 99-3 filed a request to withdraw his appeal. At the October 2000 meeting of the full SJC, the appellant's withdrawal request was approved. Then, although the appeal had been withdrawn, the SJC still considered the complaint as being out of order on the grounds of BCO 43-1.

Unfortunately, the current language in BCO 43-1 is somewhat ambiguous. It says a complaint is not permitted in a judicial case in which an appeal is "taken." However, it seems such a complaint should only be precluded while an appeal is "pending." If an appeal is withdrawn, then in a real sense it is no longer "taken." Furthermore, it does not seem reasonable that the filing of an appeal, per se, should forever preclude any and all review of anything related to the case.

## Appellate Review of Acquittals

With good reason, our BCO does not explicitly authorize a higher court to reverse a "not-guilty" verdict of a lower court. The higher court cannot declare an acquitted person "guilty" on the charges for which the lower court acquitted him. Nor does the BCO explicitly authorize a higher court to direct a lower court to retry an accused on the same charges for which the lower court acquitted him. Therefore, it was not within the power of the SJC to grant the relief requested by the complainant in this case and thus his complaint was rightly judged as being out of order.

However, should an acquittal automatically and absolutely render everything in the case off-limits for appellate review? I don't believe it should. What if the acquittal was substantially based on an erroneous interpretation of our Standards? For example, what if a woman were charged with seeking a divorce without biblical grounds and then was acquitted because the court believed "incompatibility" was grounds according to WCF 24:6? Would the husband thereby have no chance for appellate review of that lower court opinion? Or, what if the Session finds the wife "guilty" but upon her appeal the Presbytery reverses that judgment and declares her "not guilty." Would the husband have standing to seek SJC review of that Presbytery's "not guilty" verdict?

What if the trial procedures violated the BCO in a way that may have substantially effected the verdict? For example, what if the lower court did not allow the prosecutor to cross-examine defense witnesses (32-13, 35-5), or permitted judges to sit on the court who should have been disqualified (32-17), or allowed the accused to use paid professional counsel (32-19), or allowed defense witnesses to be present during the testimony of other witnesses, against the objection of the prosecution (35-4), or allowed members of the court to testify for the defense and then also to sit as judges, against the objection of the prosecution (35-11), or some combination of such errors?

Should an appellate court have the power to "set-aside" a lower court's acquittal verdict if such verdict was reached as a direct result of significant errors of

<a id="ga29-p95"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=95 printed_page=null -->

00-2
COMPLAINT OF PHILIP J. ADAMS
VS.
NORTHEAST PRESBYTERY

The Standing Judicial Commission finds that this case is out of order on the grounds that it is a complaint about a matter that is the subject of an appeal in another case (BCO 43-1) and because the complaint requests the relief that an accused be re-tried on matters he has been acquitted of by his presbytery.

17 concur, 1 concur in part, 1 dissent in part, 0 abstain, 5 absent

Concurring Opinion
2000-2
TE Phillip J. Adams
vs.
Northeast Presbytery

This concurring opinion agrees with the unanimous judgment of the SJC that the complaint is administratively out of order. However, I disagree with the first ground cited for that judgment. In addition, while I agree with the second ground, I believe the PCA should clarify the BCO on the issue of appellate review of acquittals.

The SJC ruled the complaint out of order on two grounds: first, because no complaint is allowable in a judicial case in which an appeal is taken (BCO 43-1), and second, because the complainant requested the relief that the accused be re-tried on matters for which he had been acquitted by his Presbytery.

Complaint in a Case of Appeal

In a case of process, a judicial commission of Northeast Presbytery judged a minister guilty on 3 of 12 charges. In September 1999, Northeast Presbytery approved

<a id="ga29-p96"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=96 printed_page=null -->

persons who had no other means of approaching a higher court could be brought.

10. In the light of the foregoing, the SJC finds that the Philadelphia Presbytery did not act in an unconstitutional manner in adopting the report of its Commission.

11. Additionally, the SJC finds in favor of the Presbytery in regard to the specific allegations of wrongdoing listed by the complainant. First, the Presbytery’s Commission seriously considered the concerns and assertions of Mr. Lachman. Mr. Lachman himself appeared before the Commission, though not in any adversarial context. The fact that the Commission listened to all parties as it investigated the matters was not a violation of any rules of evidence or procedure; rather it was an affirmation that the Commission seriously heard the parties. Secondly, the fact that the Commission assigned the recording of the memorial to the local Session rather than to the Presbytery was an attempt, so the Presbytery asserts, to ensure that, should the matters contained in the memorial arise again, the court of original jurisdiction would have ready access to the memorial. It would be the court best equipped to take up these matters again, should that be necessary. Thirdly, the Presbytery did not act wrongly when it decided to spreading the text of the memorial on the Presbytery’s minutes. There is no constitutional requirement for the minutes to set out in full the contents of any letter or memorial. Further, the Presbytery took this action in an attempt to avoid unnecessary publication of speculative assertions. Fourthly, while it is correct, as the complainants assert, that the Commission found no evidence of “gross unconstitutional proceedings,” this is not a ground of error on the part of the Presbytery, but rather it is the Presbytery’s considered finding after investigation. It is a finding with which this Commission agrees.

/s/ TE Robert M. Ferguson, /s/ TE John “Chip” McArthur, /s/ RE Tom Leopard

<a id="ga29-p97"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=97 printed_page=null -->

matters of procedure raised by the JBC were valid. Instead, the Presbytery adopted its Commission’s report.

6. In reviewing this matter the SJC notes that the Presbytery’s Commission did follow a very thorough procedure of “investigation” (see above in “1. Summary of the Facts”, paragraph 6-7). It is of more than passing interest to note that last year’s General Assembly assigned to the SJC the task of preparing appropriate procedures for hearing memorials. There was, in the view of General Assembly, a need to establish procedures for dealing with such matters. The SJC may find informative and helpful the procedure adopted by the Philadelphia Commission in this regard.

7. Whatever the precise nature of a “memorial” it is not in the same formal category as a judicial appeal or a complaint. This case brings that matter before us rather profoundly. The Lachman “memorial” came about because Mr. Lachman had failed to meet the formal requirements to submit, in a timely manner, a judicial complaint against his Session when it considered and acted upon the matters contained in his memorial. Thus, having failed to follow the correct procedures for establishing a “judicial case,” he brought a “memorial.” *BCO* 40-5 is a provision that gives the higher court an opportunity to “investigate” a matter that may or may not have been possible to review via another means, such as appeal or complaint. "It should be noted that charges against the Session of Calvary Presbyterian Church, included in the memorial, were essentially the same as those in the complaint of Lachman by letter to the Session dated May 18, 1998 (ROC 3,4) to which the Session responds by letter of June 11, 1998 (ROC 7,8). Therefore, it is clear that the Presbytery was not under any requirement to address the charges in the original complaint and raised again in the memorial but merely to investigate whether the Session was guilty of any "important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings" in its handling of the complaint.

8. It is clear from the wording of *BCO* 40-5 that, while all memorials must be received and considered, for a higher court to take up a matter set out in a memorial the matter must be of a very grave nature, namely “gross unconstitutional error,” or “important delinquency.” Thus mere errors or delinquencies must rise to the standard needed to empower a higher court to take up a matter that could have otherwise come before the higher court. The Presbytery’s Commission and subsequently the Presbytery, after due investigation, did not find that such errors or delinquencies had occurred.

9. Indeed the Presbytery’s Commission noted the above context in which Mr. Lachman brought his “memorial” and observed that this context could raise the question of whether or not the Memorial itself was out of order. The Presbytery and its Commission chose not to find the memorial out of order but to engage in a serious consideration of Mr. Lachman’s concerns. In this same regard it is of interest to note that when this SJC some years ago, prepared proposed amendments to its Manuel to provide for a process for hearing memorials (this section was withdrawn by the SJC at the General Assembly) the draft amendments the SJC prepared provided that only a memorial submitted by

<a id="ga29-p98"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=98 printed_page=null -->

that requires the court to follow formal judicial procedures, as is the case when a court is hearing an appeal or a complaint. While memorials concern serious matters of allegedly unconstitutional conduct, they, like the review of Presbytery records, are a method of getting a matter before the higher court. If any analogy may be taken from the processing of judicial cases it might be possible to liken a memorial to the investigation process that precedes the instigation of formal process against a minister when a matter concerning the conduct of minister is brought to the attention of a Presbytery under BCO 31-2. Under those provisions, the Presbytery is called to investigate to ascertain if a formal trial is warranted. Thus in the absence of other more specific details in the BCO or some other constitutional-like documents (such as by-laws of the Presbytery itself), the Presbytery needed to act on the matter in some serious and responsible manner but was not required to instigate formal process. Formal process should only be instigated against a person (this is what was the heart of the plea in Mr. Lochman's Memorial) after an investigation has established ground for such process. In a more general context, the mere receipt of a memorial does not instigate any proceeding before the higher court; rather it calls for some serious investigation that would enable to higher court to make an informed decision as to how act on the memorial.

4. The members of the Judicial Business Committee (JBC) of the Philadelphia Presbytery (who largely make up the number of the complainants in this case) asserted, before this SJC, a memorial needs to be "heard." For them the meaning of the word "heard" involves the taking of evidence in the formal judicial sense and providing a formal judicial and adversarial hearing. For the Complainants this was the first major issue they were bringing to the SJC. The following of strict formal process was what they recommended to the Presbytery's Commission that was appointed by the Presbytery to "hear" the matter of the Lachman memorial. When the Presbytery received the report of its Commission, these concerns for a formal process of adjudication were raised before the Presbytery. The Presbytery at that time was advised of the detailed process through which the Commission went to receive a ROC, to meet with the parties involved in the memorial and to then, after careful reflection, to make its recommendations. The Presbytery chose to adopt the actions of its Commission being satisfied, by a large majority vote, that the Commission had heard and considered the matters raised in the memorial.

5. The second major concern of the complainants was that the Presbytery's Commission had received and only in part acted upon the advice of the Presbytery's JBC. The JBC believed that the Commission was duty bound to follow any advice rendered by the JBC. That advice would have entailed the Commission proceeding as if a memorial was a judicial case at trial. Again this concern and assertion of the JBC was raised before the Presbytery when it received and acted upon the report of its Commission. It was the Presbytery that had clothed the Commission with its original "commission" and it was within the power of the Presbytery to reject the report of its Commission if it felt the

<a id="ga29-p99"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=99 printed_page=null -->

to advise the members in any way of the content or issues raised in the Memorial.

7. Most grievously, failed to deal with any of the matters raised in the Memorial, especially;

a. By the Commission’s failure to identify for itself or Presbytery what the Memorial identifies as, “gross unconstitutional proceedings”,

b. By Failing to answer affirmatively or negatively the six allegations of error raised in the Memorial,

c. By ignoring the counsel of Presbytery’s own Judicial Business Committee, mandated in at the March 13, 1999 meeting of Presbytery to instruct the Commission in handling the matter of the Memorial, especially since the counsel given by said Committee was to examine and answer the six issues raised in the Memorial.

d. When members of the Presbytery Commission now acknowledge that the issues raised in the Memorial were not examined by the Commission.

13. Neither of the parties to this case submitted briefs to the Panel. Nor were there any challenges to the Record of the Case (ROC).

## II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE

Did Philadelphia Presbytery act in an unconstitutional manner in adopting the report of Commission?

## III. JUDGMENT IN THE CASE

No.

## IV. THE REASONING AND OPINION

1. Before turning to the specific details of the issues raised by the complainants, it is important to first draw a distinction and then to set out a summary of the three major issues. The parties at the hearing drew the following distinction and identified these major issues.

2. The distinction. As the case before the SJC is a case between members of the Presbytery and the Presbytery itself, it was very important to both parties to this case that the SJC be aware that the specific issues raised in Mr. Lachman’s memorial are not before the SJC. The matters before the SJC in this case concern the methodology for dealing with a memorial and not the specific issues raised in the memorial.

3. The heart of the issues raised in this case concern the interpretation of the nature, purpose and method of dealing with memorials. BCO 40-5 clearly provides for “memorials” to be a means of getting a matter before a higher court. BCO 40-5 does not indicate that the mere receipt of a memorial sets before the higher court a judicial case. A judicial case is a matter before a court

<a id="ga29-p100"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=100 printed_page=null -->

Commission reminds the Memorializer and the Session that no further appeal or complaint is appropriate in this entire matter.

The minutes show that after rejecting a substitute motion not to receive the report of the Commission, to dismiss the Commission on the Lachman Memorial with thanks, and to hear the Lachman Memorial as a Committee as a whole, the Presbytery voted to order that the report of the Commission be entered upon the minutes (BCO 15-1), “thus recognizing it as Presbytery’s action through its Commission.”

9. On May 25, 1999 a Complaint signed by TE Erwin Morrison*, TE David Brewer*, TE Mark Herzer, RE John Harley*, and RE Ray Williams* (*members of the Presbytery Judicial Business Committee) against the May 8, 1999 action of Philadelphia Presbytery in adopting the recommendation of the Commission on the Lachman Memorial was submitted to the Presbytery.

10. At its September 9, 1999 Stated Meeting, Philadelphia Presbytery acted to (1) Find the complaint in order; (2) Docket one hour, 10:00 to 11:00 a.m., at the January 8, 2000, stated meeting, to consider the complaint on the floor; and (3) Appoint the original commission which had considered the memorial, with the addition of TEs Will Barker and Phil Ryken, a committee to prepare a proposed response of the Presbytery to the complaint (BCO 43-8).

11. At its January 8, 2000 Stated Meeting, Philadelphia Presbytery moved to consideration of the complaint. RE Lachman was granted privilege of the floor. A motion was made and seconded to sustain the complaint. After presentations by complainants and respondents and discussion, the motion to sustain the complaint was defeated (11 ayes, 35 nays).

12. On February 4, 2000 a complaint, dated February 2, 2000 and signed by Philadelphia Presbytery members TE Erwin Morrison, TE David Brewer, TE Mark Herzer, RE John Harley, and RE Ray Williams against the action of Philadelphia Presbytery in denying their complaint on the Lachman Memorial, was received by the Standing Judicial Commission of the PCA through the Office of the Stated Clerk. Specifically the complaint alleges that the Presbytery by its action on the Lachman Memorial did:

1. Act with disregard to the rights of a constituent to be heard.
2. Entertain and approve unconstitutional speculation in the report of its Commission elected to hear the matter of the Memorial.
3. Make an inappropriate and unconstitutional assignment of the Memorial to the minutes of a lower court.
4. Endorse a serious misunderstanding of the *Book of Church Order, 40-5*.
5. Sustain a fallacious argument about the context of the Memorial.
6. Fail to enter the Memorial upon the minutes of the court to which it was sent, or to make it available in any form to the members of Presbytery or

<a id="ga29-p101"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=101 printed_page=null -->

All four recommendations were adopted as presented. in addition a motion from the
floor was adopted to direct the Calvary Church Session to “turn over to the commission
all relevant documents pertaining to the matter.

6. The Commission met on April 10, 1999 to consider the Lachman Memorial.
Meeting alone, the commission reviewed the record of the case (ROC), with each
member receiving a copy. After individual members were given time to review the
copied material, the members assembled together fora discussion of central issues. The
Commission then met with the Presbytery’s JBC to receive its advice on hearing the
Memorial. After hearing from the JBC, the JBC was dismissed with prayer and thanks.
the commission then met with the full session of calvary Church, Willow Grove.
the minutes of the commissions how that TE jeff hutchinson presented the response
of the session answering the issues raised by the memorial by appealing tor oc and, in
addition, to testimony by various members of the Session. After questions from the
commission and answers from various members of the Session, the Session was
dismissed with prayer.

7. following the dismissal of the Session, the Commission decided to contact RE
Lachman to ask that he meet with the Commission. After a brief recess, the
Commission met with RE Lachman. RE Lachman was not asked for a statement (the
commission believing that the memorial wash is view of the situation), but was asked
several questions of clarification. After those questions were answered, RE Lachman
was dismissed with prayer and thanks. After determining that its report would be
drafted and circulated and discussed by phone, the Commission then adjourned with
prayer.

8. At the May 8, 1999 stated meeting of Presbytery, the report of the commission
on the Lachman Memorial was presented. the conclusion of the report was as follows:
1. the memorial is properly before the commission in spite of it sown
concerns that a better way may have been followed to address the
concerns cited in the Memorial.
2. the memorial should be printed in the minutes of the appropriate court
of jurisdiction, which is the session of calvary church willow Grove.
This court is where the Memorial should have first been lodged. It is
here that its context is plain. It is here and here alone that the concerns
memorialized in perpetuity are best recorded and will cause the least
unwarranted harm.
3. As said above, the commission finds no indication of grossly
unconstitutional proceedings, although the Commission does find itself
deeply concerned by unsuccessful efforts at Christian reconciliation on
both sides.
4. Since the Commission is duly authorized to conclude this matter, we thus
render our unanimous judgment as indicated, and stay all further
proceedings in this matter in accord with BCO 40-5. Thus the

<a id="ga29-p102"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=102 printed_page=null -->

2. On November 3, 1998 the Session received a Complaint from RE Lachman alleging that the “Session has failed to act in regard to the Complainant’s request that the Session informally admonish...” (the other elder). The Complaint further asked the Session for specific relief in the matter. In a Session meeting also on November 3, 1998 the Complaint was given initial consideration, and two ruling elders from the Session were appointed as a committee to meet jointly with the two elders to “attempt to effect reconciliation.” This meeting was held on November 5, 1998. On November 19, 1998, in a Called Meeting of the Session, it was ruled that RE Lachman’s “November 3, 1998 complaints against the Session were not submitted in the timely manner required by The Book of Church Order.” A letter to RE Lachman responding to his complaints and explaining the Session’s ruling was approved. Further, a long joint letter to RE Lachman and the other elder offering further warnings and counsel on the whole matter was approved.

3. In a long letter to the Session dated December 18, 1998, RE Lachman responded to the Session’s most recent letters in which he detailed his disagreement with the counsel of the Session contained in those letters.

4. On that same day, December 18, 1998, RE Lachman submitted a memorial to Philadelphia Presbytery requesting that the Presbytery “take notice of the grossly unconstitutional proceedings of the Session of Calvary Presbyterian Church, Willow Grove” and that the Presbytery follow the procedure mandated in BCO 40-5.

5. At the January 9, 1999 Stated Meeting of Philadelphia Presbytery a motion carried to refer the matter of the Lachman Memorial to the Presbytery’s Judicial Business Committee (JBC) for its recommendation. The JBC brought its recommendation to the March 13, 1999 Stated Meeting of the Presbytery. Its recommendation was as follows:

1. That Presbytery appoint a commission and cite the court alleged to have offended (Calvary Presbyterian Church Session) “to appear by representative or in writing...and to show what it has done or failed to do...” (BCO 40-5) in the matter in question;

2. In regard to the memorial received from RE David Lachman, Presbytery adopted a motion that all parties are not to distribute any material outside of the membership of the Calvary Presbyterian Church or of the Judicial Business Committee until such time as it so directs;

3. That the Presbytery adopt the Judicial Business Committee’s interpretation of the Book of Church Order, 15-1, that this is not a judicial commission; AND that the decision of the commission shall be final and “stay all further proceedings...” in the matter as per BCO 40-5;

4. That the Presbytery direct the commission to meet and be prepared to present its final report at the May, 1999 meeting of Presbytery.

<a id="ga29-p103"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=103 printed_page=null -->

day, is to be taken in a metaphorical or indeterminate sense, we ask to be shown where else in our Confessional Standards do we take words in such a sense? The nature of our Confessional Standards is that they confess dogma in language as clear and unambiguous as possible. Recent attempts to stretch the meaning of the phrase, in the space of six days, to cover many novel and mutually conflicting concepts regarding the length of the days of creation serve to undermine the very purpose for which we have Confessional Standards. Should these endeavors continue and prevail, our Confessional Standards will attain the status of foggy and irrelevant relics, rather than clear and vibrant expressions of our faith.

It should be obvious by the judicial cases cited in the SJC majority opinion, by the recent Study Committee on the Days of Creation, and by the most recent motion adopted at last year's General Assembly relative to this matter that we in the PCA are in confusion on this question. However, we are a Confessional Church, bound by the written words of our Confessional Standards. If those words no longer express what we mean, there are ways to amend the Confessional Standards so that they say what we mean. Meanwhile, members of the SJC are bound by vows to, "judge according to the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America...," and not to countenance a stretching of the terms of the Constitution to the point of meaninglessness in an endeavor to cover our current confusion.

/s/ William Harrell

00-1

COMPLAINT OF ERWIN MORRISON, ET AL.

VS.

PHILADELPHIA PRESBYTERY

#### I. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS

1. RE Dr. David Lachman is a member of Calvary Presbyterian Church in Willow Grove, Pennsylvania. At the time of the events described below, he was not serving as a member of the Session. On May 18, 1998 RE Lachman wrote the Session asking for specific relief in the matter of his dispute with another ruling elder of the church who also was not serving on the Session. The Session responded with a letter dated June 11, 1998 offering counsel from the Scriptures and urging RE Lachman to consider dropping his accusations. On August 4, 1998 a letter from the Session was sent to RE Lachman inviting him to a Called Session Meeting on September 8, 1998. That meeting was held as scheduled with the Session meeting with RE Lachman for one and one half hours, and with the other elder for an additional forty minutes. On September 23, 1998 the Session sent a joint letter to RE Lachman and the other elder indicating the Session's "aim to provide a response to both...in as timely a manner as possible." On October 6, the Session met and considered its response to the September 8, 1998 Called Meeting.

<a id="ga29-p104"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=104 printed_page=null -->

Yet, despite the considerable evidence cited above, upon which the discourse meaning of the phrase *in the space of six days* should be determined, the opinion maintains that determining the discourse meaning of that phrase is challenging, due to there being no record of debate regarding the phrase at the Westminster Assembly. It is only challenging when one resists granting the conclusion of the evidence at hand.

4. There is mistaken dependence upon contemporary Reformed positions on the days of creation. The positions of individuals in the PCA and seminaries respected by the PCA is one thing. What our *Confessional Standards* say and mean is quite another thing. The former represents the research and tentative conclusions of individuals and institutions of learning. By their nature, such views are in process of development. Regarding seminaries, an informed allowance of differing views may allow and promote critical examination, exploration, and discovery. However, our *Confessional Standards* are by nature expressions of relatively settled dogma. They do not claim inerrancy, and thus are subject to amendment. But neither are they so fluid and ephemeral as to accommodate an emerging host of novel and mutually contradictory views on the days of creation. The fact that there is and has been diversity in the denomination on the question of the interpretation of Genesis 1 is completely irrelevant. It tells us what the church at large has been willing to tolerate. It does not tell us what the *Confession* says. The decision of whether or not an exception is necessary in a particular case, if it is to have any meaning at all, has to be based on what the *Confession* says, not on what divergences are currently generally assumed as permissible.

5. The cases cited as Judicial precedent on the days of creation do not support the SJC majority's decision that Eastern Carolina Presbytery did not err when it deemed the licentiate's view on creation days not to be an exception to the *Confessional Standards*. Only *Bowen vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery* and *Mt. Carmel Session vs. New Jersey Presbytery* can reasonably be construed as lending support to the SJC majority decision. Of those two, *Mt. Carmel*, a quite divided decision within the SJC and the Assembly, contains the strongest and clearest expression of support for the current decision in these words: "it is by no means clear that the Westminster phrase 'in/within the space of six days' must be interpreted as involving 24-hour days." These words were not, in fact, written by the SJC, but rather by New Jersey Presbytery (*M26GA*, 1998, p. 107). To this, we respond that (1) church courts can and do err, as in this cited instance, and (2) the SJC opinion in *Mt. Carmel* states: "In this instance the SJC affirms it is not making a judgment with regard to the interpretation of the days of creation..." (*M26GA*, 1998, p. 107).

## IV. Conclusion

The original intent of the *Confessional Standards* is clear. The licentiate in question espouses a view of the days of creation which is out of accord with that original intent and with the clear meaning of the phrase, *in the space of six days*. Therefore, as a matter of integrity and for the sake of our doing all things decently and in order, his view should be deemed an exception to our *Standards*. If it be argued that the word,

<a id="ga29-p105"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=105 printed_page=null -->

1. The opening sentence of the "Reasoning and Opinion" section, states that "the Record does not indicate exactly what particular view the licentiate holds..." in this matter. Yet, the SJC majority has deemed the licentiate's view as being within the parameters of the Confessional Standards of the PCA without their knowing what that view is. We believe and declare from our deliberations in the SJC that the majority does not intend to countenance any and every view of the days of creation. Neither has the SJC determined henceforth to overturn per se judgments of other Presbyteries wherein non-literal views may be judged to be exceptions to our Standards. Yet, the majority decision to affirm the action of Eastern Carolina Presbytery, without the SJC knowing this licentiate's views, except as it is recorded above, leaves the decision open to be wrongly construed that the SJC countenances any and every view of the days of creation.

2. It is a misleading overstatement to assert that the conservative Reformed community has generally assumed for more than 150 years "that the Confession articulates no particular position on the length of the creation days." Whatever individual writers such as Shaw, Hodge, Mitchell, and Warfield may have expressed on this question, they spoke as individuals, not as church courts. Nor is it surprising that their conservative brethren did not challenge them on this matter, when there were, in their day, so many other more crucial battles being waged in their church courts. Still less surprising is it that they were not challenged by the liberal consensus in their denominations. More to the point, whatever attitudes prevailed in the PCUS, PCUSA, and Church of Scotland, our concern is solely with the more truly conservative and Reformed PCA. The fact that there has lately been contention within the PCA over this issue can be fairly attributed to the very recent articulation and emergence within the PCA of non-literal views on creation days.

3. There is erroneous assertion that the original intent of the Westminster divines cannot be determined on this matter. The opinion admits that it "seems no evidence has been found of any view other than the Calendar Day in the writings of individual Westminster divines." The opinion further admits that "Recent studies...have documented at least five divines, and perhaps as many as twenty-one, who affirmed the Calendar Day view." The opinion rightly cites wording from the report of the PCA Study Committee on Divorce and Remarriage as follows:

It is a sound principle that the constitutional documents should be interpreted according to their original intent. For creeds and confessions to function as subordinate norms, they must be read according to the grammatico-historical method of interpretation. Confessional subscription is not to anything that the words can be taken to mean, but rather to the discourse meaning of the text...The term discourse meaning points us back to the event or act of writing which is contextually informed and determinative for meaning (M20GA, 1992, pp. 59,60 and Appendix O, pp. 521-636).

<a id="ga29-p111"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=106 printed_page=111 -->

integrity requires the further specification that these 6 days were each of 24 hours in
length. I do not add this further specification. I wish to state my view for the
presbytery. I believe that Genesis 1 narrates the history of creation giving the actual
events in their true sequence and marking them by their occurrence on six distinct days.
This is inerrant history, and the text is perspicuous in its historical account. In my
attention to the text, i seethe length of these days as no more specified than the season
of the year; thus, restrained by the authority of scripture i cannot accede to the
specification of 24 hours.

II. exceptional nature of the l icentiate's views
The licentiate in question asserts: "In my attention to the text, i seethe length of
these days as no more specified than the season of the year; thus, restrained by the
authority of Scripture. i cannot accede to the specification of 24 hours." This assertion
takes exception to the confessional standards of the PCA for the following reasons:

1. When the licentiate asserts that the view maintaining that the days in Genesis 1
were of 24 hour duration is a specification of the word day unwarranted in both
Scripture and our Standards, it is the licentiate who is mistaken. it is inherent in the
primary meaning of the word, day, that a duration of 24 hours is intended. It is the
denial of this plain meaning which is a refinement or specification of the word which
goes beyond the meaning intended in Scripture and our Standards. Such further
refinement, namely, that the word, day, betaken ina metaphorical or non-specified
sense, may be legitimate in certain cases, but such a necessity is neither expressed nor
implied in the stated view of the licentiate. Without elaboration or further Scriptural
support, he baldly maintains that so far as Genesis 1 is concerned, the secondary sense
of the word, day, is the given sense, and that anyone maintaining otherwise is neither
giving sufficient "attention to the text," nor properly "restrained by the authority of
Scripture."

2. It follows from the above that the licentiate's view, there is strong insinuation
that the 24 hour day view of the days in genesis 1 is one which goes beyond Scripture
and our Confessional Standards. The licentiate may, in fact, deny this insinuation and
logical implication drawn from his view as expressed in the record of the Case, but
nowhere in the evidence considered by the SJC can any such denial be found. There
being no evidence in the record of the case or from the SJC panel having heard the
Complaint that the licentiate allows that the 24 hour day view may be supported by
Scripture and be in conformity with our Standards, this insinuation must be taken as
fairly representing the licentiate's view in question. Thus, his view represents one
which is more extreme and exclusive than any of the views considered by the recent
study committee on the days of Creation, and should, accordingly, be deemed an
exception to our Confessional Standards.

III. fallacious reason int hes jc decision
The "Reasoning and Opinion" section oft hes jc majority decision contains the
following fallacious points:

<a id="ga29-p110"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=107 printed_page=110 -->

with Chapter 19-4 of the WCF." The SJC affirmed the Presbytery’s decision and GA approved the SJC report.

This opinion was written by Panel chairman RE Howard Donahoe with the concurrence of Panel members TE Paul Kooistra and TE Dewey Roberts.

# DISSENTING OPINION IN JUDICIAL CASE 99-7
## COMPLAINT OF TE JEFFREY BLACK VS.
## EASTERN CAROLINA PRESBYTERY

We, the undersigned, do respectfully dissent from the majority of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) in the matter of Case 99-7. Specifically, the majority rendered a mistaken decision in their determining that, "Eastern Carolina Presbytery did not err when it ruled in July 1999 that the licentiate's view on the length of the creation days did not constitute an exception to the Westminster Standards." It is the contention of this dissenting opinion that Eastern Carolina Presbytery did err in this action, and that the licentiate's view regarding the length of the creation days does constitute an exception to the Westminster Standards.

## I. View of the Licentiate

The view of the licentiate in question, according to the "Reasoning &amp; Opinion" section of the decision adopted by the SJC majority in this case, has not been exactly determined. However, it is granted in the SJC majority decision that the licentiate does not affirm the six 24-hour day view of creation. In fact, the view of the licentiate is stated in the Record of the Case as follows:

WCF IV.i, LC #15, and SC #9 all affirm that God's work of creation was done "in the space of six days" and concern has arisen in some circles that constitutional

<a id="ga29-p109"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=108 printed_page=109 -->

exposition. We deny that the 24-hour day interpretation is the only exegetically possible interpretation.” The Session of Mt. Carmel complained against Presbytery’s adoption of this resolution as its position on creation. Presbytery denied the complaint and wrote that “it is by no means clear that the Westminster phrase ‘in/within the space of six days’ must be interpreted as involving 24-hour days.” The SJC affirmed the presbytery’s judgment by a vote of 12-9. Because more than one-third of the SJC signed a dissenting opinion, that dissent automatically became a substitute motion to the majority’s report. However, the GA failed to adopt the substitute as the main motion and the GA approved the judgment of the majority.

In *Snapp vs. James River*, a man was ordained holding a self-described “anthropomorphic” view of the creation days. No exception to the Standards was noted by Presbytery. The complaint was against the ordination itself, not simply the failure to record an exception. GA denied the complaint, affirmed the presbytery, and upheld the ordination. The SJC opinion reasoned that “the highest court of the PCA has not made any determination that ‘anthropomorphic’ days are out of accord with our confessional standards and the creation account in Genesis 1.” The SJC vote was 12-6. A dissenting opinion, signed by five members of the SJC, concluded that the majority of the SJC did not consider the man’s view to be an exception to the Westminster standards.

In *Long vs. James River*, the complaint was against Presbytery’s restricting the newly ordained minister’s teaching of his “anthropomorphic” view of the creation days. The GA denied the complaint and affirmed the presbytery’s decision to restrict teaching. Citing *BCO* 39-3, the SJC reasoned that James River Presbytery had better knowledge of the candidate, the issues, and the internal well being of the presbytery. The SJC vote was 13-8, with a dissenting opinion signed by five members.

## Other Related Cases

Most of the 20 cases involving theological views deal with whether a view is acceptable or not. However, in TE Black’s complaint against Eastern Carolina Presbytery, the issue involves the interpretation of the constitution. There are two pertinent non-creation cases involving constitutional interpretation and the issues of the Sabbath (*WCF* 21-8) and theonomy (*WCF* 19-4).

In 1989, in *Stine vs. South Florida*, a church member complained against a church-sponsored event, held after a brief worship service on a Lord’s Day evening, where games were played by both adults and children on the church playground. The complaint alleged the Session violated *WCF* 21-8. Presbytery denied the complaint and judged there was no violation of *WCF* 21-8 and “no impropriety in the use of the Lord’s Day.” A GA Judicial Commission affirmed the Presbytery’s judgment and denied the complaint. The Assembly approved the Judicial Commission’s decision.

In 1992, in one issue in the case of *Gunter &amp; Monroe vs. Central Florida*, the Presbytery annulled a Session resolution that judged that “theonomy is out of accord

<a id="ga29-p108"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=109 printed_page=108 -->

is that the timing of the creation days was not really an issue at the time of the Assembly, and so, clearly definitive statements were not made (and probably were not intended to be made) on this issue. (Excerpts found in Did God Create in Six Days? pp. 29-33.)

## 4. PCA Judicial Precedent on the Days of Creation

Attached to this opinion is a four-page, briefly annotated appendix of 20 PCA judicial cases involving theological views. The appendix offers an overview of how the PCA has treated different views, exceptions to the Standards, and restrictions on teaching and preaching.

- Six of the 20 cases touch on the creation issue:
- Grace Covenant Church vs. New River, 1991
- Bowen vs. Eastern Carolina, 1991
- Antioch Session vs. Eastern Carolina, 1994
- Mt. Carmel Session vs. New Jersey, 1998
- Snapp vs. James River, 1999
- Long vs. James River, 1999

In *Grace Covenant vs. New River*, the GA affirmed the presbytery’s ruling that a man (non-ordained) could not teach youth Sunday School while he held multiple exceptions to such doctrines as inerrancy, creation (held to theistic evolution), the fall of man, original sin, and the role of confessional standards.

In *Bowen vs. E. Carolina*, the GA affirmed the presbytery’s judgment that infant baptism and limited atonement “are to be considered fundamentals to our system of doctrine and that there can be no exceptions given in the case of officers of the church.” Regarding creation, the reasoning section of *Bowen* included this statement: “Other doctrines in which the PCA has granted a measure of freedom are: in the area of creation, where some may hold to a literal six 24-hour day for God’s creative acts, and others may hold to a form of ‘age-day’ creation...”

In *Antioch Session vs. Eastern Carolina*, the presbytery sustained a man’s licensure exam but prohibited him from “teaching his views on creation and the flood.” A complaint against the licensure was denied by the presbytery. The GA affirmed the Presbytery’s judgment. The SJC reported that the man believed Noah’s flood was local, not universal, and that his creation views were not the same as “theistic evolution” and that he affirmed “special creation, inerrancy, that matter did not pre-exist before creation, the historicity of the Garden of Eden, and his opposition to naturalistic evolution.” The SJC considered itself “not qualified to give a definitive pronouncement excluding certain exegetical opinions of the Candidate, while still eager to guard the scriptural teaching without compromise.” The SJC and the Assembly affirmed the presbytery’s “attempt to safeguard by prohibiting the Candidate to publicly teach or preach his views on this subject.”

In *Mt. Carmel vs. NJ*, the GA affirmed the presbytery’s judgment and denied a complaint against New Jersey’s adoption of a set of 15 affirmations and denials. The last set stated “We affirm that one natural interpretation of Genesis One is the 24-hour

<a id="ga29-p107"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=110 printed_page=107 -->

Based on that statement, the Presbyters of ECP might reasonably have concluded one of three things: either the other seminaries have declined in orthodoxy, were less than orthodox when they were founded, or a professor’s view on the length of the creation days is not a test of orthodoxy. The third conclusion is most reasonable because it seems none of the major evangelical Reformed seminaries have changed the positions they held at their inception.

Many of the major evangelical Reformed seminaries have had, and continue to have, professors who hold various non-Calendar Day views (Laird Harris, Meredith Kline, Willem Van Gemeren, Nigel Lee, R.C. Sproul, Bruce Waltke, Jack Collins, Mark Futato and others).

One example is especially worth noting. Dr. Laird Harris chaired the translation committee of the NIV and defended the inerrancy of Scripture in his book, *Inspiration and Canonicity* (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1957). More than a decade before he was elected as Moderator of the 10th PCA General Assembly in 1982 in Grand Rapids, he explained his creation views (non-Calendar Day) in a book titled, *Man, God’s Eternal Creation* (Chicago: Moody Press, 1971). While his non-Calendar Day view was fully known, he was elected repeatedly, and chaired repeatedly, the Theological Examining Committee of the PCA General Assembly.

In March 1999, the faculty of Westminster Theological Seminary adopted a statement containing the following:

The Seminary has always held that an exegetical judgment on this precise issue (twenty-four-hour days of creation) has never of itself been regarded as a test of Christian orthodoxy or confessional fidelity, until some have sought to make it so in the modern period.

While Reformed Theological Seminary has not issued any formal statement on the length of the creation days, RTS has had, and continues to have, some professors that do not hold the Calendar Day view.

In January 1998, Dr. Bryan Chapell, president of Covenant Theological Seminary, delivered a statement to the Board of the Seminary. Here are some excerpts:

The creation issue, quite frankly, is surprising to us since for generations there has been an informed allowance for difference among Bible-believing Presbyterians about how best to interpret these accounts...

Covenant Seminary has not changed its position on this issue in its 40 years of existence. That position is that the Genesis accounts are entirely true, factual, and historical. No one here denies God’s creation out of nothing, the historicity of Adam and Eve, the special creation of man, the reality of the fall. No one here endorses Evolution... All of our professors affirm that the first chapter of Genesis can be reasonably interpreted as teaching that God’s creative activity occurred in six solar days. Not all of our professors, however, believe that this is the best interpretation...

Despite some of the current debates in the PCA... we now teach nothing at Covenant that was not taught here 40 years ago when the seminary started... What seems most apparent, however,

<a id="ga29-p106"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=111 printed_page=106 -->

It is a sound principle that constitutional documents should be interpreted according to their original intent. For creeds and confessions to function as subordinate norms, they must be read according to the grammatico-historical method of interpretation. Confessional subscription is not to anything the words can be taken to mean, but rather to the discourse meaning of the text... The term discourse meaning points us back to the event or act of writing which is contextually informed and determinative for meaning. (M20GA, 1992 Roanoke, pp. 59-60 and Appendix O, pp. 521-636.)

In the 16th century Reformed church, while the doctrine of God’s creation was of great importance, the length of the creation days does not seem to have been of similar weight. This was noted recently in the Westminster Theological Journal by OPC minister Robert Letham (Ph.D. Aberdeen, professor of Systematic and Historical Theology at Chesapeake Theological Seminary and Sr. Pastor Emmanuel OPC, Wilmington, DE):

None of the great Reformed confessions make any comment on the matter. The French Confession (1559) concentrates on creation as a work of the trinity (Chapter 7). The Scots Confession (1560) stresses the sovereign action of God in creating all things for his own glory (Articles 1-2). The Belgic Confession (1561) states that the Father created ex nihilo all creatures “as it seemed good to him, giving every creature his being, shape, form, and several offices to serve its creator” (Article 12). The Heidelberg Catechism (1563) focuses on the ex nihilo work of God’s creative act and does not remotely come near mentioning the process of creation (Q 26). The Second Helvetic Confession (1566) attempts a trinitarian doctrine of creation, opposes the Manicheian idea that evil was co-created but neither does it approach our topic (Article 7). The Thirty-Nine Articles of the Church of England (1563, 1571) do not deal with creation at all! This universal absence of any reference connected even remotely to the issue of the days of creation establishes that it was not a confessional issue in the slightest in the Reformed churches. It was not a matter of definition since it was not a matter of controversy or even a point for discussion...

The Reformed tradition of the sixteenth century interpreted creation theologically. The classic Reformed creeds consider it in the context of the doctrine of God, as an ex nihilo work of the Trinity. In so doing, they affirm their continuity with the historic teaching of the church. The question of the days of creation was not even a matter of discussion. It does not appear in theses for debate by students. Its absence is striking. It was never a matter of confessional significance...

Letham later reaches a similar conclusion regarding the Westminster divines:

The single most astonishing and noteworthy feature of English Puritan theology before 1647, and the Westminster divines in particular, is the virtually complete absence of interest in creation... the days of creation were not a matter of contention... (“In the Space of Six Days”: the Days of Creation from Origen to the Westminster Assembly, WTJ 61, 1999, 149-174.)

3. Contemporary Reformed Positions on the Days of Creation

In the morning session of Eastern Carolina Presbytery’s July 1999 Stated Meeting, before the presbytery had ruled the licentiate’s view was not an exception, a visiting representative of a newer Presbyterian seminary reported that his seminary “was, in his knowledge, the only Reformed seminary which has taken the view of six-24 hour days of Genesis One and requires it of all faculty.” (100)

<a id="ga29-p105"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=112 printed_page=105 -->

Brief, he also notes this in his Summary of the Facts. The minutes of five ECP meetings record its actions on the views of licentiate Peterson (1992) and TE Curtis (1998). [192-204]

First, the minutes indicate that the view of licentiate Inman is not necessarily the same as those of Peterson and Curtis. The Record does not indicate which non-Calendar Day view Inman holds (Day-Age, Analogical, Framework, or other). However, the Record indicates more specifics regarding the views of Peterson and Curtis.

More importantly, it is not unconstitutional for courts to change their minds. The Complainant implicitly grants this when he approvingly cites ECP's new restriction of the teaching of TE Curtis. Furthermore, the minutes indicate that it was the Complainant that sought to revise ECP's long-standing position on TE Curtis' exceptions. (203) Curtis was ordained by ECP in 1985 with a statement of five exceptions. His teaching of such exceptions was not restricted by ECP until October 1998 – thirteen years later. (Note: TE Curtis complained against this action and the complaint was being considered by ECP when this opinion was written.)

Finally, in ECP, the majorities on these matters have never been overwhelming. In the 1992 decision to restrict Peterson, the vote was 11-10. (196) In the 1998 decision to restrict Curtis, the vote was 12-10. (204). In the 1999 ruling that Inman had not taken an exception, the vote was 10-8. (106) On Black's complaint against the Inman ruling, the vote to deny failed 11-13 and the vote to sustain failed 12-13. (168)

## 2. Westminster and the Length and Nature of the Creation Days

### a. Views of Westminster Divines

Recent studies by PCA minister David Hall and others have documented at least five divines, and perhaps as many as twenty-one, who affirmed the Calendar Day view. Seven pages of the Complainant's Primary Brief were a quotation from a research paper of Rev. Hall's. It addressed the views of 23 men: thirteen "explicit voting members" (Lightfoot, White, Ley, Walker, Goodwin, Twisse, Ashe, Gataker, Featley, Baillie, Selden, Caryl, Rutherford), one "explicit non-voting member" (Ussher), seven "implicit or at least not silent voting members" (Marshall, Cawdrey, Herle, Palmer, Gouge, Arrowsmith, Burroughs), and two "implicit non-voting members" (Wallis, Byfield).

It seems that no evidence has been found of any view other than the Calendar Day in the writings of individual Westminster divines.

### b. Original Intent of the Phrase "within/in the space of six days"

In seeking to understand the intent of this phrase, helpful guidance is found in the 1992 final report of the PCA Study Committee on Divorce and Remarriage. The report was divided into three chapters on historical, scriptural and pastoral perspectives. In the historical chapter, in a section titled "The Original Intent of the Confession," the report observed:

<a id="ga29-p104"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=113 printed_page=104 -->

At the same time, however, this court disagrees with the relatively recent contention that the allowance of any non-Calendar Day view is a “modern revision” in the history of Reformed churches. (This disagreement is more fully explained later in this opinion.) It has generally been assumed in the conservative Reformed community for more than 150 years that the Confession articulates no particular position on the length of the creation days and that one’s position on the subject is a matter of indifference. This assumption has been largely based on the Westminster commentaries of men like Shaw, Hodge, Mitchell and Warfield.

When the PCA formed in 1973, there was an allowed diversity of opinion on the nature of the creation days. This early diversity of opinion is acknowledged by Dr. Morton Smith, first Stated Clerk of the PCA and current dean and professor of Systematic Theology at Greenville Presbyterian Theological Seminary. (Dr. Smith’s present view, however, is that the Biblical materials teach the 24-Hour Day view and that this is the position of the Westminster Standards. He is quoted below only to establish that an allowed diversity existed when the PCA formed.)

The question of the length of the days as reflected in the action of Central Mississippi Presbytery taken just four years before the separation of the PCA from the PCUS in 1973 (i.e., opposing the PCUS allowance of theistic evolution), was not a part of that earlier debate, and thus not directly a part of the founding of the PCA. Certainly, the six literal day view with the immediate activity of God throughout the creation week was a most acceptable view. It was probably held by most of the non-ministerial members of the Church, as well as by many of the ministers. Other ministers, on the other hand, including your speaker, had been affected by the Princeton-Westminster tradition on this matter, and allowed for a certain variety of opinion on the length of the days, though generally speaking no form of evolution was condoned. (“The History of the Creation Doctrine in the American Presbyterian Churches” by Dr. Morton Smith in *Did God Create in Six Days?* Edited by Joseph A. Pipa, Jr. and David W. Hall, Greenville Seminary, Taylors, SC: Southern Presbyterian Press and Oak Ridge, TN: Covenant Foundation, 1999 p. 25 – parenthetical comment added.)

When Joining and Receiving was accomplished with the RPCES in 1982 an even greater diversity existed amongst the teaching eldership - without its being a controversial issue. Granted, the Framework view was probably not widely understood and embraced by the PCA ministry in 1973. Likewise, the Anthropomorphic or Analogical view was not widely taught until a decade and a half after the start of the PCA. However, amongst PCA ministers in 1973, the Day-Age view was widely held, but not widely considered as constitutionally significant. Therefore, the allowance of non-Calendar Day views is not a modern revision from the standard practice in the 27-year history of the PCA.

## B. Complaint Resolution 2 - Declare Licentiate’s non-24-Hour-Day View to be an Exception

### 1. Alleged Inconsistency of ECP

In the 14th whereas of his Complaint, TE Black wrote: “ECP has in the recent past recorded two men as taking an exception by not affirming 24 hour days of Genesis One and has instructed both men not to teach as true their exceptions.” (6) In his Primary

<a id="ga29-p103"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=114 printed_page=103 -->

complaint filed with the PCA Stated Clerk on November 29, 1999. Thus, the officers of the SJC never had a chance to rule whether that part of the complaint was administratively in order before they sent the complaint to the Panel. The Panel did not learn of the Complainant’s request for remanding until the day of the hearing.

If the Complainant believed the Presbytery had erred in procedure by considering his complaint without him present, he needed to complain to the Presbytery against that alleged procedural error. That complaint against procedure could have been filed after the October 16 meeting and considered at the November 6 meeting. Or, since the November meeting was a continuation of the adjourned October stated meeting, it could have been filed within 30 days of the November 6 meeting. That complaint would be fundamentally different than the complaint considered by Presbytery in October 1999 regarding ECP’s “non-exception” ruling. The SJC cannot consider any such procedural complaint because it was not first filed with the lower court (BCO 43-2). (See Lachman vs. Philadelphia, Case 94-5, M23GA and Watterson vs. PCA, M13GA)

## Issue 2: ECP’s Ruling on the Alleged Exception

The original written complaint considered at ECP’s October 16, 1999, Stated Meeting contained two resolutions asking ECP to take four actions (6). The complaint resolved that:

1. ECP “repent of its modern revisionism, particularly its ruling that the failure to affirm 24 hour days in Genesis One is in agreement with the Standards’ use of the phrase, “in the space of six days”
2. ECP “declare that the failure to affirm 24 hour days is an exception to the Westminster Confession of Faith IV.1, Westminster Larger Catechism Q&amp;A #15 and WSC #9”
3. the licentiate “have his refusal to affirm six 24 hour days for Genesis One recorded as an exception” and the licentiate “be directed not to teach as true, doctrines contrary to the Westminster Standards...”

At the October meeting, a motion passed that divided the question into the “Complaint paragraphs” and the “Resolution paragraphs.” (168) After that division, a motion to deny the complaint failed (11Y, 13N). A subsequent motion to sustain the complaint also failed (12Y, 13N). The Moderator then ruled that the part of the complaint called the “Resolution paragraphs” was now moot. Apparently, based on the division of the question, only items 1 and 2 were specifically considered by the lower court. The Complainant’s two requests regarding the licentiate (items 3 and 4) were declared moot by the Moderator after the court failed to sustain the “Complaint paragraphs” (items 1 and 2).

## A. Complaint Resolution 1 - Repent of “Modern Revisionism”

Regarding item 1, this court is unable to judge whether ECP is guilty of the alleged “modern revisionism” and in need of repentance. The complaint does not define “modern revisionism” and the Record does not deal with this alleged sin. The Complainant did not raise this issue in his Primary Brief or in oral argumentation before the Panel.

<a id="ga29-p102"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=115 printed_page=102 -->

B. Com plainant’s Absence when Com plaint was Considered
The Com plainant's excused absence from the presbytery meeting when his com plaint
was considered is also not adequate reason to remand. At the Panel hearing, the
Complainant said he was absent due to providential circumstances, and that he was
unable to notify the ECP Stated Clerk until the night before the October stated meeting.
His written complaint with supporting reasons had already been mailed with the docket
and was on the agenda for consideration. At the SJC Panel hearing, the Complainant
said he did not ask ECP to delay its consideration until such time as he could be present.

In addition, the Complainant attended the November 6 adjourned Fall Stated Meeting
and could have sought presbytery reconsideration of his complaint. While he could not
have moved for reconsideration, since he did not vote at the October meeting, anyone in
the majority of either the vote to sustain or the vote to deny could have done so.
Presumably, that would have included most of the members of Presbytery. The
Complainant did not seek such a remedy at the November meeting. The November
meeting was a continuation of the october meeting and was understood as such by the
Complainant. (He filed his complaint with the PCA Stated Clerk on November 29,
within 30 days of the november 6 portion of the meeting but more than 30 days after
the October 16 portion.)

Furthermore, this complaint involved an issue that ECP had discussed at many meetings
before, particularly in the recent past.

While the Complainant contends it was improper for the lower court to consider his
complaint without him present, there is no constitutional support for that contention.
BCO Chapter 43 on Complaints does not require a complainant to be present when the
original court considers his complaint. The first paragraph mentioning a com plainant's
physical presence is BCO 43-5:

the court against which complaint is made shall appoint one or more representatives to defend
its action, and the parties in the case shall be known as complainant and respondent. The
complainant himself may present his com plaint, or he may obtain the assistance of a communing
member of the Presbyterian Church in America, who is in good standing, in presenting his
com plaint.

it should be clear from the flow of the chapter, however, that after paragraph 43-4 the
chapter is addressing how a complaint is considered before a higher court, not before
the original court. There is no constitutional requirement or any judicial precedent
requiring the original court to have the complainant present when his com plaint is
considered.

Finally, and most importantly, Issue IB involves a procedural matter which the Panel
rules is not administratively in order before the SJC. The final sentence in the
Com plainant’s ten-page Primary Brief states, in bold print, "The complainant earnestly
desires that he may present his complaint to the lower court where it belongs. ”
However, the Com plainant’s request for remanding was not made in his original

<a id="ga29-p101"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=116 printed_page=101 -->

IV. Reasoning & Opinion
Throughout this opinion, it is important to remember that the Record does not indicate
exactly what particular view the licentiate holds regarding the length of the creation
days. The Record indicates that his view is not the 24-hour, Calendar-Day view. (See
six-sentences in statement of the facts for april 17, 1999.) The Record does not
contain any questions or answers from his committee exam or his oral transfer exam
before ECP. (After the Record was sent from the ECP Stated Clerk, the Complainant
requested that additional material be added to the Record and this was granted, but the
Complainant did not seek to add anything to the Record regarding the licentiate’s view.)
the issue in this case is not whether am an’s particular view is acceptable for licensure
or ordination. (The Complainant said he supports the m an’s licensure.) nor is the issue
whether am an’s particular view should be teachable. (Presbytery did not rule on that.)
The issue is whether any and every non-Calendar Day view should be considered as an
exception to the Westminster Standards.

It is also important to note that both ECP and the SJC Panel considered this complaint
prior to the final report of the PCA ’s Study Committee on Creation presented to the 28th
gain tampa in june 2000.

Issue I: Request for Remand
in his primary brief filed on march 2, 2000, the Complainant asked the SJC “to rem and
the complaint back to Eastern Carolina Presbytery directing them to adjudicate the
complaint, with the complainant present to argue his case, either denying or sustaining
it " (Underlining in original.) It should be noted that this request for remanding was
not made in the original complaint filed with the PCA Stated Clerk on November 29.
1999. (2-6) In his Primary Brief, the Complainant cites BCO 43-3 as support for his
request for remand. However, that paragraph does not support either his contention that
his complaint was not “adjudicated” or that it was unfairly considered with him absent.

A. Com plaint neither Sustained nor Denied
In his Primary Brief, the Complainant contends that ECP has not “adjudicated” the
complaint because it neither sustained nor denied it (both motions failed). At the Panel
hearing, however, the Respondent for ECP believed the presbytery had essentially
denied the complaint. This court agrees with the Respondent. The complaint has been
"adjudicated” by the Presbytery.

Several years ago, a similar situation occurred in ecp and involved a similar issue
(Antioch Session vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery, Judicial Case 92-9a, M 22GA Atlanta
1994, p. 88). A licentiate’s exam was sustained by a vote of 12-11. A complaint was
filed against th eli censure because of them an’s views on creation and the flood. A
motion to deny the complaint failed by a vote of 13-13. Then a motion to sustain the
complaint also failed, by a vote of 12-14. A complaint was filed with the General Assembly and the SJC found it to be in order, thereby implicitly ruling that ECP had
essentially denied the complaint.

<a id="ga29-p100"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=117 printed_page=100 -->

Oct 16 stated meeting of ECP. TE Black’s complaint was considered. At the
meeting, a motion was made and carried to divide the question into the
“Complaint paragraphs" and “Resolution paragraphs." A motion was then
made to deny the complaint. The motion to deny failed (11Y, 13N). A motion
was then made to sustain the complaint. after the order of the day to adjourn
was extended twice, the question was called on the motion to sustain the
complaint. The motion to sustain also failed (12Y-13N). The moderator then
ruled that the issue of the resolution paragraphs was now moot. The meeting
was adjourned until immediately after the already-scheduled Called Meeting for
November 6. (168)
Nov 6 continuation of ec P’s adjourned stated meeting of Oct. 16. No further
action was proposed regarding the complaint.

Nov 29 TE Black’s complaint received in office of the PCA stated Clerk.

2000
