Jan 29 ECP Stated Meeting. TE Traylor chosen as Respondent for the Presbytery.
Jan 31 complainant black receives the record of the Case.
Feb 1 Record received by SJC Panel members.
Feb 5 respondent traylor receives record of the case from ecp stated clerk
Leake.

Kooistra, TE Roberts).
Mar 13 Panel renders its Judgment and notifies parties.
April 11 Panel’s proposed decision filed with PCA Stated Clerk.

#### II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES

1. At its October 1999 meeting, by failing to either sustain or deny TE Black’s
complaint, did Eastern Carolina Presbytery fail to consider his complaint?
2. Did Eastern Carolina Presbytery err in July 1999 when it ruled that licentiate
Inman had "not taken an exception to the Westminster Confession of Faith and
Catechism regarding creation " in light of his specific written views contained
in the record of the Case?

III. JUDGMENT IN THE CASE
1. No. Presbytery did not fail to consider the complaint. While the com plaint was
not explicitly denied, it was essentially denied and therefore the Complainant
had proper right to com plain to this higher/broader court. It should not be
remanded simply because of the Presbytery’s failure to either sustain or deny.
(3-0)
2. No. Eastern Carolina Presbytery did not err when it ruled in July 1999 that the
licentiate’s view on the length of the creation days did not constitute an
exception to the Westminster Standards. (3-0)

<a id="ga29-p99"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=118 printed_page=99 -->

creation giving the actual events in their true sequence and marking them by their occurrence on six distinct days. This is inerrant history, and the text is perspicuous in its historical account. In my attention to the text, I see the length of these days as no more specified than the season of the year; thus, restrained by the authority of Scripture I cannot accede to the specification of 24 hours.” (23)

May 8 Called Meeting of ECP. Inman’s licensure transfer exam resumed and completed in English Bible, polity and sermon. Motion carried to approve the exam as a whole and to proceed to transfer his license from Philadelphia Presbytery. The four constitutional questions from BCO 19-3 were then affirmed by the licentiate. The moderator prayed and addressed licentiate with the words of BCO 19-4. A motion was then made “To record Mr. Ben Inman’s exception to the WCF and Catechisms in his refusal to affirm the 6 days of creation to be approximately 24 hour days.” Another motion was then made to postpone the previous motion until “the report of the GA’s Study Committee on Creation is presented to the General Assembly.” (1999 GA, Ft. Lauderdale) Motion to postpone consideration passed by vote of 10-7. (82)

June 7 TE Black filed one-page dissent against the action taken by ECP at its May meeting. Black dissented from ECP’s decision to “receive him on our roles as a licentiate and to postpone deciding upon the matter of his error.” Dissent was attached to the docket that was mailed before the July stated meeting. The dissent recommended that “ECP confess its error and proceed either to (1) rescind its action, enrolling Mr. Ben Inman as a licentiate of ECP, until after the GA Study Committee on Creation has reported to the General Assembly; or, (2) declare Mr. Ben Inman to take an exception to our Standards in his refusal to affirm that the Creation days are approximately 24 hour days, allow this exception but disallow him to teach it as true, in any public forum save that of the courts of the Presbyterian Church in America.” (112)

July 17 Stated Meeting of ECP. The postponed motion from the May meeting (to regard Inman’s views as an exception) was considered. A substitute motion was made which said “that ECP rules that Ben Inman has not taken an exception to the WCF and Catechism regarding creation.” After the order of the day to adjourn was extended twice, by 10 and then 15 minutes, ECP voted to make the substitute the main motion, 11-7. As the main motion, it passed, 10-8. (106)

Aug 13 TE Black filed complaint with ECP against the action of the July meeting. (159-161 &amp; 5-6) After offering supporting reasons, the complaint concluded with the following resolutions:

Therefore be it resolved that ECP repent of its modern revisionism, particularly its ruling that the failure to affirm 24 hour days in Genesis One is in agreement with the Standards’ use of the phrase, “in the space of six days” and declare that the failure to affirm 24 hour days is an exception to the Westminster Confession of Faith IV.1, Westminster Larger Catechism Q &amp; A # 15 and WSC #9.

Further resolved that Licentiate Benjamin Inman have his refusal to affirm six 24 hour days for Genesis One, recorded as an exception and he be directed not to teach as true, doctrines contrary to the Westminster Standards in his work or any other public forum save that of the courts of the Presbyterian Church in America.

99-7
COMPLAINT OF JEFFREY M. BACK
VS.
EASTERN CAROLINA PRESBYTERY

#### I. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS

At its summer stated meeting in July 1999, ECP ruled that a transferring licentiate’s view regarding the length of the creation days was not an exception to the Westminster Standards. TE Black’s complaint against this ruling was considered at ECP’s fall stated meeting in October 1999. A motion to deny the complaint failed. A motion to sustain the complaint also failed. In November 1999, TE Black complained to the General Assembly. An SJC Panel heard his complaint on March 2, 2000. The Panel affirmed the ruling of the Presbytery. Below is a brief chronology of the facts. (The licentiate’s view on the length of the creation days appears at April 17, ECP’s “non-exception” ruling is described at July 17, and TE Black’s complaint is shown at August 13.)

1999

Feb 23 ECP’s Credentials and Examinations Committee examined candidate/licentiate Ben Inman for transfer of his license from Philadelphia Presbytery. Exam approved by committee. (ROC 13)

Apr 17 Stated Meeting of ECP. Licentiate Inman received a partial transfer exam in Christian experience, call to the ministry and theology. (77) His written comments on his views had been attached to the docket that was mailed before the meeting. (23) The Record of the Case (ROC) contains no questions or answers from the oral transfer exam. The only views of his regarding creation in the ROC are quoted below:

“WCF IV.i, LC #15, and SC #9 all affirm that God’s work of creation was done “in the space of six days” and concern has arisen in some circles that constitutional integrity requires the further specification that these 6 days were each of 24 hours in length. I do not add this further specification. I wish to state my view for the presbytery. I believe that Genesis 1 narrates the history of

<a id="ga29-p97"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=120 printed_page=97 -->

The dictionary defines the “impenitent” as (1) not repenting of sin or (2) incapable of repenting.

This along with the statement in *BCO* 36-5 that the offender “give satisfactory evidence of repentance” clearly places the burden upon Dr. Shive.

Under *BCO* 37-3 it is stated that:

“. . . When the court shall be satisfied as to the reality of the repentance of an indefinitely suspended offender, he shall be admitted to profess his repentance, either in the presence of the court alone or publicly. . .”

When the penitent is restored the language of restoration in *BCO* 37-3 states that:

“Whereas, you, ______________, have now manifested such repentance as satisfies the Church. . .”

The reality of Dr. Shive’s repentance is a matter of discretion and judgment which must be determined to the satisfaction of the Session. The Session in the resolution which it adopted (ROC 75) stated that:

“. . . the Session failed to bring Dr. Shive to a point of admission of guilt and repentance to the particulars of the judgments against him (with exception of the judgment of scandalous conduct); and whereas the Session now at what it considers an impasse, does consider Dr. Shive’s condition to be incorrigible and contumacious.”

Obviously Dr. Shive did not manifest “such repentance that satisfied the Church”.

The Session has been patient. Dr. Shive did not give satisfactory evidence of repentance to the Session. The Session is not satisfied as to the reality of his repentance. After the SJC remanded Case 97-9 to the Session to reduce the censure to suspension of Sacraments, the Session immediately complied and formed a Commission to shepherd Dr. Shive and to attempt to lead him to repentance. The Shive Commission met many times and had much correspondence with him. The Session has concluded that Dr. Shive did not meet his burden of proof of repentance and therefore the Session had the right to proceed to excommunication in accordance with *BCO* 30-3.

## V. CONCLUSION

It is the prayer of the Church that the censure of excommunication will have its intended affect and that this censure will operate on Robert M. Shive “as a means of reclaiming him, to deliver the Church from the scandal of his offenses, and to inspire all with fear by the example of his discipline.” (*BCO* 30-4).

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ Dr. Charles E. McGowan
/s/ Mark Belz
/s/ W. Jack Williamson

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=121 printed_page=96 -->

lower courts were ' ‘more com petent to determine, because of its proximity to the events
in question, and because of its personal knowledge and observation of the parties and
witnesses involved”, the factual matters as to the repentance of Dr. Shive. We therefore
conclude that this higher court should follow the guidelines set out in BCO 39-3 and not
reverse “a factual finding of the lower court unless there is clear error on the part of the
lower court”. In the case before us, we find no such “clear error on the part of the lower
court”.

DR. shive ’S membership vow
Dr. shive by virtue of applying and being admitted to membership by the
session of christ covenant church is bound by the vow required under BCO 57-5 "to
submit to the government and discipline of the Church.”
This vow means nothing if a person will not honor it at those times when he
believes that he is right and the government of the church is wrong. The General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America, the highest court in the denomination,
has rendered a decision which under BCO 14-7 is binding on Dr. Shive. The Church
has exercised its “government and discipline”. Dr. Shive was obligated, under his vow
to Almighty God and the Session, to honor and submit to this government and
discipline of the Church. He has failed and refused to do so. We believe he violated
this vow.

burden of proof of repentance
The decision in Judicial Case 97-9 suggested to the session of christ covenant
Church that it “impose the censure of indefinite suspension from the sacraments upon
Dr. Robert M. Shive in accordance with BCO 36-5, and with the instruction that, until
satisfactory evidence of repentance is given to the session of christ covenant Church,
to impose such conditions concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of christ
Covenant Church as the Session may find” . (ROC 35). The question arises as to who
has the burden of providing “satisfactory evidence of repentance” to the Session? Also,
who has the responsibility of making a determination of whether or not the evidence is
“satisfactory”? The reference in the judgment in Case 97-9 to BCO 36-5 answered
these questions. This provision in BCO 36-5 provides an answer as follows:

“. . . indefinite suspension should be administered under the blessing of god of
leading him to repentance.” (Emphasis added).

This statement clearly means that Dr. Shive has to be led to repentance. Further in BCO
36-5 there is given a statement that is to be made to the offending brother when the
censure of indefinite suspension is imposed which statement is that this suspension is
“until you give satisfactory evidence of repentance.” The “you” is Dr. Shive who must
give satisfactory evidence of repentance. the burden of proving repentance is upon the
offender not upon the Court. BCO 30-3 makes this clear statement:

“. . . Indefinite Suspension is administered to the impenitent offender until he
exhibits signs of repentance. . . ” (Emphasis added).

<a id="ga29-p95"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=122 printed_page=95 -->

We agree with the statement in the decision of Central Carolina Presbytery that: "The SJC opinion does *appear* to contradict itself at certain points. Nevertheless, the final judgment and instructions of the highest court are unambiguous." (ROC 12)

We conclude that the SJC in Case 97-9 did not reverse the charges of sin and the findings of guilt against Dr. Shive.

## STANDARD OF REVIEW

*BCO 39-3* outlines certain principles that should guide any review of the judicial proceedings of a lower court by a higher court. It states that these principles are “to insure that this Constitution is not amended, violated or disregarded in judicial process.” We think principles 2 and 3 therein are particularly pertinent in setting a Standard of Review in this case. Therefore, we set them out in full below, to-wit:

## BCO 39-3(2)

“A higher court should ordinarily exhibit great deference to a lower court regarding those factual matters which the lower court is more competent to determine, because of its proximity to the events in question, and because of its personal knowledge and observations of the parties and witnesses involved. Therefore, a higher court should not reverse a factual finding of a lower court, unless there is clear error on the part of the lower court.”

## BCO 39-3(3)

“A higher court should ordinarily exhibit great deference to a lower court regarding those matters of discretion and judgment which can only be addressed by a court with familiar acquaintance of the events and parties. Such matters of discretion and judgment would include, but not be limited to: the moral character of candidates for sacred office, the appropriate censure to impose after a disciplinary trial, or judgment about the comparative credibility of conflicting witnesses. Therefore, a higher court should not reverse such a judgment by a lower court, unless there is clear error on the part of the lower court.”

The Session was charged in the decision in Case 97-9 with the instruction that, “until satisfactory evidence of repentance is given to the Session of Christ Covenant Church, to impose such conditions concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of Christ Covenant Church as the Session may find.” (ROC 35).

This instruction places the responsibility and burden upon the Session of Christ Covenant Church to determine when and how Dr. Robert M. Shive exhibits “satisfactory evidence of repentance”. To judge a man for repentance, the judges should be very familiar with the man. This case is an example of one in which the higher court should exhibit great deference to the lower court. The Discipline Commission of the Session of Christ Covenant Church voted unanimously that Dr. Shive did not exhibit such repentance. The Session of Christ Covenant Church voted unanimously that Dr. Shive did not exhibit such repentance. The Judicial Commission of Central Carolina Presbytery voted unanimously to affirm the Session’s judgment that Dr. Shive had not exhibit such repentance. Central Carolina Presbytery voted 47 to 6 to affirm the Session’s judgment that Dr. Shive had not exhibited such repentance. These

<a id="ga29-p94"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=123 printed_page=94 -->

Issue 4 . “Were there procedural and substantive errors committed by Central Carolina
Presbytery in this case which require a remand in this case?”

Judgment 4 . “No. We do not find that errors were committed by Central Carolina
Presbytery requiring reversal and remand.”

This clear and unambiguous language in defining the issue and stating the
judgment shows without question that Dr. Shive’s interpretation of the decision ins jc
Case 97-9 was in error.

Issue 5. “Did central carolina pres byte rye rr by affirming the decision of christ
Covenant Church Session’s infliction upon Dr. Robert M. Shive the highest censure
based on the record of the Case?”

Judgment 5. “Yes. central carolina presbytery erred in affirming and the session of
christ covenant church erred in going straight to the highest censure of
excommunication in light of the lack of compelling evidence in the record of the case
to reflect that Dr. Shive is contumacious or impenitent. See BCO 30-4 and 33-3.
Pursuant to BCO 42-9, we reverse that portion of the judgment by the session of christ
covenant church inflicting the censure of excommunication on Dr. Shive, and we
remand the matter back to the session of christ covenant church for it to impose the
censure of indefinite suspension from the sacraments upon Dr. Robert M. Shive in
accordance with BCO 36-5, and with the instruction that, until satisfactory evidence of
repentance is given to the session of christ covenant Church, to impose such
conditions concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of christ covenant church as
the Session may find.” (Emphasis added).

In this judgment in Case 97-9 there is absolutely no language that gives any
indication that the four guilty charges against Dr. Shive were reversed or remanded.
Only “that portion of the judgment by the session of christ covenant church inflicting
the censure of excommunication on Dr. Shive” was remanded to impose the censure of
“indefinite suspension from the Sacraments upon Dr. Robert M. Shive”. We think the
language is clear and unambiguous that the verdicts of guilty were not changed nor
reversed and the findings of guilt of Dr. Shive remained in force. Only “that portion of
the judgment” dealing with censure was remanded. The penalty was reduced from “the
censure of excommunication on Dr. Shive” to the censure of “indefinite suspension
from the Sacraments upon Dr. Shive” . There is no language in the judgment that affects
any other part of the decision by christ covenant Church. We conclude that the sin and
guilt were not overturned by the decision in SJC Case 97-9.
That the sin and guilt were not overturned is further evidenced by the
recommendation of indefinite suspension from the l ord’s Supper as well as by further
instructions “that, until satisfactory evidence of repentance is given to the session of
Christ Covenant Church” that the Session has the right to impose any further
“conditions concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of christ covenant church
as the Session may find.”

<a id="ga29-p93"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=124 printed_page=93 -->

1. SJC Manual 11.11 (2) II defines “a statement of the Issues” as a concise
framing by the court of the specific issues being judged in the case.
2. SJC Manual 11.11 (2) III defines “the Judgm ent” as a definite conclusion
rendered on specific issues being judged.
3. SJC Manual 11.11 (2) IV defines “the reasoning and opinion of the Court” as
the explanation by the court of the resolution of disputed facts and reasons for
the judgment rendered.
4. it thus seems to us that the heart and decisive portion of any judicial decision is:
its “Statement of the Issues” and “the Judgm ent.”

the summary of the facts is defined as “a presentation in chronological order of
the relevant history and facts that bear on the case,” and the reasoning and opinion of
the Court simply deals with certain facts and factors as they are applied to the principles
set forth in the Book of Church Order and sjc Manual. We therefore suggest that after
looking at the whole case, a person should center on the heart of the case which is “a
statement of the Issues” and “the Judgm ent.” we think the failure of the Appellant, Dr.
Robert M. Shive, to properly interpret the decision in SJC Case 97-9 is a failure to
center on the clear and unambiguous language stating these specific issues being judged
in the case and the definite conclusions rendered on the specific issues. Dr. Shive
interpreted the SJC decision in Case 97-9 that the “decision clearly challenged and
struck down the allegations” of the session and reversed the verdicts of guilt on all four
charges. (ROC 56-57). On the other hand, the discipline commission of the session of
christ covenant church stated its interpretation of the decision, as follows:

“. . . In so doing we are working with the understanding that General Assembly,
based on the record of the Case, has altered the censure of excommunication to
indefinite suspension. Neither Presbytery nor General Assembly have
overturned any of the Session’s judgments with respect to your guilt. . ."
(Emphasis added).

We think Dr. Shive misinterpreted the SJC decision in Case 97-9 and the
discipline commission of the session properly interpreted it. This is supported by the
interpretation given by Dr. paul gilchrist in his letter of april 16, 1998 (ROC 61) in
which he stated the following, to-wit:

“. . . first the heart of the decision is in II. statement of the issues
and III. judgment in the case . . . the SJC did not reverse the charges
of sin and the finding of guilt against Dr. shive .. . ”

two of the panel members on sjc case 97-9 confirmed that interpretation. We concur.

in the light of these facts and factors, we suggest we look at Issues 4 and 5 and
Judgments 4 and 5 in SJC 97-9.

<a id="ga29-p92"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=125 printed_page=92 -->

On July 11, 2000, a Panel of the SJC held a hearing in Charlotte, N.C., at which it heard the oral arguments of both Appellant and Appellee, framed the issues in this case, voted unanimously for the judgments, and announced these to the parties.

## II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES

1. Did Central Carolina Presbytery err by affirming Christ Covenant Church Session’s interpretation of the Judgment of SJC Case 97-9 (Robert M. Shive vs. Central Carolina Presbytery) that the guilt of Dr. Shive was not reversed but affirmed as to the 4 charges of (1) sexual immorality, abuse, and licentiousness (2) premeditated sexual exploitation, (3) lying and bearing false witness, and (4) scandalous living?

2. Did Central Carolina Presbytery err by affirming the Session of Christ Covenant Church’s decision to increase Dr. Shive’s censure from indefinite suspension to excommunication?

## III. JUDGMENT

1. No. There is no mention in the Judgment in Case 97-9 (26th GA Min. p. 137) that the guilt of Dr. Shive was reversed on any charge. Thus the guilty verdict of Christ Covenant Church Session stands unchanged on all 4 charges. The penalty of excommunication was recommended to be changed to indefinite suspension from the sacraments. With this recommendation, the Session complied. Hence the guilty verdict of the Session in SJC Case 97-9 remained unchanged.

2. No. The Judgment in SJC Case 97-9 gave the Session these instructions:
“... that, until satisfactory evidence of repentance is given to Session of Christ Covenant Church, to impose such conditions concerning Dr. Shive’s involvement in the life of Christ Covenant Church, as the Session may find.” (M26thGA, p.137)

Thus, Central Carolina Presbytery did not err in affirming the action of the Session of Christ Covenant Church in imposing the censure of excommunication on Dr. Shive after the Session determined that Dr. Shive had not given “satisfactory evidence of repentance.” Central Carolina Presbytery properly followed BCO 39-3(2) in giving “great deference to a lower court regarding these factual matters which the lower court is more competent to determine.” We do likewise.

## IV. REASONING AND OPINION

### INTERPRETATION OF A JUDICIAL DECISION

Since there was a “fundamental disagreement” between Dr. Shive and the Session as to the interpretation and intent of the SJC in its decision in SJC Case 97-9, we think it would be helpful to delineate some of the principles involved in a proper interpretation of a Judicial Decision. Of course, one must look at the entire decision, Facts, Issues, Judgments, and Reasoning and Opinion. We know that each judicial case contains the four items mentioned above. We suggest that it would be helpful to see how the SJC Manual defines certain of those items. For instance,

statement of issues
1. did the session of christ covenant commit reversible error in its december 17,
1996 conviction of dr Shive?
2. did christ covenant clearly err by not carrying out the expectations of the
General Assembly in the appeal of robert M. Shive v. Central Carolina
Presbytery (Case 97-9)?
3. did the session of christ covenant err when it proceeded from the censure of
indefinite suspension from the sacraments to the censure of excommunication on
April 13, 1999?

Judgment in the Case
1. No. The Session's 1996 judgment of guilt was affirmed by the Presbytery, the
SJC. and the General Assembly. Although the SJC Panel identified errors in Dr.
Shive’s trial, none of the four original charges were reversed. The highest court
did set aside the censure of excommunication in favor of a less severe censure
appropriate for a guilty offender willing to work toward repentance. (BCO 36-5)
2. No. Although we have reservations about the method used to assess repentance
in this particular case, there is no clear error on the part of the session in its
efforts to implement the highest court’s decision.
3. No. the session of christ covenant did no terr when it proceeded to the highest
censure in 1999. Suspension from the Sacraments is designed to be a “temporary
exclusion.” (BCO 30-3) After more than two years, Dr. Shive rem ained
unwilling to admit guilt on 3 of 4 charges. The Session had the authority to
increase the censure under BCO 33-3.

At a meeting of the DC of Christ Covenant Session on December 14, 1998, a "Status and Course of Action with Dr. Robert Shive" was unanimously adopted (ROC 71-72). This memo began:

"It has been right at one year since the appeals process initiated by Dr. Robert Shive with respect to the judgments and censure of Christ Covenant’s Session against Dr. Shive completed their course through Presbytery and General Assembly (Case 97-9). The final outcome of these appeals was to sustain the four judgments of guilt but to change the censure of excommunication imposed by Christ Covenant to indefinite suspension."

This memo then recapped the DC activity in this case in compliance with General Assembly’s expectations, listing dates and actions. This memo concluded with an action taken on October 27, 1998, as follows:

"The Commission’s conclusion is that barring a definite work of the Spirit, we should recommend to the Session that Dr. Shive be excommunicated." (ROC 72).

On February 5, 1999, the DC, unanimously adopted its “Recommended Disposition of the Discipline Case for Dr. Robert Shive” for submission to the Executive Discipline Commission of Christ Covenant Church (ROC 73-74).

This document ended with a formal recommendation as follows:

"Whereas, the Session of Christ Covenant Presbyterian Church, after a comprehensive trial on December 17, 1996, found Dr. Robert M. Shive guilty of sexual immorality, licentiousness, premeditated sexual exploitation, lying and bearing false witness and scandalous living, and

# 18. PROCEDURES FOR ASSUMING OF ORIGINAL JURISDICTION OVER A MINISTER (BCO 34-1)

18.1 Upon proper receipt of a case filed against a minister under BCO 34-1, the SJC shall in accordance with the provisions set out below:

a) Appoint a panel in accordance with RAO 15.3 to investigate the matter and determine whether or not that the case is in order, i.e., that the provisions of BCO 34-1 have been followed.

b) If the case is determined to be in order, the panel shall conduct an investigation of allegations against the minister under the provisions of BCO 31-2.

c) The panel’s findings and recommendation shall be mailed to the full SJC, said minister and the stated clerks of the INVOLVED presbyteries that instituted this action under BCO 34-1. The matter shall be scheduled for review at the SJC’s next stated meeting or a meeting called under the provisions of SJC Manual 3.2.

d) Said minister and the stated clerks of the involved presbyteries within 14 days of receipt of the panel’s findings and recommendation may submit a brief citing reasons as to whether the panel's recommendations should be approved or rejected. Such brief must comply with the requirement of Section 9 of this Manual.

e) Briefs under these paragraphs shall comply with the requirements for briefs in SJC Manual 9.4, but shall not exceed 5 pages.

18.2 At its next stated meeting or meeting called to hear the matter under the provision of Manual 3.2, the SJC in closed session shall discuss the panel’s findings and recommendation. The SJC shall vote to accept or reject the panel’s findings and recommendation.

18.3 The SJC decision shall be mailed to said minister and stated clerks of the presbyteries. Within 14 days of receipt of the decision a party may request a reconsideration of the decision by filing a supplemental brief as set forth in Section 9.2. This supplemental brief shall state the reasons for requesting reconsideration of the decision. Copies of the supplemental brief and the SJC decision shall be mailed to members of the SJC. Reconsideration of the judgment shall be granted only by majority vote of the full Commission either by mail or by conference call, as determined by the officers.

If reconsideration is granted, the Officers shall call a special meeting, under Manual 3.2 (a) and (b), to discuss the case, consider its merits, and render a final judgment. If reconsideration is not granted, the previous judgment shall become final in accordance with BCO 15.5 (a).

<a id="ga29-p128"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=130 printed_page=128 -->

18.4 If the SJC ’s final judgment is that the above investigation does not raise
“a strong presumption of the guilt of the party involved,” the SJC shall dismiss the case
and advise the parties to the case.

18.5 If, however, the SJC ’s final judgment is that the above investigation
raises “a strong presumption of the guilt of the party involved,” it shall institute process
under BCO 31. In such event, the SJC shall conduct a trial in accordance with the
provisions of BCO 32, BCO 35 and SJC Manual 13.5 through 13.10.

Current Language Proposed Language

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=131 printed_page=129 -->

# 16. PROCEDURE FOR HEARING A MEMORIAL (BCO)

16.1 The only memorial which the Commission may entertain is one which may demonstrate an important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceeding of a lower court which cannot be addressed through appeal or complaint (BCO 40-5).

(a.) When such a Memorial is received by the Stated Clerk, a determination shall be made under Section 10.1(a) of this Manual as to whether or not the Memorial is found administratively in order.

(b.) If the Memorial is found to be administratively in order, the Officers of the Commission shall determine whether or not the Commission should address the concerns memorialized.

(c.) When the officers decide that the Commission should not address the concerns memorialized, the matter shall be placed on the docket of the next stated or called meeting of the Commission for final determination.

16.2 A Memorial accepted for consideration by the Commission under Section 16.1(a) above may be addressed by a Panel established in accordance with RAO 15.3

16.3 The court alleged to have offended shall be cited to appear by representative or in writing, providing such documents as bear on the alleged important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings, showing what it has done or failed to do in the case in question (BCO 40-5). The Stated Clerk shall collate these documents which shall be considered the Relevant Documents.

16.4 Any hearing on a Memorial shall not be conducted as in a formal judicial case but in accordance with BCO 40-5.

16.5 All hearings shall convene with prayer. The Panel or Commission shall then proceed to hear the response of the representative of the court alleged to have committed an important delinquency if such a Respondent is present. The Respondent shall have a maximum of 40 minutes to respond to the citation.

16.6 At any time during which the Respondent is presenting an argument a member of the Panel or Commission may ask questions of the parties if present; the time taken for such questions shall form a part of the response time. The time for response may be extended one time for an additional 20 minutes by majority vote of those qualified members present.

16.7 The party memorializing the General Assembly shall not present a brief nor will he be given time for argument, except to answer questions posed by the Panel or Commission.

16.8 After the response has been heard, the Panel or Commission shall go into closed session to discuss the Memorial and consider its merits.

16.9 In the case of a Panel, it shall then make a full report to the Commission including such recommendations as

(a) to reverse or redress the proceedings of the court below in other than judicial cases; or

(b) to censure the delinquent court; or

<a id="ga29-p130"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=132 printed_page=130 -->

(c) to remit the whole matter to the delinquent court with an injunction to
take it up and dispose of it in a constitutional manner; or
(d) to stay all further proceedings and declare that all matters relating to the
Memorial, now or previously pending before the General Assembly, are
ended, concluded and terminated.
16.10 When the full Commission has completed its discussion and is ready to vote, the
vote shall be taken on each recommendation. The report as a whole shall then
be voted on by roll call without change or amendment. All qualified members
present shall participate in the vote.
16.11 in the case of a hearing before the full commission it shall proceed to deliberate
the merits of the matter and make a determination as provided in section 16.9
(a), (b), (c), or (d). A vote shall be taken on the decision(s). The report as a
whole shall then be voted on by roll call. All qualified members present shall
participate in the vote.
16.12 After a decision has been reached by the Full Commission, any member may
file, within 20 days after the day the decision is reached, a concurring or
dissenting opinion, which if it is couched in temperate language shall be
appended to the decision.
16.13 When a report with its recommendations on the Memorial has been adopted, a
copy thereof shall be mailed immediately to the parties and shall be included in
the final report oft hes jc to the General Assembly.

note : this communication is included at the conclusion oft hes jc report per
ga action (see pg. 69).

communication 7 From Greenville Presbyterian Theological Seminary
“ Regarding Concurring Opinion in John Wood Matter”

Chairman GPTS Board Communication to the 29th General Assembly of the
presbyterian church of America, meeting in Dallas, Texas, June 19, 2001

Fathers and Brethren:

the board of trustees of greenville presbyterian theological seminary desires
to express its concern regarding the concurring opinion attached to the report on the
John Wood matter, and signed by RE W. Jack Williamson, TE Charles McGowan. TE
Ben Konopa, TE Dominic Aquila, RE John White, and TE William Lyle. Our concern
has to do with inaccuracies in the concurring opinion, which we hope will not become a
permanent part of the minutes of the General Assembly.

First, T E ’s Morton H. Smith and Joseph Pipa, whoa remembers of our faculty,
without being informed that they would be charged with any improper activity
whatsoever, were defamed in the concurring opinion ina manner totally unbecoming of
Christian brethren. The opinion states that if they had more carefully investigated the

<a id="ga29-p131"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=133 printed_page=131 -->

circumstances, they would not have brought this matter to the church. However, the chairman of the investigating panel, RE Mark Belz, confirmed to TE Pipa and to TE Smith and TE James Phillis of Western Carolina Presbytery that Messrs. Smith and Pipa had been thorough and careful in the pursuit of the charges. They had a lengthy conference call with Mr. Wood in which the entire matter regarding Mr. Wood's opinions was thoroughly discussed. Mr. Wood admitted that he told them a woman might preach. Had Mr. Wood not changed his statement to the investigative panel, they were ready to recommend that charges be brought against him. What makes this such a serious matter to our Board is that if these brethren indeed erred as the concurring opinion says, they should be charged with disturbing the peace of the Church before their respective presbyteries.

Second, the opinion said that two Greenville Seminary students were present when the woman spoke at the evening worship service of the Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church. This is not accurate. No Greenville Seminary students were involved in the John Wood case. TE Pipa has informed our Board that an older man, who had taken courses at Greenville Seminary a year before the event, heard the message at Cedar Springs. He approached both his Session and the Session of Cedar Springs and wrote Dr. Pipa to ask if he was correctly concerned. TE Pipa responded that his concerns were valid and that he should contact men in the Presbytery of Tennessee Valley. TE Pipa has had no further contact with the gentleman. He was not at the time a student at Greenville Seminary nor has he attended since then.

Third, Greenville Seminary was accused of carrying the report across the denomination. This is untrue. The seminary never was involved in the John Wood matter. Apart from the original letter, we knew nothing about it until the issue was made public.

All of these points are well documented and we hope that this letter will be taken into account if the General Assembly chooses to consider the concurring opinion. We as a theological seminary were not a party to this matter, and unfortunately it appears that the Book of Church Order of the Presbyterian Church in America gives us no technical recourse to respond to such wholly inaccurate and unfounded charges as discussed above.

We as a theological seminary are not in a position to appeal any actions of the PCA's Standing Judicial Commission. However, some of our board members who are members of your Assembly have noted that there is no provision in the SJC Manual for concurring opinions to be attached to a report on a matter. We are told that they are only provided for in connection with the adjudication of judicial cases. The John Wood matter apparently never reached this level, and thus your Assembly might wish to consider whether it is proper for the SJC to publish such concurring opinions as a part of a report on a non-judicial matter.

<a id="ga29-p132"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=134 printed_page=132 -->

We further note that the SJC Manual does require that concurring opinions be
couched in “temperate language.” While arguably the language described may not be
“intemperate,” it would seem that expressions which are demonstrably in violation of
the Ninth Commandment should also be excluded. Therefore, as Christian brothers, we
plead that our letter be considered by the Assembly if it chooses to consider the
concurring opinion as part of the documents in the matter.

Respectfully yours,
/s/RE John Van Voorhis
chairman of the board of trustees
For Greenville Presbyterian Theological Seminary
June 13,2001

29-28 report of Committee on Constitutional Business
TE Craig Childs, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the Report.
After reviewing the report, the Assembly received it as information.

REPORT OF THE
COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS
TO THE 29th GENERAL ASSEMBLY
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

#### I. Introduction

th
Your Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met one time subsequent to the 28
General Assembly, on April 23 - 24, 2001 in Atlanta, Georgia. The CCB was able at
that meeting to complete all of the work then assigned to it. Attendance was as follows:

TE Craig D. Childs (03), Chair - present RE Frederick (Jay) Neikirk (04) - present
TE Karl F. M cCallister (04) - present RE Richard (Ric) Springer (03) - present
TE Bryan S. Chapell (02) - present RE Calvin Poole, III (01) - present
TE Morton H. Smith (01) - present RE John Ward Weiss (alternate) - present
TE Mark Rowden (alternate) - present RE Joe B. Johnson (02) - resigned

in the absence of RE Johnson, RE Weiss was seated, by proper motion, as a
voting member. TE Rowden was seated as a non-voting member. A quorum was
present and the Committee convened to conduct business.

II. minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission
1. the CCB approved the minutes of the october 19 - 20, 2000 meeting of the
Standing Judicial Commission as being in conformity to the manual oft hes jc
(20-5) and RAO 15 with the following notations.

<a id="ga29-p133"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=135 printed_page=133 -->

a. The SJC’s stated rationale for disqualification of some members in the adjudication process was only that those members were members of the presbyteries that were parties to the case. The CCB has previously issued its opinion that this alone is not a sufficient grounds for disqualification, even though it is likely that a member of such a presbytery would be disqualified for other related reasons (see Minutes of the CCB April 17, 2000, response to Question #5).

b. There is confusion as to the Attachment referred to on page 6.

c. The proposed SJC Manuel 18 dealing with “Original Jurisdiction Over a Minister”, which is referenced as being amended on pages 6 &amp; 7, is missing. If this is not a new section, then the minutes are not clear at this point.

2. The CCB approved the minutes of the March 1, 2001 meeting of the Standing Judicial Commission as being in conformity to the Manual of the SJC (20-5) and RAO 15 with the following notations.

a. Although the procedures described by the minutes are in order, it is impossible for the CCB to know precisely if the procedures involved in reaching decisions are proper without the dates indicated for timely or untimely filings relative to our standards.

b. There are typographical errors (“20001-20002” on page 16, and “Adjournment” on page 17).

3. The CCB approved the minutes of the conference call meeting of the Officers of the Standing Judicial Committee of March 30, 2001 as being in conformity to the Manual of the SJC (20-5) and RAO 15.

### III. Non-Judicial References

#### Reference 1 – Westminster Presbytery:

Question 1 – There is a desire on the part of some within Westminster Presbytery to leave the PCA, carrying with them the cherished name of Westminster Presbytery. They do not want to simply withdraw as individual churches, but rather as a group, taking with them the name Westminster Presbytery. Can this be done in the manner proposed? If not, what remedy for these brethren do you suggest that would satisfy their desires and be in accord with the BCO?”

Response: It is the advice of the CCB in reference to the inquiry of Westminster Presbytery (4/21/2001) related to withdrawal from the PCA as a presbytery that:

a. There is no provision within our Constitution for a presbytery as a whole to withdraw.

b. The right of an individual church to affiliate with whomever it will, however, cannot be impinged upon by a higher court (BCO 25-11; cf.11-3, 4). The principle of the BCO that is most closely related is that of

<a id="ga29-p134"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=136 printed_page=134 -->

Chapter 25-11, which allows, “A particular church may withdraw from any court of this body at any time for reasons, which seem to it sufficient”.

c. Should a group of churches choose to leave the PCA in order to form a new affiliation, or to continue an affiliation that they perceive as antecedent to the PCA, then they can peaceably withdraw as a group subject to the consent of each congregation in the group.

d. In the event of a group of churches withdrawing from the PCA with the consent of each church involved, those congregations remaining within the bounds of that presbytery that decline to withdraw with the departing group would stay within their present denomination. Those congregations not departing would constitute the continuing presbytery of the PCA.

e. There are no standards regarding continuing the name of a departing group and such matters should be left to the discretion and charity of the brothers involved. Additionally, any division of assets or attachment of corporate identity, where applicable, will need to be determined by the brothers involved.
Adopted by CCB

Reference 2 -- from Eastern Carolina Presbytery RE Voting Requirements:

Question: Does a Session or the Moderator of a Congregational meeting have the authority to require a greater vote than that required by the Book of Church Order for the call of a pastor or for the dissolution or a pastoral relationship of for the election of a church officer? That is, may the provision of BCO 20-4 and 24-4, “a majority of all the voters present shall be required to elect,” be made more restrictive?

Response: It is not within the purview of the Session or Moderator to require a greater than majority vote for the election of a pastor or church officer. The BCO nowhere gives such powers to Sessions or Moderators. Allowing them to claim such power would open the door to the possible thwarting of the right of the members of the Congregation to determine those who will rule over them, a right which undergirds BCO 20 and 24.
Adopted by CCB

Reference 3 -- from Southern Florida Presbytery RE Divestiture:

Question: Is the Presbytery required to divest Teaching Elders without call, “for a prolonged period, not to exceed three years,” (BCO 13-2) and is the process of divesting a minister of the gospel in BCO 34-10 in conflict with BCO 13-2.

Response: BCO 34-10 and BCO 13-2 are not in conflict, i.e., the Presbytery is not obligated to divest every minister who has been without call for three years, but the Presbytery is required to inquire into the matter and then use its discretion.
Adopted by CCB

Reference 4 -- from Pittsburgh Presbytery RE Minimum Voting Age:

Question: May a Session set a minimum age requirement for communicant membership in their church?

<a id="ga29-p135"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=137 printed_page=135 -->

Response: It is the prerogative of each Session to determine when one has a credible profession of faith and a proper understanding of the sacraments (BCO 6-2). "The time when young persons come to understand the Gospel cannot be precisely fixed." (BCO 57-2) Therefore, sessions must consider requests for admission to communicant membership on an individual basis, regardless of age.

We note that the questions raised by this Reference are separate from the issue of establishing a minimum voting age for communing members, which was the issue before the 27th General Assembly.

In addition, we would point out that if a Session refuses to receive a child (or an adult) who is being transferred that person would remain a member of his or her original church.
Adopted by CCB

## Reference 5 -- From Great Lakes Presbytery re: BCO 58-4

Question 1: What issues or concerns should a presbytery consider when it decides whether to allow an exception to BCO 58-4 in the use of "communicants in good standing in any evangelical church"?

Response: BCO 58-4 does not require the use of the exact terms of 58-4, but allows no exceptions of practice in the administration of the Lord's Supper. "The minister may either invite all those who profess the true religion, and are communicants in good standing in any evangelical church or may invite those who have been approved by the Session, after having given indication of their desire to participate." (BCO 58-4)

If a Presbytery considers receiving a TE who has expressed an exception in his views with respect to the language of BCO 58-4, then Presbytery should be guided by BCO 34-5.

Note that the Introduction to the Directory for the Worship of God indicates that BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the 11th GA.
Adopted by CCB

Question 2: Do previous actions of the GA imply that presbyteries should not allow an exception to BCO 58-4 in the use of "communicants in good standing in any evangelical church"?

Response: Yes, particularly with regard to practice. Refer to the Minutes of the 1993 GA, p. 141, 21-56, III, 18 and the Minutes of the 1986 GA, p. 330, Appendix I, 10, and the Minutes of the 1987 GA, p. 129, 15-63.
Adopted by CCB

Question 3: If Presbyteries may allow an exception to BCO 58-4 in the use of "communicants in good standing in any evangelical church", may the Teaching Elder practice this exception?

<a id="ga29-p136"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=138 printed_page=136 -->

Response: A presbytery may allow an exception as to views, but not as to practice. Presbytery must be guided by BCO 34-5 in terms of teaching of the view.
Adopted by CCB

Question 4: Since sessions have the authority over the Sacrament, what should a session do if it will not require teaching elders to use the full language of BCO 58-4 when the Lord’s Supper is administered under this provision?
Response: See our response to questions 1 and 3, above.
Adopted by CCB

Question 5: What should presbytery do if a Session takes exception to BCO 58-4 in the use of “communicants in good-standing in any evangelical church”, either in belief only or in belief and practice?
Response: See our response to questions 1 and 3, above.
Adopted by CCB

Question 6: If a particular teaching elder was granted an exception to BCO 58-4 in the use of “communicants in good standing in any evangelical church” at the time of his ordination or reception into his current presbytery (as applicable), does he have permission to practice it?
Response: See our response to questions 1 and 3, above.
Adopted by CCB

Question 7: If a given teaching elder did not previously take exception to BCO 58-4 in the use of “communicants in good standing in any evangelical church” but now does, should he make this known to his presbytery?
Response: Yes.
Adopted by CCB

Reference 6 From Philadelphia Presbytery RE Complaints

Question 1: May a person charged with an offense or offenses, who is undergoing process in a trial before a court, complain to the next higher judicatory about the manner in which the trial is being conducted?
Response: A person may not make a complaint to a higher court until after the lower court has acted on the complaint in accordance with BCO 43-2 and 43-3. “A complaint shall first be made to the court whose act or decision is alleged to be in error.” (BCO 43-2) The CCB notes the right of individuals under BCO 43-1 to complain against actions taken during a trial so long as the procedures of BCO 43-2 and 43-3 are followed.
Adopted by CCB
(6-2-0 and TE Childs requested that his negative vote be recorded)

Question 2: Do the grounds for appeal in BCO 42-3 negate the right of an accused person to complain about the manner in which a trial is being conducted?
Response: No. BCO 42-3 refers to appeals and does not negate the right to complain. However, if an appeal is taken in a judicial case, no complaint is then allowable, (BCO 43-1).
Adopted by CCB
(6-2-0 and TE Childs requested that his negative vote be recorded)

<a id="ga29-p137"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=139 printed_page=137 -->

R eferen ce 7 from new river presbytery re exceptions to the minutes of new
River Presbytery
The Chair ruled that New River Presbytery is asking the CCB to serve as a
channel to give advice to the CRPR.
Response: The reference from New River Presbytery asks CCB to advise the CRPR. It
is not within our purview to provide advise to committees who have not
solicited it (see duties in RAO 7-2). Adopted by CCB

constitutional inquiry #1 from ted on clements:
In accordance with RAO 7-2, second sub-paragraph (2), I hereby move that the
Assembly send the following constitutional inquiry to the Committee on Constitutional Business, asking them to report back to the Assembly at (or
before) the time of the report of the committee on review of presbytery
Minutes dealing with New River Presbytery. The Inquiry is as follows:

may the committee on review of presbytery minutes recommend to the
General Assembly for approval an exception to a presbytery minutes
which, if passed, would de fact o decree that an exception taken by a
candidate and/or Teaching Elder is a “fundamental of the system of
doctrine” and thus disqualify the man for service in the PCA unless such
position or view has been previously adjudged to be a “fundamental of
the system of doctrine” through proper judicial process.

it is the opinion of new river presbytery that they may not do so.

in support of our position we make reference tom inutes, 10lh General Assembly,
page 103, Section 0-75, item 25, as follows:
“Also, as the result of proper judicial processes, judgement may be made
which determinately interpret what may or may not be in accord with our
standards. any other procedure of setting forth or compiling a list of
essential ... doctrines would, in effect, amend the standards by an
unconstitutional method.”

We further make reference to the Minutes, 14th General Assembly, Section 14-
52, final paragraph concerning allowing exceptions, which reads:
“This allowance of exceptions, however, does not warrant his teaching or
preaching of that matters oas to disturb the peace and purity of the
church. the court of jurisdiction must determine in each situation
whether such unwarranted actions have occurred.”

appended to this motion is a copy of the minutes of new river presbytery of
the stated meeting of november 11, 2000, pages 3 and 4, which contain the full
language of our approved response to this year’s Committee on Review of
Presbytery Records for further detail on the issue.

<a id="ga29-p138"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=140 printed_page=138 -->

## IV. Advice to the Stated Clerk

**Item 1: Advice to the Stated Clerk in the matter of the role of in these statements.**

Since the adoption of the Creation Study Committee report as amended last year, I have received inquiries as to the status of the report in relation to the constitution. Moreover, there are overtures this year to require exceptions for any views other than 24-hour creation days (Overture 7, 23) to allow exceptions for other views (Overture 20), and to affirm the Position of the 28th General Assembly on diversity of views on creation days (Overture 30).

My advice has been as follows:

a. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of any other study committee report, such as those on the gifts of the Holy Spirit and Freemasonry, that is, it is the thinking of a particular General Assembly on a specific subject.

b. The Creation Study Committee Report, as with all other study committee reports adopted by a General Assembly, does not amend the constitution (BCO Preface, III. “The Constitution Defined” and BCO 26-1); only amendments effected by the proper constitutional procedures are actual and lawful amendments (BCO 26-2, 26-3 and 26-6).

c. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given “due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions” (BCO 14-7).

d. Overtures relating to the Creation Study Committee Report would have the same status as the report, i.e. of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given “due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions” (BCO 14-7).

**Response:** The CCB ratifies the advice of the Stated Clerk relative to the Role of *in these Statements* dated April 21, 2001, and offers the following additional advice: It is the purview of the lower courts to determine the suitability of men applying for reception/ordination with regard to the men’s views on the matters raised *in in these statements*, subject always to proper review and/or judicial process. (BCO 13-6 and 13-9.a) (BCO 24-1) (BCO 11-3,4) Adopted by CCB

**Item 2: Advice to the Stated Clerk regarding the Publication of Presbytery votes on BCO Amendments.**

I have received a letter of concern from an individual calling on me to “repent” of reporting the results of the BCO amendments in the spring Stated Clerk’s letter. My response has been that those results are public information.

**Response:** The CCB advises the Stated Clerk that the committee concurs with his advice in this matter. Adopted by CCB

<a id="ga29-p139"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=141 printed_page=139 -->

## V. Overtures

##### Overture 1 from Missouri Presbytery: Amend BCO 14-1 (12), Increase Number on Committees, Extend Terms to Five Years

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.
*Adopted by CCB*

##### Overture 2 from New Jersey: Amend BCO 58-5 Use of Leavened Bread

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.
*Adopted by CCB*

##### Overture 3 from Susquehanna Valley Presbytery: Amend BCO 20-2 to Include Pastor in Search Committee for Assistant or Associate Pastor

It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture #3 is in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution. Teaching Elders are not members of the local congregation and therefore cannot be a member of the congregation’s pulpit committee. The proposed mandate of the overture that they be so would interfere with the right of the congregation to determine those who will rule over them, a privilege which undergirds BCO 20. Having said that, the Committee does recognize the wisdom of consulting with the senior pastor in the selection of an Assistant or Associate Pastor.
*Adopted by CCB*

##### Overture 4 from Eastern Carolina Presbytery: Change Procedure for Pastoral Relationship Dissolution

It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture #4 is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution. It is also the opinion of the CCB that the offered revision offers very little substantive change to our present standards when they are properly understood and carried out – except for the proposed provision that a Commission can never be used by a presbytery to dissolve the pastoral relationship which seems unnecessarily restrictive for perfunctory cases without objection from any party.
*Adopted by CCB*

##### Overture 7 from Calvary Presbytery: Require Exceptions for Views Other Than Six 24-Hour Creation Days

It is the opinion of the CCB that Overtures 7, 20, 23, and 30 touch on issues that are not properly before the CCB since they do not seek constitutional amendment or constitutional advice.

a. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of any other study committee report, such as those on the gifts of the Holy Spirit and Freemasonry, that is, it is the thinking of a particular General Assembly on a specific subject.

b. The Creation Study Committee Report, as with all other study committee reports adopted by a General Assembly, does not amend the constitution (BCO Preface, III. “The Constitution Defined” and BCO 26-1); only amendments effected by the proper constitutional procedures are actual and lawful amendments (BCO 26-2, 26-3 and 26-6).

<a id="ga29-p140"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=142 printed_page=140 -->

c. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given “due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions” (BCO 14-7).

d. Overtures relating to the Creation Study Committee Report would have the same status as the report, i.e. of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given “due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions” (BCO 14-7).

e. It is the purview of the lower courts to determine the suitability of men applying for reception/ordination with regard to the men’s views on the matters raised in *in thesi* statements, subject always to proper review and/or judicial process. (BCO 13-6 and 13-9.a) (BCO 24-1) (BCO 11-3,4).

Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 9 from Evangel Presbytery: Procedures on Assuming Original Jurisdiction Over Church Members

It was moved, seconded and it carried that the proposed language be found not in conflict with any other part of the constitution. Also, it is noted that Overture 9 (BCO 33-1) and Overture 11 (BCO 34-1) need to stand or fall together to prevent a possible constitutional conflict.

Adopted by CCB

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.

Adopted by CCB

It was moved, seconded and it carried that the proposed language be found not in conflict with any other part of the constitution. Also, it is noted that Overture 9 (BCO 33-1) and Overture 11 (BCO 34-1) need to stand or fall together to prevent a possible constitutional conflict.

Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 13 from Central Carolina Presbytery: Amend BCO 32-3 Regarding Sequence of Meetings of a Court in Judicial Process.

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.

Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 14 from Central Carolina Presbytery: Amend BCO 34-2 regarding Delivery Requirements for Indictments and Citations

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.

Adopted by CCB

<a id="ga29-p141"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=143 printed_page=141 -->

##### Overture 15 from Central Carolina Presbytery: Amend BCO 35-7 and 32-18 Requiring Recording of Testimony and Provision for Transcription

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 16 from Central Carolina Presbytery: Amend BCO 43-1 to Allow Complaint After Appeal is Ruled Out of Order, Withdraw or Abandoned

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 17 from Central Carolina Presbytery: Add a New Chapter 44 to BCO and Appendix H

It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution.
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 19 from Presbytery of the Ascension to ask CCB to Examine the Minutes of the SJC

In response to the Overture from the Presbytery of the Ascension, the CCB understands the SJC to have acted in accord with GA’s directive to proceed according to the provisions of BCO 31-2 which required the SJC to enter an “investigation” phase of the matter before determining if there was sufficient evidence that “should result in raising a strong presumption of the guilt of the party involved” before instituting process and proceeding to trial. Presbyteries were informed by letter (dated August 29, 2000) of :
(1) their right to submit evidence supporting their allegations to the SJC panel during this investigative phase; and
(2) the time and place to submit evidentiary material related to the investigation. The understanding of the SJC was that further theological argument was appropriate for a trial, should an indictment have been rendered. Since no indictment was made, however, on the basis of the evidence presented by all notified parties it would have been inappropriate to try John Wood on the basis of theological arguments against matters neither he, nor the SJC, felt he supported. Had the matter moved to trial, it would have been proper for the presbyteries to make arguments for their allegations of theological error.

The CCB acknowledges that, according to “Whereas #7-9” in Ascension Presbytery’s Overture, it is unfortunate that there was no formal, written response from the SJC to Ascension Presbytery’s inquiry following the panel’s initial contact and prior to its hearing. In order for presbyteries to feel that they have been adequately heard and fairly treated, it is vital that there be complete, timely and official responses to their inquiries.
Adopted by CCB

RE Neikirk requested that his negative vote be recorded and notified the committee that he may file a minority report. The chair appointed TE Chapell to respond for the Committee, if appropriate.

<a id="ga29-p142"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=144 printed_page=142 -->
