# SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS TO THE 29TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY

[NOTE: Presented on Friday morning, June 22, 2001. See 29-61, p. 321]

## AMENDMENT TO THE APRIL 23-24 REPORT:

committee, in response to the receipt of new information from the GA Stated Clerk, ed to review the CCB advice on non-judicial reference #1 from Westminster bytery. A substitute for item A was moved, such that it would read:

a. “The constitution is silent on the question of whether a presbytery as a whole may withdraw from the General Assembly. We recognize the action of the 2nd General Assembly, which enrolled Westminster Presbytery with its proviso, which included its right to withdraw. We also recognize, however, that the procedures we have outlined in items B-E below must be followed.” (See 29-28,III,pg. 133.)

The remainder of the CCB advice on the matter was unchanged. MSP

## CONSTITUTIONAL INQUIRY #1 FROM TE DON CLEMENTS IN BEHALF OF NEW RIVER PRESBYTERY:

accordance with RAO 7-2, second sub-paragraph (2), I hereby move that the embly send the following constitutional inquiry to the Committee on Constitutional iness, asking them to report back to the Assembly at (or before) the time of the art of the Committee on Review of Presbytery Minutes dealing with New River bytery. The Inquiry is as follows:

May the Committee on Review of Presbytery Minutes recommend to the General Assembly for approval an exception to a Presbytery Minutes which, if passed, would de facto decree that an exception taken by a candidate and/or Teaching Elder is a “fundamental of the system of doctrin” and thus disqualify the man for service in the PCA unless such position or view has been previously adjudged to be a “fundamental of the system of doctrin” through proper judicial process.

It is the opinion of New River Presbytery that they may NOT do so.

In support of our position we make reference to the minutes of the 10th General Assembly, page 103, Section 10-75, Item 25, as follows:

“Also, as the result of proper judicial processes, judgment may be made which determinately interpret what may or may not be in accord with our standards. Any other procedure of setting forth or compiling a list of essential...doctrines would, in effect, amend the standards by an unconstitutional method.”

<a id="ga29-p144"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=146 printed_page=144 -->

We further make reference to the minutes of the 14th General Assembly, Section 14-52, final paragraph concerning allowing exceptions, which reads:

“This allowance of exceptions, however, does not warrant his teaching or preaching of that matter so as to disturb the peace and purity of the church. The court of jurisdiction must determine in each situation whether such unwarranted actions have occurred.”

**Answer:**

It is the opinion of the CCB that the CRPR may make to the GA any recommendation it wishes which are within the purview of RAO-14 (see especially RAO 14-7).

It is then up to the GA to decide how to handle such recommendations.

The CCB would note that a man who is regularly licensed or ordained may be “disqualified” only through the regular judicial process.

MSC

3) INQUIRY FROM THE BILLS AND OVERTURES COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS IN RE. TO OVERTURE 35:

“In the John Wood matter (i.e. a matter in which the SJC conducted a *BCO* 31-2 investigation but determined not to file charges), is it constitutionally permissible for the GA to amend the report of the SJC by deleting a concurring opinion?”

**Answer:**

It is the opinion of the CCB that it is not constitutional for the GA to amend the SJC report on Case 99-1 (the John Wood Matter) by deleting any or all-concurring opinions.

**Reasoning:**

1. The SJC manual 20-2 specifically delineates that a concurring and/or dissenting opinion of any member of the SJC is a component part of the SJC report.

2. *BCO* 15-5a specifically delineates that the SJC members have the constitutional right to attach concurring or dissenting opinions to any SJC decision.

3. *BCO* 15-5 has specifically been framed to assert that an SJC decision is the final decision of the GA. Additionally, *BCO* 15-5 has gone to great lengths to place into our constitution safeguards to prevent SJC decisions from being amended on the floor of GA. Unless the provisions of *BCO* 15-5c (one third of SJC submitting a minority report) are brought into play, there is a clear constitutional bar against any complaint, appeal, change, modification, or amendment to an SJC report.

4. While the CCB is aware that the *BCO* 34-1 assumption of original jurisdiction is a constitutionally unique happening, it is the advice of the CCB that the *BCO* 15-5 bar to amendment is still in effect. Our reasoning is as follows:

<a id="ga29-p145"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=147 printed_page=145 -->

a) When the 28th GA referred the “John Wood Matter” to the SJC, it recognized, at least de facto, that the matter constituted a case, since BCO 15-4 seems to allow only cases to be committed to the SJC. As such, all the provisions of BCO 15-5 will apply.

b) BO15-5 is the governing paragraph whenever a “case” is committed to the SJC.

1. Was a case submitted to the SJC? Yes.
2. Did the SJC make a decision? Yes.
3. Do the judges have a constitutional right to file a concurring and/or dissenting opinion? Yes (cf. BCO 15-5a)
4. Can the GA amend the SJC report of Case 99-1 to delete concurring opinions? NO (cf. BCO 15-5a)

MSC

## 4) CONSTITUTIONAL INQUIRY #2 FROM TE JOHN VAN DEVENDER

The Assembly received the following Constitutional Inquiry from TE John Van Devender and referred it to CCB:

WHEREAS, SJC Case 99-1 regarding the John Wood matter is scheduled to be presented to this General Assembly; and

WHEREAS, a supplemental report of a concurring opinion on this case, namely that one signed by RE Jack Williamson and others, is included in this report; and

WHEREAS, this particular supplemental report purports to comment on the action of the 28th General Assembly and further to conclude that the 28th General Assembly erred in assuming original jurisdiction of the matter involving John Wood; and

WHEREAS, BCO 15-4 and 5 authorizes the SJC to try and decide only the judicial cases committed to it; and

WHEREAS, the SJC does not seem to have any explicit authority to comment upon or rule on an action of the General Assembly; and

THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED that the 29th General Assembly directs the Committee on Constitutional Business to consider the following question and report back an answer prior to the consideration of the report from the SJC: “Does the Standing Judicial Commission have the authority to render an opinion on the legitimacy of an action of the General Assembly if not specifically asked to do so, particularly the legitimacy of an action in which the General Assembly refers business to the SJC; and

BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that, if the Committee on Constitutional Business returns with a negative answer, that the 29th General Assembly move immediately to remove this specific concurring opinion from the SJC report and further that the Assembly direct members of the SJC to refrain from making such actions in the future.

<a id="ga29-p146"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=148 printed_page=146 -->

Answer:
It is the opinion of the CCB that SJC may only address what is assigned to it. It may not render any opinion unless it is first assigned a matter. Once it is assigned a matter, the SJC may render an opinion even as to the legitimacy of an action in which the GA refers business to the SJC. MSC

5) RECEIVED THE MINORITY REPORT ON OPINION ON OVERTURE 19:

The undersigned respectfully disagree with the first paragraph of the CCB’s response to Overture 19.

The CCB is charged with reviewing the minutes of the SJC. Yet the minutes of the SJC make no references to how the SJC handled the mandate that “representatives from the petitioning presbyteries be permitted to present their allegations before the SJC” (M28GA, p. 310). So far as we can tell, the conclusions of the majority of the CCB with regard to this matter rest solely on personal correspondence/conversations with a member or members of the SJC. But such personal contacts do not constitute the official record of the SJC and ought not to be the basis for a determination of what the SJC did or did not do. In our judgment, this matter should have been handled as the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records has typically handled matters where a presbytery’s minutes do not indicate that the presbytery acted as it needed to. That is, an exception to the minutes should have been taken in the form, “The minutes of the SJC give no evidence of how or if the SJC complied with the mandate of the 28th General Assembly that ‘representatives from the petitioning presbyteries be permitted to represent their allegations before the SJC (M28GA, p.310)’.” The SJC would then respond formally to the apparent exception by correcting their minutes, adopting an explanatory note, etc. The CCB could have then reviewed that response, thus insuring that it was responding to the official position of the SJC.

RE Frederick Neikirk
TE Morton H. Smith

6) Approved Majority response to Minority report on Overture 19:

After receiving and examining the SJC’s minutes the CCB concluded that the SJC had carried out, faithfully and constitutionally, all that the 28th GA had charged them to do in regard to the Wood matter.

MSC

Respectfully submitted,

TE Craig Childs, Chairman
TE Mark Rowden, Acting Secretary

<a id="ga29-p147"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=149 printed_page=147 -->

SECOND SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS

1) CONSTITUTIONAL INQUIRY #3:

What is the status of an SJC concurring opinion, that has been reported to the General Assembly, that has not been found by the SJC to be in “temperate language?” (SJC Manual 14-7)

Response:

It is the advice of the CCB that:

SJC 14-7 indicates that the time for a determination of the temperateness of the language is prior to the concurring opinion being appended to a decision. If a concurring opinion has been appended to a decision, the opportunity to assess or reassess temperateness has lapsed.

Therefore, the status of such an SJC concurring opinion, which has been reported to the General Assembly, remains valid regardless of subsequent concerns about temperateness.

Further, the CCB observes that concurring and/or dissenting opinions are, by definition, not the opinion of the SJC. As such, they do not, of necessity, reflect the mind of the Church. They are not “...binding and conclusive on the parties who are directly involved in the matter,” nor may they be appealed to in the same sense as the majority position as to any principle which may have been expressed by the concurring and/or dissenting opinion. Such concurring and/or dissenting opinions are simply the opinions of individuals. (BCO 14-7, cf. SJC Manual 18.3)

Approved by CCB 4-3-0

Minority Report

The CCB observes that concurring and/or dissenting opinions are, by definition, not the opinion of the SJC. As such, they do not, of necessity, reflect the mind of the Church. They are not “...binding and conclusive on the parties who are directly involved in the matter...,” nor may they be appealed to in the same sense as the majority position as to any principle which may have been expressed by the concurring and/or dissenting opinion. Such concurring and/or dissenting opinions are simply the opinions of individuals (BCO 14-7, cf. SJC Manual 18.3.). The minority agrees.

However, it is the opinion of the Minority that the requirement of temperate language cannot be left solely to the author(s) of a document. The precedent of the General Assembly in dealing with documents which must be “couched in temperate language” (e.g. BCO 45-5, cf. SJC Manual 14.7) is that the court itself must determine the question of whether the language is temperate. Since the SJC, as a Commission of the General Assembly, is the court, the SJC must itself act to determine if concurring and/or dissenting opinions meet that standard.

<a id="ga29-p148"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=150 printed_page=148 -->

It is, therefore, the position of the Minority that an SJC concurring and/or dissenting opinion that has been reported to the General Assembly and that has not been found by the SJC to be temperate in language fails to meet the requirements of SJC Manual 14.7. It is not properly a part of the report of the SJC and should not be included in the minutes of the General Assembly. The only mechanism currently available for determining whether the SJC has so acted (i.e., has acted to determine that the opinion is temperate) is the CCB’s review of the SJC’s minutes which occurs prior to the meeting of the General Assembly.

Signed: TE Morton H. Smith, RE Frederick R. Neikirk, and RE Richard A. Springer.

2) CONSTITUTIONAL INQUIRY #4

Request advice on how the Assembly is to suspend our Rules of Operation: *BCO, RAO, SJC Manual and Robert’s Rules of Order to obey the King of the Church – Jesus Christ as He has spoken to us in His Word.* What is the motion, or procedure to live by and obey God’s Word when we are fenced in by our Standing Rules?

Response:

It is the advice of the CCB that the documents referenced in the inquiry are either part of our Constitution¹ or are rules we have adopted as prudential applications of Biblical patterns of polity². As such, the delegates to the General Assembly are bound by their ordination vows (*BCO* 21-5, 3&amp;4 and 24-5, 3&amp;4) to operate under these as fully and fairly as possible. Thus, for example, if a provision of Robert’s Rules contradicts the *BCO*, the latter takes precedence and the former is set aside. Similarly, if one of the documents contains provisions for its suspension, then the Assembly may avail itself of that procedure. The absence of such a procedure in certain documents means that the General Assembly is bound to operate under the provisions of those documents unless or until they are amended.

We note this always allows those who feel that Scriptural principles have been ignored to access the means of redress available in our Constitution, including the mechanisms of *BCO* 45. Additionally, they (and all of us) should pray that the Holy Spirit will ultimately affirm what is correct for the Church as the Church properly follows its standards for operations and revisions in the future. Private concerns between individuals should be handled according to Matthew 18.

The presumption that we need to suspend our rules in order to obey Christ is an opinion that the CCB is not willing to affirm. If our standards are shown to be out of accord with Scripture, then there are proper procedures to follow, at each level, in order to change those standards.

Approved by CCB 7-0-0

¹ Which consists of the Westminster Confession of Faith, together with the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and the Book of Church Order, all of which are subordinate to the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments (Preliminary Principle III)

² e.g., RAO, SJC Manual, Robert’s Rules of Order

<a id="ga29-p149"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=151 printed_page=149 -->

Respectfully submitted,
Is/ TE Craig Childs, Chairman /s/ TE Mark Rowden, Acting Secretary

29-29 committee of commissioners on in surance, annuities and relief
RE James “Bebo” Elkin, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the report (See Appendix G. p. 495). He introduced RE Jim Hughes, director of IAR,
who reported on the various ministries of IAR. RE Gary Campbell, chairman of iar
Investment Committee, reported on IAR investments. TE Robert Clarke, chairman of
IAR Relief Committee, reported on the ministerial relief work of IAR. The report was
closed with prayer by RE Elkin.

#### I. business referred to the committee

1. minutes of the board of trustees meetings of june 20, 2000; August 11,
2000; November 3, 2000; March 2, 2001; May 8, 2001; and June 19, 2001.
2. The Audit Report dated December 31, 2000.
3. the board of trustees report and recommendations to the 29th General Assembly.

#### II. statement of major issues discussed

IAR Director James L. Hughes gave an oral report touching upon various items
in the printed materials, including the recent work toward a “cap” on out of
pocket expenses for prescription drugs by plan members. He also explained the
budget increase of 17.9% as including an overlap of the incoming director and
relief representative upon the anticipated retirement of the director and the
relief representative at the end of 2002.

Trustees Gary Campbell, Bob Clark and Hal Shepherd also spoke to various
items of concern, including the increasing cost of prescription drugs, the present
state of the investment markets, and the adequacy of future ministerial relief
funding.

#### III. recommendations :

1. that the minutes of board meetings of june 20, 2000, August 11, 2000,
November 3, 2000, March 2, 2001, May 8, 2001, and June 19, 2001 be
approved. A dopted
2. That the audit report for the year ended December 31, 2000 by Capin Crouse
LLP be received. Adopted
3. that the General Assembly approve the use of cap in crouse LLP, to conduct
the 2001 audit. A dopted
4. That the 2002 budget be received with the understanding that it is a spending
plan and will be adjusted as necessary by the board of trustees to accommodate
changing circumstances during the year. deferred to ac Report, p. 254-255
5. That the General Assembly urge our member churches to participate in the
annual Christmas Offering or budget regular benevolence giving to support
relief activities through the Ministerial Relief Fund. A dopted

6. That the General Assembly approve the Thirteenth Amendment to the
Presbyterian Church in America Money Purchase Pension Plan and the
twentieth amendment to the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-Sheltered
Annuity Plan (see Appendix Two, p. 502) and that the resolutions related to
these amendments be adopted and included in the minutes of the General Assembly. A dopted
7. That the General Assembly approve the Second Amendment to the Presbyterian Church in America Health and Welfare Benefit Trust Agreement (see Appendix
Three, p. 506) and the resolution related to the Amendment. A dopted
8. That the General Assembly ratify the Board's decision to amend the wording of
the IAR Bylaws Article II, Section 1. “Board of Trustees”, paragraph 1, sentence
5. page 2 to read as follows:
The Board may make requests to the Presbyteries to nominate specific
men to the Board and may submit to the General Assembly Nominating
committee letters of recommendation concerning particular nominees
from the Presbyteries.

any called meeting of the board may take place by means of

1. That the month of October 2001, be set aside as a month of prayer for the “10/40 Window” and world evangelization, and that God would send many more laborers into His harvest field. Adopted

## 29-31 Partial Report of Committee of Commissioners on Covenant Theological Seminary

RE David Moon, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer introduced TE Bryan Chapell, who reported on the work of the Seminary. [Text of report, see 29-37, p. 160.]

The Assembly **adopted** the following statement: “In light of an unfortunate and unfair article in the Presbyterian and Reformed News accusing CTS of an apparent cover-up, this 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America reaffirms its support of and confidence in the board of directors, the administration, the staff, and the faculty of Covenant Theological Seminary”. Prayer was offered by RE Moon for the Seminary, specifically emeritus faculty members and their spouses who are struggling with ill health.

## 29-32 Committee of Commissioners on PCA Foundation

TE James Landrum, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report. He introduced TE Dave Clelland, Chairman of the PCA Foundation Board of Directors, who introduced RE Randel Stair, President of PCA Foundation, who reported on the ministry of the Foundation. The report was closed with prayer by TE Landrum.

## I. Business Referred to the Committee

1. The Report of the PCA Foundation [see Appendix L, p. 615]
2. The Minutes of the PCA Foundation
A. August 18, 2000
B. March 2, 2001
C. May 8, 2001
3. Proposed 2002 Budget of the PCA Foundation
4. Financial Statements with Independent Auditor’s Report of the PCA Foundation

## II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

## III. RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. That the first and second sentences of ARTICLE 2. DIRECTORS, Section 2.2, Number and Qualifications, and the first, third and fourth sentences in Section 2.3, Election and Term, of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED BYLAWS OF PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION,

<a id="ga29-p154"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=156 printed_page=154 -->

INC. be amended so that all ten members of the PCA Foundation’s Board are elected at large by the General Assembly, and to allow an individual who has served on the Board of another Committee or Agency to serve on the PCAF Board without any lapse in time, as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 2.2 “The Board shall consist of ten (10) members $\div$ (a) six (6) members $\div$ in classes elected through the standard nomination and election procedures by the General Assembly of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) $\div$ (the "At Large Members"); and (b) one (1) member from each of the following permanent committees or board of trustees of the agencies: (1) Covenant College; (2) Covenant Theological Seminary; (3) Mission to the World; and (4) Mission to North America (the "Appointed Members"). The Chairman of the Board shall be an At Large Member $\div$.

Section 2.3. “The $\{\text{At-Large Members}\}$ [Directors] shall serve for four (4) year terms, and shall be divided into four (4) classes, each class being as nearly equal in number as possible, so that the terms of approximately one-fourth of the $\{\text{At-Large Members}\}$ [Directors] shall expire each year. Each Director elected shall hold office until his successor shall be elected and shall qualify, or until his earlier resignation, death or removal. Directors shall be eligible for $\{\text{re-election}\}$ [reelection] to a second four (4) year term, after which there must be a one (1) year interval before $\{\text{re-election}\}$ [reelection. The restrictions set forth in the first sentence of Rule 4-5 of the Rules of Assembly Operations of Presbyterian Church in America shall not apply to the Corporation].”

Adopted

2. That the last sentence of ARTICLE 2. DIRECTORS, Section 2.2 Election and Term of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED BYLAWS OF PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC., be amended so that Deacons may be elected to the PCA Foundation’s Board, as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 2.2 “Directors need not be residents of the State of Georgia. All of the members of the Board shall be either Teaching Elders $\{\text{or}\} |, |$ Ruling Elders [or Deacons] in the Presbyterian Church in America, and at least one-half of such members shall be Ruling Elders [and Deacons].”

Adopted

3. That the first and second sentences of ARTICLE 4. OFFICERS, Section 4.4 Compensation and the first, second and third sentences of section 4.5 Term of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED BYLAWS OF PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC., be amended so that these sections are updated and properly state the compensation policies and procedures and term of office as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 4.4. “The $\{\text{salary}\}$ [compensation] of the President of the Corporation shall be $\div$ recommended by the Board to the General Assembly for approval. No other Officer shall receive compensation for his services as an Officer $\div$ [determined by the Board].”

<a id="ga29-p155"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=157 printed_page=155 -->

Section 4.5. “Each Officer -
[who is also a member of the board (other than the P resid en t)], shall hold
office fora term of one year and until his successor is chosen or until his earlier
resignation, death or removal. The President -f of -the Corporation} [and all
officers that are not members of the Board] shall hold office at the pleasure

that the second and third sentences of article 4. OFFICERS, Section 4.12
Contracts (presented in its entirety) of the Foundation’s AM ENDED AND
RESTATED BYLAWS OF PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
FOUNDATION, INC., be amended raising the dollar limitation by which an
officer, employee or agent may bind the Foundation without Board
authorization, as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold
added.)
Section 4.12 “Unless otherwise restricted in a particular instance by the
Board by a resolution, the officers of the corporations hall always have the
authority to (a) bind the Corporation by any contract or (b) render the
Corporation liable pecuniarily, in connection with the performance by the
corporation of its exempt activities and other activities in the ordinary course of
its business, including, but not limited to, the execution, delivery and
performance by the corporation of annuity contracts, trust agreements, advise
and consult fund agreements, and all expenses incurred by the Corporation
related thereto. Notwithstanding the above, unless authorized in a particular
instance by the Board by resolution, no Officer, employee or agent shall have
the authority to bind the Corporation by any contract, to pledge its assets or
credit, or to render it liable pecuniarily, respecting or in any manner related to
any un budgeted operating expense of the Corporation, if the amount involved in
such contract, pledge or obligation exceeds {$1,000} [$10,000]. If so
authorized or if less than {$1,000|- [$ 10 ,000 ], any of the foregoing officers
singly may execute contracts or deliver instruments on behalf of the
Corporation, pledge its assets or credit, or render it liable pecuniarily, with
respect to such unbudgeted operating expenses.” A dopted

That ARTICLE 5. INDEMNIFICATION, Sections 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 and 5.6
(presented in their entirety) of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED
BYLAWS OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
FOUNDATION, INC., be amended in order to reflect certain changes in the
state of georgia code of Indemnification, as follows: (Strikethroughs to be
deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)
Section 5.1. Conditional Indemnification. “Under the circumstances
prescribed in this Section 5.1, the Corporation shall indemnify and hold
harmless any {individua l! [person who was or is a party or is threatened to
be] made a party to any threatened, pending or completed action, suit or
proceeding, whether civil, criminal, adm inistrative^- or investigative, and

<a id="ga29-p156"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=158 printed_page=156 -->

whether formal or informal (a "Proceeding"), {because the individual} [by reason of the fact that he or she] is or was a Director, Officer, employee or agent of the Corporation, or, while a Director, Officer, employee or agent of the Corporation, is or was serving at the request of the Corporation as a director, officer, partner, [joint venturer,] trustee, employee or agent of another foreign or domestic {business or nonprofit} corporation, partnership, joint venture, trust, employee benefit plan or other enterprise, against {any} [the] obligation to pay a judgment, settlement, penalty, fine or reasonable expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) actually [and reasonably] incurred by him [or her in connection] with {respect to} such Proceeding (collectively a "Liability"), but only if {the individual acted in a manner he believed in good faith to be in or not opposed to} [he or she conducted himself or herself in good faith, and reasonably believed: (1) with respect to conduct in his or her "official capacity" (as that term is defined in Section 14-3-850 of the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, as amended), that such conduct was in} the best interests of the Corporation{, and, in the case of } [; (2) with respect to all other cases, only if that conduct was at least not opposed to the best interests of the Corporation; or (3) with respect to] any criminal Proceeding, {the individual} [that he or she] had no reasonable cause to believe his [or her] conduct was unlawful. Notwithstanding the above, the indemnification permitted hereunder in connection with a Proceeding by or in the right of the Corporation is limited to reasonable expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) incurred in connection with {the Proceeding} [a Proceeding in which it is determined that such person has met the standard of conduct required by this Section 5.1]. The termination of {a} [any] Proceeding by judgment, order, settlement, conviction[,] or upon a plea of nolo contendere or its equivalent shall not, of itself, {be determinative} [create a presumption] that the {individual} [person] did not meet the standard of conduct set forth in this Section 5.1. Notwithstanding the {above} [foregoing], the Corporation shall not indemnify any Director, Officer, employee or agent in connection with any Proceeding {(i) by or in the right of the Corporation in which the individual was adjudged liable to the Corporation, or (ii) in which he} [with respect to conduct for which he or she] was adjudged liable on the basis that personal benefit was improperly received by him [or her].

"Section 5.2. Mandatory Indemnification. {To the extent that a} [If any] Director, Officer, employee or agent has been [wholly] successful, on the merits or otherwise, in the defense of any Proceeding to which he {was a party, or in the defense of any claim, issue or matter therein, because he} [or she was a party because he or she] is or was a Director, Officer, employee or agent of the Corporation, the Corporation shall indemnify him [or her] against reasonable expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) {actually} incurred by him [or her] in connection therewith.

"Section 5.3. Advance or Reimbursement of Litigation Expenses. {The Corporation shall pay for or reimburse the reasonable} [Reasonable] expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) incurred by a Director, Officer,

<a id="ga29-p157"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=159 printed_page=157 -->

employee or agent who is a party to a Proceeding [shall be paid by the Corporation] in advance of [the] final disposition of {the Proceeding if: (i) the Director, Officer, employee or agent furnishes the Corporation a written affirmation of his good faith belief that he has met the standard of conduct described in Section 5.1 above; and (ii) the Director, Officer, employee or agent furnishes the Corporation a written undertaking, executed personally or on his behalf, to repay the advance if it is ultimately determined that he is not entitled to indemnification under this Article 5} [such Proceeding if the procedures set forth in Section 14-3-853 of the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, as amended, are complied with].

"Section 5.4. Procedure. Except as provided in {Section} [Sections] 5.2 [and 5.5], and except as may be ordered by a court {pursuant to Section 14-3-854 of the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, as amended (the "Code")}, the Corporation shall not indemnify any Director, Officer, employee or agent unless authorized hereunder and a determination has been made that indemnification [of such individual] is proper in the circumstances because {such individual} [he or she] has met the applicable standard of conduct set forth in Section 5.1. Such determination shall be made in accordance with Section 14-3-855 of the {Code,} [Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, as amended,] except that, in addition to those parties entitled to make such a determination under Section 14-3-855(b), the members of the General Assembly, by majority vote of a quorum of those members not parties to such Proceeding, may also make such determination.

"Section 5.5. Nonexclusivity. The indemnification provided by this Article 5 shall not be deemed exclusive of any other right to which the persons indemnified hereunder shall be entitled, including any right pursuant to Section 14-3-854 of the Code [under law or contract], and shall inure to the benefit of the heirs, executors or administrators of such persons.

"Section 5.6. Insurance. The Corporation shall {provide} [purchase and maintain] insurance on behalf of any person who is or was a Director or Officer of the Corporation[,] or who, while a Director or Officer [of the Corporation], is or was serving at the request of the Corporation as a director, officer, partner, [joint venturer,] trustee, employee or agent of another foreign or domestic {business or nonprofit} corporation, partnership, joint venture, trust, employee benefit plan or other {enterprise} [entity], against any Liability asserted against {him and} [or] incurred by him [or her] in any such capacity, or arising out of his [or her] status as such, whether or not the Corporation would have the power to indemnify {him} [or advance expenses to him or her] against such Liability under the provisions of this Article 5." Adopted

29-35 committee of commissioners on ridge haven

Challenges and victories for Ridge Haven Conference Center

#### III. RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. That the Assembly approve the Audit report from Wayne Kevitt and the Administrator’s response. Adopted
2. That the Assembly approve the requesting of all PCA churches to consider seriously how they may financially assist Ridge Haven, their camps and conference center in paying for the NC State mandated water system improvements. Adopted

4. That the Assembly adopt the first Sunday in February, 2002, as a day of prayer and promotion for Ridge Haven ministries. Adopted
5. That the Assembly commend to the denomination the WIC Love Gift for 2001. Adopted
6. That the Assembly commend TE Robert Brunson for his ten years of faithful service on the Ridge Haven Board and the three years of faithful service as its President. Adopted
7. That the Assembly commend RE Julian Davis for his five years of faithful service on the Ridge Haven Board. Adopted
8. That the Assembly approve the Board’s minutes of its meetings held on September 25, 2000, January 29, 2001, and May 14, 2001. Adopted
9. It was MSC to thank the Board of Directors and Administrators of RHCC for their diligent and faithful service to the Lord in this significant ministry to the PCA, and to encourage them to continue faithful administration of the great financial and natural resources that God has given to RHCC. Adopted

29-36 By common consent, Committee of Commissioners on MNA was excused to meet.

29-37 Partial Report of Committee of Commissioners on Covenant Theological Seminary

RE David Moon, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the Report. The report was closed with prayer by RE Moon. (See Appendix F, p. 488.)

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

- **a.** The Report of CTS
- **b.** The minutes of the following meetings of the Board of Trustees

1) May 19-20, 2000
2) September 22-23, 2000
3) January 26-27, 2001
c. The minutes of the following meetings of the Executive Committee of the Board of Trustees:
1) December 1, 2000
2) January 26, 2001
3) March 2, 2001
d. The financial audit for CTS and the proposed 2001-2002 CTS budget.

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

Dr. Chapell began his report with a request that the Committee of Commissioners make a decision regarding the extent to which they would deal with the matter of allegations in the independent press that Covenant Seminary unlawfully allowed a women to preach in a chapel service and then sought to cover up the matter. Since this matter did not appear in the minutes that were before the committee for their review, it was decided by the committee that we adhere strictly to the Rules of Assembly Operation and not make a recommendation to the Assembly regarding this matter. Dr. Chapell gave a

<a id="ga29-p161"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=163 printed_page=161 -->

brief answer to this allegation and then answered several questions with regard to it.

Other matters were reported by Dr. Chapell, including the seminary’s accreditation to offer a Master of Arts degree by extension, utilizing new technologies. For the 12th consecutive year, the seminary had a record enrollment, a total of 1,172 students last year. The new library was completed, the fiscal year ended in the black and 100% of last year’s M.Div. students who were recommended by the seminary and who were seeking a position, were able to be placed in a ministry.

### III. RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary, for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, its emphasis on the Reformed faith, and the Great Commission, for its students and graduates, its faculty and staff, its Trustees, and for those who support the Seminary through their prayers and gifts.
*Adopted*

2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary through their prayers and gifts, and by recommending the Seminary to prospective students.
*Adopted*

3. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by the General Assembly.
*Adopted*

4. That the minutes of the meetings of the Board of Trustees of Covenant Theological Seminary for May 19-20, 2000, September 22-23, 2000, and January 26-27, 2001 be approved with notations; and that the minutes of the meeting of the Executive Committee of the Board of Trustees for December 1, 2000, January 26, 2001, and March 2, 2001 be approved with notations.
*Adopted*

5. That the financial audit for Covenant Theological Seminary for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2000 by Humes and Barrington, CPA be received.
*Adopted*

6. That the proposed 2001-2002 budget of Covenant Theological Seminary be approved.
*Deferred to the AC Report, p. 254-255*

7. That the faculty, staff and Board of CTS be commended for their bold and faithful expression and promotion of Biblical truth as we enter the 21st century, and that they be encouraged to continue to do so.
*Adopted*

8. That the Assembly pray for the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary.
*Adopted*

Bland, III for his excellent leadership, with thanks to the Lord for the good results in GA/MNA ministry during 2000, and recommends his re-election as GA/MNA Coordinator for another year.
Adopted

2. That upon it's constituting Reformed University Ministries as a separate permanent committee, the General Assembly elect TE Rod S. Mays as Coordinator of Reformed University Ministries for the 2001/2002 term and that special thanks be given to former coordinators and interim coordinators TE Mark Lowry, RE Bebo Elkin, TE Marvin Padgett, and TE Wilson Benton. Under the new permanent committee, RUM will establish a formal evaluation procedure of the Coordinator in time for the 2002/2003 re-election.
Adopted as amended

4. That the General Assembly thank God for the **increasing church planting** that is occurring under local church and presbytery initiatives; thank God for the labor in the Gospel of all PCA church planters, multicultural church planters and missionaries, chaplains, Reformed University Ministries campus ministers, staff and interns, and Reformed University Fellowship International campus ministers, staff and interns. Further, that the General Assembly encourage faithful prayer that the Lord will send additional laborers through GA/MNA and Reformed University Ministries into the fields that are ripe for harvest in North America.

Adopted

5. That **PCA churches** be reminded of their **covenant obligation** to give high priority to giving GA/MNA and all other PCA Committees and Agencies the Partnership Share/Askings, so that the Gospel is advanced in greater measure.

Adopted

6. That the General Assembly express its gratitude to the Lord for a **Thanksgiving Offering** of more than $57,000, and commend to the churches of the PCA the opportunity to support Urban and Mercy Ministries, preferably through an offering taken during the Thanksgiving season; and further, that churches consider how they may directly participate in greater measure in Urban and Mercy Ministries.

Adopted

ministry of bethany christian services in the area of pregnancy counseling
and adoption, encourage continued support by churches and presbyteries, and
invite the Bethany representative to speak to the Assembly for five minutes at
this, or a more opportune time. A dopted
8. That the General Assembly commend to the churches these resources and
opportunities for ministry available through GA/MNA:
• The opportunity to seek the Lord’s leading in church planting, campus
ministry or the chaplaincy.
• The opportunity to participate in prayer and financial support (designated
support in addition to Partnership Share/Asking giving) for:
■ Church Planting among the many different regions, cultures and
ethnic groups in North America;
■ Church Planting regional coordinators and movement leaders;
* Church Planting apprenticeships (through the Timothy Fund);
■ Campus Ministries;
■ Church Vitality;
■ Chaplain Ministries (especially through The Chaplain Guardian
Corps).
• the opportunity to build relationships of fellowship and ministry with
Korean churches and other ethnic groups within the PCA, as well as with
other true believers in our local communities.
• utilize the resources of church vitality materials and consultation services.
• Recommend to church members that they subscribe (at no cost) to GA/M NA
Multiply/Intercessor.
A dopted
9. That Overture 5 from Heartland Presbytery requesting to be divided so that the
state of iowa would become a separate presbytery of the PCA effective january
1, 2002, be answered in the affirmative. A dopted

##### OVERTURE 5 from Heartland Presbytery (to MNA)

“Divide Heartland Presbytery”

Whereas, the geographical boundaries of heartland presbytery are among the
largest in the PCA; and,

Whereas, the churches within the boundaries of iowa now number 6 organized
churches numbering 813 members; two mission; and one possible church
transferring from Independency; and,

Whereas, stewardship of the local churches relates to time, talent and tithes;
and, with the current distances, it is difficult to maintain close fellowship
due to the travel time required; and,

Whereas, requests from other Presbyteries with fewer churches and members
have been approved (recognizing the same difficulties as Heartland);

<a id="ga29-p166"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=168 printed_page=166 -->

THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED, that Heartland Presbytery be divided so that the State of Iowa would become a separate Presbytery of the PCA effective January 1, 2002.

Adopted at the 36th Stated Meeting of Heartland Presbytery, on November 11, 2000.

Attested by: /s/ Lawrence N. Lunceford, Stated Clerk

10. That Overture 12 from Central Carolina Presbytery requesting to remove the eleven county area known as the “Piedmont Triad” of North Carolina from the Central Carolina Presbytery to form a new, Piedmont Triad Presbytery, be answered in the affirmative.
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 12 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to MNA)

“Divide Central Carolina Presbytery into Two Presbyteries”

Whereas, the Central Carolina Presbytery exists for the Glory of God and the furtherance on His Kingdom here on Earth;

Whereas, the Central Carolina Presbytery currently consists of Forty-Two (42) particular and mission congregations comprising more than Six Thousand (6,000) communing members within a geographical area requiring substantially more than three hours to cross;

Whereas, the 16th General Assembly adopted guidelines for the multiplication of existing presbyteries into new presbyteries, which guidelines provide a set of ideals governing the development of new presbyteries;

Whereas, pursuant to those guidelines, the Central Carolina Presbytery committed itself to a plan to grow into three separate presbyteries several years ago, which plan included developing three sub-regions (Metrolina, Triad, and Sandhills) within the presbytery and focusing church planting resources on developing new churches within each of these regions;

Whereas, the Triad Region of the Central Carolina Presbytery has grown to Six (6) particular congregations and Two (2) mission congregations comprising more than One Thousand communing members and these churches have developed a strong sense of mutual care and potential for shared ministry and joint ministry projects;

Whereas, Five of the particular congregations located in the Triad Region have communing membership in excess of One Hundred Twenty-Five, with strong prospects for continued growth in the future;

Whereas, there is a commonly recognized regional identity within the Eleven (11) County area known as the "Piedmont Triad" in North Carolina which the Triad Region of Central Carolina Presbytery was formed to build upon and which does not partition any existing metropolitan areas;

Whereas, the Central Carolina Presbytery believes it is consistent with the guidelines of the 16th General Assembly to form a new presbytery in the Eleven County area of North Carolina known as the "Piedmont Triad"

<a id="ga29-p169"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=169 printed_page=null -->

and that this multiplication of ministry would further the advance of God's Kingdom and the growth of His Church on Earth;

NOW, THEREFORE the Central Carolina Presbytery hereby petitions and overtures the General Assembly pursuant to 14-6(e) of the Book of Church Order of the Presbyterian Church in America as follows:

(1) To remove the Eleven County area commonly known as the "Piedmont Triad" of North Carolina and consisting of Surry, Yadkin, Davie, Davidson, Stokes, Forsyth, Rockingham, Guilford, Randolph, Caswell and Alamance Counties from the existing bounds of the Central Carolina Presbytery;

(2) To erect the "Piedmont Triad Presbytery of North Carolina," establishing the boundaries of such presbytery to include those Eleven counties and the particular and mission congregations currently located within them;

(3) To authorize the conduct of business by this newly established church court effective July 1,2001, consistent with Chapter 13 of the Book of Church Order and such other provisions as may apply.

It is so moved and approved by the Assembled Court of the Central Carolina Presbytery this 27th day of January, 2001.

Attested by: /s/ Howie Burkhalter, Moderator, Central Carolina Presbytery

This Overture was moved, seconded and approved at a duly constituted meeting of the Central Carolina Presbytery at which a quorum was present.

Attested by: /s/ David M. Frierson, Stated Clerk

11. That Overture 22 from Great Lakes Presbytery requesting to divide Great Lakes Presbytery and form Ohio Valley Presbytery, be answered in the affirmative.
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 22 from Great Lakes Presbytery (to MNA)

"Divide Great Lakes Presbytery and Form Ohio Valley Presbytery"

Now Therefore, Be it Resolved, that Great Lakes Presbytery unanimously overtures the 29th General Assembly to form a new “Ohio Valley” Presbytery by:

Establishing the boundary of the continuing Great Lakes Presbytery to include:

All the state of Michigan;

All of Indiana north and east of and including the counties of Porter, Jasper, Pulaski, Fulton, Wabash, Huntington, Wells, and Adams; and

All of Ohio currently in Great Lakes Presbytery north and east of and including the counties of Mercer, Shelby,

<a id="ga29-p168"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=170 printed_page=168 -->

Logan, Union, Madison, Pickaway, Hocking, Vinton, and Meigs.

Establishing the boundary of the new Ohio Valley Presbytery to include:

All of Indiana that is currently in the Great Lakes Presbytery and is south and west of but not including the counties of Porter, Jasper, Pulaski, Fulton, Wabash, Huntington, Wells, and Adams; and

All of Ohio south and west of but not including the counties of Mercer, Shelby, Logan, Union, Madison, Pickaway, Hocking, Vinton and Meigs.

All of the counties of Kentucky currently in Great Lakes Presbytery.

This action is to be effective July 1, 2001.

Adopted unanimously at the Stated Meeting of the Great Lakes Presbytery held on October 13, 2000

15. That the General Assembly pause now for a 15 minute season of prayer for the advancement of the Gospel in North America – praying for the following “renewed commitments”:

- A renewed realization that the church is built by our Lord;
- A renewed commitment to prayer;
- A renewed commitment to unity in the church;
- A renewed commitment to reaching the many cultures, ethnic/racial groups and socio-economic groups in North America;
- A renewed commitment to mercy ministries – preaching the Gospel in Word and showing the mercies of Christ in deed;
- A renewed commitment to the kind of stewardship of the Lord’s resources that will give greater priority to the advancement of the Gospel;
- A greater commitment to functional teamwork. Adopted

16. That the General Assembly call upon our presbyteries to set aside a significant portion of their meetings to pray for the advancement of Gospel through the

<a id="ga29-p169"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=171 printed_page=169 -->

revitalization of our existing churches and through the planting of new churches – throughout North America and throughout the world.
Adopted

17. That the General Assembly encourage each congregation and presbytery of the General Assembly to seek with a greater zeal the Lord’s calling for them in the advancement of the Gospel. I Corinthians 12:7 and Ephesians 4:7 promise that the Spirit of God has given each a gift for the common good and the building of the church. Just as there is a calling to every local church in reaching the local community and the world with the Gospel, so there is a calling for every congregation in church planting – reaching our “Judea and Samaria,” as well as our “Jerusalem” and the “uttermost parts.”
Adopted

18. That Overture 26 from the New York State Presbytery be answered in the affirmative (see also Communication #6).
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 26 From New York State Presbytery (to MNA)

“Redraw New York State and Ascension Presbytery Boundaries”

Whereas, the current western boundary of the New York State Presbytery includes central New York east and including Wayne, Ontario and Steuben counties; and,

Whereas, the current eastern boundary of the Ascension Presbytery includes western New York west and including Monroe, Livingston and Allegany counties; and

Whereas, the city of Rochester located in Monroe County and within the boundary of Ascension Presbytery has no PCA church; and

Whereas, there are several families who have expressed interest in the past several years in forming a core group for the purpose of starting a PCA church in the Rochester area; and

Whereas, there are some families who travel from the Rochester area to New Life Presbyterian Church in Ithaca, NY, a mission church in the New York State Presbytery, for worship; and

Whereas, these same families have indicated a desire to affiliate with the New York State Presbytery; and

Whereas, the New York State Presbytery would like to initiate and oversee this developing PCA work in the Rochester area as part of its vision to plant healthy and reproducing PCA churches in every major city in upstate New York;

Therefore, Be It Resolved that the New York State Presbytery hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to redraw the western boundary of New York State Presbytery and the northeastern New York boundary of Ascension Presbytery, to wit, that the counties east of and including Monroe and Livingston be transferred to New York State Presbytery, and furthermore, that the counties east of and including Steuben remain with the New York State Presbytery, to be effective July 1, 2001.

<a id="ga29-p170"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=172 printed_page=170 -->

Adopted by New York State Presbytery on January 20, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ James Stewart, Stated Clerk

COMMUNICATION 6 From Ascension Presbytery (to MNA)
“Ascension Presbytery Concurs with New York State Presbytery’s Request”

Dear Dr. Taylor,

This letter is to inform you that the Presbytery of the Ascension, at its Stated Meeting on April 27-28, 2001, voted to concur with Overture 26 from New York State Presbytery; thus agreeing that the New York counties of Monroe and Livingston should be transferred from the Presbytery of the Ascension to the New York State Presbytery.

Sincerely in Christ,
/s/ Frederick R. Neikirk, Stated Clerk

19. That Overture 29 From TE Andrew Lee of Northern California Presbytery be answered in the negative on the following grounds:

1. Regarding Items 1-4, the overture presents no reasons for requesting this review. There is no self-evident rationale for requiring this review. There are no allegations of impropriety or misuse of funds. Therefore, even the overture itself presents no reasons to proceed with an action that would require a significant expenditure of staff time.

2. Item 4 calls for an adjustment of 1990 - 1997 church planting budgets, based on a standard put in place in 1997. It is not common practice to apply a new standard to previous actions in matters such as this, and there is no rationale offered for doing so in this case.

3. This overture and its requested actions were not supported by Northern California Presbytery, the court to which they were originally presented.

4. Effective January 1, 2000, MNA discontinued the practice of assessing fees on project funds. Since that date, it has been the practice to apply 100% of every designated dollar to the project for which it is designated.

5. Regarding membership in ECFA, the MNA Committee believes that current accountability structures are sufficient to sustain the credibility of the ministry. Its annual audit by an independent accounting firm is reviewed by the MNA Committee and the General Assembly.

All aspects of MNA's work are subject to the review of the General Assembly annually. MNA believes that any value gained from ECFA accreditation is more than offset by the time and expense required to maintain such accreditation.

Adopted

<a id="ga29-p171"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=173 printed_page=171 -->

##### OVERTURE 29 From TE Andrew Lee of Northern California Presbytery

“Direct MNA to Report Administrative Fees”

Whereas, in October 1997 the GA MNA Committee approved a policy: (a) to limit the maximum dollar amount (or percentage), of "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [project overhead/administrative fees] taken from funds raised for church planting projects; and (b) to disclose to select donors the existence and amounts of such costs as a percentage of donations; and

Whereas, prior to October 1997, i.e., without aforementioned Committee-approved policy, GA MNA Staff prepared and administered church planting project budgets that included "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [project overhead/administrative fees]; and

Whereas, GA MNA heretofore has not been a member of the Evangelical Council for Financial Accountability (ECFA; www.ecfa.org) as has another PCA Committee (Mission to the World) and PCA Agency (Covenant Theological Seminary) - organizations that are also involved in fundraising (Romans 12: 17b). (It should be noted that while GA MNA's financial records are audited annually by an independent firm, and all records are reviewed by normal General Assembly processes, ECFA membership is helpful both in terms of accountability and in increasing the confidence of donors); and

Whereas, GA MNA as a Committee of the PCA is accountable to the General Assembly;

Therefore, Be It Resolved that the General Assembly have GA MNA report to the General Assembly on the following:

1. Written policy and limits that GA MNA Staff used to determine "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [and/or similar overhead/administrative fees] in preparing and administering church planting project funds in the absence of the October 1997 Committee-approved policy;

2. Written policy and practices GA MNA Staff followed to disclose "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [and/or similar overhead/ administrative fees] to donors and church planters in the absence of the October 1997 Committee-approved policy;

3. "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [and/or similar overhead/administrative fees] in: (a) dollar amounts; (b) as a percentage of; donations (cf. guidelines in the October 1997 Committee policy); (c) timing as to when such fees were taken from church plant funds with respect to project's budget plan; and (d) the implicit cost of capital that "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [and/or similar overhead/administrative fees] imputed to each project when netted against funding covenants. Information for items 3(a) through 3(d) to be provided for all GA MNA project budgets not covered by the October 1997

<a id="ga29-p172"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=174 printed_page=172 -->

Committee policy beginning from January 1990 through implementation of said Committee policy;

4. Recommended actions, if any, that should be taken for all projects in item #3 above if it is determined that "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [and/or similar overhead/administrative fees] were (a) lower relative to the guidelines set by the Committee's October 1997 policy; or (b) higher relative to the guidelines set by the Committee's October 1997 policy; and

5. GA MNA's current position regarding its membership in the ECFA.

NOTE: This overture was presented to, and rejected by, Northern California Presbytery at the Spring 2000 Stated Meeting. Overture requested by TE Andrew Lee. Rejection recorded in meeting's minutes approved by Presbytery at the Fall 2000 Stated Meeting.

Attested by: /s/ TE Andrew Lee

20. That Overture 32 from the Mid-America Presbytery be answered in the affirmative (see also Communication 1, page 173). Adopted

##### OVERTURE 32 From Mid-America Presbytery (to MNA)

"J &amp; R of Mid-America Presbytery into North Texas Presbytery"

Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery, at times in its history having boundaries encompassing all of Oklahoma and parts of Kansas, Missouri, and Arkansas, and as many as ten churches, now, by the departure of churches and territory on several occasions to other expanding presbyteries, has been reduced to only part of the state of Oklahoma and to only four remaining churches with a combined communing membership of between four and five hundred, and

Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery has the fewest churches of any of the contiguous PCA presbyteries, and

Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery does not believe it currently has the resources of churches, presbyters, funds and morale most effectively to staff appropriately presbytery committees and commissions and move toward further growth, and

Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery desires to gain encouragement towards future growth by being a part of a presbytery experiencing growth by church planting and the establishment of campus ministries, and

Whereas, Oklahoma City and a corridor of counties connecting it to Texas are already part of North Texas Presbytery where currently, with the help of the Southwest Church Planting Network, two new Oklahoma City area church plants and a Reformed University Fellowship at the University of Oklahoma are being established, and

<a id="ga29-p175"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=175 printed_page=null -->

Whereas, being received into North Texas Presbytery would immediately provide our churches with the option of participating in the three-presbytery, Southwest Church Planting Network (North Texas, South Texas and Southwest); and

Whereas, our four remaining churches joining with North Texas Presbytery seems the best pathway for an eventual reestablishing of a larger Oklahoma area presbytery as soon as enough particular churches are formed,

THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, that Mid-America Presbytery – pending the consent of North Texas Presbytery to receive us – petitions the 29th General Assembly meeting in Dallas, Texas, in June, 2001, to be allowed to be joined and received, with our four particular churches, their officers and members of the presbytery, and all remaining Oklahoma counties into North Texas Presbytery, effective July 1, 2001, this action effectively dissolving Mid-America Presbytery.

Adopted at the 40th Stated Meeting of Mid-America Presbytery, on April 20, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ John Owen Butler, Stated Clerk

COMMUNICATION 1 from North Texas Presbytery
May 3, 2001

L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk

RE: Mid-America Presbytery Overture to 29th GA to Join NTP

Fathers and Brothers:

At the 66th stated meeting of the North Texas Presbytery held April 27-28, 2001 at Park Cities Presbyterian Church, the Presbytery passed the following motion:

“M&amp;P that the North Texas Presbytery inform the 29th General Assembly that it concurs with the overture of Mid-America Presbytery to dissolve and to join the NTP.”

This effectively places all Indian Territory within the boundary of the NTP.

Yours in Christ,
/s/ T. John Mulkey, Stated Clerk
North Texas Presbytery

##### OVERTURE 34 From Westminster Presbytery

*(to MNA)*
“Revise Boundaries of Westminster Presbytery”

Whereas, the counties of Pulaski and Carroll lie geographically in the New River Valley and at the extreme northeastern end of Westminster Presbytery, closer to all the churches in New River than to those in the entire southern cluster in Westminster; and

Whereas, in the formative studies of potential boundaries for Westminster and New River Presbyteries, significant consideration was given to the possibility of our two Counties being assigned to New River; and

Whereas, the geographical and cultural issues involving our two churches and those in New River Presbytery have led to very positive ministry and outreach sharing, particularly between the Virginia congregations of that Presbytery and ourselves; and

Whereas, New River Presbytery has indicated a desire to receive those two Counties with a notation that adding our two Counties would strengthen their presbytery without unduly weakening a stronger Westminster; and

Whereas, New River Presbytery has formed an ad-hoc committee to explore with some of the churches in James River Presbytery, and our two churches, the possibility of asking the General Assembly in the future to form a new Presbytery in the west-central area of Virginia;

Now Therefore, be it resolved that Westminster Presbytery herewith overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to redraw the boundaries of Westminster and New River Presbyteries by transferring Pulaski County and that portion of Carroll County that lies east of Interstate 77 in the Commonwealth of Virginia, and the ministers and churches therein, from Westminster to New River Presbytery, effective September 30, 2001; and

Be it further resolved that the Walthall-Wood Scholarship Fund assets be transferred to the New River Presbytery with the approval of this change of boundary since they are closely tied to the Draper’s Valley Presbyterian Church of Draper, Virginia.

Adopted by Westminster Presbytery on April 21, 2001.

Attested by: /s/ D. Steven Meyerhoff, Stated Clerk

NOTE: New River Presbytery, acting in its 81st Stated Meeting on March 20, 2001, took the following action in regards to a proposed Overture of Westminster Presbytery requesting boundary changes:

It was M/S/C to concur with that portion of a proposed overture by Westminster Presbytery dealing with the transfer of Pulaski and Carroll Counties in Virginia and/or the churches and Teaching Elders residing therein to New River Presbytery.

Attested by: /s/ Don Clements, Stated Clerk

<a id="ga29-p175"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=177 printed_page=175 -->

22. That Overture 36 from the Central Carolina Presbytery be answered by noting that the North Hills Church rescinded its Overture, thus rendering this recommendation moot. No vote needed. *Adopted as amended*

##### OVERTURE 36 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to MNA)

“North Hills PCA in Salisbury, NC, Seeking Admission into Newly-formed Triad Presbytery”

Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church exists for the Glory of God and the furtherance of His Kingdom on Earth;

Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church was admitted to the Central Carolina Presbytery in October of 1998 and has been blessed by its association with Central Carolina Presbytery and the Presbyterian Church in America;

Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church is located in Rowan County, a county that adjoins the proposed boundaries of the Triad Presbytery;

Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church lies in the extreme north of Rowan County next to the proposed boundary of the Triad Presbytery;

Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church has a strong affinity to the Triad geographical region as well as to the churches in the new presbytery;

Whereas, this proximity and affinity has already resulted in filial and ministry ties to the churches in the new presbytery;

Whereas, official connection with the Triad Presbytery would strengthen North Hills Presbyterian Church, further its mission, and encourage its members to love and good deeds;

Now Therefore North Hills Presbyterian Church hereby petitions and overtures the Central Carolina Presbytery and the General Assembly that it be allowed to join the newly-formed Triad Presbytery outside its geographical bounds effective July 1, 2001.

Adopted by the Moderator of Session, North Hills Presbyterian Church and the Clerk of Session, North Hills Presbyterian Church
/s/ Jerry C. Currin, Moderator and /s/ M. J. Smith, III, Clerk of Session

5. The current Stated Clerk of Westminster Presbytery, TE Steve Meyerhoff, shall convene the January 2002 meeting of the New Presbytery, and preside until the election of a Moderator.

The Committee adopted the following as a substitute motion:

Proposed Response of the 29th General Assembly to
Communication 5 and Overture 38
From Westminster Presbytery

Dear Fathers and Brothers of Westminster Presbytery,

Greetings in the Name of the Lord. We lament the occasion of this communication, but testify to our trust that the Lord of the Church is working in all things, even these unhappy circumstances, for His glory and the good of our branch of His Church.

With respect to your letter May 16, 2001 (Communication 5), this Assembly raises no question concerning your ecclesiastical and civil right as a presbytery to withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America. However, this Assembly would be profoundly remiss in its duty to "recommend measures for the promotion of charity, truth and holiness through all the churches under its care" (BCO 14-6.k.) were it to fail to challenge Westminster Presbytery's judgment in this action. We urge you to consider again your action, and reverse your judgment.

As we understand your communication, you have, by majority vote, determined to withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America. At the same time, you ask this Assembly to make provision for erecting a new presbytery composed of the Teaching Elders and Churches that do not wish to leave the PCA. Such a request might be above reproach, if the Teaching Elders and Churches in question were just those that voted against the action of presbytery to withdraw, and who were convinced in conscience that submission to the Presbytery in this matter was sin. However, we are reliably informed that in fact a portion of those voting with the majority in favor of withdrawing from the PCA, did so with the undisguised intention of joining the new PCA presbytery. We are further informed that another portion of those voting with the majority, actually intending to depart as a presbytery, and thus claim the name and traditions of Westminster Presbytery, knew of the true intention of the aforementioned portion, and acquiesced in the matter. This circumstance, fathers and brothers, raises serious questions about the integrity of this vote, questions which, as your brothers in the Lord, we press upon you with the utmost urgency.

First, with respect to the portion of the majority voting to withdraw, who did so with no intention of in fact withdrawing—please consider the following. You have apparently voted on a proposal of great moment, a proposal crying out for a principled consideration, out of mere expediency. Surely this is unworthy of the solemn privilege and responsibility of voting.

<a id="ga29-p178"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=180 printed_page=178 -->

Further, you apparently voted knowing that the resultant continuing Westminster Presbytery, composed of those Teaching Elders and Churches that actually do intend to withdraw from the PCA, will proclaim to the world that she, unlike the proposed new PCA presbytery, is the true, faithful presbytery, sincerely committed to the Westminster standards. Your affirmative vote makes possible this circumstance, while at the same time you cannot possibly in conscience support this allegation—for in your intention to be restored to the PCA, you profess yourselves desirous of being members of a body sincerely committed to those same Standards.

Finally, you are duty bound, by your vow to submit to the government of the church, to submit to *this* decision of Westminster Presbytery, and withdraw from the PCA. The only justification you could have for repudiating the authority of Westminster Presbytery, would be if you in good conscience could not comply; that is to say, if it would be a violation of your loyalty to Christ for you to submit. But how can you credibly claim that it violates your conscience to do what you in fact voted in favor of Westminster Presbytery doing? Surely there are serious questions of integrity raised in such behavior.

Second, with respect to the portion of the majority voting to withdraw, who did so with the intention to withdraw, and in so doing claim to be acting, not as independents, but as Presbyterians, and continuing Westminster Presbytery as she was originally constituted, to be an Old School Presbytery committed to the Westminster Standards—please consider the following. You have allied your cause with brothers whose actions are at odds with their true intentions, and at odds with your purpose, and in so doing you have made your cause dependent upon behavior of questionable integrity. Surely duty in this case required you to plead with such brethren, out of your responsibilities to them as set forth in the exposition of Scripture found in Larger Catechism Questions 140-145, *not* to vote in favor of the action to withdraw, unless they genuinely believed that the PCA had abandoned the biblical fidelity you suppose to be preserved at Westminster Presbytery, and unless they genuinely intended to submit to the Presbytery’s action to withdraw upon its passage.

Further, surely this vote is but a Pyrrhic victory for your cause: for although Westminster Presbytery has *formally* withdrawn from the PCA, all parties know full well that *materially* the action was not of the Westminster Presbytery you intend in the outcome. This action cannot, with any integrity, be said to sustain the claims you apparently intend to set before the world concerning the continuing Westminster Presbytery. *That* Presbytery, simply did not have the votes to withdraw, and you, and everyone else, will know it.

Finally, this unhappy scheme circumvents what we suppose otherwise to have been your duty and right, not as independents, but as Presbyterians, however costly it may have been. We refer to principles from the earliest days of American Presbyterian history, set forth in the union of the Synods of New York and Philadelphia, and embraced particularly by the Old School Assembly in the controversies of a latter age. Consider their counsel:

when any Matter is determined by a Major Vote, every Mem[ber] Shall either actively concur with, or passively Submit to Such Deter[min]ation; or, if his

<a id="ga29-p179"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=181 printed_page=179 -->

Conscience permit him to do neither, he Shall, [after] Sufficient Liberty modestly to reason and remonstrate, peaceab[ly with]draw from our Communion, without attempting to make any Sc[hism:] provided always, that this Shall be understood to extend only to [Such] Determinations, as the Body Shall Judge indispensable in Doct[rine] or Presbyterian Gover[n]ment. (Plan of Union, Synods of New York and Philadelphia. Minutes of the Synod of New York and Philadelphia, 1758, p. 3. [Klett, ed., 1976, p. 341].)

Charles Hodge later commended this statement as laying a foundation for unquestioned principles of the Presbyterian church:

Notwithstanding, therefore, the ardent debates and mutual criminations on this subject, it appears the two parties were of one mind. They were agreed in disclaiming all legislative power in religious matters. They were agreed in the right of Synods to set down rules for the government of the church. They were agreed in the binding authority of these rules even over dissentients, except when such dissentients believed them to be sinful. They were agreed that when a member could not obey a given rule with a good conscience, it was his duty peaceably to withdraw. Finally, they were agreed that when a Synod saw that the minority were opposed to any measure, not in judgment only, but in conscience, they ought not to insist upon it, and thus necessitate a schism, unless they believed the measure to be essential to the well-being of the churches. These principles are all so plain and so reasonable, that we need not wonder they commanded the unanimous consent of both parties, or that they have remained the unquestioned principles of our church from that day to this. (The Constitutional History of the Presbyterian Church in the United States of America, part II, 1741-1788 (Philadelphia: Presbyterian Board of Publication, 1851), pp. 206-207.)

According to historic Old School Presbyterian polity it is clear that anyone who cannot in good conscience submit to the brethren in a matter, has the burden of withdrawing from the fellowship. He may well claim, that it is not he who has departed, but the majority; and he has every right to try and sustain that claim. Nonetheless, in our polity the burden to withdraw belongs to the dissident. Of course our best hope is that those disagreeing can find a way to maintain the bonds of fellowship, even in the midst of serious disputes. We are reminded of the wholesome words of Robert L. Dabney, describing the pattern of Christ-honoring thought proper to a faithful minority, and commend their careful consideration to our brothers:

We believe these measures wrong, even unconstitutional; we testify against their wrong, but we leave the responsibility of them to the majority who enacted them, and whose will must prevail in all republican bodies. Their wrongfulness does not compel us to separate from this, which we believe to be the true church of Jesus Christ, though in this matter erring. We exercise our Christian Liberty in testifying against her fault, but we go on as before, to labor for her good; for

<a id="ga29-p180"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=182 printed_page=180 -->

though in fault, it is Christ’s bride. (“Our Position.” The Central Presbyterian
2:28 (July 11, 1857): 110; reprint, Discussions by Robert L. Dabney, D.D.,
LL.D., edited by C.R. Vaughan (Richmond: presbyterian committee of
Publication, 1890); reprint, Harrisonburg, VA: Sprinkle Publications, 1982),
11:176-183.)

Fathers and brothers, in light of these considerations, we ask again, for the sake
of Christ’s glory and the good of his Church, reconsider your action to withdraw, and
reverse your judgment.

Finally, this Assembly answers Overture 38 in the negative, on the grounds that:

1) this Assembly cannot countenance the action which gave rise to the request,
without expressing our deepest concern for the integrity of the presbytery in
so acting, and sustaining a hope that our counsels may well be heeded by our
brothers;
2) as a complaint against Presbytery’s action to withdraw has not yet been heard,
this Assembly should not act to erect a new Presbytery until all judicial
remedies of dissenting members of presbytery have been exhausted;
3) that the overture improperly an dim prudently contemplates an action of the
General Assembly of the PCA as being qualified by the actions of an
anticipated ecclesiastical body with which the Assembly will have no
relations; and
4) that at this time there a reno churches or teaching elders qualified to make a
request for admission to the PCA as a presbytery.

Should Westminster Presbytery, contrary to this A ssem bly’s brotherly advice
and sincere hope, persevere in the course set out in its action to withdraw from the
Presbyterian Church in America, it is the judgment of this assembly that at the
effective date of the withdrawal, all the congregations and teaching elders of
westminster presbytery will no longer be members of the Presbyterian Church in America. Should Teaching Elders or congregations find that they are unable in good
conscience to continue as members of westminster presbytery as seceded, and by
appropriate act renounce its jurisdiction, they may seek admission to the Presbyterian Church in America.

Finally, we appeal to westminster Presbytery, if Presbytery remains determined to
continue in their announced course, that, for the sake of those who may find their
insurance coverage threatened by Presbytery’s withdrawal, to meet and change the
effective date of the action to withdraw, until the conclusion of the 30th General Assembly, so that those who desire to remain members of the PCA may immediately be
admitted as a presbytery of the PCA.
adopted as amended

<a id="ga29-p181"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=183 printed_page=181 -->

presbyteries per their request, hereby established a new presbytery, in

Westminster Presbytery by the January 2002 stated meeting of said presbytery

certified to the stated clerk of westminster presbytery by the january 2002
stated meeting of said presbytery that they wish to remain in westminster
