communication 5 From Westminster Presbytery (to MNA)
“Erect New' Presbytery”

Dear Dr. Taylor, May 16, 2001
a ta called meeting of westminster presbytery on may 15, 2001, the
Presbytery took action to withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America,
effective at its January Stated Meeting, and to overture the PCA General Assembly to recognize this fact and constitute a new Presbytery, with the same
geographic boundaries, out of the teaching elders and churches that do not
desire to leave the PCA. a copy of the Presbytery’s action is enclosed with this
letter.

<a id="ga29-p182"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=184 printed_page=182 -->

Please consider the enclosed to be both a Communication and an Overture to the 29th General Assembly, informing and requesting action, on this matter.

Also enclosed is a copy of the CCB advise to Westminster Presbytery, which we sought to follow in our action, and a copy of action taken by Westminster Presbytery and the General Assembly in 1974, reserving to Westminster Presbytery the right to withdraw from the PCA. We are exercising our right to withdraw in this action, but we are also seeking to be considerate of the current BCO and practices within the PCA.

Please convey this material to the proper channels for action by the Assembly.

Sincerely, in Christ,
/s/ D. Steven Meyerhoff, Stated Clerk

## Action to Withdraw and Realign Westminster Presbytery
May 15, 2001

1. That, in light of:

- **a.** the declared intention of some churches and teaching elders in Westminster Presbytery to seek to continue Westminster Presbytery as she was originally constituted, and
- **b.** the advice given from the GA CCB, “Should a group of churches choose to leave the PCA in order to form a new affiliation, or to continue an affiliation that they perceive as antecedent to the PCA, then they can peaceably withdraw as a group subject to the consent of each congregation in the group...” and,
- **c.** the minutes of the Second GA where the GA of the PCA acknowledged that Westminster Presbytery reserves the right to withdraw as a Presbytery from the denomination at any time, Westminster Presbytery votes to withdraw from the PCA as of its January 2002 Stated Meeting.

2. To safeguard the integrity of the congregation’s right of self-determination, all Sessions in Westminster Presbytery are hereby asked to consider calling a congregational meeting sometime between May 15 and the January 2002 Stated Meeting of Presbytery for the purpose of voting on whether to leave the PCA and stay within Westminster Presbytery as she leaves the PCA. This call for a congregational meeting is to safeguard the provisions of BCO 25-11 and in accordance with the advice of the GA CCB. If they vote to leave the PCA they will declare either their intent to join in the continuance of Westminster Presbytery or go to some other denomination. This action is a plea for a peaceful solution to our gridlock. This information is to be distributed to the churches of Presbytery by a letter from Presbytery’s Stated Clerk, including a copy of this proposal, calling Sessions to examine these issues, consider calling a congregational meeting to vote on whether to

<a id="ga29-p183"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=185 printed_page=183 -->

leave the PCA with Westminster Presbytery and then to respond in writing to Presbytery by the January 2002 Stated Meeting of Presbytery. It is understood by all parties that only the congregations voting to leave the PCA and to stay with Westminster Presbytery shall have ever left the PCA. Those congregations taking no action will be understood to still be in the PCA, in a Presbytery yet to be named, with the same geographical boundaries as the present Westminster Presbytery. Any Teaching Elder(s) on the roll of Westminster Presbytery desiring to withdraw will inform the Presbytery by the January 2002 Stated Meeting.

3. Westminster Presbytery hereby notifies the General Assembly of its intention to withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America, effective at the January 2002 Stated Meeting of Presbytery; and the General Assembly be overtured to take the following action:

That the General Assembly, under the powers granted to it under the PCA Constitution to erect new presbyteries and to unite and divide presbyteries per their request, hereby established a new presbytery, in accordance with the following criteria:

Those churches and ministers which have certified to the Stated Clerk of Westminster Presbytery by the January 2002 Stated Meeting of said Presbytery that they wish to remain in Westminster Presbytery, shall be constituted as Westminster Presbytery; while those churches and ministers which have not certified to the Stated Clerk of Westminster Presbytery by the January 2002 Stated Meeting of said Presbytery that they wish to remain in Westminster Presbytery, shall be constituted as a new Presbytery, effective that date, with the same boundaries as the present Westminster Presbytery.

4. That Westminster Presbytery approve the disbursal of assets between the two Presbyteries, (to be named) and Westminster, on the basis of the percentage of churches in each Presbytery as of the January 2002 Stated Meeting, the exception being the MNA funds (which are designated for the RUF/ETSU work) which shall remain in (to be named) Presbytery. This is in accordance with the advice of the GA CCB, “...There are no standards regarding continuing the name of a departing group and such matters should be left to the discretion and charity of the brothers involved. Additionally, any division of assets or attachment of corporate identity, where applicable, will need to be determined by the brothers involved.”

5. That Westminster Presbytery shall continue to have the documents and minutes of her court and that the Stated Clerk, Dr. Steven Meyerhoff, shall make copies of these minutes and all her official documents for (to be named) Presbytery and shall deliver the originals, by December 31, 2001, to Mr. Brent Bradley, or a designee of his choice, as the Stated Clerk Pro Tem of Westminster Presbytery. This is in accordance with the advice of the GA CCB. “...Additionally, any division of assets or

<a id="ga29-p184"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=186 printed_page=184 -->

attachment of the corporate identity, where applicable, will need to be
determined by the brothers involved.”
6. That Westminster Presbytery ask that her Sessions discuss the
controversies that raged in our Presbytery and the GA. the sessions of
each congregations hall either commit themselves as elders and a session
to:
a. their present covenant obligation as part of the PCA which
commits churches, Sessions and elders to either support whole
heartedly the official positions, institutions and programs of the
PCA, or do all within their power to change the official positions,
institutions and programs of the PCA that they cannot whole
heartedly support, or
b. call for a congregational meeting to vote to leave the PCA and
continue to covenant together with the other congregations to
continue together as Westminster Presbytery as she was
originally constituted, to be an Old School Presbytery committed
to the westminster standards in the plain sense of the meaning of
the words, or to leave to join some other evangelical
denomination.
7. the following intention is set forth as the intent of some elder swishing
to leave the PCA and continue Westminster Presbytery. This intention
cannot bind the Presbytery (for she alone can make her own self-
determination) and will only become the rule when approved by
Westminster Presbytery, Unaffiliated. but it is the intention of these
elders to propose to Westminster Presbytery, Unaffiliated, to continue
Westminster in the following terms: Westminster Presbytery will
declare herself at the January 2002 Stated Meeting no longer affiliated
with the PCA, that she will continue the position stated in W estm inster's
original founding document to be an Old School Presbytery. She will
remain unaffiliated with any other denomination for at least three years.

by the action of Presbytery, May 15, 2001,
/s/ D. Steven Meyerhoff, Stated Clerk

constitutional inquiry from westminster

it is the advice of the CCB in reference to the inquiry of westminster presbytery
related to withdrawal from the PCA as a presbytery that:

There is no provision within our Constitution for a
presbytery as a whole to withdraw. the right of an individual
church to affiliate with whom ever it will, however, cannot be
impinged upon by a higher court (BCO 25-11; cf.11-3, 4). The
principle of the BCO that is most closely related is that of chapter
25-11. which allows, “A particular church may withdraw from any

<a id="ga29-p185"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=187 printed_page=185 -->

court of this body at any time for reasons, which seem to it sufficient.” Should a group of churches choose to leave the PCA in order to form a new affiliation, or to continue an affiliation that they perceive as antecedent to the PCA, then they can peaceably withdraw as a group subject to the consent of each congregation in the group. In the event of a group of churches withdrawing from the PCA with the consent of each church involved, those congregations remaining within the bounds of that presbytery that decline to withdraw with the departing group would stay within their present denomination. Those congregations not departing would constitute the continuing presbytery of the PCA. There are no standards regarding continuing the name of a departing group and such matters should be left to the discretion and charity of the brothers involved. Additionally, any division of assets or attachment of corporate identity, where applicable, will need to be determined by the brothers involved.

Dr. L. Roy Taylor
Stated Clerk
April 25, 2001

“Be it further Resolved that pursuant to the above Resolution, the Presbytery of Westminster does hereby request formal recognition of it by the Second General Assembly of the National Presbyterian Church as a member Presbytery of the National Presbyterian Church (A Corporation), with all the rights and obligations belonging to member presbyteries as set forth in the Book of Church Order of the National Presbyterian Church. The Presbytery of Westminster reserves to itself the right to withdraw from said Assembly at any time under conditions and in accordance with such rules as may be prescribed by the Presbytery. The Presbytery reserves to itself the right of ownership of all property of every sort, nature or description now owned or hereafter acquired. This is not to be construed as a claim by the Presbytery upon the properties of its constituent congregations, since the properties of congregations under the National Presbyterian Church may not be claimed by the higher courts, except as voluntarily given by the congregation to the said higher court.

“Done on this 20th day of July, 1974, in Stated Meeting of the Presbytery being met at Camp Evangel, Claypool, Virginia.” (See M2GA, p. 124-125)

Adopted by Westminster Presbytery on May 15, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ D. Steven Meyerhoff, Stated Clerk

## 29-41 Committee of Commissioners on MNA (continued)

The Assembly continued discussion of Recommendation 24 and 25 (see text at 29-38 above). The Moderator ruled that the main motion will be considered the adoption of Attachment A (which is the Committee of Commissioners recommendation), and the substitute motion will be considered to be Recommendations 24 and 25. Time was extended for 15 minutes, then 10 more minutes. The question was called on all matters before the floor. The substitute motion was **defeated** 411-484. The main motion was **adopted**.

The report was closed with prayer by TE Whited, Chairman. Further prayer was offered for the churches and ministers in Westminster Presbytery. The Assembly paused to sing Psalm 121 from the Psalter.

The Moderator reassumed the chair.

## 29-42 Reconsideration of Mission to the World Report

A motion to reconsider Committee of Commissioners on MTW Recommendation 3 was **adopted**. Recommendation 3 was moved with the date changed from Nov 11 to Nov 18, the motion was **adopted as amended**, [See 29-30, p. 151 for text of the report.]

## 29-43 RAO Amendment

Recommendation 6 was **amended** to add after “the Assembly,” “the Moderator with”, and the amended recommendation was **adopted** according to RAO 18. [See text of Committee of Commissioners on Administrative Committee report, 29-52, p. 250.]

## 29-44 Committee of Commissioners on Bills and Overtures

RE E. J. Nusbaum, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report.

### I. Business Referred to the Committee

Overtures 1,2,3,4,7,8,9,10,11,13,14,15,16,17,19,20,21,23,24,25,27,28,30,31, 33,35

Communication 4

Personal Resolutions 1 and 3

### II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed

See listing above.

## III. RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. That Overture 1 from Missouri Presbytery (“Amend BCO 14-1(12), Increase Number on Committees, Extend Terms to Five Years”) be answered in the affirmative as amended.

Grounds: Amendments have been made to accommodate the possible addition of the Reformed University Ministries (RUM) committee and propose a plan of implementation.

Adopted

<a id="ga29-p188"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=190 printed_page=188 -->

##### OVERTURE 1 from Missouri Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 14-1(12), Increase Number on Committees, Extend Terms to Five Years”

Whereas, the founders of our denomination, in order to minimize the concentration of power in the hierarchy of our denomination, created the structure of our General Assembly Committees and Agencies so that Committee members would be elected, in classes, for 4-year terms and are only eligible for re-election after a year has elapsed,

Whereas, the responsibility and complexity of our Program Committees have grown as our denomination has grown,

Whereas, it is very difficult for newly elected Committee members to function effectively until their 4-year term is almost half over,

Whereas, this frequent turnover of Committee members coupled with the increased size and complexity of Committee operations tends to place more power in the hands of the permanent staff which is opposed to the intent of the founders of our denomination,

Whereas, the proposed change provides for greater continuity in our Program Committees while still providing for the regular rotation of Committee members,

Therefore Be it Resolved that BCO paragraph 14-1(12) in the second to the last paragraph be revised as follows:

“Committee on Christian Education and Publications, Committee on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed University Ministries shall consist of fifteen (15) men divided into five classes of three men each, with two men being TEs and one RE or two men being REs and one TE on alternate years on odd years and four men each on even years, elected to serve five-year terms. Committees on Christian Education and Publications, Mission to North America, Mission to the World and Reformed University Ministries shall have one ruling elder and one teaching elder as alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the year.”

Be it further resolved, that the first class of three (3) men to serve a five year term shall begin in 2003, should the 29th General Assembly approve this Overture, the consent of two-thirds (2/3) of the Presbyteries is given and the 30th General Assembly approves the same; and that each year thereafter there shall be an additional class of three (3) men who shall serve a five year term.

Adopted by Missouri Presbytery on April 18, 2000.
Attested by: /s/ David R. Stain, Stated Clerk

2. That Overture 2 from New Jersey Presbytery (“Amend BCO 58-5 – Use Unleavened Bread in Communion”) be answered in the negative.

<a id="ga29-p189"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=191 printed_page=189 -->

Grounds: There are no Scriptural grounds sufficient to mandate the exclusive use of unleavened bread in Communion.

Adopted

##### OVERTURE 2 from New Jersey Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 58-5 – Use Unleavened Bread in Communion”

Whereas, the first paragraph of *The Directory for the Worship of God*, states that “BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the Presbyteries,” and

Whereas, Exodus 12:8, 15-20 reveals that the Passover celebration and accompanying Feast of Unleavened Bread used unleavened bread,

Whereas, Luke 22:1 reveals that the Lord Jesus, when He instituted the Lord’s Supper, was sharing a Passover meal with His disciples, and

Whereas, 1 Corinthians 5:7 specifically states that believers are “unleavened” because Christ “our Passover” has been sacrificed, and

Whereas, “leaven” is sometimes used in the Bible to denote sin, (cf. Matthew 16:6, 11-12), and

Whereas, Jesus Christ is sinless, (Hebrews 4:15),

Therefore, be it so ordered that the BCO 58-5 be revised by the insertion of the word “unleavened” before each occurrence of the word “bread” in the first, second, and fourth paragraphs and by adding these words at the end of the first paragraph: “If unleavened bread is unavailable, bread with leaven (yeast) may be used, but this is not to be the usual or normal practice of a congregation.”

The first four paragraphs of BCO 58-5 shall now read (added words are underlined):

“The table, on which the elements are placed, being decently covered, and furnished with *unleavened* bread and wine, and the communicants orderly and gravely sitting around it (or in their seats before it), the elders in a convenient place together, the minister should then set the elements apart by prayer and thanksgiving. *If unleavened bread is unavailable, bread with leaven (yeast) may be used, but this is not to be the usual or normal practice of a congregation.*

The *unleavened* bread and wine being thus set apart by prayer and thanksgiving, the minister is to take the *unleavened* bread, and break it, in the view of the people saying:

That the Lord Jesus Christ on the same night in which He was betrayed took bread; and when He had given thanks, He broke it, gave it to His disciples, as I, ministering in His name, give this bread to you, and said, “Take, eat; this is My body which is broken for you; do this in remembrance of Me.”

<a id="ga29-p190"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=192 printed_page=190 -->

Here the *unleavened* bread is to be distributed. After having given the *unleavened* bread, he shall take the cup, and say...

Adopted by the Presbytery of New Jersey on May 20, 2000.

Attested by: James A. Smith, Stated Clerk

3. That Overture 3 from Susquehanna Valley Presbytery (“Amend BCO 20-2 to Include Pastor in Search Committee for Assistant or Associate Pastor”) be answered in the negative.

Grounds: There are many ways for a church to involve a senior pastor in the search process. It is not wise to mandate a specific BCO procedure regarding senior pastor involvement in calling an assistant or an associate pastor.

Adopted

##### OVERTURE 3 from Susquehanna Valley Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 20-2 to Include Pastor in Search Committee for Assistant or Associate Pastor”

Whereas, the *Book of Church Order* does not address the matter of searching for an assistant or associate pastor, and

Whereas, for the peace and unity of the church it is vital that there be harmony among the pastoral staff of a church, and

Whereas, any new assistant or associate pastor must be agreeable to and compatible with the senior pastor,

Therefore be it resolved that the following be added to the BCO 20-2:

“When a search committee for an associate pastor is formed by a congregation, the senior pastor shall automatically be a member of the committee. Before proposing a candidate to the congregation, the committee shall assure itself that the candidate is acceptable to and has the full support of the senior pastor.

“In the case of a call to an assistant pastor, Session may act as the search committee or it may appoint church members as a search committee to bring a candidate for Session approval. In either case, the senior pastor shall be a member of the search committee.”

Adopted by the Susquehanna Valley Presbytery on September 16, 2000.

Attested by: D. F. Rundle, Stated Clerk

4. That Overture 4 be answered in the negative.

Adopted as amended

##### OVERTURE 4 from Eastern Carolina Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Change Procedure for Pastoral Relationship Dissolution, BCO 23-1”

<a id="ga29-p191"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=193 printed_page=191 -->

Be it resolved that the Book of Church Order (BCO) 23-1 be amended as follows:

**Existing provision**: BCO 23-1, first two paragraphs

“BCO 23-1. When any minister shall tender the resignation of his pastoral charge to Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite the church to appear by its commissioners, to show cause why the Presbytery should or should not accept the resignation. If the church fail to appear, or if its reasons for retaining its pastor be deemed insufficient, his resignation shall be accepted and the pastoral relation dissolved.

If any church desires to be relieved of its pastor, a similar procedure shall be observed. But whether the minister or the church initiates proceedings for a dissolution of the relation, there shall always be a meeting of the congregation called and conducted in the same manner as the call of the pastor. In any case, the minister must not physically leave the field until the Presbytery or its commission empowered to handle uncontested requests for dissolution has dissolved the relation.”

The **proposed revision** of the first two paragraphs only, follows:

“BCO 23-1. When any minister shall tender the resignation of his pastoral charge to Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite the church to appear by its commissioners, to show cause why the Presbytery should or should not accept the resignation. A meeting of the congregation shall be called and conducted in the same manner as the call of a pastor, and either accept the resignation or be prepared to show cause why the Presbytery should not accept it.

If any church desires to be relieved of its pastor, a meeting of the congregation shall be called to request the Presbytery to dissolve the pastoral relation, and the meeting shall be conducted in the same manner as the call of a pastor. If the pastor is agreeable to the dissolution, he shall not leave the field until the Presbytery or its commissioners empowered to handle the congregation’s request has dissolved the relation. If the minister shall contest the requested dissolution, both the church and its pastor shall be cited to appear before the Presbytery to present their reasons for dissolution and reasons for contesting the dissolution. Since the bare fact that a majority of the members of a particular church desires to be relieved of its pastor is not per se a sufficient ground for dissolution, the Presbytery shall first hear from the church, then from the pastor in order to determine whether the dissolution is consistent with the Word of God in regard to the causes heard and would therefore be in the interest of both parties. In no case shall a contested request for dissolution be heard by a commission of Presbytery.

**Supporting Reasons**:

1. This change in the BCO is needed to make clear that a movement for dissolution on the part of a pastor or a congregation must come in the form of a

<a id="ga29-p192"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=194 printed_page=192 -->

request to the Presbytery and, as such, it is to be carefully weighed in the light of all the circumstances. Dissolution of a pastoral bond is within the power of the Presbytery only and is not a “rubber stamp” action based solely on the will of either the congregation or its pastor.

Especially in regard to congregational requests for dissolution, there is a growing sentiment within the PCA that the will of the majority of a congregation should prevail regardless of the issues that led to the request. In other words, a congregation, like a democracy, is entitled to be relieved of its pastor simply if a majority wishes it to be so, whatever the circumstances. Such a view may be congregational, but it is not Presbyterian. The majority of a congregation may have faulty, erroneous or unbiblical grounds for wishing the removal of its pastor, and in such cases the Presbytery may deny their request in favor of a minority in the congregation who wish to continue under the ministry of a faithful pastor. A majority of Israel wished to be relieved of Moses and return to Egypt, but they were clearly in error. Had a majority returned to Egypt, Moses no doubt would have continued to pastor the faithful minority and lead them on to the land of promise. A majority of the elders, and presumably the people, wished to be relieved of Jeremiah’s ministry and challenged his authority. “Thou speakest falsely: the Lord our God hath not sent thee...”(Jer. 43:2), they said; but our sympathy and support would have been with Jeremiah and the remnant who were faithful to his ministry.

2. A contested request of the congregation for dissolution of the pastoral bond is too weighty a matter to be handled by a commission of Presbytery. The Presbytery alone can authorize the formation of a pastoral bond, and the Presbytery alone should dissolve that bond. BCO 23-1 in its present form does not expressly grant Presbyteries authority to act through commissions in such serious matters although the language has been interpreted broadly. On the other hand, the present wording, “The minister must not leave the field until the Presbytery or its commission empowered to handle uncontested requests for dissolution has dissolved the relation”, could be said to suggest that only uncontested requests are appropriately handled by a commission.

3. There is need in the PCA to set in place a clear rule of discernment and judgment especially in matters of contested dissolution requests. To say that the majority of the congregation is entitled to its wish without regard to the circumstances and without evaluating the cause of the majority in the light of Scripture, is neither wise nor safe. To “save” a church by yielding to a majority in rejecting a faithful pastor without legitimate grounds may be to sacrifice a faithful minority to an unfaithful majority. This is not to save a church in the true sense of the word, for the blessing of God may be withdrawn from a church in such circumstances. If the congregation rejects one appointed by Christ to serve, may it not be viewed by the Head of the Church as a rejection of Him? This principle is illustrated in the words of God to Samuel: “They have not rejected you, but they have rejected me” (I Sam. 8:7). When the prophets were

<a id="ga29-p193"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=195 printed_page=193 -->

refused, God was refused; and history often revealed the divine displeasure. “Touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm” (Psalm 105: 15). When Paul’s apostleship was not acknowledged, Christ was not acknowledged. Examples of churches left to themselves after rejecting a faithful undershepherd of Christ may be found in every age.

This is not to suggest that the Presbytery should never dissolve the pastoral bond between a faithful pastor and a congregation in cases of contested requests for dissolution. There may be circumstances in which a pastor not accused of any personal fault in life, doctrine or ministry should step aside. Such actions warrant Presbytery’s most careful deliberation in the light of Scripture and sound reason.

The changes proposed in this overture are offered in the interest of providing a mechanism for Presbyteries to carefully address these important and often difficult matters. May the King and Head of the Church be pleased to assist us in promoting His revealed will for the governance of His Church.

Adopted at the Fall Stated Meeting of Eastern Carolina Presbytery, on October 21, 2000. Attested by : /s/ Austin Leake, Stated Clerk

5. That Overtures 7 from Calvary Presbytery, #20 from New River Presbytery, and #23 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery [dealing with exceptions to the length of creation days] be answered in the negative.

Grounds: It is the prerogative of the lower courts to determine if a man’s view is an exception to the standards.

Adopted

## Minority Report (Overtures 7, 20, and 23)

We the undersigned respectfully disagree with the majority report of the Bills and Overtures Committee on the matter of Overtures 7, 20 and 23, and offer the following Minority Report as a substitute:

“Handling of views of Creation regarding our Confessional Standards”

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America is a confessional church based upon the Scriptures and certain doctrinal statements as interpreted in the Westminster Standards; and

Whereas, the Scriptures in Genesis 1, Exodus 20:11 and Exodus 31:17 teach that God created the heavens and the earth, the sea and all that is in them in six days; and

Whereas, “[b]y faith we understand that the universe was formed at God's command, so that what is seen was not made out of what was visible” (Hebrews 11:3, NIV); and

<a id="ga29-p194"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=196 printed_page=194 -->

Whereas, our confession of faith states that god created the universe “in the
space of six days” (W CF 4A; WLC 15, 120; WSC 9, 58 and 59); and

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly affirmed that “a diversity of views on
creation days...is acceptable” thus, in essence, invalidating any definitive
creedal statement; and

Whereas, many in the denomination interpret the decision of the 28th General Assembly as removing the right of the courts of original jurisdiction to
determine the acceptability of am an’s particular view, as long as his
view is one of those numerous addressed in the report of the creation
Study Committee; and

Whereas, the words “in the space of six days” are reasonably and normally
interpreted to means ix consecutive days of normal duration and any
alleged ambiguity to the words “in the space of six days” is clarified by
the growing historical and scholarly evidence that demonstrates the
intent of the westminster assembly to define the “space of six days” as
days of normal duration; and

Whereas, integrity compels a man to make known his particular views to the
courts of original jurisdiction, especially when such views differ from
our Confessional Standards; and

Whereas, the courts of original jurisdiction ought to examine a man and to
determine whether his particular views are consistent with or differ from
our Confessional Standards (BCO Preliminary Principle III; BCO 26-1);
th erefore, be it resolved , that the 29th General Assembly of the PCA
acknowledge, with charity toward all our members, and with integrity
regarding our Confessional Standards, that the Westminster Standards'
phrase “in the space of six days” means that the six days of creation were
days of normal duration with evening and morning; and
be it further resolved , that, for the peace of the church, any man who holds a
view which may differ with the above meaning of the phrase “in the
space of six days” shall inform the court of original jurisdiction of such
view for its consideration and determination; and
be it finally resolved , that courts of original jurisdiction make all
determinations on the acceptability of a man and his views with care,
charity, honesty, and impartiality.

Signed,
RE Mark A. Buckner RE Fredrick T. Greco
TE Carl W. Bogue RE Dennis W. Baker
TE Paul Honomichl TE G. Brent Bradley
TE Jerry I. Maguire RE M. Dale Peacock
RE Andrew J. Webb TE Byron Snapp

<a id="ga29-p195"></a>

R. Reap, TE Stephen Arrick, TE Byron Snapp, RE Marc Moss, RE Bob Totty, TE Steve Wilkins, RE Dean Moore, RE Lonn Oswalt, RE Sherman Stanford, TE Bill Smith, RE James Nabours, TE Mark Kreitzer, TE Michael Schuelke, TE Gary Englestad, RE John Mardirosian, TE James Chesnutt, RE Virgil Roberts, RE John Ropp, TE John Carroll, TE Don Stone, RE Hugh Sung, RE Andrew Webb, RE James Stuart, TE Alan McCall, TE Jim Bowen, TE Stu Sherard, TE Ken Hash, TE Gary Roop, TE King Counts, TE Craig Bulkeley, TE Morton Smith, TE Dion Marshall, TE Carel Van Der Merwe, TE Ross Lindley, RE John Robbins, RE Joe Reynolds, RE Roger Schultz, TE Phillip Blevins, TE Trevor Downie, TE Frank J. ##### OVERTURE 7 From Calvary Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

"Require Exceptions for Views Other Than Six 24-Hour Creation Days"

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America is a confessional church based upon the Westminster Standards; and

Whereas, our Confession of Faith states the time frame of God's original creative activity with the phrase, "in the space of six days" (WCF 4:1); and

Whereas, growing historical evidence demonstrates the intent of the Westminster Assembly was to define the six creation days as 24-hour calendar days (At least 13 Westminster divines have been identified as attesting to a 24-hour creation day in their writings and recorded sermons; while none has been documented as holding to other time frames.); and

<a id="ga29-p196"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=198 printed_page=196 -->

Whereas, the official position of Calvary Presbytery states that the doctrine of the Confessional standards of the Presbyterian Church in America explicitly asserts that the world was created by divine fiat, literally “in the space of six days”. Because the Confession’s teaching on creation is unassailably grounded in Scripture, we joyfully proclaim that our preaching and teaching should conform to its content and emphases. (Adopted October 24, 1995); and

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly affirmed that “...a diversity of views on creation days...is acceptable” thus, in essence, invalidating any definitive creedal statement; and

Whereas, it has been our historic practice during elder examinations to note any differences from our standards as exceptions to be discussed by the church court;

Therefore Be It Resolved, that the 29th General Assembly of the PCA declare that, with charity toward all our members, and with fidelity to the original authors of our confessional standards, and until evidence to the contrary is found, we understand the Westminster divines intended the phrase “in the space of six days” to specify that the six days of creation were days of normal duration with evening and morning; and

Be It Further Resolved, that, for the peace of the church, any future candidates who differ with this original meaning should request an exception to the sense of the Confession unless documentation that the Westminster divines held other views is firmly established or unless the Standards are duly amended; and

Be It Finally Resolved, that courts of original jurisdiction make these future determinations with care, charity, honesty, and impartiality.

Adopted by Calvary Presbytery on January 27, 2001.

Attested by: /s/ Charles E. Champion, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 20 from New River Presbytery (to B&amp;O &amp; CCB)

“Allow Exceptions on Length of Creation Days”

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly rejected the unanimous recommendation of the Creation Study Committee to wait two years before taking any action on the subject of the interpretation of the length of the days of creation in Genesis 1 and 2; and

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly gave approval to an action which, while described by the Moderator as simply ‘pious advice’, is being perceived by many in the denomination as pressure to approve candidates for office without regard to their views on these issues, therefore,

BE IT RESOLVED, that New River Presbytery, voting at its 80th Stated Meeting in Roanoke, Virginia on November 10, 2000, hereby encourages and requests the 29th General Assembly of the PCA to take

<a id="ga29-p197"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=199 printed_page=197 -->

the following actions as additions and further explanation of the Assembly’s response to the Creation Study Committee report:

A. While the PCA recognizes both a historic and current diversity of views in the area of the length of creation days described in Genesis 1 and 2, it remains the primary and original responsibility of Presbyteries and Sessions to examine their candidates and determine if their views, no matter how diverse, are in accord with the Constitution of the church, and to determine if such views are exceptions.

B. Presbyteries and Sessions who define any other view than the 24 hour day view as an exception to the standards in this area, in keeping with the actions of the 14th General Assembly, Section 143-52, Report of the Committee of Commissioners on Judicial Business, are to make the determination as to whether the teaching of these defined exceptions may or may not be allowed.

C. Presbyteries and Sessions are counseled that, until such time as a view other than six, 24-hour solar day creation is adjudged to be out of accord with the fundamentals of the system of doctrine of the church through a change to the constitution, a final action in a judicial case, or the approval of a recommendation by a study committee, no officer should be refused ordination based on that exception alone.

Adopted by New River Presbytery on November 10, 2000, at its 80th Stated Meeting. Attested by: /s/ Don K. Clements, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 23 From Mississippi Valley Presbytery (to B&amp;O &amp;CCB) “Require Exceptions for Views Other than Six 24-Hour Creation Days”

Whereas, the Bible, in Genesis 1, Exodus 20:11, and Exodus 31:17 teaches that God created the heavens and the earth, the sea and all that is in them in six days, and

Whereas, our doctrinal standards (WCF 4, LC 15, and SC 9) affirm that it pleased God, by the word of his power, to create the world and all things in the space of six days, and all very good.

Therefore, Be It Resolved, with charity toward all members and with fidelity to the original authors of our confessional standards, our understanding is that, unless evidence to the contrary is found, the Westminster Standards’ phrase “in the space of six days” means that the six days of creation were days of normal duration with evening and morning. Accordingly, for the peace of the church, any future candidates who differ with this original meaning should request an exception to the Confession, unless documentation that the Westminster Assembly meant otherwise is firmly established. We also pledge to make these future determinations, with care, charity, honesty and impartiality.

<a id="ga29-p198"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=200 printed_page=198 -->

adopted at the stated meeting of mississippi valley presbytery on february 6,
2001. Attested by: /s/R o ger G. Collins, Stated Clerk

6. that overture 30 from North Georgia Presbytery (“Affirm position of 28th
assembly on diversity of views on creation Days”) be answered in the
negative.
Grounds:
1) it is the responsibility of the lower courts to examine and determine if a
m an’s views are in accord with the constitution, not the man himself, not
the General Assembly, except in cases of judicial process.
2) Using general revelation to determine doctrine is contrary to W CF 1.6.
A dopted

##### overture 30 From North Georgia Presbytery (to B&O)

“Affirm position of 28th assembly on diversity of views on creation
Days”

Be it Resolved, that the North Georgia Presbytery overtures the 29th
General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to affirm the position
of the 28th General Assembly, allowing a diversity of creation views, and
likewise, with charity toward all our members, declare liberty-of-opinion
concerning the intent of the westminster divines in their use of the phrase “in
the space of six days.”
be it further r esolved, that the 29th General Assembly recognizes that,
within the church body, there are strongly held views concerning the days of
creation; and. the Assembly acknowledges that for a creedal-based church,
positions of diversity-of-view and liberty-of-opinion are not permanently
satisfactory. Therefore, Presbyteries, Sessions and individual church members
are encouraged to explore ways by which the scientific evidence of general
revelation can be objectively studied and validated so that the church can move
toward a unified understanding of how god created the universe and brought the
earth into existence. Meanwhile, let us rejoice in our bonds of brotherhood and
the hope we share in the reformed faith until our knowledge of his truth is more
fully developed.

Adopted by North Georgia Presbytery on April 17, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ Dwight Allen, Stated Clerk

that communication #4 from Western Carolina Presbytery be received
as information, and spread upon the minutes of the 29th General Assembly.
Grounds: We appreciate the concern and call for purity, peace, and unity in the
denomination.
A dopted

<a id="ga29-p199"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=201 printed_page=199 -->

COMMUNICATION #4 From Western Carolina Presbytery (to B&amp;O)
"A call for Purity, Peace and Unity of the Church"

Fathers and Brethren:

Two years ago, Western Carolina Presbytery initiated inquiry into the question of a woman preaching at the Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church of Tennessee Valley Presbytery, and also into the views of the Rev. John Wood on the subject of women preaching.

Our concern, shared by many throughout the denomination, was that a woman should not be allowed to preach in a PCA pulpit. Tolerating false doctrine and false practice is one way in which other denominations have degenerated in the past. We believe that failing to express our concern would not have fostered the purity, peace and unity of our Church.

It has been our wholehearted desire to encourage and promote the peace of the church. While we confess that we have been far from perfect in that quest, we believe that we can, with clear conscience, testify to the fact that we were earnest and sincere in all of our actions with regard to the John Wood matter. It was never our intention or desire that the Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church should leave the PCA. We wanted to know the facts. Our hope all along was for this congregation and its pastor to conform to the obvious teaching of Holy Scripture with regard to female preaching.

With the events that have transpired since that time, and the departure of the Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church from the PCA, Western Carolina Presbytery desires to issue a call to the whole PCA to bring closure to this matter and to endeavor to pursue together both the purity, peace and unity of the Church.

A Committee of Western Carolina Presbytery interviewed members of the Standing Judicial Commission regarding various reports that had been circulated and found that the investigative committee of the SJC found and reported that there were no discrepancies in the testimony of witnesses. Mr. Wood acknowledged that the testimony of the witnesses was accurate, and he stated before the panel "he should have been more careful to articulate his belief that women should not be ordained to the teaching or ruling eldership and that they should not be permitted to 'preach' in the traditional sense of authoritative teaching from the word of God, and teaching elders are called and ordained to do."

Though one may differ with the finding of the SJC, the WCP Committee found that the SJC had a full and frank deliberation of the investigating panel's report and voted unanimously that "the investigation has not resulted in a strong resumption of guilt on the part of TE John Wood . . . and therefore judicial process should not be instituted against TE John Wood for his expressed views regarding women." We accept the decision of the SJC.

The WCP investigation found that the SJC followed the Manual of Operations in determining to disqualify any one of the SJC members from participating in the deliberation.

<a id="ga29-p200"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=202 printed_page=200 -->

It is because of all the controversy and alleged irregularities surrounding the John Wood matter that we, once again, found ourselves duty-bound to raise the question of possible impropriety when reports were circulated that one or more members of the SJC had deliberately sought to protect John Wood at the Vision 2000 Caucus, but had not recused themselves from debating and voting. The WCP Committee investigated these allegations, and are happy to report that we have not been able to confirm them. In a statement made to two members of our Presbytery, RE John White, Chairman of the SJC, stated that he had not tried to advocate any particular course of action for the Assembly to take prior to the meeting of the Assembly itself. Mr. White indicated that at a meeting of the Vision 2000 Caucus several months prior to the 2000 General Assembly, he had set forth what he thought was the Constitutional position, viz., that the Assembly could not overturn the SJC's determination in the John Wood matter. We hereby report Mr. White's recounting of the facts, in the hope that the rumor that one or more SJC members have engaged in improper lobbying regarding the John Wood matter can now be put to rest.

As we come to closure on the John Wood Judicial Matter, we hope and pray that all of us in the Presbyterian Church in America have learned from it. Perhaps the foremost lesson is that in order to maintain the bond peace (Ephesians 4:3-6), which we all desire, there must be genuine unity; and that, in order to have proper unity, humility (Eph. 4:2, Philippians 2:1-22) is the necessary foundation. Of course unity can only be maintained between differing brethren when there is a genuine respect for the integrity of all parties, a gentle patience that bears with one another (Eph. 4:2). For whatever the reason, there seems to have been a general breakdown of trust across the denomination. The biblical way to restore trust is for individuals to pursue each other to resolve differences and clear up misunderstanding and miscommunication (Matthew 5 and 18). We pray that the Lord may be pleased to restore peace to us and maintain purity, peace and unity among us.

Again, we urge that the John Wood matter be put to rest by this year's General Assembly, recognizing that she must handle the several questions that have been raised by Ascension and Calvary Presbyteries. We trust that we can now move forward as a denomination, in the bonds of purity, peace and unity in which we can all rejoice.

Adopted at a called meeting of Western Carolina Presbytery on May 8, 2001.

Attested by: /s/ Skip Gilliken, Stated Clerk

8. That Overture 8 from Calvary Presbytery (“Condemn Action of SJC in Judicial Matter 99-1 (re. TE John Wood)”) be answered in the negative with reference to Communication 4, which states our grounds.

Adopted

Arrick, TE Peter Jones, RE Bill Boney, TE Jay Nelken, TE Decherd Stevens, TE Matt Baugh, TE Robert Thacker, TE Mark O'Neill, TE Carl W. Bogue, TE Mark A. Herzer, TE C.N. Willborn, RE Philip T. Proctor, TE A. Trevor Downie, TE Irfon Hughes, TE Henry Fernandez, TE Robert Spears, TE Daniel Foreman, TE Earl Fair, TE Gary Roop, RE Andrew Webb, TE Morton H. Smith, TE Patrick J. Morgan, RE Daniel Jarstfer, TE Bill Leuzinger, RE Vaughn R. Hamilton, RE Hugh Sung, TE Scott Hill, TE Rick Steele, TE G. Brent Bradley, TE Mark A Scholten, TE Henry E.
