##### OVERTURE 8 From Calvary Presbytery (to B&amp;O)

"Condemn Action of SJC in Judicial Matter 99-1 (re. TE John Wood)"

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly instructed the SJC to hear from the Presbyteries concerned in the charges against John Wood; and

Whereas, the SJC nor its panel of three men gave the Presbyteries an opportunity to speak to the theological and constitutional issues involved in the John Wood case; and

Whereas, the SJC made theological interpretations without hearing from the Presbyteries; and

Whereas, the SJC was instructed to determine Mr. Wood's views, in their report they do not focus on his views but that he has not agitated for the view that women may preach in the church; and

Whereas, in the report of the panel there is a conflict between the testimony of Drs. Smith and Pipa and that of Mr. Wood with respect to Mr. Wood's definition of preaching and there was no opportunity for cross examination; and

Whereas, there is a conflict between the report of Mr. Wood's position in the SJC ruling and his expressed position in his letter of October 2, 1999; and

Whereas, such conflict can only be resolved through a trial with cross examination; and

Whereas, in its spring meeting in 2000 the SJC would not allow its members who were from the Presbyteries that brought the original overtures to the SJC to vote on the issue, even though it was pointed out that in cases of original jurisdiction no member of the court was required to recuse himself.

Therefore Be It Resolved, Calvary Presbytery overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to condemn the report of the 28th Assembly through the SJC (not to draw up charges against Mr. Wood and proceed to a trial).

<a id="ga29-p202"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=204 printed_page=202 -->

Adopted by Calvary Presbytery on January 27, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ Charles E. Champion, Stated Clerk

9. That Overtures 9 and 11 from Evangel Presbytery (“Procedures on Assuming Original Jurisdiction”) be answered in the negative.

Grounds: The concept of original jurisdiction is strong and dear to the denomination and must be preserved. However, the proposed changes to the BCO lead to the possibility of the court of original jurisdiction becoming the court of exclusive jurisdiction. The details of the proposed administrative procedures produce more difficulties than benefits in dealing with spiritual problems.

Adopted

##### OVERTURE 9 From Evangel Presbytery

(to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)
“Procedures on Assuming Original Jurisdiction Over Church Members”

A New BCO 33-1

Process against all church members, other than ministers of the Gospel, shall be entered before the Session of the church to which such members belong, except in cases of appeal. This principle of original jurisdiction is designed to protect the right of the Session to investigate and adjudicate matters of sin within its bounds. Additionally, this principle of original jurisdiction is designed to protect the church member against judicial charges from those outside the Session who do not necessarily have access to the facts or access to the right of investigation.

However, if there are other sessions in the same Presbytery which are not satisfied with how the Original Jurisdiction Session (OJS) has dealt with a matter of allegation against one of its members, then the following process shall be followed:

1. Those other sessions in the same Presbytery must formally request the OJS to conduct a BCO 31-2 investigation of allegations against or reports surrounding this member within the OJS’s jurisdiction.
2. If the OJS conducts a BCO 31-2 investigation, then the matter will remain totally within the jurisdictional bounds of the OJS, subject, of course, to BCO provisions of complaint appeal, review and control.
3. If these other sessions in the same Presbytery (which followed step one above) do not believe that a BCO 31-2 investigation has taken place within a reasonable time period, these other sessions may request their Presbytery to assume original jurisdiction.
4. If at least two of these other sessions in the same Presbytery (who followed step one above) make such a request for their Presbytery to assume original jurisdiction, the matter shall be dealt with at the next stated meeting of the Presbytery at which time a commission will be appointed to conduct a preliminary hearing as to whether or not the OJS has conducted a BCO 31-2

<a id="ga29-p203"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=205 printed_page=203 -->

investigation prior to the time of the commission's preliminary hearing. The commission may consider written briefs and oral presentations from the OJS and the two (or more) accusing sessions. The only question to be considered at this hearing is this: At the time of this commission's preliminary hearing, has the OJS conducted a *BCO 31-2* investigation of the alleged behavior or belief of its member.

5. If the commission determines that the OJS has conducted the *BCO 31-2* investigation, then the commission shall rule that the OJS has acted to exercise its right of original jurisdiction.

6. If the commission determines that the OJS has not conducted the *BCO 31-2* investigation (even though it has been properly asked to do so and has had sufficient time to do so), then the commission shall rule that the OJS has forfeited its right of original jurisdiction, shall rule that Presbytery has assumed the right of original jurisdiction and shall try the case as the court of original jurisdiction.

## Grounds/Rationale:

A. The controversy at our 28th General Assembly surrounding the John Wood matter engulfed our Standing Judicial Commission, our GA Stated Clerk, our Committee on Constitutional Business, our Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners, and our floor of the General Assembly in a constitutional quagmire because the existing *BCO 34-1* does not adequately answer several questions:

1. What does "refuses to act" mean?
2. Who gets to decide what "refuses to act" means?
3. Does the Stated Clerk of the GA give this *BCO 34-1* matter to the SJC for adjudication or route it directly to the GA via the Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners?
4. How can we balance the concerns between the need to protect the doctrinal purity of our church and the need to protect our principle of original jurisdiction which serves in turn to protect our ministers from judicial charges from those who may not necessarily have access to all of the facts or access to the right of investigation

B. *BCO 34-1* is amended, then *BCO 33-1* must also be amended so that there is a consistency in our constitutional principles as they apply to our Presbyteries and our Sessions.

Approved at the Stated Meeting of Evangel Presbytery on Tuesday 23 January 2001. Attested by: /s/ Dr. Richard Trucks, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 11 From Evangel Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend *BCO 34-1* on Assuming Original Jurisdiction of Ministers”

<a id="ga29-p204"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=206 printed_page=204 -->

## A New BCO 34-1

Process against a minister shall be entered before the Presbytery of which he is a member. This principle of original jurisdiction is designed to protect the right of the Presbytery to investigate and adjudicate matters of sin within its bounds. Additionally, this principle of original jurisdiction is designed to protect the minister against judicial charges from those outside the Presbytery who do not necessarily have access to the facts or access to the right of investigation.

However, if there are other presbyteries which are not satisfied with how the Original Jurisdiction Presbytery (OJP) has dealt with a matter of allegation against one of its member ministers, then the following process shall be followed:

1. Those other presbyteries must formally request the OJP to conduct a BCO 31-2 investigation of allegations against or reports surrounding this minister within the OJP'S jurisdiction.
2. If the OJP conducts a BCO 31-2 investigation, then the matter will remain totally within the jurisdictional bounds of the OJP, subject, of course, to BCO provisions of complaint, appeal, review and control.
3. If these other presbyteries (which followed step one above) do not believe that a BCO 31-2 investigation has taken place within a reasonable time period, these other presbyteries may request the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction.
4. If at least two of these other presbyteries (who followed step one above) make such a request for the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction, the matter shall be referred immediately by the GA Stated Clerk to the GA Standing Judicial Commission for a preliminary hearing as to whether or not the OJP has conducted a BCO 31-2 investigation prior to the time of the SJC preliminary hearing. The SJC may consider written briefs and oral presentations from the OJP and the two (or more) accusing presbyteries. The only question to be considered at this hearing is: At the time of this SJC preliminary hearing, has the OJP conducted a BCO 31-2 investigation of the alleged behavior or belief of its member minister?
5. If the SJC determines that the OJP has conducted the BCO 31-2 investigation, then the SJC shall rule that the OJP has acted to exercise its right of original jurisdiction.
6. If the SJC determines that the OJP has not conducted the BCO 31-2 investigation (even though it has been properly asked to do so and has had sufficient time to do so), then the SJC shall rule that the OJP has forfeited its right of original jurisdiction, shall rule that General Assembly has assumed the right of original jurisdiction, and shall try the case as the court of original jurisdiction.

<a id="ga29-p205"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=207 printed_page=205 -->

## Grounds/Rationale:

A. The controversy at our 28th General Assembly surrounding the John Wood matter engulfed our Standing Judicial Commission, our GA Stated Clerk, our Committee on Constitutional Business, our Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners. And our floor of the General Assembly in a constitutional quagmire because the existing BCO 34-1 does not adequately answer several questions:

1. What does "refuses to act" mean?
2. Who gets to decide what "refuses to act" means?
3. Does the Stated Clerk of the GA give this BCO 34-1 matter to the SJC for adjudication or route it directly to the GA via the Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners?
4. How can we balance the concerns between the need to protect the doctrinal purity of our church and the need to protect our principle of original jurisdiction which serves in turn to protect our ministers from judicial charges from those who may not necessarily have access to all of the facts or access to the right of investigation.

B. If BCO 34-1 is amended, then BCO 33-1 must also be amended so that there is a consistency in our constitutional principles as they apply to our Presbyteries and our Sessions.

Approved at the Stated Meeting of Evangel Presbytery on Tuesday 23 January 2001. Attested by: /s/ Dr. Richard Trucks, Stated Clerk

10. That Overture 10 from Evangel Presbytery (“Amend BCO 26-3 and 26-5 on Procedure for Amending Westminster Standards”) be answered in the negative.
Grounds: The current requirement is appropriate for amending the Westminster Standards.

Adopted

##### OVERTURE 10 From Evangel Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 26-3 and 26-5 on Procedure for Amending Westminster Standards”

## A New BCO 26-3

Amendments to the Confession of Faith and the Larger and Shorter Catechisms may be made only in the following manner:

1. Approval of the proposed amendment by two-thirds (2/3) of those present and voting in the General Assembly, and its recommendation to the Presbyteries.
2. The advice and consent of two-thirds (2/3) of the Presbyteries.
3. The approval and enactment by a subsequent General Assembly by two-thirds (2/3) of those present and voting.

<a id="ga29-p206"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=208 printed_page=206 -->

This paragraph (BCO 26-3) can be amended only by the same method prescribed for the amendment of the Confession of Faith and Catechisms of the church.

## Ground/Rationale:

1. Dr. Tim Kellers paper "The Original PCA Contract" (dated Aug. 1999) accurately demonstrates the cause of the tension in the PCA today and brilliantly suggests that the solution may be found in the amending of our confession.

2. The high jump bar as it now stands in the present BCO 26-3 is too high. .. pragmatically the Confession is unamendable. There has never been passed and only rarely has there been suggested an amendment to the Confession. This results in two erroneous postures:

A. Ignore the teachings of the Confession or
B. Believe that the Confession equals the Scriptures and treat it accordingly

3. This amendment will encourage debate where it belongs, i.e. the perceived differences between Scripture and Confession.

## Amendment to BCO 26-5:

If this amendment to BCO 26-3 above should pass, then the last paragraph of BCO 26-5 must be changed. Therefore, a simultaneous but contingent amendment would have to read:

This paragraph (BCO 26-5) can be amended only by the same method prescribed in this paragraph (BCO 26-5) for the approval of full organic union.

Approved at the Stated Meeting of Evangel Presbytery on Tuesday 23 January 2001.
Attested by: /s/ Dr. Richard Trucks, Stated Clerk

11. Answer Overtures 13 (“Amend BCO 32-3 Regarding Sequence of Meetings of a Court in Judicial Process”, #14 (“Amend BCO 32-4 Regarding Delivery Requirements for Indictments and Citations”), and #16 (“Amend BCO 43-1 to Allow Complaint After Appeal is Ruled Out of Order, Withdrawn or Abandoned”) from Central Carolina Presbytery in the affirmative.
Adopted as amended

##### OVERTURE 13 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 32-3 Regarding Sequence of Meetings of a Court in Judicial Process”

Chapter 32 - General Provisions Applicable to all Cases of Process 32-3. It is appropriate that with each citation the moderator or clerk call the attention of the parties to the Rules of Discipline (BCO 27 through 46) and assist the parties to obtain access to them. When a charge is laid before the Session or

<a id="ga29-p207"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=209 printed_page=207 -->

Presbytery, it shall be reduced to writing and nothing shall be done at the first meeting of the court unless by consent of parties, except:

1. to appoint a prosecutor,
2. to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with names of witnesses then known to support it, served on the accused, and
3. to cite all parties and their witnesses the accused to appear and be heard at another meeting which shall not be sooner than ten days after such citation.

At the second meeting of the court, the charges shall be read to the accused, if present, and he shall be called upon to say whether he be guilty or not. If the accused confesses, the court may deal with him according to its discretion; if he plead and take issue, the trial shall proceed be scheduled and all parties and their witnesses cited to appear. The trial shall not be sooner than 14 days after such citation. Accused parties may plead in writing when they cannot be personally present. Parties necessarily absent should have counsel assigned to them.

Rationale – Current wording explicitly mentions only two meetings of the court. The proposed change would result in three meetings in any full case of process – indictment meeting, arraignment, and trial. The current wording often results in unnecessary efforts. First, if the witnesses appear at the second meeting but the accused pleads guilty, then the witnesses appeared unnecessarily. Second, if the accused fails to appear, then the witnesses also appeared unnecessarily. Third, current wording implies that if the accused pleads not guilty, the trial could “proceed” at that second meeting. Thus, the prosecutor would need to completely prepare his case for the second meeting. However, if the accused pleads guilty the prosecutor would have fully prepared a case that he does not prosecute.

On the other hand, if this second meeting is treated like an arraignment hearing, the problems go away. There would be no need to cite witness to appear at that meeting nor for the prosecutor to completely prepare the case prior to that meeting. If the accused pleads guilty at the second meeting (arraignment), the court can deal with him without ever needing to cite witnesses or to prepare a full prosecution. If he pleads not guilty at that meeting, then the witnesses will be cited to appear at the trial and the prosecutor can prepare his case in full, knowing there will be a trial.

Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of Central Carolina Presbytery on January 26, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ David Frierson, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 14 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 32-4 Regarding Delivery Requirements for Indictments and Citations”

<a id="ga29-p208"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=210 printed_page=208 -->

## Chapter 32 - General Provisions Applicable to all Cases of Process

32-4. The citation shall be issued and signed by the moderator or clerk by order and in the name of the court. He shall also issue citations to such witnesses as either party shall nominate to appear on his behalf. Indictments and citations shall be delivered in person or in another manner providing verification of the date of receipt. Compliance with these requirements shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept delivery.

**Rationale** – This change would ensure a record exists of the date a citation is received. Because certain time periods are based on when such indictments and citations are received, it is necessary to record the exact date of receipt. Furthermore, *BCO 32-10* requires that “Before proceeding to trial, courts ought to ascertain that their citations have been duly served.” This proposed change to *BCO 32-4* would ensure both those requirements are met. The last sentence regarding compliance is essentially the same provision as contained in SJC Manual 19.10.c.

Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of Central Carolina Presbytery on January 26, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ David Frierson, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 16 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend *BCO 43-1* to Allow Complaint After Appeal is Ruled Out of Order, Withdrawn or Abandoned”

## Chapter 43 – Complaints

43-1. A complaint is a written representation made against some act or decision of a court of the Church. It is the right of any communing member of the church in good standing to make complaint against any action of a court to whose jurisdiction he is subject, except that no complaint is to be considered in a judicial case while an appeal is ~~taken~~ pending.

**Rationale** – This change is intended to clarify the current wording of 43-1. We believe the word “taken” needs to be qualified. Otherwise, it might be understood as forever disallowing any complaint simply because an appeal was once filed (“taken”), regardless of the disposition of the appeal. For example, current wording might be understood as disallowing a complaint even if an appeal is ruled out of order. Or, if an appeal is abandoned or withdrawn, some might conclude that since an appeal was “taken” no complaint would ever be in order - even if the abandonment or withdrawal occurred shortly after the appeal was filed. Current wording might imply that the mere act of filing an appeal would forever preclude anyone from seeking review of any aspect of the original trial by either the original court or the next higher court.

<a id="ga29-p209"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=211 printed_page=209 -->

The proposed change would allow consideration of a previously timely-filed complaint if the appeal disappeared, even though the appeal was at one time “taken.” An appeal would disappear if it is ruled out of order, withdrawn or abandoned. Presumably the main intent of the existing restriction is to preclude having to review a case twice, or to preclude giving a party “two bites at the apple.” However, if an appeal is never adjudicated, there is no such risk. The revised paragraph would essentially “table” consideration of any complaint while an appeal is pending, but would still clearly disallow any complaint once an appeal has been adjudicated.

Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of Central Carolina Presbytery on January 26, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ David Frierson, Stated Clerk

12. Answer Overture 15 from Central Carolina Presbytery (“Amend BCO 35-7 and 32-18 Requiring Recording of Testimony and Provision for Transcription”) in the affirmative as amended.

Grounds: The burden of the cost of the transcription should not necessarily be borne by a single party.

Adopted

##### OVERTURE 15 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O)

“Amend BCO 35-7 and 32-18 Requiring Recording of Testimony and Provision for Transcription”

This overture would substitute new language for BCO 35-7, and strike one word from BCO 32-18.

Chapter 35 - Evidence

35-7. Every question put to a witness shall, if required, be reduced to writing. When answered, it shall, together with the answer, be recorded, if deemed by the court or by either party of sufficient importance, and the testimony of the witness shall be read to him for his approbation and subscription.

Proposed new 35-7 All testimony shall be recorded (transcription, audiotape, videotape, or some other electronic means) and witnesses informed of such prior to testifying. Such recording becomes part of the Record of the Case. However, in order to be referenced in written or oral briefs, such recording must be transcribed and the transcription authenticated by the trial court. The court of final appeal may assess the cost of transcription equitably among the parties.

Chapter 32 – General Provisions Applicable to all Cases of Process

32-18 Minutes of the trial shall be kept by the clerk, which shall exhibit the charges, the answer, written record of the testimony, as defined by BCO

<a id="ga29-p210"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=212 printed_page=210 -->

35-7, and all such acts, orders, and decisions of the court relating to the case, as either party may desire, and also the judgment...

Rationale – The current wording of *BCO 35-7* does not ensure a complete record of all the testimony in a trial. Therefore, if an appeal or a complaint is filed, there may not be a sufficient record of the testimony. This change would ensure that a complete record of testimony is available. This recording provision is similar to that in SJC Manual 19.2: “All oral arguments by the parties before the Commission or a Judicial Panel thereof shall be tape recorded.” Furthermore, the current language of 35-7 is unclear and cumbersome. What is meant by “if required” in the first sentence? Who determines if it is required? The second sentence implies that only some of the testimony might be recorded. Nowadays, since electronic means are easily available for recording, there is no reason not to record all the testimony. If *BCO 35-7* is changed as above, the word “written” needs to be stricken from *BCO 32-18*. The recorded testimony would be considered part of the minutes, regardless of the form of the recording.

Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of Central Carolina Presbytery on January 26, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ David Frierson, Stated Clerk

13. Answer Overture 17 from Central Carolina Presbytery (“Add a New Chapter 44 to *BCO* and Appendix H”) in the affirmative, with amendment, by appending the proposed language with suitable amendments and the flowchart to the *BCO* as Appendix H – “Suggested Procedures for Presbytery Judicial Commissions Acting as Appellate Courts.”

Grounds:

1) The overture is intended to offer assistance in cases in which commissions act as appellate courts. To make this procedural advice available quickly, it was deemed wise to avoid the amendment procedure.

2) We believe this extensive procedure best serves the Church by being placed in the Appendix, where it is non-binding.

Adopted

<a id="ga29-p211"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=213 printed_page=211 -->

Appendix H
Suggested Procedures for Presbytery Judicial Commissions Acting as Appellate Courts

1. These procedures are suggested when a Presbytery, as an appellate court, decides that an appeal or complaint arising from a Session is in order, and in accordance with *BCO* 15, chooses to appoint a Judicial Commission to hear the case. The Presbytery Stated Clerk shall promptly notify the parties of the names of the Commission members and the initial contact information. (See flowchart.)

2. A hearing shall not be conducted until the Commission determines that the Record of the Case is complete (perfected), the parties have had opportunity to file briefs, and the members of the Commission have had adequate time to read the Record and all briefs.

3. The convener or chairman of the Commission shall convene the first meeting within 10 days of the Presbytery meeting at which the Commission was appointed. This meeting may be held the same day Presbytery appoints the Commission. At this convening meeting the Commission shall elect its chairman and secretary, unless Presbytery had previously appointed them. In addition to other preliminary matters, the Commission may discuss potential dates for a hearing and the current status of the Record. The chairman shall promptly notify the parties of all addresses necessary for filing purposes.

4. Conference calls are acceptable for all Commission meetings other than the hearing. This includes the meetings to: 1) convene the Commission, 2) resolve disputes on the Record, 3) adopt a Judgment, and 4) adopt a written decision and approve minutes.

5. At its discretion and with the consent of both parties, the Commission may decide to accept email, fax or similar means for filings after establishing procedures to confirm the date that a document is filed and received.

6. Record of the Case – After the Commission is appointed, the Presbytery Stated Clerk shall promptly ensure delivery of the Record to each Commission member and to all parties to the case. It shall be delivered to the appellant/complainant in person or in some other manner providing verification of the date of receipt. If the appellant/complainant believes the Record is incomplete or incorrect, he shall, within 14 days of receiving such Record, file written notice with the Commission and with the Session (clerk or respondent). Such notice shall specify the alleged defects and suggestions for corrections. Failure to file a timely objection will

<a id="ga29-p212"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=214 printed_page=212 -->

constitute acceptance of the Record. Within 14 days of receiving such written notice, the Session shall file a written response with the Commission and with the appellant/complainant. The chairman shall promptly send to the commissioners the notice of objection and the Session’s response. If no objection is filed, the parties are considered to have received the perfected Record on the day they received the original Record.

7. Resolving Disputes on the Record - If the Session agrees to any suggested correction, the Record shall be revised accordingly. If the Session does not agree to a suggested correction, the Commission shall consider the written arguments of both parties and rule whether the change(s) should be made. This ruling shall be made within 14 days of when the Commission received the Session’s response and the chairman shall promptly notify the parties of the ruling. If the original Record is revised, the chairman shall ensure both parties receive the perfected Record and the date of its receipt shall be recorded. In this event, the chairman shall also ensure the commissioners receive copies of the revision.

8. Filing Briefs - After the Record has been perfected, the parties may file briefs with the Commission. Such briefs are optional and additional to the notice of appeal/complaint (BCO 42-4, 43-3) and to the Session’s initial response (BCO 42-5, 43-6).

1. The appellant/complainant’s brief shall be filed no later than 14 days after he receives the perfected Record (or the original Record if undisputed.) The Commission will immediately send this brief to the Session and the delivery date shall be recorded. The chairman will promptly send this brief to the commissioners.
2. The Session’s brief shall be filed no later than 28 days after it receives the perfected Record (or the original Record if undisputed) or 14 days after it receives the appellant/complainant’s brief, whichever is later. The Commission will immediately send this brief to the appellant/complainant. The chairman will promptly send this brief to the commissioners.
3. The Commission should not schedule the hearing for sooner than 42 days after the date the parties receive the perfected Record (or the original Record if undisputed), unless both parties, in writing, waive their right to file briefs.
4. The briefs shall include the party’s position with regard to: a summary of the facts, a summary of the proceedings before the Session, a proposed statement of the issue(s), the proposed judgment &amp; relief, and the argument in favor of judgment &amp; relief.
5. No brief shall make reference to any evidence not already part of the perfected Record. The Commission may, at its discretion, strike all or the part of any brief that makes such reference. Parties may

<a id="ga29-p213"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=215 printed_page=213 -->

petition the Commission to strike any alleged new evidence from a brief. At the same time, briefs may mention and the Commission may take judicial notice of facts not subject to reasonable dispute.

6. A brief is limited to 10 pages. It must be typewritten on $8 \frac{1}{2} \times 11$-inch paper, with 1-inch margins on all sides, and no smaller than 10-point type. Any brief that does not meet these standards of form shall be returned to the sending party with reasons. If this occurs, a revised brief may be submitted, provided such brief is filed with the Commission within 5 days of when the party received notification that the brief did not meet the standard of form.

9. Before the hearing begins, the chairman shall call the members of the Commission to order and he shall:

1. Open the meeting with prayer and certify a quorum is present.
2. Ask each member individually if he has read the Record and all briefs. Any member who cannot affirm such is disqualified from deliberating and voting.
3. Remind the members to disregard all evidence not in the Record, even though such evidence may be found in the briefs of the parties or in oral argument.
4. Read to the members the four principles adopted as Standards of Review in $BCO\ 39-3$.

10. During the hearing:

1. Before arguments begin, commissioners may question the parties on any matter before the court.
2. The arguments shall be heard in the following order: 1) the appellant/complainant opens, 2) the Session responds, and 3) the appellant/complainant closes. Each party should be present to hear the arguments of the other.
3. A party shall have a maximum of 30 minutes to argue his case. For the appellant/complainant, these 30 minutes are inclusive of both his opening and closing arguments. He may divide that time as he chooses.
4. At any time during which a party is presenting an argument, a commissioner may ask questions of that party. The time taken for such questions shall not form a part of the argument time of the party questioned.

11. Judgment - As soon as possible after hearing oral arguments, the Commission shall go into closed session and discuss the issues in the case. In that discussion, the Commission may frame the issues and vote on a Judgment. Or, the Commission may adjourn and reconvene within the next 10 days, as often as necessary, to do so.

<a id="ga29-p214"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=216 printed_page=214 -->

12. Written Decision - After the Judgment is adopted, the chairman shall designate a member who voted with the majority to draft the written decision. If the chairman did not vote in the majority, the majority will select their own drafter. The decision shall contain four parts:

1. Summary of Facts (chronological summary of relevant history and facts bearing on case)
2. Statement of the Issue(s) (concise framing by the court of specific issue(s) being judged in case)
3. Judgment (definite conclusion rendered on the specific issue(s) being adjudged)
4. Reasoning and Opinion (reasons for judgment rendered and resolution of any disputed facts)

The drafter of the decision shall send it to the commissioners promptly enough so that within 21 days of adopting the Judgment, the Commission shall adopt a written decision and approve the minutes of its meetings. Any Commission member may file a concurring or dissenting opinion.

13. Reporting - Within 42 days after the hearing, the Commission shall file its report with the Presbytery Stated Clerk. In addition, any commissioner writing a concurring or dissenting opinion shall file it with the Presbytery Stated Clerk within 42 days after the hearing. The Commission’s report shall include the following:

1. Commission’s written decision, including the vote.
2. Minutes of the meetings of the Commission.
3. Briefs of the Parties
4. Record of the Case as perfected

14. Distribution - The Presbytery Stated Clerk shall promptly forward to both parties a copy of the Commission’s decision and any concurring or dissenting opinions. The Stated Clerk shall send the same to the members of the Presbytery, in sufficient time for them to review prior to voting on the Commission’s judgment.

15. For the purposes of computing times, the day of the act or event from which a designated period of time begins shall not be included. The last day of the period shall be included, unless it is a Saturday, Sunday or postal holiday, in which case the period extends until the end of the next day which is not a Saturday, Sunday or postal holiday. “Filing” shall not be timely unless the documents are received by the appropriate party within the time fixed for such filing. “Received” shall be when the documents are actually delivered to the party.

<a id="ga29-p215"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=217 printed_page=215 -->

16. The Commission may extend any of the deadline dates in this chapter Appendix, or page limits for briefs, upon its determination that so doing is required in the interest of justice.

SUMMARY – This new chapter procedure would only apply to a Presbytery Judicial Commission acting as an appellate court. This procedure does not apply if a Presbytery chooses to hear any case as a whole body. Furthermore, Presbytery would still need to vote on adopting any decision judgment of its Judicial Commission.

These procedures provide three distinct six-week (42 day) periods:

1. Resolving any disputes on the Record
2. Filing, distributing and reading briefs
3. Conducting hearing, deliberating and filing full report with Stated Clerk

Long Process – If the Record is disputed, and the parties choose to file briefs, and the full time is taken for all deadlines, the Commission might not file its final report with the Stated Clerk until four months (126 days) after the Presbytery meeting at which it was appointed. However, if the full time is not taken for all deadlines, the Commission may be able to file its report in time for consideration at the next Presbytery meeting, even if the Record is disputed and briefs are filed.

Medium Process – If either the Record is not disputed or the parties waive their right to file briefs, and the full time is taken for all deadlines, a Commission should file its final report within 84 days (12 weeks). In most Presbyteries, this should be in time for the next meeting.

Short Process – If the Record is not disputed, and the parties waive their right to file briefs, the Commission should file its report within 42 days of the hearing, which could be held as soon as the Commissioners have read the Record. This scenario would also result in Presbytery considering the Commission’s report at the next Presbytery meeting.

<a id="ga29-p216"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=218 printed_page=216 -->

presbytery clerk forwards decision to parties and pres byte rs

14. That Overture 19 from Ascension Presbytery (“Ask CCB to Examine Minutes
of sjc regarding original jurisdiction in john wood judicial m atter”) be
answered in the negative.

<a id="ga29-p217"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=219 printed_page=217 -->

Grounds: CCB has already reviewed SJC minutes and issued a report. [See 29-28, II, p. 132.]

##### OVERTURE 19 From Ascension Presbytery (to B&amp;O)

“Ask CCB to Examine Minutes of SJC regarding Original Jurisdiction in John Wood Judicial Matter”

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly voted to take jurisdiction of Dr. John Wood with respect to allegations set forth in overtures from three presbyteries; and

Whereas, the matter was referred to the SJC to be considered “after the manner of BCO 31-2;” and

Whereas, the 28th General Assembly directed that, as part of this process, “representatives from the petitioning presbyteries be permitted to represent their allegations before the SJC” (M28GA, p. 310); and

Whereas, Presbytery received, from the Investigative Panel appointed by the SJC, a letter stating that the Panel would be meeting “...for the sole purpose of gathering information...” and asking that we identify witnesses “...who have direct knowledge of relevant facts...;” and

Whereas, Presbytery understood “represent their allegations” to entail, not just a right to identify witnesses, but also a right to explain our reasoning and concerns; and

Whereas, the contents of the Panel’s letter indicated to us that there would not be an opportunity for us to “represent our allegations” before the Panel in the way in which the 28th General Assembly meant that phrase; and

Whereas, we sent a letter to the Panel detailing this and other concerns, and asking for a fuller explanation of the SJC’s planned process, particularly as it related to “permitting us to represent our allegations;” and

Whereas, Presbytery never received a formal response to its letter; and

Whereas, in a telephone call, initiated by the Stated Clerk of Presbytery, the Secretary of the Panel stated that it was his understanding that representatives from the Presbytery of the Ascension would not be allowed to speak before the Investigative Panel on the grounds that they had no ability to testify from direct knowledge regarding the facts of the case; and

Whereas, on the basis of the above letter and conversation, Presbytery’s Commission determined that it would not be good stewardship to send a representative to the Panel’s meeting if he would not be able to speak to Presbytery’s position (and having no information as to whether we would be permitted to address the full SJC); and

Whereas, it is our understanding that the two men from the other petitioning presbyteries (one of whom was an elected representative of his presbytery) were allowed to speak to the Panel only with regard to their knowledge of facts, and not with regard to a presentation of the allegations; and

<a id="ga29-p218"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=220 printed_page=218 -->

Whereas, no one from this Presbytery received any notification or permission to attend the meeting of the full SJC, much less to “represent any allegations;” and

Whereas, to the best of our knowledge, neither of the other two petitioning presbyteries received such an invitation; and

Whereas, the above facts seem to indicate that the Standing Judicial Commission acted in direct violation of the instructions of the 28th General Assembly; and

Whereas, the current Standing Judicial Commission is clearly a commission of the 28th GA (see M28GA, p. 245); and

Whereas, commissions, by their very nature, must operate within the procedures and guidelines mandated by the body appointing them lest their power be unchecked; and

Whereas, this is particularly true in this matter which was assigned to the SJC by the General Assembly, and which does not fall into any of the categories mentioned in the RAO and SJC Manual as being under the ordinary purview of the SJC [i.e., appeals, complaints, and judicial references (see RAO 15-1 and SJC Manual sec. 13-15)]; and

Whereas, RAO 15-1 and SJC Manual 20.5 charge the Committee on Constitutional Business with reviewing the minutes of the SJC to determine if the SJC has conformed to the requirements of the RAO and Manual; and

Whereas, if the SJC failed to carry out the instructions of the 28th General Assembly it has failed to act properly as a commission, thus violating the BCO and RAO; and

Whereas, this overture raises questions only about the procedures followed by the SJC, not its reasoning;

Therefore Be it Resolved, that the Presbytery of the Ascension hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly, through its Committee on Constitutional Business, asking that the CCB examine the minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission to determine if the instructions of the 28th General Assembly were followed with regard to the matter of permitting representatives of the petitioning presbyteries to “represent their allegations before the SJC;” and, it not, to take or recommend appropriate action as per RAO 15-1, para. 4 (cf. BCO 40-4, last sentence).

Adopted at the January Stated Meeting of the Presbytery of the Ascension on January 27, 2001. Attested by: /s/ Frederick R. Neikirk, Stated Clerk

15. That Overture 21 from Ellisville Presbyterian Church (“Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow at Least Two Ruling Elders to Represent Each Congregation in Presbytery Meetings”) be answered in the affirmative. Adopted

<a id="ga29-p219"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=221 printed_page=219 -->

##### OVERTURE 21 From Ellisville Presbyterian Church (to B&amp;O &amp; CCB)

“Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow at Least Two Ruling Elders to Represent Each Congregation in Presbytery Meetings”

Whereas, the formation of the Presbyterian Church in America was the result of the Lord, in His Providence, convicting elders of the need to remain true to the Biblical principles and teachings upon which our faith is based, and

Whereas, our elder fathers in the faith were the first to point out and take a stand against errors being made and taught in the old Southern church, and

Whereas, John Edward Richard, in his History of the Presbyterian Church in America (1986, p. 131) posits, “There seems to be a desire in the pre-birth movement of the new church to give right concern and positions to all officers, whether lay or ministerial. We may even hope to return to decentralized polity so wisely advocated by James H. Thornwell near the birth of the Southern church,” and

Whereas, the Scriptures of the Old Testament in 1 Kings 12:1-5, 18, which record King Rehoboam’s fateful rejecting and discarding of the wisdom of “the elders (who) had stood before his father Solomon while he still lived” and how Rehoboam instead consulted and followed only the advice of “the young men who had grown up with him, who stood before him,” clearly demonstrate the tragic results of rejecting and discarding the wisdom of old and wise elders, and

Whereas, the Scriptures of the New Testament in 1 Timothy 3:1-7, especially require that the elder be a man who “is not a novice” plainly declaring the desirability and advantage of those elders who are men not only of demonstrable godliness but of rich and lengthy experience in walking with the Lord, that they may wisely serve in the courts of the church, and

Whereas, ruling elders have played an integral part in the organization and growth of the Presbyterian Church in America, serving diligently in all courts of the church, and

Whereas, the parity of the eldership is compromised when the composition of church courts contain substantially more teaching elder commissioners than ruling elder commissioners, and

Whereas, in 1999, 2873 teaching elders held membership in the 58 presbyteries numbering 1206 churches (Minutes, 28th General Assembly), and

Whereas, in presbytery meetings, 2873 teaching elders might dominate the business of the church brought from the Sessions of the 1206 churches, and could exert clergical [sic] influence at the presbytery court level that may disregard the needs of grass roots Session courts, and

Whereas, Proverbs 11:14 and 24:6 make it clear that the more counsel (wisdom) brought to the issues before the church, the greater the probability the building of the kingdom will be achieved, and

<a id="ga29-p220"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=222 printed_page=220 -->

Whereas, the more participation by ruling elders at the lower court levels (Session and Presbytery), the greater will be the probability that there will be interest in and counsel provided by ruling elders on the issues and problems that confront the churches at the grass roots level of the denomination, and

Whereas, ruling elders serve the church generally, when called thereunto (BCO 8-3; Acts 20:28f), as well as the local congregation, and

Whereas, the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America early recognized the need for more ruling elder involvement and participation in said Court by amending the Book of Church Order so as to increase the number of ruling elder commissioners from each church from one (1) to two (2), with additional commissioners for larger congregations; and

Whereas, there is a similar need for more ruling elder involvement and participation in the church presbytery court,

Now therefore Be it Resolved that the undersigned Session overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to amend to Book of Church Order, 13-1, to increase the number of ruling elder commissioners to presbytery meetings from each church from one (1) to two (2), with additional commissioners for larger congregations, and

Be It Further Resolved, that the Book of Church Order 13-1 be amended by deleting the present paragraphs and substituting the following, to wit:

The Presbytery consists of all the teaching elders and churches within its bounds that have been accepted by the Presbytery. When the Presbytery meets as a court it shall comprise all teaching elders and ruling elders as elected by their Session. Each congregation is entitled to two (2) ruling elder representatives for the first 350 communing members or fraction thereof, and one additional ruling elder for each additional 500 communing members or fraction thereof.

Adopted by the Session at a stated meeting on the 12th day of November, 2000.

Attested by: /s/ Charlie H. Probst, Clerk of Session of Ellisville Presbyterian Church

This overture was submitted at the 90th Stated Meeting of Grace Presbytery on January 9, 2001. Grace Presbytery answered the overture in the negative.

Attested by: /s/ Charlie H. Probst, Clerk of Session
Ellisville Presbyterian Church

16. That Overture 24 from Grace Presbytery (“Set Conditions for Use of Executive Session”) be answered in the negative.

<a id="ga29-p223"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=223 printed_page=null -->

Grounds: It is imprudent to restrict the use of executive session to the cases enumerated in this overture.
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 24 From Grace Presbytery

(to B&amp;O)
"Set Conditions for Use of Executive Session"

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America was founded as a “grass roots” denomination; and

Whereas, one of the manifestations of the “grass roots” concept is the fact that the meetings of the committees of the church were assumed to be open to all members of the church (M13GA, p. 90); and

Whereas, this openness, which has been mandated by the General Assembly, is consonant with the fact that in the PCA we operate with subservient “committees” rather than semi-autonomous “boards”; and

Whereas, the election of men to the various committees is an act by which the Assembly selects representatives; and

Whereas, the meetings of church committees ought to be open to all the members of the denomination with the possible exception of certain highly sensitive matters, the knowledge of which being widespread might be detrimental; and

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church (USA) has adopted strict guidelines for when its boards may go into executive session, so as to guarantee openness in the deliberations; and

Whereas, we in the PCA should not be less open and honest than our liberal counterpart; and

Whereas, this is especially the case since we self-professedly operate with committees rather than boards, as is done in the PC(USA); and

Whereas, we want to preserve this openness which was set forth as the ideal in the PCA from early in its existence:

Now Therefore, Be It Resolved, that Grace Presbytery hereby overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to take the following actions:

1. to reaffirm that all General Assembly committees, both permanent and ad-interim, committees of commissioners and agencies will operate in open session.

2. to require that said bodies may go into executive session only for the following reasons: property negotiations, discussion of active or pending criminal matters or civil litigation, discussion of personnel performance, personal safety, or approval of reports if required to be done in executive session.

3. to declare that whenever a committee or agency enters executive session, only the elected members and ex-officio members of that committee or agency may remain in the room, the only exception to this policy being that a person may be invited to stay if he has specific information, is otherwise involved in the matter under

<a id="ga29-p222"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=224 printed_page=222 -->

consideration, or not otherwise available to the committee or agency.

4. To determine that any decisions made in executive session must be reported in public and access to said decisions guaranteed to any member of the church, and that all members are guaranteed access to the minutes and official records of all committees and agencies.

5. To amend the Rules for Assembly Operation by adding a new section (4-19) to read as follows:

All General Assembly committees, both permanent and ad-interim, committees of commissioners, and agencies will operate in open session. Said bodies may go into executive session only for the following reasons: property negotiations, discussion of active or pending criminal matters or civil litigation, discussion of personnel performance, personal safety, or approval of reports if required to be done in executive session. Whenever a committee or agency enters executive session, only the elected members or ex-officio members of the committee or agency may remain in the room, the only exception to this policy being that a person may be invited to stay if he has specific information, is otherwise involved in the matter under consideration, or not otherwise available to the committee or agency.

Adopted by the 96th Stated Meeting of Grace Presbytery on the 11th day of January, 2001. Attested by: /s/ Eugene C. Case, Stated Clerk

17. That Overture 25 from Grace Presbytery (“Forbid Women to Preach or Teach in Corporate Worship”) be answered in the negative.

Grounds: Our constitution addresses the issues sufficiently (BCO 7-2), and we do not believe our Assembly should by majority vote make a statement defining constitutional principles. Adopted

##### OVERTURE 25 From Grace Presbytery (to B&amp;O &amp; CCB) “Forbid Women to Preach or Teach in Corporate Worship”

Whereas, the Word of God and the Confessional Standards of the Presbyterian Church in America declare without ambiguity that a woman shall not teach or exercise authority over a man; and

Whereas, this prohibition is proclaimed as a result of the creation ordinance and not for the reason of first century cultural expediency; and

Whereas, no ecclesiastical body of the Presbyterian Church in America (be it congregation, session, presbytery, or assembly) may, under any assumed authority, set aside the clear teaching of God’s Word or the Westminster Standards; and

<a id="ga29-p223"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=225 printed_page=223 -->

Whereas, a contrary view to both Scripture and the Standards is being taught and adopted, specifically, that session may allow the instruction of men by women in ecclesiastical assembly under the “authority and oversight” of the elders;

Therefore the Presbytery of Grace, meeting this 12th day of September, 2000, overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America:

1. to affirm that a woman may not preach or teach in the corporate worship of any church or in the corporate worship conducted by any church court or by any committee, board, and/or agency of a church court; and
2. to reaffirm that the Presbyterian Church in America believes that the Scripture teaches that the offices of Elder and Deacon are open to men only.

Adopted at the 98th Stated Meeting of Grace Presbytery on the 12th day of September, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Eugene C. Case, Stated Clerk

18. That Overture 27 from the Presbytery of Philadelphia (“Amend BCO 12-5e to Prohibit Women from Preaching”) be answered in the negative.

Grounds: Our constitution addresses the issues sufficiently.

Adopted

## Minority Report (Overture 27)

We the undersigned respectfully disagree with the majority report of the Bills and Overture Committee on the matter of Overture 27 (“Amend BCO 12-5e to Prohibit Women from Preaching”) and offer as a substitute the following:

That Overture 27 be answered in the affirmative as amended:

Adopted as amended

That the General Assembly amend the BCO, par. 12-5.e

BCO 12-5.e

To exercise in accordance with the Directory for Worship, authority over the time and place of the preaching of the Word and the administration of the Sacraments, over all other religious services, over the music in the services, and over the uses to which the church building and associated properties may be put; to take the oversight of the singing in the public worship of God; to ensure that the Word of God is preached only by such men as are sufficiently qualified (BCO 4-4, 53-2, 1 Timothy 2:11-12); to assemble the people for worship when there is no minister; to determine the best measures for promoting the spiritual interests of the church and congregation;

<a id="ga29-p224"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=226 printed_page=224 -->

[the portion amended by addition is in bold.]

## GROUNDS:

1. It is absolutely clear that Scripture prohibits women preaching;
2. Some within the PCA have questioned whether our standards prohibit women from preaching.
3. This amendment places responsibility for ensuring that women do not preach at the feet of the session where it belongs; and
4. The amendment properly belongs in the section of the BCO which discusses the duties of the session.

Signed,

RE Andrew J. Webb
RE M. Dale Peacock
TE Carl W. Bogue
RE Fredrick T. Greco
RE Mark A. Buckner
TE Brent Bradley
TE Byron Snapp
TE Paul Honomichl
TE Don Codling
RE Dennis W. Baker
TE Jerry I. Maguire

##### OVERTURE 27 From the Presbytery of Philadelphia (to CCB &amp; B&amp;O) "Amend BCO 12-5e to Prohibit Women from Preaching"

Whereas, there are some among us who believe that Scripture explicitly prohibits women from teaching and having authority over men in the church (1 Cor. 14:34, 35; 1 Tim. 2:11, 12); and

Whereas, women may encourage each other and the children under them (Titus 2:3-5); and

Whereas, our Confession explicitly and implicitly envisioned the ordinance of preaching and teaching, or "the ministry of the Word", to be limited to ordained male ministers of the Word (WCF 27.3, 4 [Scripture proofs]; LC 54 [Scripture proofs], 156, 158, 159, 169, cf. 176); and

Whereas, the BCO teaches that candidates for the ministry of the Word (18-1), those licensed to the ministry of the Word (19-1), and interns who are to be ordained to the ministry of the Word (19-7) are to be males; and

Whereas, BCO 19-1 teaches that "no man" can preach without licensure in the presbytery "where he will preach"; and

Whereas, there are some who believe that there is considerable confusion within our church as to the proper and biblical role of women in regards to the ordinance of preaching and teaching during stated worship services; and

Whereas, there are some who believe that Paul's prohibition was primarily related to the activity of teaching itself and not to the office of teaching

<a id="ga29-p225"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=227 printed_page=225 -->

(though it is necessarily implied, "good and necessary consequence", WCF 1:vi); and

Whereas, it is impossible for women to remain silent (1 Cor. 14:34; 1 Tim. 2:12) while teaching and preaching to men during stated worship services; and

Whereas, it is impossible for females to instruct men and women, to exhort from Scripture before men, or to teach or explain passages of the Bible from the pulpit during ordinary stated worship services before men and still fulfill the Biblical command of 1 Tim. 2:12 which states, "But I do not allow a woman to teach or exercise authority over a man, but to remain quiet."; and

Whereas, our denomination has historically seen clearly on the issue of the role of women in the public ministry of the Word;

Therefore Be It Resolved, that the General Assembly amend the BCO, par. 12-5.e d to read:

To call congregational meetings when necessary; to establish and control Sunday schools and Bible classes with special reference to the children of the church; to establish and control all special groups in the church such as Men in the Church, Women in the Church and special Bible Study groups; to encourage the women of the church to hold meetings and give instruction, appropriate to their calling (e.g. Titus 2:3-5), while prohibiting women from expounding or preaching the Word of God as an ordinance of public worship (cf. BCO 4-4; 53-2), as this is clearly forbidden in Scripture (1 Tim. 2:11-12); to promote World missions; to promote obedience to the Great Commission in its totality at home and abroad; to order collections for pious uses;

To exercise in accordance with the Directory for Worship, authority over the time and place of the preaching of the Word and the administration of the Sacraments, over all other religious services, over the music in the services, and over the uses to which the church building and associated properties may be put; to take the oversight of the singing in the public worship of God; to ensure that the Word of God is preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office (cf. WLC 158) and in accordance with this to prohibit women from preaching the Word of God as part of the corporate worship of the church (cf. BCO 4-4; 53-2), as this is forbidden in Scripture (1 Tim. 2:11-12); to assemble the people for worship when there is no minister; to determine the best measures for promoting the spiritual interests of the church and congregation;

Adopted at the March 10, 2001, stated meeting of Philadelphia Presbytery.

Attested by: /s/ Frank D. Moser, Stated Clerk

<a id="ga29-p226"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=228 printed_page=226 -->

19. That Overture 28 from the Presbytery of Philadelphia (“Pray for Religious Liberty”) be answered in the affirmative, as amended.

Grounds for changes: The phrase “a summary of” has been added to reduce mailing costs. The overture has been edited to make the language applicable to all members of the PCA regardless of their national origin.

Adopted as amended

##### OVERTURE 28 From the Presbytery of Philadelphia

“Pray for Religious Liberty” (to B&amp;O)

Whereas, the PCA is a Protestant and Reformed denomination serving our God especially in the United States of America, which celebrates its 225th anniversary of Independence this July 4th, 2001, whose original states declared themselves to be committed to religious liberty and the Protestant faith as seen in the following citations from the Journal of the First Continental Congress of 1774:

THAT it is an indispensable duty which we owe to God, our country, ourselves and posterity, by all lawful ways and means in our power to maintain, defend and preserve these civil and religious rights and liberties for which many of our fathers fought, bled and died, and to hand them down entire to future generations.

THAT the late act of Parliament for establishing the Roman Catholic religion and the French laws in that extensive country now called Quebec, is dangerous in an extreme degree to the Protestant religion and to the civil rights and liberties of all America; and therefore as men and protestant Christians, we are indispensably obliged to take all proper measures for our security...;

And in prosecution of the same system, several late, cruel and oppressive Acts have been passed respecting the town of Boston and the Massachusetts-Bay and also an Act for extending the province of Quebec, so as to border on the western frontiers of these Colonies, establishing an arbitrary government therein, and discouraging the settlement of British subjects in that wide extended country; thus by the influence of civil principles and ancient prejudices to dispose the inhabitants to act with hostility against the free Protestant Colonies, whenever a wicked Ministry shall chose so to direct them.

THAT we think the Legislature of Great Britain is not authorized by the condition to establish a religion fraught with sanguinary and impious tenets, or, to erect an arbitrary form of government in any quarter of the globe. These rights we, as well as you, deem sacred. And yet sacred as they are, they have, with many others, been repeatedly and flagrantly violated...

<a id="ga29-p227"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=229 printed_page=227 -->

As the conclusion of the late war—a war rendered glorious by the abilities and integrity of a Minister, to whose efforts the British empire owes its safety and its fame: At the conclusion of this war, which was succeeded by an inglorious peace, formed under the auspices of a Minister of principles, and of a family unfriendly to the protestant cause, and inimical to liberty---We say at this period, and under the influence of that man, a plan for enslaving your fellow subjects in America was concerted, and has ever since been pertinaciously carrying into execution.

And by another Act the dominion of Canada is to be so extended, modeled, and governed, as that by being disunited from us, detached from our interests, by civil as well as religious prejudices, that by their numbers daily swelling with Catholic emigrants from Europe, and by their devotion to Administration, so friendly to their religion, they might become formidable to us, and on occasion, to fit instruments in the hands of power, to reduce the ancient free Protestant Colonies to the same fate of slavery with themselves.

The people of England will soon have an opportunity of declaring their sentiments concerning our cause. In their piety, generosity, and good sense, we repose high confidence; and cannot, upon a review of past events, be persuaded, that they, the defenders of true religion, and the assertors of the rights of mankind, will take part against their affectionate protestant brethren in the colonies, in favour of our open and their own secret enemies, whose intrigues, for several years past, have been wholly exercised in sapping the foundations of civil and religious liberty.

Resolved, THAT this Congress in their own names and in behalf of all those, whom they represent, do present their most grateful acknowledgments to those truly noble, honourable and patriotic advocates of civil and religious liberty, who have so generously and powerfully, though unsuccessfully espoused and defended the cause of America, both in and out of Parliament.

...and a fourth for extending the limits of Quebec, abolishing the English and restoring the French laws, whereby great numbers of British freemen are subjected to the latter, and establishing an absolute Government and the Roman Catholic Religion throughout those vast regions, that border on the westerly and northerly boundaries of the free, protestant English settlements...

And whereas, our confession, *The Westminster Confession of Faith*, declares its commitment to the idea of the liberty of the conscience, an idea developed especially by Puritans such as William Perkins and Non-conformists such as John Owen, in Chapter XX.2,

“God alone is Lord of the conscience, and hath left it free from the doctrines and commandments of men,

<a id="ga29-p228"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=230 printed_page=228 -->

which are, in anything, contrary to His Word; or beside it, if matters of faith, or worship. So that, to believe such doctrines, or to obey such commands, out of conscience, is to betray true liberty of conscience: and the requiring of an implicit faith, and an absolute and blind obedience, is to destroy liberty of conscience, and reason also.”

And whereas, our Confession affirms the freedom of religion in XXIII. 3, Civil magistrates may not assume to themselves the administration of the Word and Sacraments; or the power of the keys of the kingdom of heaven; or, in the least, interfere in matters of faith. Yet, as nursing fathers, it is the duty of civil magistrates to protect the Church of our common Lord, without giving the preference to any denomination of Christians above the rest, in such a manner that all ecclesiastical persons whatever shall enjoy the full, free, and unquestioned liberty of discharging every part of their sacred functions, without violence or danger. And, as Jesus Christ hath appointed a regular government and discipline in his Church, no law of any commonwealth should interfere with, let, or hinder, the due exercise thereof, among the voluntary members of any denomination of Christians, according to their own profession and belief. It is the duty of civil magistrates to protect the person and good name of all their people, in such an effectual manner as that no person be suffered, either upon pretense of religion or of infidelity, to offer any indignity, violence, abuse, or injury to any other person whatsoever: and to take order, that all religious and ecclesiastical assemblies be held without molestation or disturbance.

And whereas this year A.D. 2001 is the 300th anniversary of religious liberty in America as provided by William Penn’s Charter of Privileges written on October 28, 1701, that declares:

I the said William Penn doe Declare Grant and Confirme unto all the Freemen Planters and Adventurers and other Inhabitants of and in the said Province and Territories thereunto Annexed for ever first. Because noe people can be truly happy though under the Greatest Enjoyments of Civil Liberties if Abridged of the Freedom of their Consciences as to their Religious Profession and worship. And Almighty God being the only Lord of Conscience Father of Lights and Spirits and the Author as well as Object of all divine knowledge Faith and Worship who only {[can]} Enlighten the mind and persuade and Convince the understandings of people. I doe hereby Grant and Declare that noe person or persons Inhabiting in this Province or Territories who shall Confesse and Acknowledge one Almighty God the Creator upholder and Ruler of the world and professe him or themselves Obliged to live quietly under the Civil Government shall be in any case molested or prejudiced in his or their person or Estate because of his or their

<a id="ga29-p229"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=231 printed_page=229 -->

Conscientious persuasion or practice nor be compelled to frequent or mention any Religious Worship place or Ministry contrary to his or their mind or doe or suffer any other act or thing contrary to their Religious persuasion. And that all persons who also professe to believe in Jesus Christ the Savior of the world shall be capable (notwithstanding their other persuasions and practices in point of Conscience and Religion) to Serve this Government in any capacity both Legislatively and Executively he or they Solemnly promising when lawfully required Allegience to the King as Soveraigne and fidelity to the Proprietary and Governour And taking the Attests as now Establish by the law made at Newcastle in the year One thousand Seven hundred Intituled an Act directing the Attests of Severall Officers and Ministers as now amended and Confirmed his present Assembly.

And noe Act Law or Ordinance whatsoever shall at any time hereafter be made or done to Alter Change or Diminish the forme or Effect of this Charter or of any part or Clause therein Contrary to the True intent and meaning thereof without the Consent of the Governour for the [time being and] six parts of Seven of the Assembly [mett] But because the happiness of Mankind Descends So much upon the Enjoying of Libertie of their Consciences as aforesaid I Doe hereby Solemnly Declare Promise and Grant for me my heires and Assignees that the first Article of this Charter Relateing to Liberty of Conscience and every part and Clause therein according to the True Intent and meaning thereof shall be kept and remaine without any Alteration Inviolably for ever.

And whereas William Penn’s conceptions of religious liberty were informed by his studies under reformed theologians such as Non-conformist John Owen and Huguenot scholar Moses Amyrault, and because Penn’s broadly conceived provision of religious liberty had been anticipated and attempted on a much lesser scale by Roger Williams’ Providence Plantation in Rhode Island in the years 1636 and following, who also had been deeply influenced by the Puritan concept of the liberty of conscience;

And whereas this provision of religious liberty enabled our Presbyterian heritage to flourish in Pennsylvania and ultimately throughout America as this right of religious liberty was asserted and guaranteed by the First Amendment of our the United States Constitution, which constitution was written in Philadelphia, the first capitol city of the United States of America; and which Amendment was ratified 90 years after Penn’s Charter on December 15, 1791, declaring,

Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press, or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the Government for a redress of grievances.

<a id="ga29-p230"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=232 printed_page=230 -->

And whereas this year also marks the 250th anniversary of the ordering of the Liberty Bell on Nov. 1, 1751, which carries the Scriptural Old Testament Jubilee year text from Leviticus 25:10 on its crown which says, “Proclaim Liberty throughout all the land unto all the inhabitants thereof.”

And whereas this Bell, ordered fifty years after Penn’s Charter, celebrating Penn’s grant of religious liberty in his proprietary colony, has become the symbol of not only religious liberty but also of civil liberty, inasmuch as it rang out the public news of American Independence on July 8th, 1776; and was hidden for safe-keeping in the basement of Zion Reformed Church in Allentown, PA, during the British occupation of Philadelphia;

And whereas upon its cracking and becoming silent around 1846, this Bell first became known as “The Liberty Bell” and also became the visible and mobile symbol of the abolitionist movement that furthered the cause of ending human slavery in the United States of America; and after the Civil War was the symbol of reconciliation for a reunited country as the wounds of war were healed by its symbolic travels across our nation;

And whereas over half of the world’s inhabitants still do not have these most precious and basic freedoms enjoyed by Americans and the most members of the Presbyterian Church in America on a daily basis, namely, the liberty of conscience and the liberty to worship God according to the dictates of one’s own conscience;

Therefore be it resolved that the Philadelphia Presbytery humbly overtures the Twenty-Ninth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America, assembled in Dallas Texas in the 300th year of religious liberty in America, to once again “Proclaim Liberty Throughout All the Land Unto All The Inhabitants Thereof” by adopting the following recommendations:

1. To have a brief season of prayer and thanksgiving at our 29th General Assembly for our the freedom as a people of Americans, and for our the religious freedom as a “denomination of Christians” enjoyed by our denomination in the United States of America.

1. To request the Stated Clerk to include a summary of this overture in his letter to the churches and sessions of the PCA wherein he reports on the actions of the 29th General Assembly.

2. To request all the churches and agencies of the PCA to have a special season of prayer and worship with thanksgiving in their churches and institutions either around July 4th of this year, or around October 28th, or early in November (when prayers are often offered for the persecuted church worldwide) in order to thank God for His gracious gift of religious liberty in our American nation the United States of America and to intercede

<a id="ga29-p231"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=233 printed_page=231 -->

for the persecuted Church worldwide that continues to face religious persecution often resulting in the loss of property and personal civil liberty, including even torture and martyrdom due to their faithful conscientious convictions for Christ and His Word.

3. To inform our related churches and agencies through the NAE, WRF, and NAPARC of this call for worship, thanksgiving and intercessory prayer, encouraging them to join with us in their own communions and communities in an appropriate season of prayer and thanksgiving.

Adopted at the March 10, 2001, stated meeting of Philadelphia
Presbytery. Attested by: /s/ Frank D. Moser, Stated Clerk

20. That Overture 31 from Mid-America Presbytery (“Declare Denominational Employees Not Eligible For Membership on SJC”) be answered in the negative.

Grounds: Permanent employees of the denomination may bring useful gifts and perspectives to SJC matters. Professional and personal integrity should lead men to recuse themselves appropriately from deliberations and votes on various matters. It is unfair to exclude gifted men from serving simply because they hold specific professional positions. Adopted

##### OVERTURE 31 From Mid-America Presbytery (to B&amp;O)

“Declare Denominational Employees Not Eligible For Membership On SJC”

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America has prided itself on being a "grass roots" denomination; and

Whereas, the PCA has put this principle into effect with respect to the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) by prohibiting anyone from serving on the SJC and a Permanent Committee of the General Assembly simultaneously (BCO 15-4); and

Whereas, the same principle is seen to be at work in that "Employees of the Assembly's committees or agencies are not eligible for office on an Assembly's committee or agency which administers matters directly related to their area of employment" (RAO 4-7); and

Whereas, the work of the SJC covers the denomination as a whole; and

Whereas, at present, four of the twenty-four members of the SJC are denominational employees; and

Whereas, it seems prudent that denominational employees should not serve simultaneously on the SJC;

Now Therefore, Be It Resolved, that Mid-America Presbytery hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly to declare that no denominational employee is

<a id="ga29-p232"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=234 printed_page=232 -->

eligible to serve on the SJC, excepting those already elected before General Assembly’s ruling on this overture;

**And Be It Further Resolved** that Mid-America Presbytery hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly to amend the Rules for Assembly Operations by adding to RAO 15-1 a sentence, immediately after the first sentence, as follows:

&gt; "No one who is employed by a denominational committee or agency may serve on the Standing Judicial Commission."

Adopted at the 40th Stated Meeting of Mid-America Presbytery, on April 20, 2001.
Attested by: /s/ John Owen Butler, Stated Clerk

21. That **Overture 33** from TE Dan Bartel of Central Carolina Presbytery (“Call for PCA to Repent for Abandonment of Covenantal Patriarchy”) be answered in the negative.

**Grounds:**

1) While affirming biblical concepts of the covenant and of the family, we do not agree with all the reasonings of and definitions in this overture.

2) No evidence is presented in the overture to support the accusation against the PCA.

Adopted

##### OVERTURE 33 From TE Dan Bartel of Central Carolina Presbytery

“Call for PCA to Repent for Abandonment of Covenantal Patriarchy”

Whereas, Philosophy is concerned with “the correction and regulation of manners”³ which is reflected in a community’s culture⁴ which is either God-centered,⁵ that is based on biblical philosophy; or man-centered⁶ also known as Greek Philosophy or Pagan Culture;⁷ and

³ St.. Augustine, *The Confessions, The City of God, On Christian Doctrine*, Great Books of the Western World, ed. Whereas, While Plato entertained thoughts of one true God⁸ he continued to advocate that sacred rites be performed in honor of many gods.⁹ His philosophy [culture], summarized in his work, *The Republic*, exalts itself against the truth of God’s Word;¹⁰ by advancing a philosophy of education¹¹, women in combat¹², and parenting;¹³ and

Whereas, Alexander the Great, a student of Plato through his tutor, Aristotle, imposed Greek philosophy and culture throughout the Greek Empire.¹⁴

⁶ Plato, *The Republic*, translated by A.D. Lindsay (Rutland, VT: Charles E. Tuttle Co., 1996), 59. The existence and character of the Greek Gods were deduced from human reason. “If any one makes a poem of the . . . history of the children of Pelops, or the Trojan war, or any similar story, either he must not be allowed to say that these were the deeds of God, or if he says that, he must find some such explanation as we are now looking for. He must say that God did what was just and good, and the sufferers were benefitted by punishment. **We must not allow** (emphasis added) the poet to say that those who were punished were miserable because they needed punishment, and were benefitted by being punished at God’s hand.” 2:380) The knowledge of God and the existence of God, according to Greek Philosophy, are both, therefore, constructs of the mind of men. Samuel Enoch Stumpf, *Socrates to Sartre: A History of Philosophy* (St. Louis, MO: McGraw-Hill Book Company, 1975), “[For Socrates (Plato’s teacher and a possible contemporary of Queen Esther), the foundation of knowledge rested] not in the facts of the world outside of man but within man.” (39)
