Exception: September 25, 1999: No response to 1995 Minutes.

Response: Regarding the Minutes for May 5, 2000, inasmuch as no response was offered to an exception taken to minutes of May, 1995, in which a Commission is established by the Presbytery to discipline a church member without describing the reasons (BCO 11-4, 31-1, 13-9), the Pacific Presbytery offers its apology to the General Assembly for the oversight in our minutes of May 20, 1995, and for our failure to respond to the General Assembly's exception. Although it is difficult at present to reconstruct the reasons for which a Commission was formed to discipline a church member, we do commit ourselves to give greater diligence in the future both to the proper form of our minutes and to timely responses to the General Assembly.

Exception: 1997 Minutes: Although 1997 minutes were submitted, the minutes did not contain a copy of the Directory or Standing Rules.

Response: Regarding an Exception of Substance to the 1997 minutes in that they did not contain a copy of the Directory or the Standing Rules, the Pacific Presbytery offers its apology to the General Assembly for our oversight in failing submitting our minutes for review along with the standing rules and directories of Presbytery. Due to a lack of adequate

<a id="ga29-p349"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=351 printed_page=349 -->

record keeping during that part of our history, these documents would be difficult to reconstruct at this point. However, we do commit ourselves to give greater diligence in the future both to the proper form of our minutes and to timely responses to the General Assembly.

Adopted

53. that the minutes of southern florida Presbytery:
a. Be approved without exception: January 18, 2000, April 18, 2000, July
18, 2000, October 17, 2000;

- **b.** be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** be approved with exceptions of substance: None.

<a id="ga29-p352"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=354 printed_page=352 -->

d. That the following responses to the exceptions of the 28th be found unsatisfactory with the reminder that the Presbytery will need to respond to the 30th General Assembly:

**Rationale with regard to the first exception:** While recognizing Presbytery’s care in examination, the fact remains that the man in question did include in his paper the statement, “I believe that God gives “extraordinary” gifts (miracles, healing, and tongues)...”

The man does say that such gifts must be used “according to the teaching of Scripture, and under the sovereignty of God”; and thus they “are not superior to other gifts” and “are to result in growth and unity in the church.”

The man’s statement, however, does not demonstrate how the negations of WCF I-1 (“…those former ways of God’s revealing His will unto His people being now ceased”), WCF 18-3 (“…he may, without extraordinary revelation in the right use with human means, attain there unto”) WLC #80 (“may, without extraordinary revelation…”), and BCO 7-1 (Such officers and gifts related to new revelation have no successors since God completed His revelation at the conclusion of the Apostolic Age.”) are not violated. Similarly, Presbytery’s action in approving the man’s statement does not contain any indication of how the statement, which appears to be inconsistent with our documents, does not further allow for continuing revelation or the use of extraordinary gifts as a means of assurance. (cf. Landrum vs. Mississippi Valley Presbytery, 1998 and other previous judicial cases on this matter.)

**Exception:** January 19, 1999 (16.h): Theological paper approved in which candidate stated “I believe that God gives extraordinary gifts (miracles, healing, and tongues)”. WCF 18-3; WLC #80; BCO 7-1.

**Response:** Presbytery respectfully disagrees with the exception. The candidate was thoroughly examined by our Credentials Committee and on the floor of Presbytery. Both the Committee and Presbytery concluded that the views of the candidate were in complete conformity with the Constitution of the PCA. Presbytery is puzzled by the citations that were listed as justification for the exception: WCF 18 is of Assurance of Grace and Salvation and section 3 elaborates on assurance. WLC #80 asks, “Can true believers be infallibly assured that they are in the estate of grace, and that they shall persevere therein unto salvation.” BCO 7-1 ties extraordinary gifts to new revelation. No where in his Theological paper did the candidate make any mention of new or continuing revelation or challenge or question the PCA position on assurance of salvation. The exception should read in the full context in its section of “Views on Charismatic Movement.”

**Rationale with regard to the second and third exceptions:** While Presbytery is correct with regard to the second sentence of each exception (“no extraordinary clause stated as rational”), the response to

<a id="ga29-p353"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=355 printed_page=353 -->

the first sentence of each (“BCO 13-6 requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas”) is not acceptable. When a man is received from outside the PCA BCO 13-6 requires more elements to the examination than are listed for these men. BCO 13-6 specifies that for men from other denominations all areas of examinations specified in BCO 21-4 were (and are) required.

Exception: July 20, 1999: BCO 13-6 requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas. No extraordinary clause stated as rationale.

Response: Presbytery respectfully disagrees with both exceptions. The men transferring were given a knowledge examination and were asked the questions put to candidates at their ordination which they answered in the affirmative (BCO 13-6). The examination followed the requirements of D-21, Check List For Reception of a Minister From Another Denomination (copy enclosed). This Check List is from the “Handbook For Presbytery Clerks – July 1999” prepared by the Office of the GA Stated Clerk. On page one of examiner David M. Smith’s worksheet in answer to Question he wrote, “Only partial – not complete” in answer to Question he wrote, Only partial – not complete.”

Exception: October 19, 1999 (11.f): BCO 13-6 requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas. No extraordinary clause found.

Response: See above response.

Adopted

58. That the Minutes of Tennessee Valley Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: None.
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 8, 2000, February 17, 2000, April 15, 2000, July 11, 2000, August 10, 2000, October 10, 2000;
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance: None.
- **d.** That the following responses to the 28th GA exceptions be found satisfactory:

Exception: January 9, 1999 (Minister and His Work Committee): Presbytery granted a TE permission to continue laboring out of bounds as a pastor of an independent reformed church in Michigan. 1) The record doesn't include a call or Presbytery's approval of a call, 2) There is no assurance that the TE will be allowed to teach and preach the whole council of God's word, and 3) There is no record that the TE had contacted Great Lakes Presbytery for approval to work within the bounds of that Presbytery. BCO 20-11 &amp; 21-1.

Response: In January of 1999 the Tennessee Valley Presbytery (TVP) improperly granted TE Steve Simmons permission to labor out of bound of the TVP because of his call to serve a non-PCA congregation in Michigan.

The TVP should have corresponded with the Great Lakes Presbytery concerning TE Simmons' pending move. Furthermore, they should have encouraged TE Simmons to transfer his credentials to the Great Lakes Presbytery that he might receive their permission to labor out of bounds within the Presbytery.

The TVP regrets its error in this matter.

In the Lord's gracious providence, TE Simmons has, as of October 2000, been received as a member of the Great Lakes Presbytery, thus correcting the TVP's error.

Thank you for calling our attention to this error.

Adopted

<a id="ga29-p355"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=357 printed_page=355 -->

IV. general recommendations
A. That the 29th General Assembly instruct the Presbyteries to give diligence in the
following matters:
1. Presbyteries are encouraged to adopt the RAO guidelines for the
examination and approval of sessional records. (RAO 14)
2. record of examination of candidates should list specific parts of
examinations and trials for licensure, candidacy, internship, transfer, and
ordination. (RAO 14-3. e. 5)
3. Submitted attachments for Presbytery minutes should be clearly labeled.
4. the establishment of commissions and their quorums should be recorded
and their reports included. (RAO 14-3. e. 4.)
5. A commission is only authorized to deliberate upon and conclude the
business referred to it. each commissions hall keep a full record of its
proceedings, which shall be submitted to the court appointing it.
6. responses toga exceptions of substance should be adopted by
Presbytery action and included in its minutes. The Stated Clerk shall
send a letter to the GA stating Presbytery’s response to the Assembly
concerning disposition of any exceptions of substance taken by the past
Assembly. Responses should be couched in temperate and respectful
language. (RAO 14-4. c. 3.)
7. Committees overseeing interns should report on every intern at each
stated meeting of Presbytery. In addition, an annual report by all interns
is required and the receipt thereof recorded in the minutes. (BCO 19-12)

<a id="ga29-p357"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=359 printed_page=357 -->

8. Teaching elders laboring out of bounds shall make a report to Presbytery at least once each year, and the receipt thereof recorded in the minutes. (BCO 8-4)

9. Presbytery should clearly state the reasons for invoking the extraordinary clause in waiving portions of an exam and/or trial. (BCO 18-2, 19-2, 19-16, 21-4)

Adopted

B. That the 29th General Assembly, acknowledging the progress of our fathers and brothers in the Korean Language Presbyteries in their task of conforming their record keeping to meet General Assembly Committee review standards, as an expression of brotherly love and encouragement, allow members of the CRPR to pursue mentoring relationships with the stated clerk of each Korean Language Presbyteries for assisting in their progress.

Adopted

C. That Personal Resolution #2 from TE Bill Leuzinger be answered by the following statement:

“The Committee on Review of Presbytery Records has cited the correspondence included in 08:37 of the 5/19/00 minutes as an exception of form to be referred directly to the Stated Clerk of North Florida Presbytery, (RAO 14-7.c.2); and that in light of the exception of form, the 29th General Assembly requests that the North Florida Presbytery consider expunging its records and issuing an apology.”

Adopted as amended

Whereas, the Presbytery of North Florida did, at its May 19, 2000, Stated Meeting, spread upon its minutes an email from one of its members; and

Whereas, that email attacked the character of a minister who is a member in good standing of Westminster Presbytery; and

Whereas, among the things said in that email were strong suggestions that the minister under attack lacked integrity; and

Whereas, at the end of that email, the author quoted Titus 3:10, and stated that he would have no further contact with the minister until he “repented”; and

Whereas, the Presbytery of North Florida has declined to honor the minister’s request for an official extract from the minutes; and

Whereas, the Presbytery of North Florida did not grant the minister’s request that the action of recording the email be rescinded and the document expunged; and

Whereas, it is unseemly and unrighteous for a church court thus to allow the character of a minister to be attacked; and

Whereas, for a church court to spread such a malicious document on its minutes is itself a violation of the ninth commandment; and

Whereas, the General Assembly has already ruled on a similar issue, in that the high court took exception to the minutes of Mid-America Presbytery (“Records indicate a TE was denied constitutional protection in that the charge against him was printed in the minutes without being received by

<a id="ga29-p358"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=360 printed_page=358 -->

presbytery according to BCO 32-3 and was distributed publicly for a period of six months. “Furthermore steps of BCO 32-3 were not followed: the charges were not expunged from minutes ..., and the accusers were not informed how they could pursue judicial process...” [M16GA (1988), p.234];

Now Therefore, Be It Resolved, that [Westminster Presbytery hereby overtures] the General Assembly [to] take exception to the minutes of the Presbytery of North Florida, in accord with BCO 40; and to instruct the Presbytery of North Florida to expunge the offending document from its minutes and to issue an apology to the minister who was attacked.

## 29-65 Report of the Committee on Thanks

RE Dwight Allen led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report, which was adopted.

## COMMITTEE ON THANKS
## 29TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
## PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

EBENEZER! Hitherto has our gracious and Sovereign God led us to this 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America, in a nation at peace, a church that has grown in the past year, even as the Lord has added to the church such as He has called to Salvation.

As we arrived we were given a warm Texas welcome by our hosts, North Texas Presbytery, including the officers and membership of the Women in the Church, who have spared no pains to care for every need and catered to every comfort. Likewise, the management and staff of the Adam’s Mark Hotel, Dallas, Texas, have cheerfully served us, with the management urging this Assembly to return here for future Assemblies, even promising to give consideration to including that delectable manna, grits (Lev. 2:14, NASB) on future dayspring menus.

The Assembly is grateful for the tireless labor of its retiring and new moderators, the Assembly officers, the Stated Clerk and his staff, the floor clerks, and a vast host of others who cheerfully served this Assembly. “You may rest in the confidence of duty faithfully performed, and we pray that our merciful God will bestow on you His blessing.”

Our heartfelt thanks to all who have conducted the worship services, shared the Word in preaching, and who have led in “praising God in the Assembly of the elders” (Ps. 107:32), both vocally and instrumentally.

The lone name we would place here for special mention is that of Mrs. Laurel De Bert, desiring that the Assembly would have the opportunity to join the Administrative Committee in appreciation for her eighteen years of gracious service, rendered with such firmness as seemed requisite for the occasion!

“Ever praising march we onward,” keeping before us the vision theme of this Assembly, “Unity and Peace,” neither “forgetting all His benefits” (Ps. 103:2) nor that in Him we are victors in the midst of strife. “Let us be steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord.” (1Corinthians 15:58)

Joyfully submitted,
TE Henry Lewis Smith
RE Dwight L. Allen

# REPORT OF THE STATED CLERK to the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America

## Interchurch Relations

I serve on the Interchurch Relations Committee as part of my responsibilities (“RAO” 3-2 j.).

- An important development last year was the expansion of the World Fellowship of Reformed Churches into the World Reformed Fellowship (see Interchurch Relations Committee Report).

- The National Association of Evangelicals decision in 2000 to change the membership rules so as to allow Churches affiliated with the National Council of Churches to hold simultaneous membership in the NAE and the NCC contingent upon their subscribing to the NAE Statement of Faith was a concern of the IRC. The committee brought the matter to the attention of the Assembly last year. The IRC has sought to get the NAE to overturn that rule change (See IRC Report).

## Correspondence

- As directed by the Twenty-eighth General Assembly, I have written to the General Synod of the Bible Presbyterian Church (M28GA, p. 266) communicating to them our Assembly’s reply to their admonition to us. I included a copy of the text of the First General Assembly’s, “An Address to All Churches.”

- As directed by the Twenty-eighth General Assembly, I have written to the General Assembly of the Evangelical Presbyterian Church concerning their Presbytery of Mid-America’s receiving a PCA minister who was under the discipline of Illiana Presbytery (see Interchurch Relations Committee Report).

## Pending Lawsuit

There is one lawsuit currently pending against the PCA (A Corporation) and Mission to the World, Incorporated. Andrew S. Robertson v. the Presbyterian Church in America was filed in the Commonwealth of Virginia in October 1999 (see AC Report, “Actions of the Board of Directors”). The case was scheduled for trial June 5-6, 2001. The PCA offered to deal with the matter through Christian Conciliation. The plaintiff declined the offer of Christian Conciliation but did “non-suit” the case, i.e. hold

<a id="ga29-p363"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=365 printed_page=363 -->

the case in abeyance for a time. Notification has recently been received that the plaintiff has reactivated the suit.

## Corrections

### Advice to the Stated Clerk from the Committee on Constitutional Business

- Since the adoption of the Creation Study Committee report as amended last year, I have received inquires as to the status of the report in relation to the constitution. Moreover, there are overtures this year to require exceptions for any views other than 24-hour creation days (Overtures 7, 23) to allow exceptions for other view (Overture 20), and to affirm the Position of the 28th General Assembly on diversity of views on creation days (Overture 30). My advice has been as follows:

a. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of any other study committee report, such as those on the gifts of the Holy Spirit and Freemasonry; that is, it is the thinking of a particular General Assembly on a specific subject.

b. The Creation Study Committee Report, as with all other study committee reports adopted by a General Assembly, does not amend the constitution (BCO Preface, III. "The Constitution Defined" and BCO 26-1); only amendments effected by the proper constitutional procedures are actual and lawful amendments (BCO 26-2, 26-3 and 26-6).

c. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given "due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions" (BCO 14-7).

d. Overtures relating to the Creation Study Committee Report would have the same status as the report, i.e. of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given "due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions" (BCO 14-7).

e. The CCB ratified the advice of the Stated Clerk relative to the Role of in thesi Statements dated April 21, 2001, and offered the following additional advice: It is the purview of the lower courts to determine the suitability of men applying for reception/ordination with regard to the men's views on the matters raised in in thesi statements, subject always to proper review and/or judicial process. (BCO 13-6, 13-9.a., 24-1, and 11-3,4).

- I received a letter of concern from an individual calling on me to repent of reporting the results of the BCO amendments in the spring Stated Clerk's letter. The results were also reported on PCANews.com. The concern was set forth the ground that reporting the results of Presbytery voting on BCO amendments is improper (in the opinion of the person concerned) until all Presbyteries have voted on all proposed BCO amendments. My response has been that those results are public information

<a id="ga29-p364"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=366 printed_page=364 -->

that may be obtained by anyone who makes the effort to obtain the information. Moreover, I intend to make such information available in the future. The CCB advised the Stated Clerk that the committee concurred with his advice in this matter.

## Revision of the Korean Translation of the BCO

The Office of the stated Clerk holds the copyright to the Book of Church Order. The translation of the BCO into Korean was finished in 1992. A previous General Assembly determined, “That the translation be funded by designated funds to the Stated Clerk’s Office and that CE&amp;P be instructed to make the BCO and amendments available in Korean at the cost for English version – any extra being subsidized in the CE&amp;P’s budget” (M17GA, p. 133, M19GA, p. 257). The Korean Presbyteries have indicated a need for a new translation since the present supply of Korean-version Books of Church Order is exhausted, new amendments need to be included (no BCO amendments had been translated since 1996), and the overall translation needs to be improved. TE Young Pal Cho was contacted by the Office of the SC last year to translate the needed amendments. TE Paul H. Kim and TE Chang Ho Lee have been conferring with TE Choon the translation work of the entire BCO and appendices at the urging of the Korean Presbyteries. To date;

1. All amendments through the year 2000 (28th GA) have been translated.
2. The 5th Edition, 1992, Korean BCO, does not include any of the appendices to the BCO.
3. Two or three of the seven appendices have now been translated
4. All of the 5th Edition needs to be “re-phrased” to more reflect the specific meaning of the English version. Translation is complete through Chapter 19.

In order to complete the project, the Office of the Stated Clerk will:

1. Work with the translation team, TE Paul T. Kim, TE Chang Ho Lim, and TE Young Pal Cho.
2. Ask each Korean Presbytery (as the General Assembly specified in the first translation project) to elect a representative to review the translation work done thus far and the completed work and that each Presbytery provide funds to cover the expenses of their representative when they meet to review the work
3. Operate on the understanding that CE&amp;P, as previously instructed by the Assembly, will make the Korean version of the BCO available at the same costs as the English version with any extra costs being subsidized in CE&amp;P’s budget (M17GA, p. 133, M19GA, p. 257).
4. Receive designated gifts from Presbyteries, churches and individuals to help underwrite the costs.
5. Ask the Korean Presbyteries to ratify the new translation of the BCO before it is ratified by the General Assembly (see M17GA, p. 133) in keeping with the procedure used in the first translation of the BCO.
6. Report to the General Assembly on the progress of the project.

<a id="ga29-p365"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=367 printed_page=365 -->

presbytery votes Book of Church Order on amend men ts.
With sixty Presbyteries, forty Presbyteries must vote in the affirmative to fulfill
the requirements of BCO 26-2.

Statistics
The Church is more than its statistics, but the statistics tell us some things about
th estate of the Church. whereas there was decrease in the number of mission churches
from 1998 to 1999, there was an 18% increase in the number of mission churches from
1999 to 2000. the total membership of the PCA increased by 1.37% which is
noteworthy when many denominations are declining. The previous year’s increase,
however, was 3.16%. Total reported disbursements increased 9% to $483,509,840.
Contributions to Presbytery ministries increased by a healthy 22%, much of that is due
to church planting efforts. whereas contributions to General Assembly ministries
decreased by $646,206 in the previous year, there was a $181,987 increase for 2000.
There were other decreases as well. Sunday School attendance decreased by 2%
reflecting a trend inmost Churches. there we regains and losses in the number of
congregations with a net gain of fifty-three. Forty-four of those we remission churches.

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=368 printed_page=366 -->

ATTACHMENT 1

M EM ORANDUM

September 27, 2000

from : L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk

TO : board of directors

subject : pending lawsuit update

1. At the March 2000 meeting i reported that a lawsuit had been filed in the
commonwealth of virginia against the Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation) and Mission to the World, Inc. and was pending. This information
was received and the office staff was directed to seek a biblical means of
resolving the suit within the church.

2. We have proposed such a resolution through our liability insurance attorneys
who have contacted the local church and the plain tiffs attorney. We have also
had direct contact with the local church and with RE Dan Carrell (attorney in the
local church) who is familiar with the case. He is also familiar with the plaintiff
and indicates there is no animosity. They are only seeking to “preserve potential
future right.”

3. The consensus was that “at the present time nothing lends itself to the
conciliation proceeding. if the court finds damages then (you) could go to
conciliation.”

4. We have been advised by our insurance attorneys that, assuming this case is not
dismissed or settled this matter has been set for trial on June 5-6, 2001.

## RECOMMENDATIONS

**BD-3/01-1** MSP that the Minutes reflect, as a Board of Directors, that the annual RPCES corporate filings have been accomplished in a timely manner where required.

**Delaware Corporations:** World Presbyterian Missions, Inc.; National Presbyterian Missions, Inc.; Christian Training, Inc.

**Michigan Corporation:** Board of Home Ministries

**BD-3/01-2** MSP that the corporate minutes reflect that the annual corporate filings have been accomplished in a timely manner in all states where the corporation is

<a id="ga29-p371"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=373 printed_page=371 -->

registered to conduct business. [Note: The Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) is currently registered as a foreign corporation in Georgia, Missouri and Mississippi.]

BD-3/01-3 MSP that the Board of Directors recommend to the General Assembly that the PCA Corporate Bylaws, Article VI-5 be amended as follows:

The Board of Directors of Presbyterian Church in America Foundation, Inc. shall consist of ten (10) members: (a) six (6) members in classes elected through the standard nomination and election procedures (the Aat-large@ members); and one (1) member from each of the following permanent committees or board of trustees of the agencies: (1) Covenant College; (2) Covenant Theological Seminary; (3) Mission to the World; and (4) Mission to North America (the AAppointed Members@). The Chairman of the Board of Directors of Presbyterian Church in America Foundation, Inc. shall be an at-large member.

The at-large members Directors shall serve for four (4) year terms, and shall be divided into four (4) classes, each class being as nearly equal in number as possible, so that the terms of approximately one-fourth of the at-large members Directors shall expire each year. Directors shall be eligible for reelection to a second four-year term after which there must be a one-year interval before further election.

Each program committee and agencies listed above shall designate its appointed member each year at the last meeting of such committee or the board of such agency before the meeting of the General Assembly. As stated above, the at-large members Directors shall be elected through the standard nomination and election procedures, except the Board of Directors may make requests to the Presbyteries to nominate specific men for election as at-large members Directors, and may submit to the Assembly Nominating Committee letters of recommendation concerning particular nominees for election as at-large members Directors from the Presbyteries.

All of the members of the Board of Directors shall be either Teaching Elders, Ruling Elders or Deacons, and at least one-half of such members shall be Ruling Elders.

Lawsuit update:

At the March 2000 Directors meeting I informed you of a suite that was filed in the Commonwealth of Virginia, Andrew S. Robinson v. the Presbyterian Church in America, et al. (Case No.: CL98-1043).

On November 28, 2000, the following notice was received from Mr. Mark C. Nanavati, of the law firm Sinnott Nuckols &amp; Logan, P.C. representing the PCA interests and that of our insurance company, GuideOne. "I am writing to advise you that the plaintiff has elected to nonsuit his case against the Church. As such, trial will not take place on June 5-6, 2001. Mr. Robinson has six months to refile his case. If he does and the Church is served, please contact GuideOne and me immediately."

<a id="ga29-p372"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=374 printed_page=372 -->

the next scheduled meeting of the board of directors will be Tuesday, June 19, 2001,
in Dallas, Texas, at the Adams Mark Hotel. The meeting was adjourned at 3:43 p.m.
with TE Curtis McDaniel giving the closing prayer.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ RE William H. Moore, IV, President /s/ TE L. Roy Taylor, Secretary /Treasurer

# Report of the Administrative Committee to the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America

## I. Meetings

June 20, 2000
October 6, 2000
March 16, 2001

## II. Actions of the Board of Directors

1. The people listed below were elected to their designated positions until 12/31/2001 or until such time as they are re-elected by the Administrative Committee or the General Assembly as the constitution requires:

**President** – current Chairman of the AC [set by RAO 4-3; Corporate Bylaws, IV-2] Mr. William Moore, IV

**Secretary** – current Stated Clerk of the General Assembly [BD-10/98-1; set by Corporate Bylaws, IV-3] Dr. L. Roy Taylor

**Assistant Secretary:**

Rev. John W. Robertson, Business Administrator [BD-10/98-2]
Mrs. Laurel De Bert, Office Administrator/Event Planner [BD-3/89-4]

**Treasurer** – current Stated Clerk of the General Assembly [BD-10/98-1; set by Corporate Bylaws IV-4] Dr. L. Roy Taylor

**Assistant Treasurer:**

Rev. John W. Robertson, Business Administrator [BD-10/98-2]
Mrs. Laurel De Bert, Office Administrator/Event Planner [BD-10/93-4]

2. Received reports concerning a lawsuit that had been filed in the Commonwealth of Virginia against the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) and Mission to the World, Inc. This information was received and the office Staff was directed to seek a Biblical means of resolving the suit within the Church. The plaintiff declined the offer for such conciliation. Our insurance attorneys have advised us that, assuming this case is not dismissed or settled this matter was set for trial on June 5-6, 2001. The plaintiff decided, after the trial date was set, to hold the suit in abeyance. [Since the March 2001 meeting of the Board of Directors, the Stated Clerk has received notification that the plaintiff has reactivated the suit].

3. The annual RPCES corporate filings have been accomplished in a timely manner where required.

IV. Staff Information

1. Mrs. Laurel De Bert has served with the Administrative Committee/Office of the Stated Clerk for eighteen years. Many have had dealings with Laurel in her role as our Office Manager and Meeting Planner. Laurel is seeking a new position nearer her home as she is facing the likelihood of a longer commute time in her present position. We appreciate Laurel’s faithfulness and commitment and we pray God’s best for her. Between now and the end of the General Assembly, Laurel will be mentoring her successor.

1. Mrs. Diane Hitzfeld has been employed as our Office Manager and Meeting Planner. She has extensive experience in office management and holds an M.B.A. Most recently she and her husband, RE Gary Hitzfeld, have been serving with the Church Renewal Ministry of Dr. Archie Parrish under MNA. Gary will continue in his ministry with MNA.

2. Our editor, Dr. Dominic Aquila, has accepted a call to become the President of New Geneva Theological Seminary, effective August 31. He will continue as part-time editor after his move to Colorado Springs. We appreciate his ministry in getting PCANews.com up and going.

3. The Committee completed the annual review of the Stated Clerk as required by “RAO” 3-3 (see recommendation 15).

V. News Office, PCANews.com

PCANews.com has been providing a valuable service of informing the PCA and all others who avail themselves of the service. In our start up we worked with New City Media to write the initial programs and get the electronic magazine into operation. Another company, Christianity.com, has a more extensive electronic platform. We have moved the hosting of PCANews.com to a new web site host, Christianity.com (not to be confused with “Christianity Today”). The new web site host will enable us to have increased viewership and to give expanded and upgraded service at a lower cost of

<a id="ga29-p375"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=377 printed_page=375 -->

operation. Dominic Aquila will continue to serve as editor on a part-time basis. Other support staff and services will operate out of the AC offices.

## VI. Strategic Planning

The project was approved by the Twenty-eighth General Assembly. The Strategic Planning group has met twice since the last General Assembly. They are meeting again during the week of General Assembly. They are in preliminary stages of planning and will make a more detailed report to the next General Assembly.

## VII. General Assembly Arrangements

The Hyatt Hotel at DFW Airport, the scheduled site for the Assembly meeting, cancelled our contract due to the Airport Authority’s plan to demolish one of the hotel towers to build a new concourse. We were able to secure alternative arrangements with the Adams Mark Hotel in downtown Dallas due to diligent efforts by the AC staff and the Local Arrangements Committee. North Texas Presbytery is sponsoring a pre-assembly convocation dealing with the topic of doctrinal subscription. The convocation will be held Tuesday, June 19, 12:30-4:30 p.m. MNA was willing to forego their usual pre-assembly seminars in order to have the convocation. The cancellation of the contract by the Hyatt was the second such occurrence (the first being planning the 14th GA in Philadelphia) in the history of our denomination. This brought to the fore the need for some procedure to deal with such situations. The only present possibility is *BCO 14-3*, which deals with an emergency meeting of the Assembly. The AC recommends that the “RAO” be amended by the addition of a new provision to cover extraordinary events and unusual circumstances (see recommendation 6).

## VIII. PCA Office Building

The Twenty-eighth General Assembly approved the sale of the 1852 Century Place Office Building in Atlanta and authorized the purchase of land and construction of new office buildings (*M28GA*, pp. 368-371, 264-265). Accordingly, the 1852 Century Place Office Building was sold to Micron Optics, a growing company located in an adjacent building. A building site has been purchased near the intersection of I-85 and Sugarloaf Parkway in Gwinnett County, Georgia, approximately eighteen miles northeast of the Century Place Building. Ground breaking was held in January 2001. The complex was named the Cassels Ministry Center in honor of Mrs. Rosalie O. Cassels, a benefactor of many PCA causes. Two buildings of approximately 25,000 square feet each are under construction. MTW offices will be housed in one building; AC, CE&amp;P, RUM, IAR, PCAF, MNA offices will be housed in the other building. There is room on the site for future expansion for both. The total costs of the building and relocation project are approximately $6,800,000. With the proceeds (approximately $2,880,000) from the sale of the Century Place Building, together with contributions and pledges received thus far, we need only about $500,000 to be able to move into the Ministry Center without a long-term debt. Development efforts are under way to reach that goal so that funds that would have had to be used for debt retirement may be used for the ministries of the various committees and agencies. In keeping with the General Assembly action MTW will have title to their section of the site and their building. The

Dr the AC, CE&P, RUM, IAR, PCAF, M NA (see recommendation 2).

```markdown
## XI. Financial Matters

1. The Committee evaluated the CEO Compensation Guidelines as required (see recommendation 7).
2. The Committee reviewed the budgets of the various Committees and Agencies (see recommendation 8).
3. The Committee reviewed General Assembly Commissioner’s Registration Fee (see recommendation 9).
4. The Committee received and approved a recommendation from the Building Management Committee regarding the amount of rent charged to Committees and Agencies occupying the PCA Office Building (see recommendation 11).
5. The Committee approved auditors for the various Committees and Agencies (see recommendations 12, 13,14).

## XII. Development

The Committee and Staff continue to grapple with the issue of more complete funding not only for the AC but also for all the Committees and Agencies. The present “Partnership-Shares” method is not providing full funding. The “Partnership-Shares” system is based on several assumptions; (1) that all churches will contribute, (2) that all churches will contribute to all General Assembly Committees and Agencies, and (3) that all churches have the same *per capita* giving potential. These assumptions are not accurate. We do not yet have an alternative or additional proposal, but we want the Assembly to be aware that we are working on the issue.

## XIII. Other Matters Discussed by the Committee

1. The Committee approved the request of the PCA Foundation that the PCA Corporate Bylaws be revised to reflect a change in the membership of the Board of Directors of the PCA Foundation (see recommendation 1).
2. The Committee considered Overture 6 (see recommendation 3) regarding Options for Lower Costs and Greater Ruling Elder Participation at the General Assembly.
3. The Committee considered Overture 18 regarding Publication of Newly Edited and Hardback version of the Westminster Standards (see recommendation 4).
4. The Committee approved and declined to approve applications for exhibitors (“RAO” 9-5).
5. Received progress reports concerning the PCA Historical Center.
6. Approved recommendations for the docket of the General Assembly.

## XIV. Recommendations

1. That the PCA Corporate Bylaws, Article VI-5 be amended as follows:

The Board of Directors of Presbyterian Church in America Foundation, Inc. shall consist of ten (10) members: (a) six (6) members in classes elected through the standard nomination and election procedures (the Aat-large@-members); and one (1) member from each of the following permanent committees or board
```

<a id="ga29-p378"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=380 printed_page=378 -->

of trustees of the agencies: (1) Covenant College; (2) Covenant Theological Seminary; (3) Mission to the World; and (4) Mission to North America (the "appointed members@). The Chairman of the Board of Directors of Presbyterian Church in America Foundation, Inc. shall be an at large member.

The at large members Directors shall serve for four (4) year terms, and shall be divided into four (4) classes, each class being as nearly equal in number as possible, so that the terms of approximately one-fourth of the at large members Directors shall expire each year. Directors shall be eligible for reelection to a second four-year term after which there must be a one-year interval before further re-election.

Each program committee and agencies listed above shall designate its appointed member each year at the last meeting of such committee or the board of such agency before the meeting of the General Assembly. As stated above, the at large members Directors shall be elected through the standard nomination and election procedures, except the Board of Directors may make requests to the Presbyteries to nominate specific men for election as at large members Directors, and may submit to the Assembly Nominating Committee letters of recommendation concerning particular nominees for election as at large members Directors from the Presbyteries.

All of the members of the Board of Directors shall be either Teaching Elders, Ruling Elders or Deacons, and at least one-half of such members shall be Ruling Elders.

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=384 printed_page=382 -->

ATTACHMENT A

BIBLICAL CONFLICT RESOLUTION

# Preface

As we continue to seek to become a church holy before God, the manner in which we respond to sin and conflicts in the body, and gently shepherd those caught in sin, will reflect our commitment to the authority of God’s Word and the spirit of love which should define all of our actions and relationships. Biblical peacemaking is one of God’s highest priorities (Matt. 5:23-24; Rom. 12:18; Gal.6:1); therefore, it must be one of our highest priorities. The Book of Church Order (BCO) reflects our commitment to following God’s procedure for reclaiming those going astray. It says, “Scriptural law is the basis of all discipline because it is the revelation of God’s Holy will. Proper disciplinary principles are set forth in the Scriptures and must be followed” (BCO 27-5). It also says, “An injured party shall not become a prosecutor of personal offenses without having tried the means of reconciliation and of reclaiming the offender, required by Christ” (BCO 31-5). The purpose of this appendix is to provide guidance through the steps of biblical peacemaking required as pre-conditions to judicial process in cases of personal offense (BCO 31-5), and for use whenever possible in cases of general offense (BCO 31-7).

Each presbytery should endeavor to have several elders trained in the methods of “Christian conciliation” (including mediation and arbitration), and available to serve as Christian conciliators in cases that could and should be resolved privately before judicial process is initiated. We would do well to follow the wisdom of those who have considered the importance of this matter. For example, Elder Edmond Clowney states, “Discipline...is not first an exercise of negative judgment, a matter of church courts and censures. It begins with the care of friends with whom we strive to follow Christ” (Clowney, E.P., The Church, IVP, 1995).

# The Biblical Steps of Discipline as they Relate to Biblical Peacemaking

BCO 27-5 outlines the proper principles for the exercise of church discipline. This BCO section emphasizes that the steps indicated, (a) through (d), must be followed in proper order. The steps are:

- **a)** Instruction in the Word;
- **b)** Individuals’ responsibility to admonish one another (Matt. 18:15; Gal. 6:1);
- **c)** If the admonition is rejected, then the calling of one or more witnesses (Matt. 18:16);
- **d)** If rejection persists, then the church must act through her court unto admonition, suspension, excommunication and deposition.

This appendix addresses the first three steps, (a) through (c), by providing an expanded discussion of the implications raised by Scriptural direction for a complete process at each

<a id="ga29-p383"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=385 printed_page=383 -->

step. Step (d), formal church discipline, is addressed by part ii of the Book of Church Order, The Rules of Discipline.

1. The first step - Instruction in the Word.
Many Christians do not fully understand the emphasis God places on living at peace
and in unity with others ( Eph. 4: 3). Preaching and teaching should regularly address this
emphasis and the purposes for church discipline as set forth in BCO 27-3 and BCO 27-4.
Attention should be drawn to the fact that the main procedural passage related to church
discipline, Matthew 18:15-20, is set forth in the context of two powerful parables teaching
the extent and depth of God’s love in reclaiming those who have strayed (Matt. 18:10-14),
and the vast measure of his forgiveness and the expansive forgiveness he expects from his
children toward one another (Matt. 18:21-35). the extent of instruction on reconciliation
provided to church members in ‘"new member classes” and regular preaching from the
pulpit will have a significant bearing on the extent to which a church member understands
the benefits of church discipline and has consented to ecclesiastical jurisdiction (which is
helpful for the church if it is to avoid legal liability for the proper exercise of church
discipline).

Teaching God’s Word concerning sin and conflicts that result from it, and how
Christians should respond biblically to conflict, will equip church members to become
peacemakers themselves. Peacemaking has always been one of a Christian’s most
important ministries. as we are reminded in 2 Corinthians 5:18, God “reconciled us to
himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation.” one of the most
powerful ways that we can encourage reconciliation with God is to model reconciliation
among people. When others see us resolve our differences in a loving and biblical manner,
they are inclined to give more weight to what we say about the Lord (John 13:34-35; 17:20-
23). On the other hand, when they see Christians embroiled in disputes, they tend to write
God's people off as hypocrites and dismiss the claims of Christ (Rom. 2:21-24; 1 Cor. 6:1-
8 ).

the bible sets forth a process for resolving personal and substantive disputes ina
constructive manner. This process is sometimes referred to as “Christian conciliation” and
it may be used to resolve everything from minor personal differences to church divisions or
lawsuits involving church members. As indicated in passages such as Proverbs 19:11,
Matthew 5:23-25 and 18:15-20, 1 Corinthians 6:1-8, and Galatians 6:1, conciliation
involves three basic steps: (1) when Christians are involved in a conflict that is too serious
to overlook, the first thing that they should do is meet together privately and in person to
try to resolve their differences; (2) if this effort is unsuccessful, they should ask one or more
other Christians to meet with them and help them to seek reconciliation and a voluntary
settlement of their differences (a process sometimes referred to as mediation); and (3) if
they cannot arrive at a voluntary settlement, they should ask one or more other Christians to
hear both sides of the matter and render a biblical ly based decision that both sides are
obligated to accept (a process sometimes referred to as arbitration).

There are many benefits to resolving disputes through Christian conciliation. It
prevents a public quarrel that would dishonor the Lord Jesus Christ and diminish the
credibility and witness of his church. Conciliation also allows Christians to demonstrate

<a id="ga29-p384"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=386 printed_page=384 -->

their faith in Christ and their confidence in His teachings (John 13:34; 14:15; 17:20-23). In
addition, conciliation encourages forgiveness and promotes reconciliation, which can help
to preserve valuable relationships and strengthen the church (Eph. 4:29-32; Col. 3:12-17).
conciliation also helps people to identify and deal with the root causes of conflict, which
may include such things as pride, selfishness, fear, vengeance, greed, bitterness, or
unforgiveness (see Matt. 7:3-5). This allows people to make changes in their lives so that
they will enjoy more peaceful relationships in the future (Eph. 4:1-3, 22-24).

Christian conciliation is especially beneficial for people who sincerely want to do
what is right and are open to learning where they have been wrong (Prov. 15:31-32).
Conciliators can help them to identify improper attitudes or unwise practices, to understand
more fully the effects of their decision and actions, and to make improvements in their lives
that will help them to honor and serve the Lord more effectively in the future (1 Peter 2:12).

Teaching and Ruling elders are encouraged to learn all they can about Christian
conciliation and develop local ministries that will equip members under their care to
respond to conflicts ina manner that reflects the power of christ working in their lives. The
more church members understand these concepts and benefits, the more likely they will be
to apply biblical principles when conflicts occur.

2. The second step - An individual’s responsibility to admonish another (Matt.
18:15; Gal. 6:1). scripture warns against making a premature judgment about a matter
(Matt. 7:1-5). By first going personally and in private to those who have offended, we
provide the opportunity for clarification of misunderstandings and avoid premature
judgment. This step includes the three responses of personal peacemaking as follows:

Overlook an offense: an individual may overlook an offense of another if it is minor in
nature and it has not significantly dishonored God, damaged a personal relationship, or hurt
other people (including the offender). “A man’s wisdom gives him patience; it is to his
glory to overlook an offense” (Prov. 19:11).

Discussion: When personal wrongs are too serious to overlook, the parties are required to
seek to resolve them privately through loving confrontation and confession, ‘i f your
brother has something against you...go and be reconciled” (Matt. 5:23-24). “If your brother
sins against you, go and show him his fault, just between the two of you” (Matt. 18:15).

Negotiation: When a substantive issue related to money, property, or other material issues
or rights divides Christians, a process of biblical negotiation should be followed that will
meet the interests of all those involved. “ Each of you should look not only to your own
interests, but also to the interests of others” (Phil.2:4).

Privately responding to conflict early, before it escalates, through the means of
these three forms of personal peacemaking will often result in reconciliation and no need
for further peacemaking attention. Successful personal peacemaking is usually based on at
least one parties' willingness to accept responsibility for how they have contributed to the
conflict and by being willing to humble themselves for the greater goal of God’s glory
through “making every effort” (Eph. 4:3) to keep the unity of the spirit.

<a id="ga29-p385"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=387 printed_page=385 -->

3. The third step - If private admonition is rejected, then calling of one or more witnesses (Matt. 18:16; 1 Cor. 6:1-9). Involving others is a serious escalation of a conflict and should be considered only after the elements of step two have been fully exhausted. Step three includes the three assisted peacemaking responses as follows:

Mediation: If a dispute cannot be resolved through personal peacemaking, the parties should ask one or more other Christians to meet with them to help the parties communicate more effectively and explore possible solutions. The mediators may give advice but have no power to impose solutions. They may later act as witnesses in formal church disciplinary proceedings should mediation fail due to hardness of heart or the unrepentant sin of one or both parties. “If he will not listen, take one or two others along, so that every matter may be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.” (Matt. 18:16).

Arbitration: When two parties to a conflict cannot come to a voluntary agreement on a substantive issue, one or more arbitrators may be appointed to listen to the arguments and evidence of each side and render a binding decision. “If you have disputes about such matters, appoint as judges even men of little account in the church” (1 Cor. 6:1-8).

Church Discipline: Formal church discipline, pursuant to the procedures of BCO Part II, is reserved for those cases where the person who professes to be a Christian refuses to be reconciled, repent of personal sin, and do what is right. Church leaders bearing ecclesiastical jurisdiction over such persons should formally intervene to promote justice, repentance, and forgiveness. “If he refuses to listen, tell it to the church” (Matt. 18:17-20).

## Application of the Principles

Judicial cases that come to the attention of church courts frequently begin as personal disputes between two or more individuals. Typically, when one side feels they are not getting what they want, they bring charges of sin against the other side. In this manner, sessions and presbyteries are drawn into conflicts that would better be resolved at a personal level through Christian conciliation. Of course, when conflicted parties refuse to be reconciled, the accountability afforded by formal church discipline should be used to further the goals of purity in the church, the rebuke of offenses, the removal of scandal, and the spiritual good of offenders (1 Cor. 5:5). Every effort should be made, however, to resolve the conflict through repentance, confession, forgiveness and reconciliation before formal charges are entertained by a church court. The intent of the relevant BCO provisions is to encourage Christian unity before the formal provisions of church discipline are brought to bear on a situation. Of course, not every situation will lend itself to Christian conciliation. However, every effort should be made to utilize private confrontation, mediation, and arbitration in order to reserve the use of formal church discipline, and the time of church courts, for those matters related to the purity of the church and the keeping and reclaiming of disobedient sinners (BCO 27-3). Many of these ends will be realized through the processes of Christian conciliation and, therefore, should be viewed as prerequisite procedures to the formal disciplinary process.

<a id="ga29-p386"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=388 printed_page=386 -->

Failure to Follow the Steps

Churches unwilling or unable to instruct their members completely in the full
breadth of the steps of conciliation and discipline encourage resorting to unbiblical, worldly
responses to conflict. the escape responses of denial, flight, and even suicide can be
expected if church members are not positively directed to the biblical processes of christian
conciliation. Others, when not given the hope of having matters responded to within the
body of Christ, will resort to civil litigation, verbal and physical attack, and possibly even
murder to remove an opponent where there is no avenue for a just resolution.

Moving a case too quickly to formal church discipline can build barriers that could
otherwise be dealt with in mediation or arbitration. Every church and presbytery is
encouraged to equip elders and mature members with the skills of christian conciliation,
and to exhaust conciliation processes and remedies before moving to formal church
discipline.

Use of Conciliation Clauses in Membership Documents

One significant way church members can be educated and prepared for biblical
conflict resolution is through the use of a conciliation clause in the membership covenant or
other agreement signed by new members when they join the church. The recommended
language for this clause is set forth below. It should be noted that signing this clause cannot
be made a condition for membership, but may be encouraged as a voluntary biblical
commitment. It should be clearly offered as an optional statement for the resolution of any
disputes that may arise.

Christian Conciliation
Members are encouraged but not required to make this additional commitment.

If 1 ever have a dispute with the church that cannot be resolved through its own
internal procedures, I agree to resolve the dispute according to biblical principles (such as
those set forth in Matthew 5:23-25 and 18:15-20, and 1 Corinthians 6:1-8) by submitting
the matter to mediation and, if necessary, arbitration, according to the bylaws of this church
and the rules of procedure of the institute for christian Conciliation. I understand that
arbitration is a legally binding process, and that judgment upon an arbitration award may be
entered in any court otherwise having jurisdiction.

Dated th is day of _______________, 200 .

Name (Printed) Signature

Name (Printed) Signature

Parent's or Guardian’s Name (for minors) Parent’s or Guardian’s Signature

<a id="ga29-p387"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=389 printed_page=387 -->

this agreement is subject to arbitration pursuant to the f state !
ARBITRATION ACT, TITLE # # . CHAPTER # . fSTATE 1 CODE ANNOTATED.

[The institute for christian conciliation is a division of peacemaker Ministries. For a copy
of the rules of Procedure, see www.HisPeace.org or call (800) 711-7118. Other
conciliation clauses for use in employment, construction, and service contracts are also
available.]

Amend Church Bylaws to Allow Continuing Church Jurisdiction

The following clause is recommended for inclusion in local church bylaws to allow
the continuing ecclesiastical jurisdiction over a member who may otherwise simply flee
from the church to avoid biblical discipline:

Members may be removed from membership at their own request by informing the
Session of their intention to withdraw and the reasons therefore. If a member
requests to withdraw because of specific problems or disappointments with the
church, the Session shall attempt to resolve those matters so that the member may
remain in the church and enjoy greater fruitfulness and personal spiritual growth. If
the Session is unable to resolve those matters, it shall offer to assist the member in
locating a church of like faith and practice that can respond more effectively to his
gifts and needs. If it appears to the Session that a member has requested removal
merely to avoid church discipline, that request shall not be given effect until the
disciplinary process has been properly concluded.

use of this clause will also help the church avoid serious legal liability for following
through with all of the steps of church discipline. Members should be informed of its
provisions and carefully taught the Scriptural basis for continuing shepherding and pastoral
care especially when caught in sin (see also BCO 38-3 and 38-4).

Conclusion

the courts of the church are encouraged to employ the means of christian
conciliation whenever possible. That is, churches and presbyteries are encouraged to defer
judicial action until all other remedies have been exhausted. Peace, unity, and the mission
of the church can be furthered through careful and complete attention to every step of the
process as set forth in BCO 27-5 as understood through the expanded guidance concerning
Christian conciliation as set forth above.

ATTACHEM NT B

RESOLUTION

Whereas, in the providence of Almighty God, in 1982 He brought Mrs. Laurel De Bert to labor
in the administrative offices of the Presbyterian Church in America, and

Whereas, the church at large, the General Assembly, Presbyteries, individual churches and
members have all benefited from her heart for the concerns of the Church, and

Whereas, her energy, enthusiasm, experience, expertise in meeting planning, and sacrificial
service have resulted in the smooth, timely and efficient functioning of numerous
Assemblies, committees, and meetings for the
past 19 years, and

Whereas, over the course of these years her ability to get the job done with limited financial
and personnel, and

Whereas, providing leadership as Administrative Assistant to the Stated Clerk year in and year
out during the tenure of three Stated Clerks and working alongside and supervising
many different staff members and volunteers has meant the effective operation of
the Stated Clerk’s Office, and

Whereas, she has performed the many complex and demanding tasks with diligence,
perseverance and cheerfulness, and

Whereas, her caring concern, graciousness in the midst of transition, and example as a sister in
the Lord have ministered to countless members of the Body of Christ, therefore

Whereas, she now find it necessary to leave her position in the Stated Clerk’s Office -
Administrative Committee, therefore

Be It Resolved that the members of the Administrative Committee, meeting in Dallas, Texas on
Tuesday. June 19 do hereby UNANIMOUSLY thank Mrs. Laurel DeBert for her
labor of love these many years and for her service in support of the work of the
Church of the Lord Jesus Christ, and commend her to this 29th General Assembly of
the Presbyterian Church in America.

Adopted at the Administrative Committee Meeting, June 19, 2001,

Attested by: Is/ William H. Moore, IV, Chairman

/s/ L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk

<a id="ga29-p394"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=396 printed_page=394 -->

ATTACHMENT C

REPORT OF THE PCA HISTORICAL CENTER TO THE ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE

Our Mission: Since its establishment in 1985, the PCA Historical Center has sought to preserve and promote the record of what God has done and is doing through the people of the PCA. We do this by:

- Preserving the official records of the PCA, its agencies and committees.
- Collecting the personal papers of key individuals in the PCA.
- Promoting the writing of church histories by our local congregations.
- Working to develop in the people a deeper appreciation of the value of our legacy.

Our New Home: September of 2000 saw the completion of work on the newly expanded J. Oliver Buswell, Jr. Library, where the Historical Center is located. Over the course of that month, some 2000 boxes were moved into place, some on shelving but most set on the floor, awaiting the purchase and installation of high-density moveable shelving. The Historical Center’s new facility consists of a 440 sq. ft. Reading Room and behind that another room where the Center’s collections are stored, measuring approximately 1500 sq. ft.

The new facility has a much more visible location on the Covenant Seminary campus, and as a result, the number of visitors to the Center has greatly increased. I have estimated that we have thus far seen a 400% increase in patronage overall, including requests coming in from our web site.

The one thing lacking at this point is that we still have not been able to raise all of the needed money to purchase the shelving, so the boxes are still on the floor, though neatly arranged in rows. Sufficient funds were raised and have been spent on the installation of the track and deck that the shelving will set on; all that remains is the purchase and installation of the shelving itself. We urge you to pray with us for God’s provision of this great need, that we might properly preserve our legacy and better serve our people.

Website: At the time of last year’s General Assembly, a major revision of the Historical Center’s web site took place. Finding aids (indexes) to most of the collections were posted, along with many other resources. The greatly increased size of our site also resulted in a new and simpler address: www.pcanet.org/history. Then as 2001 began, we were able to begin uploads directly from our St. Louis location. This has meant that we are now able to keep the site up to date, with frequent additions of new content. A web journal page has been added which allows visitors to keep up on the latest news of activities and new acquisitions.

<a id="ga29-p395"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=397 printed_page=395 -->

Statistics have indicated that the site has roughly 1000 visitors and 8-10,000 “hits” per month.

Requests for services have come from South Africa, Wales and Australia, among others.

The Administrative Committee has authorized the Historical Center to pursue its own separate web site, using an address such as www.pcahistory.org. Such a move is still under consideration, but for the time being, the existing address seems to be working well for us and costs the Center nothing. The latter point is important, given our very modest operating budget.

**Publications:** Two new brochures have been published in the past year. *Researching the Archive: A Guide to Its Resources* is a basic introduction to using the facilities of an archive or manuscript repository. The other brochure, *Should I Donate My Papers?*, speaks to the common questions frequently asked by those considering a donation of documents to the Historical Center.

**Records Processing:** Due to the ongoing construction this last year, processing of collections slowed considerably. Almost all of our boxes had to be wrapped in plastic to protect them from dust and other construction hazards, and so I was only able to work with a few select collections. Much of my time was spent in moving the boxes to one location or another to stay ahead of construction crews. Now that the move to the new facility is more or less complete, processing has resumed.

## Collections Development

Notable materials received in the last year include the Papers of the Rev. B.I. Anderson, Stated Clerk of Mississippi Valley Presbytery and an almost complete bound set of Minutes from Mississippi Valley Presbytery, donated by TE Roger Collins. Also received were the Papers of the Rev. Dr. Frederick W. Evans, Jr. The latter collection is important to the Historical Center’s effort to document something of the whole of the conservative Presbyterian movement in the 20th century. Rev. Evans was one who left the PCUSA in 1955 to independency and only late in life did he transfer his credentials to the PCA. Nonetheless, his life is an important witness to the larger story of the Continuing Presbyterian Church and a testimony to God’s covenant faithfulness displayed across the generations of a family. His father, Frederick, Sr., is said to have been the last true evangelical to serve as moderator of the PCUSA; his son John is a PCA missionary in Zambia and his oldest son William is a professor at Erskine [Bill Evans was also the ARP fraternal delegate to our General Assembly in 1999]. Lastly, there was a substantial addition to the Papers of the Rev. Henry G. Welbon, donated by TE Stu Sherard upon the final dissolution of Rev. Welbon’s estate and we have just received a major addition to the Papers of the Rev. Dr. R. Laird Harris.

Some 200 of our PCA churches are currently participating in the Local Church History program. Of these, about 150 actually have an appointed church historian who writes

<a id="ga29-p396"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=398 printed_page=396 -->

and submits an annual account. The remaining 50 or so simply send newsletters or other materials. Beyond these numbers, the Historical Center has some sort of file on about 600 PCA churches. In some cases we may only have a single Sunday bulletin to represent all that we know of a church’s history. In a few cases we have several large boxes of materials. Most of our collections average perhaps a dozen file folders each. One goal for the coming year is to greatly increase the number of churches reporting information to the Center.

Staff: As of September 1, 2000, RE Wayne Sparkman has been moved to full-time status as Director of the Historical Center. Mrs. Florence Graham, who has worked here as a volunteer almost since the inception of the Historical Center, was unable to work in the Center this past year because of the construction work on the Library. She does anticipate returning to her volunteer duties soon. Additional volunteer workers will be recruited in coming months from among the Covenant Seminary students, now that the Center is situated in its new facility. M.Div. students will be able to earn a portion of the Field Education requirement by working in the Center.

## New Endeavors:

In May of 2000, I was pleased to be awarded a scholarship which substantially undercut the cost of attending a conference in Philadelphia. Hosted by the Conservation Center for Art and Historic Artifacts (CCAHA), the conference provided training in the preservation of architectural records. A number of our PCA churches have historically significant buildings and several are on the National Historic Register. Among the more notable of these is First Presbyterian Church in Augusta, Georgia, whose building was designed by Robert Mills, architect of the Washington Monument and other significant structures. Thus far, the PCA Historical Center had not ventured into this area of preserving architectural records. It is my hope that we can now more fully assist our churches in the preservation of all of their records, including the record of their “built environment.” A brochure will soon be prepared which will allow me to share with our churches something of what I learned at the conference.

## Historical Center Subcommittee:

The Historical Center Subcommittee continues to faithfully provide guidance and oversight for the operations of the Center. It is a distinct honor to be able to work with these men and women of God. Members of the Subcommittee are:

Dr. David Calhoun
Dr. Frederic Mau
Mr. Edward S. Harris
Dr. Donald J. MacNair
Miss Lannae Graham
Mrs. Shirley Duncan
Dr. Will Barker [newly elected]
Mr. John Spencer

TE L. Roy Taylor and TE John Robertson are ex-officio members of this committee.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ RE Wayne Sparkman, M.A.R., M.Div.

Director, PCA Historical Center

<a id="ga29-p397"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=399 printed_page=397 -->

ATTACHMENT D

Donald J. MacNair

1922-2001

The PCA Historical Center Committee lost one of its valued members this year, Dr. Donald MacNair. Don born in 1922, was born again in 1936. He enrolled graduated from Rutgers University, as a mechanical engineer. He was called into the ministry in 1944, the same year he was married to his childhood sweetheart, Evelyn, and attended Faith Theological Seminary from which he graduated with a B.D. degree in 1949.

He served the Bible Presbyterian Church of Coatsville, PA 1944-1953. Donald accepted the call of the First Bible Presbyterian Church in St. Louis, in 1953 following the ministry of Francis Schaeffer. The Bible Presbyterian Church changed its location to Town and Country and adopted a new name, The Covenant Presbyterian Church, where Don served from 1953 to 1964. During that time, the congregation grew to over 400.

In 1955, Don and five other men formed the Board of Directors for a new college and a new seminary, both called Covenant. Covenant Church and the MacNair home served as centers for these institutions, as well as the denominations and Synod of which they were a part. Don became president of the National Presbyterian Missions Committee of the Bible Presbyterian Church, General Synod

In 1962, Executive Director of NPM. In 1982 Don held a firm belief that denominations of like doctrine should merge, and was a leader in the formation of the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod and in 1982 the RPCES joined with the Presbyterian Church in America. Don organized Churches Vitalized, Inc, a consulting firm, in 1972. In 1982, Geneva College awarded Don an honorary doctorate for his "ability to combine a behavioral engineering approach with the ministry." He authored seven books and continued to teach courses in practical theology at Covenant Seminary, where his life experiences and wisdom prepared students for the realities of the pastorate. He had a keen interest in the history of the conservative Presbyterian and Reformed movement in North America.

In 1974 Don was asked to serve on the Board of Directors for Life Care Retirement Facilities. He also served on the boards of Friendship Villages, West and South, and on the PCA Historical Center Committee until his death, March 3, 2001.

(1) The PARTNERSHIP SHARE is that portion of the approved expense budget which is dependent on contributions from the PCA churches and individuals. (3) The PCA Office building budget is not included in the PARTNERSHIP SHARE. Gifts for the Office Building Fund are over and above the PARTNERSHIP SHARE and benefit all of the committees and agencies in Atlanta.

(4) The Mission to the World PARTNERSHIP SHARE includes the consolidated portion of the MTW budget identified as Unrestricted or General Fund. It does not include any items which are considered Restricted or "donor designated" funds, including, but not limited to, such items as: missionary support, program ministry and project budgets, etc.

ATTACHMENT F

administrative committee
proposed budget 2002

budget philosophy
The budget is built primarily on the job description of the Stated Clerk in the RAO,
which determines the services that are to be provided by the Office of the Stated Clerk to
the churches, presbyteries, committees and agencies, and to the General Assembly.

GENERAL COMMENTS
Many of the activities and responsibilities of the Administrative Committee are
directly affected by the size and growth of the PCA, which in turn are reflected in annual
budget increases for many line items. The economic inflation rate also affects most budget
items and is applied to most line items, except as noted in the Economic Assumptions
shown below.

The budgets are presented in a format to comply with the standards for not-forprofit organizations adopted by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB). The
FASB standards provide a definition of “supporting activities” which they call
“management and general”. Therefore, compensation for the Stated Clerk and his staff is
allocated according to the estimated time spent by each person in “program.”
administration, and fund raising areas.

3.0% PCA growth rate

2.9% Atlanta Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
3.4% National

10.0 % Health Insurance premiums (Per IAR; Atlanta is 5.1%)
3.1 % Transportation

The Full Time Equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and end of
the year will be 11.75.

0% Rent remains at $14.00 per square foot for 2002

<a id="ga29-p404"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=406 printed_page=404 -->

CHRISTIAN EDUCATION &amp; PUBLICATIONS
PROPOSED BUDGET 2002

## Philosophy:

CE&amp;P's ministry program is driven by the Strategic Faith Plan. The plan defines CE&amp;P's mission, goals, strategies and programs. The proposed budget is a "program-based" budget and represents the resources needed to adequately fund the planned activities, which have been approved and/or directed by the Permanent Committee and the General Assembly.

## Summary:

The proposed 2002 expense budget represents a total increase of $140,960 or 5.20% from the 2001 budget. CE&amp;P's contribution need increases by $75,960 or 4.51%. These increases are detailed and explained below.

## Assumptions:

Underlying assumptions include: 1) the PCA will grow between 3% and 5%; 2) the consumer price index or inflation rate will be approximately 4%; 3) Occupancy cost will remain at $14 per square foot (unless the building capital campaign raises the remaining balance); and 4) health insurance premiums are expected to increase 10%. Both the approved budget for 2001 and the proposed budget for 2002 are based on 24.55 full time equivalent employees. Currently, CE&amp;P is not fully staffed so no increase is anticipated.

## Notes:

- Other Revenues (line 2) for 2002 are anticipated as follows:

|  Bookstore Sales/Postage | $725,000  |
| --- | --- |
|  Seminar/Conference Fees | $182,000  |
|  Subscriptions and Advertising | $88,000  |
|  Video Library Memberships | $50,000  |
|  Reimbursements (travel, postage, etc.) | $45,000  |
|  Total | $1,090,000  |

- Training, Conferences and Consulting (page 2, line 3) represents a sustained increase that began in 1999 with a conference schedule of more than 15 conferences and 50 seminars and continues into 2001 with the Mercy Ministry conference. CE&amp;P anticipates meeting the growing demand for training through regional multi-purpose conferences and a full complement of specialized training events. The Regional Trainers become more strategic in extending CE&amp;P's reach to the local church.

- The Women in the Church (page 2, line 4) is anticipated to increase at a higher than average rate due to increased size and scope of the annual training meeting for women in leadership. Also, CE&amp;P has recently added ten trainers to help multiply the denominational WIC ministry.

<a id="ga29-p407"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=407 printed_page=null -->

- CE&amp;P continues, in a limited way, to help local churches that request assistance in developing *Men's Ministries* (page 2, line 5). Currently, most of CE&amp;P's ministry to men occurs through officer training events and consultation.

- Multi-cultural ministries (page 2, line 6) remains the same as the 2001 budget as CE&amp;P believes it would be most effective to contract with various consultants who have expertise working with different ethnic groups.

- The significant increase in *Youth Ministries* (page 2, line 7) represents CE&amp;P's assimilation of Presbyterian Youth of America. The expenses associated with CE&amp;P's management of these conferences are offset by a corresponding increase in *Revenue* (page 2, line 2). This increase is likewise represented in *Meeting Facility*, *Special Events*, *Travel* and *Honorariums* (page 1, lines 33, 38, 22, 35.) CE&amp;P will continue to develop the network of regional youth trainers and anticipates additional participants in the internship program.

- While CE&amp;P has always provided training to those who work with children, CE&amp;P is committed to establishing a *Children's Ministries* department (page 2, line 8) with a staff person to assist PCA churches in training workers, staffing positions and identifying resources available to them. The slight decrease is due to reevaluation of staffing needs for this department.

- Publications and Curriculum (page 2, line 9) includes the periodicals *Equip for Ministry* and the *Bulletin Supplement* and a portion of the *WIC Resource Letter*. It also includes the costs associated with developing and producing other annual materials for Stewardship season, PCA Week of Prayer and Christian Education Sunday as well as other video series and training manuals.

- The Bookstore (page 2, line 10) is the largest portion of the proposed budget. The increase of the Bookstore budget represents additional resources needed to further enhance the Bookstore's product offerings, services and related promotion.

- Management and General (page 2, line 12) is anticipated to increase as there will likely be unanticipated expenses associated with the office relocation which were not included in the 2001 budget and will need to be addressed in 2002.

- The decrease in *Fund Raising* (page 2, line 15) is due to the decision of CE&amp;P to not renew the contract with a marketing consulting firm and instead pursue a plan of regionalized development representatives. Also see *Professional Services* (page 1, line 21). The 2002 budget represents two individuals being in place along with current CE&amp;P staff development efforts.

- Though CE&amp;P has temporarily been relieved of the responsibility of funding a portion of the PCA Foundation (page 2, line 16 and page 1, line 30), CE&amp;P's management earnestly desires to participate in this program once again if funds are available.

- The Coordinator, his assistant and related expenses are allocated to the various expense categories as follows: Training 20%, Fund Raising 10%, Administration 10%, Bookstore 15%, WIC 10%, Men's Ministry 5%, Youth Ministries 10%, Publications and Curriculum 20%.
