COVENANT CO LLEGE
PROPOSED BUDGET 2001 - 2002

The college uses the NACUBO (National Association of College and University
Business Officers) definitions of revenue and expense categories. This insures that the
college will be able to directly compare various ratios with other colleges and assess our
effectiveness in accordance with our assessment systems. While the categories do not
exactly parallel the definitions used by the Accounting and Financial Reporting Guide for
Christian Ministries, there is some similarity. NACUBO categories including
Instructional, Academic Support, Library, Student Services, Public Service and Student
Aid could be broadly considered "Program Services.” Maintenance of Plant and
Institutional Support could be considered "Supporting Activities."
Several major accounting changes were made in 1996-1997 in accordance with
changes in standards published by the Financial Accounting Standards Board. Included in
these are two that have significantly changed the figures shown from previous years and
made comparisons with those years unproductive. Therefore, the proposed budget
spreadsheets show only years 1996-1997 and following.
First, depreciation is now an operating expense rather than an expense only in the
plant fund. the requirement to fund depreciation comes from a variety of directions.
Funding depreciation is a good thing for an organization as it provides funds to repair and
replace facilities as they age without additional fund raising. Generally accepted
accounting principles also require depreciation to be accounted for as an operating expense
and these rules must be followed to obtain an external audit in good standing. Finally, the
accrediting body in which Covenant participates requires a balanced bottom line for
continued accreditation, including depreciation. For these three reasons, the college must
actually fund the depreciation expense from increased revenues. Due to this change, tuition
will rise 3% more than otherwise would be the case for the next several years. These
tuition increases will fund depreciation, allow Covenant to show a net positive bottom line
and reduce pressure on future capital campaigns by providing funds for facility and
equipment replacement and repair. This expense is shown on the expense spreadsheet
divided between Maintenance of Plant Depreciation, representing the depreciation costs of
the facilities used for educational purposes, and Auxiliary Depreciation, representing the
depreciation costs of the residence halls, food service and for-profit facilities.
Second, since capital gifts are shown as revenue in the year they are used for
construction, but the construction expense only appears over time through depreciation,
capital projects tend to positively overstate the operating results of the college. In order to
show the results more clearly, the operating net results for the college are shown on the
expense budget at the bottom of the page on the line titled “Increase (Decrease) in
Undesignated Net Assets." This line will show a negative figure until the completion of
funding the depreciation expense through the tuition increases mentioned in the previous
paragraph.

COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY
NEW BUDGET HIGHLIGHTS
FY 2001-02

I. Revenue:
A. Tuition and Fees:
1. Tuition in most programs will increase from $250 per credit hour to
$260. This 4% increase is a little more than last year (3.3%) and the
second lowest since at least 1990. Because we plan to discontinue the
discount for any hours over 14 in a semester, the annual tuition for a
student taking a full 30 hours would be $7,800. Tuition will pay about
41.9% of the cost to educate a seminarian. This is down from 42.2%
this year.
2. We are projecting enrollment in the Fall of 2001 to be 4 full time
equivalents higher, although head count will be down.

B. Endowment: Earnings used after management expenses are budgeted to be
7.2%. This is up from 6.63% actually experienced for FY99-00 and the same
as was budgeted for FY00-01. Endowment income in all areas (unrestricted,
apartments, and student aid) will increase by $35,000, versus $223,600 for
this year and $396,000 the year before.

C. Gifts & Grants: The Gifts & Grants goal (PCA Partnership Share) will
increase from $1,760,000 to $1,785,000. this is an increase of 1.42% and
follows an increase of $30,000 or 1.73% for this year. This gifts & grants
goal is an increasingly smaller percentage of the total operating budget
compared to other typical seminaries. We are projecting Restricted Gifts of
$233,000 for next year, up from $215,000 for this year. The increase is for
Art @ The Institute, a program of the Francis Schaeffer Institute.

D. Student Aid: This source of income is expected to remain flat at $219,400.
These funds come from Endowment earnings.

E. Other Income: We expect this source of income to decrease to $182,350 in
the new year from $207,350 in the current year. Most of this decrease is
from lower interest income due to funds held for the Youth In Ministry
Institute, as those funds are expended. The interest income is unrestricted.

F. Auxiliary Enterprises: Rents for the apartments will see smaller increases
this year, as shown below. This follows about a 3% increase for this year.

Single student housing will be increasing about 3% for single occupancy and
4% for double occupancy. The additional income from all rents will be
about $7,370. We are making a conscious effort to keep our rents market-
competitive, while minimizing overall costs to our seminary students.

<a id="ga29-p414"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=416 printed_page=414 -->

‘00-~01 Rent ‘01 -'02 Rent Increase
2-Bedroom $535 $545 1.87%
3-Bedroom $650 $650 0%

II. Expenditures:
A. General:
1. The budget assumes overall salary increases of 3% for staff and 8% for
faculty.
2. Medical Insurance premiums will change July 1, 2001. Preliminary
quotes reflect a 15% increase, which has been the experience of other
local employers.
3. General expenses (where appropriate) were held flat. The tight budget
will mean that departments will have to reduce usage overall to allow
for expected price inflation of about 3%
4. The Youth in Ministry Institute (YIMI) expenses are not shown in the
budget since they are covered by restricted gifts also not shown.

B. Educational & General:
1. President & Trustees: A small provision is made to help the President
deal with the lack of equity build-up in a home, since his family resides
in seminary housing that would be relinquished at his retirement.
2. Instruction - General Budget:. A full year’s salary and benefits are
reflected for a new professor to be added in July. A part-time faculty
secretary position shared with D.Min. is converted to full-time. Faculty
increases average 8%, due to the higher salaries now prevalent at similar
institutions.
3. Instruction - doctor of Ministry: Reflects salary increases and a
portion of the faculty secretary position just described above.
4. Instruction - master of Theology: Provision is made for 1 more class.
5. Instruction - Evening Program: Budgeted for 17 3-hour classes at
$725 per credit hour.
6. Instruction - Extension Program: Increases for printing and computer
hardware
7. Instruction - Counseling: Provision is made for professional
advancement and furnishings for new counseling rooms.
8. Instruction - Francis Schaeffer Institute: Increases reflect the part-
time person administering Art @ The Institute, which is covered by
restricted gifts.
9. Instruction - Church Planting: Reflects increases in salaries and
benefits.
10. Registrar’s Office: Provision is made for fabrication of new
graduation hoods.
11. Library: Provision is made for some additional staffing.
12. Student Services: Normal increases

<a id="ga29-p415"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=417 printed_page=415 -->

13. Family Nurture: Normal increases
14. Student Aid:

- **a.** Administration - Normal increases
- **b.** Scholarships - Normal increase, considering enrollment projections and this year’s experience.

15. Development: Normal increases
16. Public Relations: Unchanged
17. Gift & Estate Planning: Normal increases
18. Admissions: Provision for staffing changes
19. Electronic Media Production: Retirement provision for new director;
a reduction in expenses for media production equipment
20. Business Office: Some staffing changes and a higher provision for
legal services
21 . Computer Services: A reduction in staffing and an increased provision
for new network servers
22. Physical Plant: Increases include more student labor for housekeeping
due to the new library, and higher utilities and insurance expenses based
on experience.

C. Auxiliary Enterprises:
1. Operations: Increases for utilities
2. Student Apartments: Increases for utilities and insurance
3. tape of the month Club: Normal adjustments

D. Transfers:
1. Educational & General:
a. Money is annually transferred to the Plant Fund so that we can
replace the President's auto every four to five years. Also, $13,410 is
transferred to Renewal and Debt to amortize the expenses associated
with the Administrative Software Project. This project will be fully
amortized in July, 2005.

2. Auxiliary Enterprises:
a. $36,000 is set aside as reserves for future apartment repairs.

E. Contingency: The provision is for $ 12,000.

# INSURANCE ANNUITIES, AND RELIEF PROPOSED BUDGET 2002

## Statement of Purpose

The purpose of the Board of Trustees of Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God through serving His servants who minister in the Church by offering financial products to provide for the health and welfare, retirement and relief needs of the denomination’s ministers, missionaries, lay church workers and their families, and to serve the churches, committees and agencies of the PCA and related organizations by sponsoring and endorsing a denominational property and liability insurance program.

- This budget reflects the costs incurred to administer the trust funds for Insurance, Retirement, and Relief. This budget does not reflect the financial activity in those trust funds. (Complete financial activity in the trust funds may be found in the IAR Annual Report, which includes audited financial statements.)

- The 2002 budget reflects an increase of 17.95% over the 2001 budget. 2000 actuals are unaudited.

- The increase in the Retirement portion of Support and Revenue is a more accurate representation of the allocation of expenses between supporting activities. The increase in the Relief portion of Support and Revenue reflects costs associated with moving the Relief virtual office back to the PCA building and transition costs for hiring and training a new Relief Representative.

- The total number of staff budgeted for in 2002 is 10 FTE. Currently, ten of these positions are filled.

- An overall net increase of 9.6% in salaries and benefits is assumed in 2002. Budgeted positions include a 4% average salary increase and a 10% health insurance increase. This increase does not include the positions discussed in the next bulleted item.

- Because the Director and the Relief Representative will retire at the end of 2002, we have budgeted salary and benefits for the new Director at three months overlap and for the new Relief Representative at five months overlap.

- An overall net increase of 39.33% in rent is assumed in 2002 due to the additional square footage utilized by IAR in the new facility.

- An overall net increase of 20% in board meeting expense is assumed in 2002. Due to the building relocation, IAR board meetings will no longer be held at the current or the new PCA building. We also anticipate increased travel expense based on the probable composition of the Board of Trustees.

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=423 printed_page=421 -->

MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
PROPOSED BUDGET 2002

The MNA Mission:

This budget reflects our continuing emphasis on regionalization — assisting and working with
presbyteries to develop church planting in local areas.

Due to the impending transition of Reformed University Ministries to separate committee
status, the budget for 2002 is being presented without the revenue and expense associated with
campus ministries. This is creating a decrease of 9.4% compared to the 2001 budget which
included campus ministries.

While the total revenue and expenses will decrease 9.4% compared to the 2001 budget, the
portion included in the “partnership share” will decrease only 2%. These decreases are the
result of Reformed University Ministries’ budget being removed from the overall GA/MNA
budget. The Partnership Share only decreases by 2%, because a lesser portion of the RUM
budget is covered by Partnership Share than the GA/MNA budget. The remainder of the RUM
budget is covered by affiliated campus transfers.

An overall net increase of 5% in salaries and benefits is assumed. That is an aggregate of cost
of living, merit increases and health insurance costs.

The total number of multicultural missionaries budgeted for is 4. Between 59 and 77 projects
are budgeted to receive direct support in church planting, multicultural church planting and
urban and mercy ministries.

The total number of full-time equivalent staff budgeted for in the 2002 budget is 30-1/2, which
is the same as in the 2001 budget. Twenty-nine and one-half of these positions are currently

filled. The unfilled position is for an assistant coordinator for church planting.

The cost being charged by the Administrative Committee for office space is assumed to be at
$14 per square foot; more square footage will be occupied in the new building.

The complete and separate 2002 budget for Reformed University Ministries (MNA Campus
Ministries and the affiliated presbytery campus committees) is provided.

MISSION TO THE WORLD
MTW docket memo

TO: Finance/Management Subcommittee
FROM: Roger R. Kooistra
DATE: February 13. 2001

SUBJECT: Mission to the World Proposed Budget for 2002*

The proposed Mission to the World Consolidated Operating Budget for the calendar year
2002 is presented on the following pages. The consolidation includes career missionaries
and projects. MTW/IMPACT, the Home Office, the Medial Insurance Fund (MIF) and the
Endowment Fund.

The Presbyterian Church in America Administrative Committee has scheduled the review
of budgets to take place March 15, 2001.

RECOMMENDATION: That the Proposed Consolidated 2002 Budget for Mission to the
World, including MTW/IMPACT. the Medical Insurance Fund and the Endowment Fund
be approved for the calendar year 2002 in the amount of $37.696.015 for expenses and the
MTW Capital Budget be approved for the calendar year 2002 in the amount of $90.000 for
presentation to the PCA Administrative Committee.

RRK/jm

MISSION TO THE WORLD
PROPOSED CONSOLIDATED 2002 BUDGET

I. methodology and background :

the 2 0 0 2 budget is proposed from an analysis of key factors that influence the income and
expenses of mission to the world operating ina global context with a rapidly changing global
economy . we reviewed the results of 2 0 0 0 and extended these indicators into 2001 and 2 002.

the year 2 0 0 0 saw the us dollar remain relatively strong throughout the year gaining slightly
against the euro and they en. for mission work this can sometimes be help fu l. our overseas
missionary expense sinus dollars ended the year at approximately ninety nine percent of budge t,
which included some expansion to program con ten t. however, we saw a faltering us economy

<a id="ga29-p425"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=427 printed_page=425 -->

i loss in the value of the stock market. The economic environment was positive at the
ling of the year, but slowed as the year went on. This pattern had little impact on
jutions from churches and individuals to our missionaries and programs, but could impact
ar 2001. We continued to see additional gifts and trusts coming into the endowment fund,
were three items of income for the new building program that totaled $3,200,000, which are
pected to repeat. The total income for the year was just under one hundred twelve percent of
t.

nbering that it is all by the grace of God 2000 was a very positive year for Mission to the
. The cooling US economy, a strong dollar, a weak stock market, a growing base of home
les, and a stable force of missionaries all made their mark on MTW.

iture Directions for 2002:

are several major goals coming from MTW’s strategic direction that will be part of the 2002
im. The church-planting center concept has begun to appear in various forms during 2001
the direction of missionary Bill Goodman in Mexico City. We plan to implement two or
:raining programs in 2001 and continue to expand in 2002. The goals of The Global Training
velopment (GTD) group are to actively support and involve our missionaries and national
s in proven methods and best practices in promoting church-planting movements.

rogram for National Worker’s Training, which received initial funding in 1997, will be fully
:ing in 2001 and 2002. The expanded involvement of national workers in church planting is a
part of the future vision developed by the New Horizon Project.

al new countries were approved in 2000 and more are part of the expansion plan for ministry,
fields are being opened with short-term teams and mercy-ministry teams going in first to
ish a link with existing national churches and ministries.

unctional redesign taskforce report was completed and approved in 2000. The Committee
ved the expansion from two Area Directors to five International Directors for our field
ed efforts in the home office. Plans in the home office are to strengthen ongoing support of
issionary staff because the world is undergoing such rapid change. Other increases in home
staff in 2001 and 2002 are for short-term programs that are projected to expand, church
>ns and administrative growth to keep pace with the ministry growth. A substantial portion of
rowth is planned to come from designated funding arranged by the Partner Services Group.

for information technology in 2002 focus mainly on better utilization of global
lunications (the Internet and an MTW Intranet) and the integration and sharing of data
;en the donor services system, the human resource information system, the contact
gement/recruiting system and the short-term project management system. No major hardware
ision is planned, but normal replacement of older equipment is planned.

r development efforts will continue to focus on raising endowment funds. The endowment
le will go to reduce the administrative assessment and to fund major new programs like the
h-planting center. Our partner relations teams have also set goals to continue to strengthen
onships with churches who are the major revenue source for MTW and a growing factor in
ng the home office.

<a id="ga29-p426"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=428 printed_page=426 -->

## III. Projection Base for 2002:

Projections have been made regarding the number of missionaries, home office staff, annual income and annual expenses. In making these projections the following assumptions have been used:

We anticipate that continued efforts to recruit missionaries in 2001 will show additional results during 2002. The number of career missionaries is projected to grow to 545, two-year missionaries to 155, internship missionaries to 300 and two-week missionaries to 7000.

We will have limited home office staff increases in 2002 for the ministry initiatives that have recently started and for growth in our short-term missions programs.

We anticipate that the US dollar will continue at its current value level. We expect other global economic factors to be unstable, with higher inflation than here in the US, but somewhat offset by currency devaluation.

Income has grown from $7.8 million in 1985 to $36.2 million in 2000 and is projected to be $38 million in 2002. Income projections have assumed a continuing strong support for missionaries from churches and individuals in a reasonably stable US economy and in a gradually growing PCA denomination. We have projected the support requirements of missionaries, adjusted for inflation, as a basis for future income. For expenses, the combined effect of salary adjustments, growth and currency value, using historic trends indicates an increase of approximately 5 percent for 2001 and 4 to 5 percent for 2002.

Missionary support accounts with deficit balances continue to remain low. Total deficits for all missionaries have gone from $400,000 in 1994 down to only $73,015 in 2000 indicating the strong support of MTW’s ministry partners.

Partnership share giving for the home office grew from $300,000 in 1994 to $1,107,000 in 2000 and is projected to grow to $1,200,000 in 2002. We have assumed that good church relations and increased services to churches will allow a gradual annual increase in partnership share giving.

Project and field income is calculated as a part of the support requirements of missionaries and we expect a 4 to 5 percent growth in 2002.

Investment income projections assume that interest rates will remain stable near their current levels. In 2002, we will see some endowment earnings available as needed in the general fund and the church-planting center (Global Training &amp; Development) to cover operational costs. In addition, the GTD has set aside funds for the next two years operations.

The 2002 proposed budget for IMPACT is based on a summer program of 7000 students, an internship program of 300 persons and a two-year missionary staff of 155 missionaries. The following major factors form the basis for projecting the income and expenses for IMPACT:

A. Office Staff—The cost in the home office reflect several changes and new staff added during 2000. Four additional full-time equivalent positions are added for 2002.

<a id="ga29-p427"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=429 printed_page=427 -->

B. Expanded programs in most areas will generate both higher income and expenses that are being designed to stay in balance.

The medical insurance fund (MIF) had an above average expense year in 2000. We expect that costs will increase slightly faster than inflation. The operating capital reserve showed a slight loss in 2000 and will produce very little investment income in 2002.

With the income from partnership share giving leveling off at about 25% of the requested amount, we project that the fixed monthly administrative assessment charge per missionary will start increasing in future years. We anticipate that this will be based on the South Consumer Price Index rate each year.

Major programs that will influence expenses in 2002 are the church-planting movement with training and mentoring, the StreetChild ministry, mercy/medical ministry and the continued growth of the summer two-week program.

There are three tables showing the consolidated income and expenses budget proposed for 2002. The first table presents a historical perspective showing 2000, 2001 approved at General Assembly and 2002 information. The second shows a functional breakdown between program, administration and fund raising. The third table shows changes in budget from 2001 to 2002.

In addition to the income and expense budget, the capital expense budget is presented on the last table in the amount of $90,000. It assumes all major facility changes for the new building will be completed in 2001. The major items to be purchased relate to information technology, improved telecommunication and some office reconfigurations to maximize space utilization for staff growth.

PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC.
2002 PROPOSED BUDGET SUMMARY

GENERAL COMMENTS:

The PCA Foundation has shown significant growth in the last several years due to the re
engineering that took place in 1997-1998. Also, as a result, the Foundation successfully
achieved self-supporting status in 2000. Gifts, distributions and total assets have
increased considerably. This has resulted in actual and projected increases in Support and
Revenue. Likewise, the expenses necessary to operate the Foundation and implement new
initiatives have and will continue to increase accordingly.

NOTES TO NATURAL EXPENSE STATEMENT - (Notes generally relate to notable
comparisons of 2002 Budget to 2001 Budget.)

SUPPORT & REVENUE

UNDESIGNATED EARNINGS (line 1) - These are earnings on funds held by the
PCA Foundation, mainly from Advise & Consult Funds and the PCAF
Endowment, which help underwrite the Foundation’s operating expenses. The
2002 Budget of $455,000 represents a 30% increase over the 2001 amount
budgeted. This increase is primarily the result of the past and projected future
growth of A&C Funds.

C & A SUPPORT (line 2) - General Assembly mandated support from the four
remaining Committees and Agencies (Covenant College, Covenant Theological
Seminary, Mission to North America, and Mission to the World) was eliminated in
2000. Their mandatory support in 1999 was $15,000 each, for a total of $60,000.
having been reduced from $30,000 each in 1998, $34,000 each in 1997 (after a
$10,000 refund to each), and $44,000 each in 1996. The Foundation has now
successfully achieved self-supporting status.

FEES (line 3) - There are two components of the 2002 budgeted fees: 1)
Administrative fees charged on funds held for long term administration such as
Charitable Remainder Trusts, Endowments, Annuities, Designated Funds, etc., and
2) Investment management fees on the accounts held by the Foundation. The 2002
budgeted increase ($67,000) over the 2001 Budget is warranted due to an
expected increase ($7,000) in administrative fees resulting from an increase in the
number of charitable remainder trusts and endowments and new, first time,
investment management fees ($60,000) anticipated to be received beginning in
2002. The Foundation’s lead bank presently provides investment management
services for those Foundation accounts held in custody at the bank and those
accounts are charged accordingly. The PCAF anticipates that, beginning in 2002,

<a id="ga29-p431"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=433 printed_page=431 -->

it may begin performing the investment management of these accounts itself, and
accordingly, the PCAF operating fund would then receive the investment
management fee income. In the event that this occurs, there would be an offsetting
operating expense associated with performing this function and, accordingly, the
2002 Budget also includes $60,000 for investment management expense.

CONTRIBUTIONS (line 6) - Gifts made to help underwrite the Foundation
Operating Budget. the budgeted amount of $150,000 is due primarily to a
$60,000 matching challenge contribution and the corresponding matches for a
period of 10 years. These contributions helped provide the resources necessary for
the Foundation to successfully achieve self-supported status in 2000.

OPERATIONS EXPENSES

STAFF WAGES & BENEFITS (line 8) - 2002 Budget reflects compensation
increases of 5% and the recent hiring of an individual for the position of VP-
Development. The 2001 Budget reflected expenses relating to a development
position as part of Professional Services expense (line 10).

TRAVEL EXPENSES (line 9) - 2002 budgeted increase compared to 2001 Budget
is primarily due to travel expenses associated with the new VP-Development
position, and monthly payments for a new automobile for President (present
automobile provided to President is fully paid for and will be used by VP-
Development in 2002).

PROFESSIONAL SERVICES (line 10) - 2002 Budget includes $60,000 for
investment management expenses not previously budgeted, but which may be
incurred beginning in 2002. These costs to be funded by new investment
management fees budgeted in Support and Revenue. (See explanation for Fees -
line 3 above.)

2002 Budget does not include $61,000 for development expense fees, which had
been budgeted on this line in 2001. (See explanation for Staff Wages & Benefits -
line 8 above.)

PROMOTIONAL EXPENSES (line 1 1 )- 2002 budgeted increase primarily due to
the use of a marketing consultant, not budgeted for in 2001 Budget.

RENT (line 15) - 2002 Budget increase due to increase in space rented in new
PCA Building (occupancy estimated in August, 2001). Space is budgeted at
$ 14.00/sq. foot, the same rate used in the 2001 Budget. The actual charge per
square foot may be less, depending on final results of the campaign to obtain
funding for the new PCA Building.

REFORMED UNIVERSITY MINISTRIES
PROPOSED BUDGET 2002

The RUM Mission:

Reformed University Ministries has the goal of building the church now and for the future
by reaching students for Christ and equipping students to serve. This is accomplished by
supporting the RUF works of presbyteries and churches in the areas of administration,
finance, development, intern program, training, conferences, recruiting, and general
ministry operation.

• This budget reflects our continuing growth as we assist and work with presbyteries and
churchcs to develop new RUF works on campuses nationwide. For 2002 we project to
be on 72 campuses with well over 100 field staff, including 58 interns.

• The total expense growth of 36.6% can be attributed to ministry support growth, new
staff additions, permanent committee formation and new building rent increase.

• The total number of full-time equivalent staff budgeted for 2002 is 10. This is two
more than in the 2001 budget. eight of these positions are currently filled. The
unfilled positions are an Assistant Coordinator and a receptionist.

• An overall net increase of 5% in salaries and related adjustments to benefits is assumed
for all existing staff positions. This is an aggregate of cost of living and merit
increases.

• Permanent Committee formation costs make up most of the 46.4% expense increase of
Support Services. This includes significant additions of such items as printing,
postage, equipment, database maintenance, supplies. General Assembly relations and
committee expenses.

• The cost being charged by the Administrative Committee for office space is assumed to
be at $15 per square foot; more square footage will be occupied in the new building.

• The 201.5% increase in budgeted Contributions is partially attributed to the loss of
transfers from MNA of $100,000.

• The 2002 Budget for the entire ministry, including affiliated committees is included in
the RUM General Assembly report for information.

RIDGE HAVEN CONFERENCE CENTER
PROPOSED BUDGET 2002

Introduction
Though the Ridge Haven budget has continued to expand reflecting our growth in ministry
and service, our partnership share needs have continued to decrease since 1999. From our
perspective these two trends seem an unlikely pair, thus worthy of note and praise to the
Lord. This phenomenon certainly reflects the Lord’s blessing both in an increasing
awareness of the value of the ministry and opportunities that Ridge Haven is providing to
the PCA and Christian community at large, and how by His protection, good stewardship
of all resources, and the spreading of fixed costs over a greater number of users, we are
able to control expenses. The 2002 budget reflects our trust in Him and His continuing
blessing.

We would be negligent however, if we did not express our continuing need for the
churches of the PCA to support Ridge Haven! The opportunities available and the on
going needs continuing to face us are not less but greater. The mandate of the State of
North Carolina for the upgrading of our water system (estimated to be $300,000), the need
for facility renovations (estimated to be a minimum of $200,000), and our desire to provide
new and enlarged facilities to serve the growing opportunities (especially a new cafeteria),
are only a part of our needs. A review of the 2000 budget verses actual figures (especially
line items 30, 34, 35, 40, 44, and 45) shows that in some areas of maintenance,
improvements, and ministry the money is not there to spend due to the partnership share
not being met. From the human perspective it is extremely difficult if not impossible for
us to meet these needs with partnership share giving that since 1999 has continued less
than 25%, the lowest of all the Committees and Agencies. As the 2002 Ridge Haven
budget is considered, we ask the Church to join us in praying for the Lord to open the
windows of heaven and pour out a blessing that cannot be contained, not only a blessing
for Ridge Haven but on all of the Presbyterian Church in America.

Notes
1. Line 2, C/C (Camps/Conferences) Facilities - During 2000 this category was
discontinued with the income being added/included into the C/C registrations
category, line 1. In future budgets the line item will disappear all together.

2. Line 5. Resident Leases - This category includes income from lot leases, lot lease
interest, lot lease maintenance fees (the administrative amount we charge for
keeping all lot information on file and current), water hookups, water usage fees
and road maintenance fees. The amount projected for 2002 is the actual amount
expected without lot sales or water hookup fees of which we have no guarantee or
expectation of income. The budgeted amount for 2001 should also have been
$23,474.

<a id="ga29-p438"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=440 printed_page=438 -->

3. Line 6, Other Contributions - The category includes contributions from WIC
groups, other groups, and individuals toward the daily operations of Ridge Haven.

4. Line 7. Non-medical Reimbursements - The category includes amounts paid to
Ridge Haven by individuals or groups for the use of Ridge Haven postage, office
copier, vehicle parts, fuel, pay phones, etc.

5. Line 13, Sale of Assets - The category reflects the liquidation of material items no
longer needed. The amounts reported are unpredictable. there is no certainty of
income from the sale of assets from year-to-year.

6. Line 17, Other Salary/Benefits - Beginning in 2000, payroll taxes (also line 22)
have been included in/with salaries and benefits. In future budgets the line item
will disappear all together.

The Ridge Haven staff consists of a total of 9 year-around employees. The
Administrator, Administrative Assistant, Ministry Director, Maintenance
Superintendent, and Food Service Director are salaried, full-time employees. The
Accounting Manager, Food Service Assistant Director, and two House Keeping
staff are all year-around part-time hourly paid employees. In reccnt months we
have had a part-time office assistant and maintenance assistant. The present (2001)
budget allows for both of these positions to be full-time as employees are available.
The present (2001) budget also includes temporary food service staff and allows for
the addition of a year-around but part-time Resident Manager. The amount
provided in the 2002 budget reflects the addition of a Camps Director (intern
status), increased compensation for the Maintenance Superintendent bringing him
up to the average compensation level for employees in comparable positions in
other area camps and conference ministries, for the Food Service Assistant Director
to become a full-time salaried position, and a 3% cost-of-living increase for all
other employees.

7. Line 19, Counselor and Camp Support Staff - The budgeted figure provides for an
amount needed to allow us to remain competitive in compensating counselors and
support staff and adding additional counselors and support staff for larger camps
and a junior counselor program.

8. Line 21. Workers’ Comp - The amount reflects the corresponding cost for
increasing compensation and added staff identified in the line 17 note.

9. Line 22. Payroll Taxes - Beginning in 2000 payroll taxes were included in/with
salaries and benefits with the exception of one employment position that has now
been discontinued.

<a id="ga29-p439"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=441 printed_page=439 -->

10. Line 23, Other Fees - This category includes North Carolina Fees (usually a small
amount). Miscellaneous fees (also usually a small amount), Solid Waste Fees, Pool
Fees (the amounts we pay for county inspection at the beginning of each season),
etc. The 2000 Actual amount was inflated by a one-time loan-origination fee
created by the establishment of a line of credit to finance the water system upgrades
approved by the 2000 General Assembly.

11. Line 25, Planning/Promotion/and Recruiting - This category includes all printing
costs.

12. Line 34, Equipment/Furnishings - We continue to need additional equipment and
furnishings for several of our buildings. These items are purchased as the money
becomes available.

13. Line 35, Grounds - We have several erosion and landscape projects we are seeking
to implement. The amount indicated will allow us to do these as the money comes
in.

14. Line 40, Vehicles - Ridge Haven owns and operates a fleet of 8 vehicles. The
budgeted amount allows for us to repair and/or replace aging and/or failing vehicles
as the money comes in.

15. Line 45, Facilities Renovation - All 21 of the Ridge Haven buildings/facilities need
renovations from time-to-time that are not otherwise provided for in the day-to-day
maintenance category. The amount provided in this category reflects renovations
for 9 different buildings that could use immediate attention. The renovations
desired were professionally estimated.

16. Line 46, Water System - The decrease in the budgeted amount reflects the actual
projected cost of maintenance for the water system once the Assembly's approved
$300,000 upgrading project mandated by the State of North Carolina is complete.

17. Line 47, Water System Loan - This budgeted amount will allow us to repay the
money borrowed in the event that contributed funds are not sufficient to cover
upgrading costs as they are progressing.

18. Line 48, New Programming - The addition of new conferences and/or other similar
ministries requires an upfront investment of about $10,000-$ 12,000 per conference.
It takes approximately three years for a conference to reach the "break-even" point.
At least two new conferences are being planned for 2002.

<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=442 printed_page=440 -->

<a id="ga29-p442"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=444 printed_page=442 -->

CHRISTIAN EDUCATION AND PUBLICATIONS
REPORT to the
29™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY

introduction

As we present this report to the 29"’ P.C.A. General Assembly, we are more aware than
ever of the challenge and urgent need for g od’s people to be trained and equipped in the
Christian faith. We are aware that in our North Am erican culture there is no distinctly
Christian mind to help us navigate the pluralistic, even secular and postmodern, waters.
Our culture communicates so many uncertainties. more and more choices of more and
more things are being offered to people who are not that equipped in the truth to make
the best choices. Church attendance is beginning to decline for the first time in fifty
years and the dropout rate of the older teenagers is alarming.

The Presbyterian Church in America does not exist in a cultural vacuum; and, as part of
the church of jesus Christ, it is not supposed to. The Bible describes the church as the
“ground and pillar of truth,” and yet its influence is being marginalized either by
withdrawal and isolation from the world or by becoming too much like the world.
either extreme neutralizes the potential effectiveness of the church. Somehow, even in
the more evangelical churches, people have either lost their connection between what
the Bible teaches and how they live their daily lives, or they never saw the connection in
the first place. Our own surveys, as well as other studies are revealing that the younger
generation is not impressed with a church that chooses either of the two above extremes.

one of the things that has concerned the christian education and publications
committee and staff is the lack of cohesion and unity that is being demonstrated by the
church in general and often our own denomination. we are both aware of and involved
in the General Assembly’s strategic planning process, which will hopefully serve to
unify the PCA as we are moving further into the 21s1 century.

as the heart of g od’s kingdom, the church has a growing challenge to prepare and
equip its people to be Kingdom people. The church has a key role in actually
coordinating the training and learning process. Failure to do that only evidences the
tendency for one part of the kingdom activity to work against another. This is definitely
true in the area of education and training. The church, the home, and the school should
have a well-coordinated strategy where each part mutually benefits from the efforts of
the other parts.

<a id="ga29-p443"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=445 printed_page=443 -->

Starting from the Kingdom of God perspective, which is all inclusive of God’s reign and realm, it is important that the church step up to the plate to coordinate these activities. No one of the Kingdom entities can rear covenant children alone. But, how do the various parts come together in a mutually supportive way? In seeking to be a biblically reformed church, by taking the great truths of our reformed tradition and dealing with them contextually, the PCA from its beginning has been committed to a quality of excellence in educating the church’s covenant people, youth, children, and adults.

CE/P’s prayer and desire is that we might continue challenging and assisting the churches in challenging, training, and equipping our people to think and live like Kingdom of God people—to live with a biblically reformed worldview. We believe that part of the church’s present day shallowness and lack of effectiveness are attributed to a sub-standard of training and learning. The people are therefore not as equipped for ministry and not as prepared to live in this kind of world as they should be.

As our summary mission statement says, CE/P exists in the PCA for the purpose of assisting its people to:

know the Lord, know the Word,
know the world, and know themselves;

Through our diversity of programs, we target churches and families in general, and children, youth, and adults specifically. CE/P is committed to that task.

People often ask, “Who is CE/P and what do you do?” The answer is both simple and complex. We are the PCA’s agency of resources and training. Our resources (curriculum, books, media, and variety of other publications) are available to churches and individuals from CE/P and our training services, made up both of events and consulting, are also available. When the Apostle Paul said, “The church grows and builds itself up in love as each part does its work,” he preceded those words with the reminder that God has given to the church those who are to train and equip those people for ministry and Kingdom service. He has also instructed us to pass on His Gospel truth, his story, to the next generation that they might know the wonder and power of God.

It is both our challenge and privilege to be a part of this ministry in the PCA. It is our humble joy and delight to connect our churches, large and small, rural or urban, metropolitan and inner city with peoples and resources to help them be more effective in their ministry. We have experienced in many ways the blessings of God as we have coordinated events, resources, and meetings with local church leaders as well as key lay people across the PCA, some of which you will read about in this report.

<a id="ga29-p444"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=446 printed_page=444 -->

According to reliable polls, the church is losing some of its authenticity and integrity
with the rising generations. That must not be. there are two kinds of people that
motivate us to move forward: those who need to be ministered to and those who need to
see us (the church) ministering to those in need. The late Francis Schaeffer, to whom
the PCA is deeply indebted, said that if the young people do not see the church being
and doing what the church is suppose to be and do, “they will not listen” and “they
should not listen."

Our ministry previewed and summarized in this report represents CE/P’s desire to help
the church, particularly the PCA, create a vision of kingdom consciousness in order to
seethe challenge of the moment and to develop, expand, and network a more effective
ministry in the PCA, as we seek to make the best of training and resources available to
our churches.

the body of this report will be focused on our two main areas— resources and training.

resources

we have two categories of resources that we make available to the PCA and other
interested churches. First, there are the resources that we publish and produce, and
second, the resources that others have developed and made available to us. The CE/P
bookstore and video library are the center of our resources in the area of education and
training.

1. Bookstore

The CE/P bookstore serves around 90% of the PCA churches. Materials can be
purchased using the Internet or our toll free number. Not only does the bookstore carry
the materials produced by CE/P but also the official documents of the PCA, and general
tracts, and brochures on various topics. Discounts are available to PCA churches and
customers. Satisfactory working arrangements with various publishers and distributors
enable the bookstore to quickly process orders and respond to customer needs.

The bookstore personnel are a key to linking churches and people to resources, and
linking CE/P program staff to churches and people, particularly in the area of
recommending resources. Children and youth materials are available, as well as
materials for women in the church, men in the church, and other leadership training
resources.

From the bookstore Website, customers can link to other CE/P services in education and
training. Also, through the bookstore ministry, CE/P has helped numbers of churches
develop their own book tables or has provided some churches with books to make
available at retreats and various events. The CE/P staff attempts to stay abreast of the

<a id="ga29-p445"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=447 printed_page=445 -->

best resources, past, present, and future. A bookstore catalogue is printed annually to make local churches more aware of the variety of resources available to them. As the committee assigned to provide Christian education resources for PCA churches, the CE/P bookstore is committed to meeting and expanding its services and resources in the future. In many ways, all the other services and ministries of CE/P grow out of this central ministry resource.

2. Video Library

The CE/P Video Library annually services over four hundred churches with over six hundred titles including training and educational titles. The video lending library not only provides videotapes to local churches, but also offers counsel as to how best to use videos in education and training. Like the bookstore, the video library publishes a catalogue of available titles. CE/P is planning to expand this resource so that we can serve more churches.

3. Equip for Ministry

The CE/P bi-monthly publication *Equip for Ministry* is one of the three communication centerpieces to keep ideas, resources, and other educational and training needs (especially good books) before the readership. *Equip for Ministry* circulates some 11,000- 12,000 copies bi-monthly, either by individual subscription or by bulk subscription to local churches. *Equip for Ministry* seeks to selectively advertise good resources that can serve PCA churches in a variety of ways. Plus it contains some general information about the PCA that is not available from other sources. CE/P provides a one-year subscription to those who participate in our various training events. *Equip for Ministry* focuses on the whole of God’s covenant family while giving special attention to those in leadership roles in the home, the church, and the broader Kingdom.

4. Website

The CE/P Website is the second of the two major centerpieces. The Website is easily accessible and regularly visited by people in the church. Materials, resources, and other needs are listed on the Website. There are a number of links to other ministry resources on the net, as well as to other sections of our own site. From the Website a church or individual can learn of conferences and seminars, and print out the registration forms. They can learn of other CE/P resources such as the CE/P regional trainers for youth, children, and adults, as well as for the women in the church ministry. The Website, www.pcanet.org/cep quickly provides information on a variety of things consistent with CE/P’s and the PCA’s purpose.

<a id="ga29-p446"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=448 printed_page=446 -->

## 5. WIC Resource Letter

The Women in the Church ministry department of CE/P produces a quarterly *WIC Resource Letter* that is mailed to local WIC presidents and pastor’s wives. This is the third communication centerpiece for CE/P. The Resource Letter, along with a quarterly *WIC Bulletin Insert*, is used to keep the ministry of WIC before the people in the local church. The resource letter contains helpful information and ideas for women’s ministry in the local church. Soon we will begin to distribute this communication piece to the local diaconal chairmen.

## 6. Other publications

The monthly *Bulletin Supplement* is designed to share ministry ideas across the denomination and encourage creative thinking in local churches. Some 40,000 copies are printed and circulated to over 300 subscribing churches.

## 7. Great Commission Publications

Through its 26-year partnership with the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, CE/P, representing the PCA, shares joint ownership of Great Commission Publications, a publishing ministry of the two denominations. GCP primarily publishes Sunday school curriculum for all ages, the Trinity Hymnal used in many PCA churches, and church bulletins. Through the GCP curriculum, Sunday after Sunday PCA people have the opportunity to study the truths of God from a biblically reformed and redemptive historical perspective. The joint venture also enables both denominations to serve a growing number of non-PCA and non-OPC churches as well. CE/P regional trainers, mentioned below, are trained to assist churches with the use of this curriculum.

## TRAINING

The second half of CE/P’s ministry comes under the category of training. Training includes both events (conferences and seminars) and consulting (working directly with church leadership).

### 1. Trainers

Along with our fulltime staff, we have regional trainers across the country to help minister to the needs of our growing denomination. We presently have thirteen regional trainers, eight regional youth trainers, and eleven WIC trainers. CE/P has been committed to multiplying our ministry in the most effective and efficient ways possible. Carefully selected and trained regional trainers are a part of CE/P’s ministry of multiplication. Using regional people allows CE/P to meet the needs of local churches in a cost effective way, to give CE/P a personal presence across the church, and to assist local churches in an ongoing consulting relationship.

<a id="ga29-p447"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=449 printed_page=447 -->

Regional trainers are equipped to do things such as teacher training, particularly (but not
exclusively) for those churches using our GCP curriculum. Many churches ask CE/P
for help with teacher training. as apart of that service, we can also help a church
evaluate its Christian education and training program and work with local personnel to
develop a more effective ministry in that area. The trainers also link local churches to
available resources. Together with the CE/P staff, regional trainers served over one
hundred churches during the past year.

2. Leadership

most of the leadership training, including officer training and general consulting, is
handled by the fulltime CE/P staff. Other more specialized categories such as conflict
management, small group training, worship, strategic planning, cultural awareness, etc.
are also generally done by the CE/P staff.

3. Equip Conferences

CE/P also works in coordination with local churches and presbyteries for larger
conference called equip conferences. This past year three conferences were held in
North Carolina, New Hampshire, and Mississippi with several hundred people
participating.

4. Children and Youth

CE/P has conducted its third annual conference for children ’s ministry staff in local
churches. One hundred people attended the March 2001 conference in Atlanta. CE/P
also serves as a clearinghouse and resource center for individuals and churches
regarding their needs in both youth and children’s personnel.

In February 2001, CE/P and North Georgia Presbytery hosted an Equip conference for
youth workers. Nearly one hundred youth workers and volunteers from several
neighboring states came together to discuss the family and church roles in ministering to
the millennial generation.

presbyterian youth of america is part of CE/P’s leadership training teenagers. Two
conferences are held each summer, one in Colorado, and one at Covenant College.
Around two hundred teenagers come together annually for intensive Christian
leadership training. plans for other regions of the PCA are being considered.

5. Mercy Ministries Conference

the big conference of the year was the mercy ministry conference (The call of the
Gospel: Sharing Christ, Showing Mercy), which was jointly sponsored by CE/P and
Mission to North America. Six hundred PCA men and women came together in Atlanta

<a id="ga29-p448"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=450 printed_page=448 -->

for a challenging and inspiring weekend. In addition to plenary speakers and
workshops, several local church ministry examples were highlighted. This conference
built on the mercy ministry training CE/P has included in our Equip and WIC
conferences. The unique thing about this conference was that it brought together men
and women to discuss this vital ministry. In the PCA, MNA has a mercy ministry
department and oversees the “deed” aspect of that ministry and CE/P has the “word” or
training aspect. This was a timely and strategic opportunity for both agencies to co-host
such a conference.

6. Women in the Church

one hundred and eighty women attended the annual women in the church leadership
training seminar for local church and presbytery WIC leaders, which was held in
conjunction with the mercy ministry conference. The new regional WIC trainers were
introduced, and the new WIC Bible Study Series, published by CE/P, was announced.
Dr. Morton Smith brought the message at the seminar’s closing worship service.

6. Other conferences

In addition to the training mentioned above, CE/P conducts conferences ina variety of
areas. "Partners in Ministry" is designed primarily for pastors, youth pastors, and their
wives. Two were conducted during the past year in Augusta, Georgia, and Ocean City,
Maryland. CE/P has been facilitating music and worship conferences since the early
1980’s. This past year two events were conducted: one in Atlanta with over 130
participants and a conference in Dallas, co-sponsored with Park Cities PCA (scheduled
for April 2001).

CE/P also works with Ridge Haven, Mission to North America, Mission to the World,
and to co-sponsor the annual YOW AW (Youth World Awareness Weekend) conference
for high school students. Over two hundred students came together at Ridge Haven for
this conference in February. Plans are being made for the 2002 conference.

7. Audio/Visual Training

In addition to their central position as resources, the video library and bookstore also
play key roles in training through the medium of audio and video materials. CE/P seeks
to make the best of training available through all of its resources, hence the video
library, the bookstore, and the availability of staff and trainers are key to the success of
the training programs. audio and videotapes of CE/P conferences and events are
available through the video library and bookstore.

<a id="ga29-p449"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=451 printed_page=449 -->

## CONCLUSION

The heartbeat of CE/P is training and equipping God’s people to live for Him in this pluralistic postmodern world. Our strategy is to challenge and assist local churches in training their people to think, to live biblically, and to know how to effectively pass on the Christian faith to the next generation. Because of who we are as the covenant people of God, and because we realize that God has placed us here at this moment in time to serve His purpose to this generation, our present thrust is to encourage adults to reach out and into the lives of the younger generation. The church, as we have known it, is changing. We believe our role is to help steer a course through that change process and help churches apply biblical truth to today’s world in a way that respects the past, that builds upon it by presenting that truth to today’s world, and that hopes for the full and soon coming of the Kingdom of God —“reformed and always reforming.”

## RECOMMENDATIONS

1. That the above report be received as an accurate accounting to the Assembly for CE/P’s service during the past year.
2. That the CE/P minutes of September 14-15, 2000 and February 22-23, 2001 be approved.
3. That the PCA Assembly express its thanks to those who are have diligently served CE/P during the most recent term: TE Terry Johnson, RE James Baird, and TE Michael Woodham, and that the assembly also express thanks to Mrs. Carolyn Muse for her years of service to CE/P on the Women’s Advisory Subcommittee.
4. That the Women in the Church be thanked for their generous support of the 2000 Love Gift for Mission to North America. (The 2001 Love Gift is directed to Ridge Haven, and the 2002 to CE/P).
5. That as a result of the positive response of the Mercy Ministry conference jointly sponsored by CE/P and MNA, a prayer of thanksgiving and supplication be offered at this assembly, particularly that the fruit of that conference will continue to impact many people throughout our church and North America.
6. That the 2002 Budget for CE/P be approved, as presented by the Administration Committee.
7. That, having completed the annual evaluation, TE Charles Dunahoo be elected to serve as Coordinator for the coming year.
8. That the General Assembly commend the resources and training highlighted in the above report to the churches of the PCA to assist them in their task of “making disciples and teaching them to observe all things that I have commanded.”

<a id="ga29-p450"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=452 printed_page=450 -->

The 1999-2000 Institutional Effectiveness Report
Covenant College
Office of Planning

Introduction

Assessment, as we know it today, has been apart of higher education for the last 10 -
15 years. Assessment methods continued to evolve during that time. in the early days of
assessment, the process was more concerned about the inputs to the educational process.
Therefore, measurement items include student/faculty ratio, the percentage of faculty
with earned doctorate, and faculty teaching within their field of discipline. Over time,
assessment became more concerned with the results of student learning than the inputs
of the educational process. the items of greater concern became student results on
standardized tests such as the Major Fields Achievement Tests, reporting a student’s
ability to get into graduate school, and general student learning as measured often
through value-added tests.

in the spring of 2000, a major but subtle change occurred in the evolution of
assessment. A test project, co-sponsored by the Carnegie Foundation for the
advancement of teaching and the pew forum for undergraduate Learning, was
developed and implemented. This survey examined students’ behaviors that show
correlation with student learning. this shift begins a change of focus back to inputs.
But, rather than the inputs of facilities, faculty, and finance, it addresses the behaviors
of student learning. covenant college participated in this pilot program of the national
survey of student engagement (NSSE) in the spring of 2000. A more detailed review
of the results of that program will be presented later in this report.

in light of the change that seems to betaking place in assessment, again giving
consideration to the inputs of education, the format of this year’s Institutional
Effectiveness Report has been somewhat modified. within each major section, issues of
input can be found in italic type while issues of outcomes can be found in bold type. It
is hoped that this change will more clearly identify actions taken by the college to
improve student learning and effectiveness of the institution. You will find over 135
inputs identified and over 95 outcomes identified throughout the report.

The report is divided into six major sections: Academic Affairs; Student Affairs;
Planning and Research; Financial Affairs; Advancement; and the new Three-year Plan
2000-2003. each section contains information which provides evidence of the health of
the institution. some of that evidence is in the form of student out com es, some as
inputs, and some as results of various performance indicators.

<a id="ga29-p451"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=453 printed_page=451 -->

the academic affairs section includes information about students, academic
assessment, faculty, and academic support functions. The student inform ation provides
both student outcomes and student profiles. both types of information give evidence of
the type of student attracted to covenant college and the results of their academic
experience. faculty information provides input evidence of the college’s efforts to
provide a quality educational opportunity for the students. Academic support
information demonstrates additional input evidence of an atmosphere conducive to
learning.

the student affairs section presents evidence of services provided to our students by
the Student Development Department. This information includes both input and
outcome evidence.

the planning and research section includes the activities and findings of the planning
and research functions of the college as they impact changes.

the financial affairs section of the report furnishes evidence of institutional financial
stability and how resources were used to provide students with learning opportunities.
All financial aid information is included in this section.

the section on advancement includes all financial development and new student
recruitment and fund raising information. While this evidence tends to be input in
nature, it also demonstrates the commitment of new students and donors to the
institution.

The sixth, and final, section of the report is a copy of the new 2 0 00-2003 th ree-v ear
Plan, which was completed this year.

it is our hope that this new format will provide a clearer picture of what covenant
College has been doing over the last year to ensure a high quality Christian education
for its students. The results as seen through both institutional and student outcomes are
driven by the mission of the college.

the following excerpts of the covenant college mission statement should provide a
basis upon which the college is driven.

Covenant College is a Christ-centered institution of higher education
emphasizing liberal arts. It is the college’s purpose to help students make
significant progress toward maturity in the following areas:
1. Identity in Christ. a covenant student should be a person who is united
with Christ and committed to Him. This union and commitment should
lead to an understanding both of one’s sin and of one’s significance as
a person redeemed by Christ, resulting ina growing awareness of
purpose. this awareness of purpose should facilitate the development
of goals, priorities, and practices that foster spiritual effectiveness and

<a id="ga29-p452"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=454 printed_page=452 -->

well-being, including the emotional, social, physical, and intellectual
aspects of the individual student.
2. biblical frame of reference. Students should be acquiring the ability to
orient their whole lives by a perspective based on scriptural revelatio.
for realization of this goal the following are important:

- **a.** Scriptural knowledge
- **b.** Academic inquiry
- **c.** Analytical skills
- **d.** Communication skills.

3. Service that is Christ-like.

- **a.** Students should be assuming responsibilities within a local congregation a swell a sin the community of all believers.
- **b.** Students should be assuming responsibilities in society as servants of God.

September 26, 2000

## Section 1: Academic Affairs

Institutions of higher education often boast about the characteristics of their incoming students as a means of expressing the quality of the academic program. While there is some merit to the concept that good students attract good students, such reporting does not express the effectiveness of the institution in educating those students. The first portion of Section 1 will provide a profile of the students, their entry-level academic skills, enrollment, retention and completion. However, the real measure of the college’s effectiveness is seen in the outcome information found later in this section and the student learning behaviors found in the results of the National Survey of Student Engagement found in Section 3.

## Student Profile

The Fall 2000 student body is made up of 58% female and 42% male. This one percent change continues a trend seen last year at Covenant and throughout the nation. The denominational mix of our students is still strongly PCA with 58% of our students coming from our own denomination. New students totaled 247, eleven less than last year. However, total enrollment is now at 1,158, 34 more than last year. The increase comes mainly from a strong retention rate among the returning students. Details of enrollment, SAT scores, retention, and graduation follow.

## Enrollment

Covenant had enjoyed an average growth of a little over 5% per year since the inception of the Quest and Master of Education programs. This year’s growth was 3%.

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

The breakdown of student enrollment by category follows.

<a id="ga29-p456"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=458 printed_page=456 -->

|  New Students | 247  |
| --- | --- |
|  All Other Traditional | 592  |
|  Total Traditional | 839  |
|  Quest | 244  |
|  Master of Education | 75  |
|  TOTAL ENROLLMENT | 1,158  |

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

This represents an increase in traditional students of 34.

## Diversity

![img-1.jpeg](img-1.jpeg)

Fall 2000 student body n. includes 4.5% domestic minorities, 4% missionary children, 2.5% international, and 89% domestic Caucasia

## Academic Preparedness

## SAT Scores

The following graph shows the average SAT scores of Covenant's entering first-time freshmen over the last five years as compared with the national average. Incoming freshmen scores were 15 points higher than last year. The average score for this year's entering freshmen was 1178 compared to last year's 1163. Those scores are 15.6% higher than the national average.

![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)

<a id="ga29-p457"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=459 printed_page=457 -->

## Secondary Marks of Entering Freshmen

As demonstrated by the graph below, a larger percentage of our freshmen are reporting grades of mostly A’s than ever before.

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

## Student Retention

Covenant was again ranked in the top tier of regional liberal arts colleges in the South according to the 2001 issue of the U. S. News and World Report Best Colleges. The college moved up from a tie for twenty-fourth position last year to a tie for nineteenth position this year. The average freshmen retention level for those recognized as the Best Regional Liberal Arts Colleges in the South (33 schools) was 73.4%. Covenant’s freshmen retention rate was 80%. Only one college in the first tier (Fisk University) had a better rate of retention (87%) than Covenant College.

Retention rates continued to increase this year. While the junior to senior rate dropped by 4% (further study will be done to see if a cause can be found), all other classes improved. Last Summer, orientation for new students was quite different than in the past. Students spent time with professors in their first college academic experience as part of their orientation to college life. This early introduction to the rigors of college study might have better prepared students for their freshman year and account for the 2% rise in retention. College-wide retention rose from 82% last year to 85% this year.

graduation rate
the college experience is intended to lead to graduation from the four year of college
study. The graduation rate measures how many students in any given freshman class
graduate within 150% of the planned time. Since Covenant offers baccalaureate degrees
based on a four-year curriculum, the graduation rate is based on a six-year period. To be
considered in the graduation rate, students who came to Covenant as first-time freshmen
in the fall of 1994 (n=l 69) must have graduated from covenant by august of 2000.

academic assessment

a variety of measures and assessments have been collected to provide evidences for
reflection of past performance and direction for future action. Both quantitative and
qualitative app ara ti have been employed in an effort to synthesize a holistic picture of
collect performance in the academic arena. a caution regarding the limits of evidence
needs to be considered as assessments are reviewed. While the data gathered here is not
a complete picture, nor are we free from bias, the following information has something
to say to us.

There is much to thank God for in these data. generally the health of the college as a
faithful learning community is strong according to many measures relative to both
internal and external standards.

traditional program
the integral presentation of faith embedded in academic disciplines continues to be a
strength of the college. the reformed perspective of the college is evidenced in the
variety of assessments and measurements.

The following results were noted for the 1999-2000 academic year:

<a id="ga29-p459"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=461 printed_page=459 -->

## Confirmations:

- High scores on the social problems essay including reasoning 4.0, worldview 4.1, biblical themes 4.1, responsibility 4.0, and historical/structural considerations 3.4. All scores on a five-point scale.
- High scores on the faith statement of showing growth in all areas. Scores on a five-point scale from 1 = falling away to 5 = significant growth were: relationship with God 4.9, relationship with others 4.5, and relationship with creation 3.6.
- Senior integration projects received a mean rating of 6.3 on a scale of 1 – 8 for faith integration.
- Measurements from departmental assessments indicate a high satisfaction of faith integration from information gathered from focus groups.
- Professors received a mean rating of 4.0 on a five-point scale for their ability to bring a Christian perspective to their teaching.

## Content Mastery – Traditional Program

1. Covenant College is dedicated (as shown in the Mission Statement goal 2. on page 3) to presenting an academically rigorous program which challenges students to excel in scholarship based on biblical principles.

## Confirmations

- Sophomores took a biblical literacy test which was designed by the members of the Biblical Studies Department and achieved a mean score of 90% on the objective portion of the test. This was the first administration of the test and no previous benchmarks have been established.
- Senior integration projects received a mean rating of 6.3 on a scale of 1-8 for scholarly content.
- Students are able to communicate well on SIPs as indicated by ratings of 6.2 and 6.4 on eight-point scales for written and oral expression respectively.
- Major Field Achievement Tests (MFATs) scores had a weighted mean in the 75th percentile, indicating that our average student taking the test ranked at the threshold of the top quartile of similar college graduates.
- Departmental assessments indicate student responses and performance which is consistent with these confirmations.

## Concerns

- Some departmental MFAT scores need further study. Perhaps the recommendation of the biology department to statistically correlate results with GPAs would be a helpful step in assessing our assessing.
- Various departments have made specific recommendations involving slight adjustments of course offerings or delivery systems what may have positive influence on achievement of these goals.

<a id="ga29-p460"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=462 printed_page=460 -->

## Instructional Effectiveness – Traditional

*Effective teaching is described in the faculty manual as*

...[including] *concern for the learner, the learning process, the subject matter, teaching methodology, and the teacher*. For the process to be called Christian, it must occur in the context of viewing each of the above dimensions with biblical insight. Such biblical insight can then lead to a Christian practice.

Students are made in the image of God and are called to participate in God’s work and reflect His character. This means that *students* are created to be active, creative, productive, purposeful beings who make sense of ideas and experiences in order to exercise some control over the world around them. They cannot be allowed to be merely passive recipients of information. Rather, they *must be challenged to think and conceptualize, make judgments, discern the implications of ideas, and act upon their knowledge for the glory of God and benefit of humankind.* Instructional practices *must foster the development of these characteristics in the students.*

Classroom instruction is assessed through various means at the college. In the traditional program, it is the backbone of the college accounting for one half of the evaluation of professors. Student *course evaluations* are distributed and the dean of faculty and peers of professors perform observations. Indications *strongly support a positive performance* in this area college-wide.

### Confirmations

- Course evaluations by students indicate a high regard for the instruction offered. *Mean ratings near or above 4 on a five-point scale are achieved in all measures.* Students (n=1743) consistently rate *all areas of instruction* high.
- Focus groups reported in departmental assessments also give *high praise for classroom instruction* and the *role of professors* in the instructional process.
- Observations by the dean of faculty give further attestation to the high quality of instruction.
- The National Survey of Student Engagement (NSSE) *mean ratings for relationships with professors and encouragement to think critically are statistically significantly above peer institutions.*

### Concerns

- Both course evaluations and the National Survey of Student Engagement indicate a *need to investigate student involvement in the learning process through projects and groups work.*
- Course evaluations tend to focus on more senior level courses (an average of second semester sophomore, first semester junior) when appreciation for leaning may be at its best. A distribution of ratings that would follow more core courses and students who are earlier in the college career may be helpful.

<a id="ga29-p461"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=463 printed_page=461 -->

## New Initiatives – Traditional

As new programs are initiated or former programs are changed significantly, a comprehensive assessment is applied. This year, *a delivery system for Self in Society I was implemented during orientation* and the first semester of the freshman year, creating a “freshman experience” for new students. The students were placed in small groups with professors who would also serve as their advisors. The experience was assessed through course evaluations, student focus groups, and a faculty questionnaire. The assessment resulted in the following recommendation:

- The delivery system will remain largely intact for the coming year. Attention for improvement will be directed toward the content of the course. There is a significant group of faculty who would like to see the course become a foundational introduction to the distinctives of the college and leave discussions of social science to introductory courses within the disciplines. The curriculum committee of the faculty is currently addressing this issue.

# Senior Integration Papers (SIPs)

Every department on campus provides a version of a Senior Integration Paper. The specific assignment varies from major to major but is to be rated on a four-item, eight-point scale. The purpose of the paper is to provide a capstone experience for the student and an assessment for the department. Students are to select a topic or project and to approach its study in a scholarly manner from a Christian world and life view. Professors rate the performance of the students relative to written expression, oral expression, content of the discipline, and the integration of faith.

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

# Quest

The Quest program continues to be the largest department of the college and continues to make positive changes in curriculum and delivery resulting in positive ratings by current students and recent alumni. The Quest program has grown much in the past few years to the point that this past year Quest leased and maintained classroom facilities in downtown Chattanooga and Cleveland, Tennessee, Dalton and Canton, Georgia. The program is in the process now of leasing space at Floyd College in Rome, Georgia, for a new program that is preparing to begin there this year. There are several curricular

<a id="ga29-p464"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=466 printed_page=464 -->

changes that have also taken place during the last year and are being piloted as this next
year begins. The Business Communications course has been rewritten and upgraded;
divisional research module has been revised to become applied decision making
(using resource principles to improve students’ decision making skills by including
primary and secondary data in the process); and the original Creative Problem Solving
course has been integrated into the new Applied Decision Making course, it’s place in
the curriculum taken by Managerial Marketing.

in an attempt to ensure that quest stays on the cutting edge of programs of this type, we
have recently met with members of the information and computer science faculty to
plan a pilot program which will allow offering the current Quest degree in
organizational management with a two- to six-course emphasis in Information
Management. These changes would help the program line up better to prepare graduates
form ba programs which require marketing and abetter understanding of the role of
information management.

In May 2000, the quest program graduated 156 students. This was a new record
number of graduates for the program. The Quest program also had a record enrollment
providing the largest revenue to date.

A one-year post-graduation survey was conducted in May 1999 to determine how the
graduates’ covenant education has contributed to their work perform an ce,
career/person al develop men t, and world view . Concepts taught in the program
affecting their career/p er son al development resulted in positive scores ranging from
92% to 98% . 85% of the students reported that philosophy of culture has a positive
im pact on their world and life view, with 92% reporting this impact for the old and
new testament courses, also.
