faculty profile
As mentioned in the introduction, t hens se begins a shift in assessment practices to
look at inputs that are closely tied to student learning. Faculty members play an
important role in engaging students in the learning process. Faculty at a college are
becoming more than lecturers. They not only bring first-hand content knowledge into
the classroom, they also model to students the process of educational engagement.
Students have the opportunity to see their professors engage in the learning process as
they often work together and become a community of learners. These opportunities may
come through faculty preparation for class, pedagogical engagement techniques, or
collaborative learning projects. Therefore, an examination of the teaching faculty at
covenant provides the first evidence of student learning potentials.

the covenant college faculty have long held a strong commitment for providing a
strong liberal arts core curriculum. The Academic Bulletin states that “the core
curriculum is based on the faculty ’s belief that a liberal arts education should be broad
and inclusive, introducing students to ideas and values of continuing concern and
providing them with historical and spiritual perspectives on the complex problems of
our society. "

This year the faculty voted to make some changes in the core curriculum that will
further enhance a broad and inclusive educational experience. The freshman course.
Self and Society, has been replaced with the teaching of the christian mind and a
distribution course in the social sciences. The two-hour course on microcomputer
applications has been lengthened to three hours, allowing for the inclusion of christian
per spec ti val dimensions of technology. This should provide a more uniform approach to

<a id="ga29-p466"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=468 printed_page=466 -->

that portion of the course that attempts to help students to examine their educational
experience through the “spectacles of the Scripture” and perm it students to select an
area of interest within the humanities.

another evidence of quality in the examination of the faculty is the progress of
individual faculty members moving through the academic ranks and receiving tenure at
the college. the length of contract offered to a faculty member at covenant college is
dependent on quality of teaching, the longevity of service, and a scholarly presentation
which demonstrates integration of the discipline and the christian faith. the board of
Trustees approves a five-year contract (tenure) when faculty have demonstrated a high
level of quality over a period of time. following is a table showing the current status of
faculty.

longevity of highly qualified faculty speaks well for the academic opportunities

## Academic Support

### Diversity Scholarship Recipient Program (DSR)

During the 1999-2000 academic year, the DSR Program provided $311,125 in financial assistance for seventy-three students. Forty-six students were projected to return as scholarship students for Fall 2000. Of the remaining 27, 16 graduated, 5 did not reapply, and six are not returning.

Of the sixteen graduating DSR seniors in 2000, nine took the MFAT; the other students were majors in disciplines which did not offer the test. Five students had scores above the mean department score at Covenant. Three others had scores within eleven points of the mean department score.

The DSR program developed a new leadership format this year which involved providing training for 10 upperclassmen who would serve as mentors to fellow DSR students. These mentors were able to help students and also track their progress in terms of academic, social, and spiritual adjustment at the college. Also, the new director of the program, Daphne Haddad, held a weekly tea time on campus to provide a more convenient way for students to make contact with each other and with her.

## Library

This past year, a new three-year plan was completed which will guide the college through the years 2000 to 2003. The entire plan can be found in Section 6 of this report. One of the major objectives in the plan is to “dramatically increase the prominence and usefulness of Library resources and space to provide the campus with a center for serious research and collaborative inquiry.” While the Library has grown much this year in a variety of ways, both inputs and usage, there are still major strides that need to be made to bring it to the level of effectiveness desired. Some of the tactics in the plan include an increase of $35,000 each year for the next three years to increase the library print materials, additional periodical subscriptions in both paper and electronic form, and the hiring of an additional professional librarian and technical support staff.

Library circulation for this past year totaled 32,955 items. This is almost double the circulation of the previous year, partly due to a new statistical report by the automated

<a id="ga29-p468"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=470 printed_page=468 -->

system. Use of on-line databases also increases every year as more and more databases become available. Request by faculty for classroom presentations of library research methods have also increased resulting in much more intelligent use of our existing library resources. Total items in the library are now 129,881. These items include books, periodical subscriptions, microfilm reels, microfiche cards, records and compact disks, audio and video tapes, films, slides, and computer media. However, when compared to several different sources, our library seems to have many major holes that need to be filled in both physical and virtual areas. The most recent comparison was done with Choices Outstanding Academic Books, 1992-1997. That list contains 332 titles that should be held by undergraduate and graduate libraries of our size. Our library has only 83 of those titles, or 25%, when we should be at 75%. While the physical resources still need major improvement, the virtual library has been growing. GALILEO now contains 87 databases. In addition to those databases, it contains 18 subject category links to sites on the worldwide web. During this past year, phase one of JSTOR was completed. This phase contains the complete run of 124 scholarly journals, with many individual journals and full-length articles available.

Student ranking of the importance of library resources and services has been very high over the last three years. However, their satisfaction has been low enough to rank this area as the number one issue needing attention according to the Student Satisfaction Inventory. Details of this research can be found in Section 3 of this report.

Over the last three years, the Master of Education students and seniors writing their Senior Integration Papers were asked to rate the library resources and services. Sixty to eighty percent agreed or strongly agreed that the services and resources were adequate. The apparent incongruity between these two surveys may demonstrate the difference between planned research needs and short-term resource needs. However, comments and focus groups from the participants demonstrate a strong desire to see library resources and services improved.

## Experiential Studies

The Experiential Studies program continues to look for unique study-abroad group experiences for Covenant students. During the 1999-2000 academic year, 33 Covenant students participated in semester-long off-campus study program. Twenty-two of those students participated in experiences sponsored by other organizations, such as the Oxford study abroad program, Jerusalem University College, John Cabot (Italy), and Dordt College. Eleven Covenant students studied in Slovakia in a program at Trnava, which a joint venture with Mission to the World and Covenant College. Additionally, 61 Covenant students spent their spring break on short-term mission projects in Scotland, Ireland, New York, and Miami.

Those students who attended the cross-cultural semester in Slovakia provided the following evaluation of their experience on a scale of 1 – 10, 1 being very poor and 10 being very good:

audiovisual services
the workload fora v services steadily increases each year, and 1999-2000 was no
exception. Courses such as CHOW Art and Music, Christ and Culture, the film course,
and Self and Society (now The Christian Mind) were heavily dependent upon
technology. The chapel program continues to demand greater technological support as
well. To address the resulting staffing need, two additional work study positions will be
added for the 2000-01 academic year. With six workers, AV can begin to allow for
some specialization of jobs.

During October 1999, it was discovered that the sound system in the Great Hall had
been damaged. The speakers were all replaced and Skip Gienapp, AV consultanta, has
been contracted to assess all sound systems three times a year. Four Motorola radios
and microphones were purchased specifically to meet the needs of communication for
Madrigal Dinners, large concerts, plays, etc.

there area number of av services pressures that began appearing during the year. A
significant amount of sound equipment will be ordered in the 2000-01 budget.
Equipment related to PA systems are expensive, easily damaged, and much of the
equipment is aging. Also, with more sophisticated concerts, plays, etc., heavier demand
is placed on AV Services to provide more sophisticated service. The projection screen
in Sanderson 215 and the classroom televisions and VCRs are aging and will begin to
need replacements.

<a id="ga29-p470"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=472 printed_page=470 -->

## Technology Services/Instructional Technology/Telephone

The 1999-2000 academic year was very busy for Technical Services. Search for a campus-wide database system began in the fall of 1999 resulting in the selection of SCT's Banner. Sufficient progress was made to allow the Accounting and Purchasing offices to begin using Banner Finance on July 1, 2000.

Technology Services was also kept busy upgrading and installing new computers throughout the campus. Faculty and staff were provided with 29 new or upgraded computers. The student labs received 47 new computers for student use.

There are now four student computer labs and three computer clusters in the residence halls providing 133 computers for student use. The labs are open to students, faculty, and staff, except when a class is scheduled in the lab. The hours of operation are 8:00 a.m. to 12:00 midnight, Monday through Thursday, 8:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. Friday and 8:00 a.m. to 12:00 midnight on Saturday.

A new Remote Access Server was implemented for faculty allowing them to access the campus network from home. The Telephone Department added a new Y2K compliant voice mail server giving all students access to voicemail. Because of the large number of potential students accessing the college web site (http://www.covenant.edu), it was given a new "look and feel" better complimenting other promotional materials produced by the school.

Two (2) new "smart classrooms" were added. This now provides three classrooms equipped with a high level of multi-media equipment for teaching. A "mobile smart cart" was also equipped which can be moved into other classrooms. This provides portability of multi-media equipment similar to that found in the "smart classrooms."

Additionally, Technology Services/Telephone installed and upgraded over 47,000 feet of cable. A Microsoft Proxy Server and Trend Micro's virus scanner were installed providing greater security from outside attacks and email viruses installed on the firewall enabled scanning of all inbound and outbound email messages. CyberPatrol's Internet filter was also installed, enabling blocking of pornographic sites on campus.

The Instructional Technology department continued their efforts to ensure faculty and staff are well trained in the use of all technology currently available to them. On-site training for implementation of the Banner line of products was held for various offices throughout the spring and summer.

## Section 2: Student Development

The Student Development portion of the 1999-2000 Institutional Effectiveness Report focuses on student spiritual vitality, intercollegiate athletics, intramural athletics, Priesthill services, and health education programs.

<a id="ga29-p471"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=473 printed_page=471 -->

## Spiritual Vitality

While measuring the “spiritual vitality” of the student body is very difficult, assessment efforts indicate that our students are very satisfied with their personal growth in Christ, their Christian frame of reference and their service for Christ.

1. For three years in a row, students have completed the Student Satisfaction Survey and consistently ranted the following two items among the top seven?

- “My understanding of God is being strengthened by classroom and/or campus experiences.”
- “Being at Covenant is contributing to my spiritual growth.”

2. Over the past three years, Covenant students consistently reported their “spiritual growth” higher than the Council of Christian Colleges and Universities norms in both importance and satisfaction.

3. Covenant alumni reported to be “very satisfied” with their spiritual growth and academic experience while a Covenant College. The following statements ranked first and second respectively in level of satisfaction:

- “My overall educational experience at Covenant was good.”
- “Covenant has provided a positive impact on my spiritual life.”

4. The alumni were asked about their satisfaction with Covenant College in seven areas. The item ranked highest is satisfaction was “Christian, personal, and social growth.”

## Residence Life Growth

The college’s growth brought us to a 97% residential occupancy for the fall of 1999. Anticipated growth led to the final construction of the Rymer wing to Maclellan Hall which was opened in August 2000. Rymer Hall is a suite-style apartment complex that houses 96 students. The beds now available in Maclellan Hall totals 168. The Chalmers Center and the Business Department currently occupy the first floor of Rymer Hall, which will be converted back to housing for the fall of 2002. At that point, Maclellan Hall, Founders’ Center, Carter Hall and the Student Apartments will provide housing for 862 students.

## The Intercollegiate Athletic Program

After an extended construction period, the Ashe Activity Center was completed for the fall of 2000. Ashe Center is primarily an intramural facility housing three new basketball courts, weight rooms with state of the art machines, a laundry room, team storage, trainer facilities, and four locker rooms.

Coaches continued to emphasize the importance that student-athletes are students first and athletes second. The average GPA of the athletes was the highest it has been since 1994.

## Intercollegiate Athletics

Student-athletes recruited as new students in the Fall 1999 had an overall SAT of 1140 compared to all the college’s new students of 1163 and national new student score of 1016. The average high school and college transfer GPA of the Fall 1999 new student-athletes was 3.478.

## Intramural Athletics

Approximately 80% of our student body have been involved in intramurals this past year. Over the past three years, students responding to the Student Satisfaction Survey have consistently ranked intramurals “lower” in importance but “higher” in satisfaction when compared to the CCCU institutions.

## Priesthill Services

Covenant College continues to enjoy a working relationship with the Psychological Studies Institutes (PSI) at the Chattanooga Bible Institute (CBI). Three counselors were assigned to the campus this year to complete a 35-hour contract for counseling service. The counselors spend 93% of this on-campus time providing direct or indirect counseling. These counselors provide the following services to our students:

1. Professional counseling from a biblical perspective to assist students, faculty and staff
2. Testing and evaluations of students
3. Out-patient referrals for psychiatric and mental health care
4. Consultation with Resident Advisors on resident mental health concerns
5. Opportunities for small group interaction on various topics related to emotional, mental, physical, and spiritual issues.

## Health Education Programs and Services

The following health programs and services were provided in response to student need and assessment.

1. Nutrition
2. Alcohol and drug awareness
3. Stress &amp; depression management
4. Study skills and time management
5. Conflict resolution
6. Anxiety disorders
7. Eating disorders
8. Women’s health issues
9. Men’s health issues
10. Smoking
11. Sexuality
12. Blood drives
13. Flu vaccinations
14. OSHA/Bloodborne pathogens
15. Resident assistant training
16. CPR class

Section 3: planning and research

planning office

th ree-year Plan Process
The work on developing the new Three-year Plan was completed this year. Five faculty
members spent much of their time helping in this process to ensure the plan would
reflect the academic needs of the college. The final plan with goal statement, strategies,
objectives, and tactics was presented to the Board in March 2000. Since then, the
planning committee has prioritized initiatives totaling almost $120,000. They include
additional academic operating supplies, academically focused work study positions,
outside speakers, additional library book and periodical acquisition, library support
staff, and a minority recruiter.

In stitu tional Research

national survey of student engagement
covenant college participated in the first year of the national survey of student
Engagement (NSSE) in the spring of 2000. there were a total of 276 participating fouryear colleges and universities involved in the study. There were 151,910 first-year and
senior students participating. The study was co-sponsored by the Carnegie Foundation
for the advancement of teaching and the pew forum for undergraduate learning and
supported by the Pew Charitable Trust.

In October 2000, national benchmarks of good educational practice will be released
based on the results from the NSSE 2000. This study will probably play an important
role in the discussions regarding what educators will consider to be good educational
practice. they may eventually play an important role in national rankings of schools. A
brief profile of the nearly 152,000 participants follows:

Class: First-year Students 49%
Seniors 51%
Gender: Women 67%
Men 33%
Enrollment Status: Full-time 86%
Part-time 14%

there were four major areas of the survey. The first was College Activities, which
included: academic, intellectual, and social experiences; reading and writing; nature of
exams; character of mental activities and weekly activities. The second section was
Educational and Personal which included knowledge, skills, and personal development.
The third section was Opinions About Your School, which included institutional
emphasis, quality of relationships, and satisfaction. the fourth section consisted of
questions designed by the council of christian colleges and universities (CCCU).

<a id="ga29-p474"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=476 printed_page=474 -->

The survey report gives information for our first-year students and senior students and compared those results with the CCCU and National means. Another piece of data given is the Effect Size. This is the indicator of the “practical significance” of the magnitude of the difference between the means. In essence, this is a process to determine if the Covenant average, as compared with other averages, is of any significance. An Effect Size between .2 and .5 is considered rather small, .5 to .8 is moderate, and .8 and higher is large. Therefore, when viewing Effect Size, one should take notice of anything that is .6 or .7 and above. That Effect Size shows some significant difference between Covenant and the other schools of comparison. The data that is reported in the remaining portion of this section is intended to demonstrate benchmarks which will later be compared with future NSSE reports.

There were six areas in the NSSE results which showed some level of statistical difference between Covenant students, the students of the Council for Christian Colleges and Universities (CCCU), and the national sample. These areas were academic, intellectual, and social experiences; reading and writing; knowledge, skills, and personal development; institutional emphasis; quality of relationships; and three questions posed by the CCCU.

There were three responses within the area of academic, intellectual, and social experiences that were slightly lower at a small or moderate level of statistical significance. When compared to the mean scores of CCCU schools or the national pool, our students were below the norm concerning working with other students on a project in class or a faculty member on a research project. The issue of working with a student on a class project moved from a small significance for freshmen to a moderate difference among the seniors. The mean score for our seniors was also below the mean for CCCU schools and the national schools at the small level of significance in the area of coming to class prepared. However, the use of e-mail to communicate with an instructor or other students was higher at both the freshman and senior level when compared to both groups.

An area of strength for Covenant students was thinking critically and analytically. Our seniors demonstrated a moderate level of difference when compared with the CCCU and national schools. While our freshmen responses resulted in a small negative difference in the issue of “understanding people of other racial and ethnic backgrounds,” the seniors did not show any difference. It appears that time spent at Covenant College helps students in this area.

The largest positive differences were in the issues of relationships. Our seniors’ responses demonstrated a moderate level of statistical significance when compared to the national mean in the area of “helping you cope with your non-academic responsibilities,” “providing the support you need to thrive socially,” and “relationships with administrative personnel and offices.” “Relationships with other students” and “faculty members” were also ranked at a small level when compared with the national group.

<a id="ga29-p475"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=477 printed_page=475 -->

two responses among the cc cu questions gave our students a small positive level of

personal identification with christ as their mean score ranked a ta statistically higher

these two responses a revery important pieces of information. Our students are better

Student Satisfaction Inventory
This past year ended a three-year period of researching student satisfaction at Covenant.
the purpose of the study was to examine many areas of service based on the student’s
rankings of importance and satisfaction. results include comparisons of theme an
scores of our students and that of two other groups - other four-year private colleges and
CCCU schools.
the survey examined areas of academic advising, campus climate, campus life, campus
support services, concern for the individual, instructional effectiveness, recruitment and
financial aid, registration effectiveness, responsiveness to diverse populations, safety
and security, service excellence, and student centeredness. Students were asked to give
two responses to each question - one rating their view of the importance of the issue and
the other their satisfaction with the issue. The difference between importance and
satisfaction is termed the performance gap.

Covenant’s ranking is higher (meaning a lower performance gap) in all 12 of the areas
listed above when compared to public colleges and 11 of the 12 when compared to the
private colleges.

Section 4: financial affairs

Finance / A ccoun ting

operating budget performance
After com pleting another year with an operating surplus, the college remains in good
financial condition. this year ’s operating budget was $21,622,397. The independent
auditor issued a clean opinion regarding the financial statements. Long-term debt
continues to decline and now represents less than 0.3% of the book value of fixed
assets. Pre-audited financial results for the five budget areas of the college areas
follows:

Contingency: $368,000
Tuition/financial aid: $ 171,172 over budget
Business Affairs: $ 55,064 under budget
Academic Affairs: $152,102 over budget
Advancement: $ 61,449 over budget
Student Affairs: $ 9.167 under budget
Total College: $ 47,508 under budget

As shown above, the college ended the year in the black by almost $50,000. This was
a significant improvement over last year. The large deficit in academic affairs was due
to a much lower profit from the Quest program than budgeted.

<a id="ga29-p477"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=479 printed_page=477 -->

Institutional aid. Decreased to 31.5% from 33.1% in 1998-1999 but still exceeded the budget target of 30.5%. Covenant continues to seek additional endowed and fully funded scholarships, as well as increased church giving in order to attract qualified students from all socio-economic backgrounds. Until those efforts are more successful, Covenant will be increasingly less accessible to average ability, high need applicants.

Depreciation continues to be added to the budget over a multi-year phased plan, as required by financial prudence and accrediting and accounting standards. Cash generated by this addition ($1,000,000 in 2000-2001) will be used by the President's Council for capital budget items and building repairs. For 2000-2001, the capital budget is primarily allocated to technology purchases and the replacement of the heating system in Carter Hall. It is hope the college will have completed funding depreciation (currently totaling $1,750,000) by 2004-2005, freeing a considerable amount of revenue for other needed expenditures.

Endowment investment return for 1999-2000 was approximately 18.7% compared to 17.9% for 1998-1999. The 1998-1999 performance earned Covenant a ranking of 31st out of over 500 institutions participating in the national endowment return survey.

## Section 5: Advancement

### Admissions

The 1999-2000 academic year provided a great change for the manner in which the Admissions office was managed. An Admissions Self-Managed Team (ASMT) was organized to provide all managerial oversight for admissions functions. Each team member was assigned various responsibilities, such as training, campus preview weekend, hiring, office management, etc. As they continued to improve the process of being a self-managed team, several members have taken on additional administrative responsibilities in conjunction with the needs of the office and the college.

The team is seeking new strategic relationships and further the development of current relationships within the PCA. They are also seeking to increase visibility in targeted regions. With the addition of a diversity recruiter, there are now six team members traveling to represent the college at various college fairs, schools, churches, and in-home visits. This year was also the first year when a large number of inquiries and applicants were received via the internet. This is a rather new method of recruiting for Covenant College, causing the ASMT to seek better ways to communicate the mission to those students inquiring about Covenant College through the worldwide web.

While the goal of converting 40% of applications into matriculants was met, the number of new students fell short of budget projections. There were several factors contributing to this small new student count. The 1999-2000 recruitment year was the first year that the college awarded financial aid using a financial leveraging system. This type of system attempts to meet 100% of the financial need for the more qualified students as defined by the merit index point system. Index points are awarded based on academic, spiritual, and leadership characteristics. Financial aid from other sources such as Church Scholarship Partnership (CSP) program, and state or federal programs are not affected by this system.

Those students receiving the highest level of index points and those in the CSP program were awarded first. Awarding then continued in the second group. The students in the second group who had extremely high need were awarded last within that group. This ensures that a larger number of qualified students can be awarded 100% of their need before the financial aid funds are depleted. The process continued, meeting 100% of the need for those students awarded, until the institutional financial aid budget was expended. The major difference between this system and the systems used in previous years was the percentage of need met of those students who were awarded was higher. While fewer students were awarded from this particular fund, those students who were awarded received close to 100% of their need.

Past experience has shown that meeting a smaller portion of need for a great quantity of students did not serve either the students or the college well. The financial aid leveraging system attempts to provide financial assistance at a higher level to those students who are best qualified to enter Covenant College. Because of the change of

<a id="ga29-p481"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=483 printed_page=481 -->

awarding system, 39 students were placed on a waiting list for financial aid. Research
shows that most schools that shift to financial leveraging suffer a drop in applications
the following year while the matriculation percentage increases. This is evidenced in the
figures below:

1995 1996 1997 1998 1999 2000
Total Applications 463 577 616 603 683 609
Total Matriculates 185 250 246 260 258 247
Conversion Rate 40% 43% 40% 43% 38% 41

## Alumni

This past year was the final year of the alumni portion of the Capital Campaign. Goals included building and endowing the Probasco Alumni/Visitor Center and equipping the weight room in the new Ashe Activity Center. Alumni Giving was extraordinary in this last year, meeting and exceeded all goals. Not only was the Probasco Alumni/Visitor Center goal of $1.1 million reached to build and endow the building, but was exceeded by $100,000. The following chart shows how quickly alumni giving has grown from the over the last five years

|   | # of Donors | # of Gifts | % Giving | Total Giving  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Fiscal 95/96 | 233 | 585 | 8% | $174,338  |

The campaign for the weight room, directed mainly to alumni athletes, was another success that raised over $100,000 in just four months. A great deal of credit goes to our new Physical Education professor Will Stern '70, who designed the weight room and helped make the project worthy of investment by alumni athletes. The result is an outstanding weight room facility which is now one of the best in the Chattanooga area.

<a id="ga29-p483"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=485 printed_page=483 -->

A local doctor commented upon seeing the facility: “This changes my whole concept of how Covenant does things.”

Alumni are also receiving the college magazine, *The View*, on a regular basis as well. Campaign updates, President’s Reports, and personal letters. Many alumni have praised these new means of communication, and it appears there is a growing positive attitude toward the college among alumni.

Homecoming weekends also have continued to improve. There have been record-breaking crowds in the last three years as attendance has tripled to more than 600 alumni and family. This provides a wonderful opportunity for alumni to get back on campus and stay in touch with the uniqueness of Covenant College.

This summer, alumni director Marshall Rowe ’87 was able to visit the campuses of Williams, Harvard, Dartmouth, and Wheaton to study their alumni programs in an ongoing effort to improve the program at Covenant.

Section 6: Three-year Plan 2000-2003

## The Plan for 2000-2003

**GOAL**: Strengthen the College’s academic culture with its Reformed emphasis, to serve Christ, His church and His world.

Covenant College—its Board of Trustees, faculty, staff and students—serves as an agency of the PCA for the purpose of preparing God’s people to extend Christ’s ministry to the church and the world. This preparation necessarily requires both spiritual and intellectual development. As an academic institution, the unique task of this agency is to teach people to think christially and to serve as an intellectual resource. This plan builds on the College’s historically strong commitment to academic training informed by our Reformed heritage. The College’s purpose statement spells out the essential character of the Reformed academic culture we seek to strengthen: fostering an environment that encourages a zeal for God’s truth, submissive to God’s Word and a spirit of love and humility. Zeal for the truth involves both joyfully searching for it in every area of life, and confidently defending God’s truth by critiquing those forms of thought and life antithetical to it. Christ—like love and humility require that these activities be characterized by gentleness and respect for others. Cultivating this attitude about the truth and the ability to apply it to Christ’s service requires that concern for intellectual development inform every aspect of the College’s operations. An environment that encourages this kind of cultivation requires a campus-wide commitment. Not only must the curriculum foster a delight in academic rigor and intellectual engagement, but the atmosphere outside of class must promote it as well. This plan seeks to focus the College’s energy for the next three years on reviewing all our efforts with this end in mind. The following ten objectives will be implemented during that time to help us better serve the mission of the college.

<a id="ga29-p484"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=486 printed_page=484 -->

1. Become more intentional about continuously improving the quality and breadth of both teaching and learning and the strength and coherence of the academic program.
- Develop a comprehensive long-range academic plan.
- Establish new Procedures and Criteria for the regular review of academic (and administrative) departments and the overall college curriculum.
- Establish or identify the appropriate body to be responsible for the oversight and coordination of the Core Curriculum.
- Direct the attentions of the faculty development process to the mentoring of good professional skills for new and continuing faculty.
- Become more proactive in recruiting potential faculty candidates.
- Establish an honors program.
- Increase the visibility of the college’s academic programs.

2. Cultivate a campus climate that encourages academic excellence.
- Add more academically oriented work study positions
- Help students with work loads and financial pressures by raising work study
- Encourage and recognize staff contributions to the academic climate
- Change presidential scholarship point system to encourage primacy of academic tasks
- Establish additional outside speakers programs
- Establish a hiring policy which increases diversity
- Continue to develop and deepen freshman orientation’s focus on academic tasks
- Strengthen departmental identity among faculty members and students in the disciplines

3. Enhance the overall coherence of each student’s Covenant experience.
- Establish an institutional-wide policy concerning complete and uninterrupted daytime access for students to all offices that provide student services.
- The student senate will collaborate with the appropriate faculty committee to develop a document that specifies the “Expectations and Priorities of the Academic Culture.”
- The teaching faculty and the residence life staff will work cooperatively to ensure that the residence policies support and enhance instruction and learning.
- The Career Development office will collaborate with the appropriate faculty department to develop discipline-related life experience opportunities for students.
- Explicate the chapel and practical service graduation requirements rendering them coherent with the academic program.
- Develop a college philosophy for academic support services and establish procedures regarding reasonable accommodation and access to academic instruction.
- The office of academic affairs will coordinate and promote an enhanced schedule of on-campus scholarly exchanges and performances.

<a id="ga29-p485"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=487 printed_page=485 -->

- Evaluate the college’s national athletic affiliation and propose actions that are consistent with the college’s purpose and objectives for its athletic program.

4. Nurture a climate of genuine spiritual vitality, consistent with our identity and role as a Christ-centered college, that is characterized by godly relationships, effective communication and mutual trust.

- Increase understanding of and commitment to the proper role and witness of faculty, administration, and staff in nurturing a climate of genuine spiritual vitality at Covenant.

- Various methods of assessment will be developed by the Chapel committee to evaluate the current climate of spiritual vitality on campus. These methods should be generally narrative and descriptive rather than quantitative.

- Students, faculty, and staff will be offered opportunities to learn and grow in the area of biblical conflict resolution, in the effective and edifying use of personal and electronic communications, and in mutual accountability.

- Following an audit of all print and electronic sources of official on and off campus communications, a review should be made of the overall coherence and effectiveness of these products with respect to the distinctive academic and spiritual climate of the college and the need for complete and timely communication on important issues.

5. Explicate Covenant's educational philosophy and organizational values.

- Develop a process leading to action on a consensus educational philosophy document that is commonly understood by most faculty regarding its meaning and uses.

- Develop a consensus organizational values document that is commonly understood by employees of the college regarding its meaning and uses. Also, develop tools that help examine the consistency of institutional decisions with that document.

6. Evaluate and address faculty privileges and load responsibilities for teaching, advising, research, and committee service; and nurture the faculty as a collegium of teacher-scholars whose scholarship enriches the classroom, engages their disciplines, and provides visible intellectual resources for Christ’s church and his world.

- Expand the existing faculty “load” mechanism beyond contact hours in class with students in order to include faculty responsibilities in the core areas of advising, scholarship, committee work and (otherwise uncompensated) service to the college. Using this mechanism, identify a normal faculty “load.”

- Reward faculty for service above and outside their normal responsibilities.

- Provide practical encouragement for faculty collaboration in research, pedagogy-development, and publication.

- Offer at least two faculty-led educational services directly to PCA churches (weekend seminars on current issues, distance-learning opportunities for RUF students, pastor’s wives, missionaries, homeschoolers).

<a id="ga29-p486"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=488 printed_page=486 -->

7. Dramatically increase the prominence and usefulness of library resources and space to provide the campus with a center for serious research and collaborative inquiry.
- Contract with a professional librarian experienced in library program planning to assist in preparing a written Program.
- Budget permitting, increase the library print materials budget by $35,000 each year for the next three years.
- Purchase additional periodical subscriptions in paper form for each academic major as recommended by faculty.
- Purchase electronic periodical subscriptions from Project Muse.
- Hire an additional professional librarian.
- Hire an additional technical assistant as a support position for an additional librarian.

8. Clarify the college's governance and employer/employee relations to ensure that the mission, goals and operations of the college are carried out effectively, and that all aspects of institutional governance are made more explicit, more predictable, and more widely understood.
- Adopt interim governance procedures for the 2000-2001 academic
- The president will appoint a governance task force including three teaching faculty to analyze governance concerns and recommend changes to current governance practice.
- Implement an interim Staff governance structure that includes a grievance procedure, and establish a review process.

9. Develop an enrollment management plan to ensure access and retention of a diverse population of qualified students.
- Reach consensus about the roles that gender balance and racial diversity ought to play in the college's admissions efforts and the ways that Covenant's commitment to diversity will be made visible.
- Hire an African-American recruiter who will help the college better serve the higher education needs of the African-American community.
- Establish a permanent enrollment management team.
- Explore a pilot joint venture with the Center for Christian Studies at the University of Virginia.
- Raise $10,000 per student in designated funds to support newly recruited diversity students.

10. Develop a long range Financial and Campus Master Plan that will enable the college to grow to full potential while safeguarding the academic culture of the college.
- A long range Financial Plan will be developed which takes into account concerns about tuition dependence, donor potential (both capital and annual), and financial aid as required to build the academic culture of the college.

<a id="ga29-p487"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=489 printed_page=487 -->

• the campus master plan will be updated to reflect the educational priorities of
this plan, addressing the lon grange facility needs of the college and assist in
future resource management.
• the budget process will be more closely linked to the tactics of the three-year
plan.

April 6, 2000 Edition

<a id="ga29-p488"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=490 printed_page=488 -->

# REPORT OF

# COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY

# TO THE 29TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY

# OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

We are delighted and humbled to report that the Lord has again graciously provided for future generations of ministers and Christian workers “faithful to the Scriptures, the Reformed faith and the Great Commission” by blessing this year’s ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary.

Our Savior reminds us that, “unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required” (Luke 12:48). What God has given is the enlargement of our boundaries, provision for our needs, and the great joy of sensing that his hand is upon us. While it is our responsibility and privilege to recount those blessings again this year, we want to begin by telling you how we are responding in gratitude to the mercies of our God. Since our Lord requires that we make good use of his gifts, we are attempting to identify opportunities to serve his Church more effectively and to proclaim his name more broadly among the nations.

## I. New Opportunities to Serve Our Church

### Covenant Worldwide Extension Program:

Covenant Worldwide is the extension training and media ministry program of Covenant Seminary that is taking an accredited degree and lay training resources around the world with new technologies. This past February marked the beginning of a new era in Seminary Extension Training. We were already offering external Master of Arts degrees in four cities across the country (as well as offering accredited course work in dozens of cities across this nation and internationally). Now we have received approval to offer a fully-accredited Master of Arts degree any time, anywhere. This degree is offered through a combination of audio and visual resources, phone support, three one-week residencies – all enhanced by internet resources and support.

We are one of only two accredited seminaries in North America that has permission to offer this extension degree in such a format. Now we will be able to serve church leaders throughout the world who want advanced seminary training but are not able to move to a seminary. Elders, deacons, youth and campus workers, Sunday School teachers, Christian school teachers, missionaries, and devoted lay persons from all walks of life will be able to obtain the highest quality seminary training both in this degree program and through a variety of non-degree, media resources.

While we do not plan to offer the Master of Divinity through this program, students preparing for the ministry will also be able to earn significant portions of their degrees through Covenant’s Worldwide Extension Training. We are firmly committed

<a id="ga29-p489"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=491 printed_page=489 -->

to a residential training program for pastoral training that involves interaction in community with peers, professors and local churches. Still, we want to share with our church and world the wonderful resources that God has given us to defend and spread faith in the inerrancy of his Word and the wonder of his grace.

Already we have the Master of Arts curriculum available for extension students. This program will be upgraded and expanded over the next three years with nearly one million dollars in additional funding. Dr. Brad Hough (Ph.D. in Instructional Technology from Vanderbilt), Vice President for Enrollment and External Programs Kevin Vanden Brink, and our experienced External Program Director Dan Gilchrist are providing excellent leadership for this exciting new ministry opportunity.

## Youth in Ministry Institute:

Last year we told you that that Covenant Seminary had also been awarded a $1.2 million grant from the Lilly Endowment to develop our Youth in Ministry Institute (YIMI).

The Youth in Ministry Institute first seeks to provide high-school-age youth with seminary resources to think in a deeper way about vocational ministry. Second, the Youth in Ministry Institute provides new academic programs to prepare seminarians (and those presently ministering to young people) for youth ministry in the PCA and beyond.

This program is off to a wonderful start. We have conducted three institutes in various regions of the country. We have offered three youth ministry courses. And we are seeing new students coming to Covenant for a focus in youth ministry. We presented an extraordinary report to the Lilly Endowment regarding these efforts and it has offered us the opportunity to apply for increased funding for this important program that is so vital for the future of our Church’s youth and youth pastors.

## Francis Schaeffer Institute:

The mission of the Francis Schaeffer Institute is to help Christians address the concerns of our culture with the claims of Christ. We help students and fellow believers learn to bring the light of the Gospel to areas of culture that traditionally challenge our efforts to honor the Savior. Through a variety of special lectures, courses and art exhibits the Schaeffer Institute has become one of the Seminary’s primary means of equipping students attitudinally as well as intellectually to reach the diverse culture around us.

The major lecture series of the Schaeffer Institute this year were:

- Time, Money and God: Does Faith Make a Difference
- Gender and Faith: An Examination of Women’s Roles in Society

Visiting lecturers also addressed the following:

- Perspectives on Presenting the Gospel in Russian Society
- Islam and Christianity
- Apologetics from an African-American Perspective
- Loving Those Who Hate the Church

The popular Friday Nights at the Institute, where students and professors address topics at local bookstores, continues to be an effective way of speaking to persons

<a id="ga29-p490"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=492 printed_page=490 -->

outside traditional church circles. A number of churches around the country have taken up the model begun at Covenant Seminary as a way of reaching the unchurched in their communities. Topics addressed on Friday nights this year have included:

- The Yearning for Spirituality in Pop Music
- Have Missionaries Really Been Bad for Culture?
- Restoration in the Inner City

As part of the spring Schaeffer Lecture Series we again invited PCA Women in church staff positions across the nation to attend to a conference to discuss how to serve the Lord biblically in their settings. We have received words of great appreciation from these women for this opportunity to share ideas and challenges with one another.

## Archaeology Institute:

Through the marvelous provision of caring donors we have received endowed funds that will allow Dr. David Chapman to succeed Emeritus Professor of New Testament Dr. W. Harold Mare as Curator of the Mare Institute for Biblical and Archaeological Studies. This provision will enable Covenant to continue providing students with archaeological evidence and explanation for the truth of Scripture, as well as providing a consistent, Evangelical voice in the field of Biblical Archaeology. Dr. Mare will continue his supervision of the Archaeology Institute as Dr. Chapman “learns the ropes” both here and at the Abila Project excavations in Jordan.

## Field Education:

Increasingly we are cooperating with local churches in the training of students for ministry. We require Covenant Seminary students to “do theology in the context of the church” in order to receive our degree. We see as increasingly important the requirement that every ordination-track student have at least 300 hours of field education in a local ministry supervised by a local pastor or session. Earning an M.Div. from Covenant Seminary means more than simply doing well in class. We are more than a graduate school. In addition to training students under professors of the highest academic credentials, we believe that it is necessary for students (and professors) to be integrally involved in the life of the local church. Preaching, teaching, visitation, session meeting involvement, evangelism, and mentoring by mature church leaders are now vital components of student ministry preparation.

Having students in community with peers, professors and pastors creates a vital matrix that we believe is necessary for training the next generation of leaders with the very best resources and wisdom that this generation of leaders can provide. Our goal is to engage heart as well as mind, and to use church experiences as well as academic challenges to instill wisdom regarding God’s Word and love for his people.

## II. God’s Gifts and Blessings:

### Enrollment:

In this twelfth year of consecutive record enrollments, the Lord brought 935 students from 47 states and 16 nations to Covenant Seminary for the fall semester.

<a id="ga29-p491"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=493 printed_page=491 -->

Over 1,172 students throughout the entire year were registered in Covenant Seminary classes.

Not only are we thankful for the increased number of students but also for the increasing support of PCA churches as indicated by student referrals. The number of incoming students referred to us by churches and pastors doubled in the last two years.

## Finances:

God enabled Covenant to finish this fiscal year in the black. This has been our consistent report through the last decade. Our God is great and greatly to be praised for so faithfully providing for his Church and her future servants.

Through the Lord’s wonderful provision we are typically able to provide at least half of the tuition through financial scholarships for each PCA student in the ordination track and under care of his presbytery.

## Placement:

We praise God for his extraordinary blessing in providing placement for our growing number of graduates. Every M.Div. graduate seeking placement in an ordained ministry position and having Seminary recommendation was placed this year (100% placement rate for the second consecutive year). Over the past eleven years, over 98% percent of Master of Divinity graduates with Seminary recommendation seeking ordained placement have secured ministry positions. Additionally, since the first class graduated in 1995, 97% of the graduates of the Master of Arts in Counseling program have also been placed. We are very grateful for the confidence the churches of the PCA have shown in our ministry-training focus as is evidenced by their reception of our graduates.

## Pastor/Scholar Faculty:

For over forty years, the faculty of Covenant Theological Seminary has sought exceptionally gifted pastor/scholars to defend and communicate the historic Christian faith.

In January 2001, the Board of Trustees approved Dr. David Chapman to join our faculty to teach New Testament and Biblical Archaeology. Dr. Chapman completed his doctorate at Cambridge University after years of college campus ministry.

The Board also approved the Rev. Zachary Eswine, senior pastor of Grace Presbyterian Church (PCA) in Hudson, OH, to join the Homiletics faculty. Rev. Eswine is a gifted preacher and musician who is completing a doctorate in Speech Communication at Regent University.

Both of these gifted teachers reflect the pastor/scholar profile that Covenant seeks in our faculty, combining high academic achievement with significant ministry experience.

Significant contributions to the following works have been made by our Faculty this year:

|  Rev. Jerram Barrs | The Heart of Evangelism (Crossway) Women of the Word (Crossway)  |
| --- | --- |
|  Dr. Hans Bayer | New Testament Introduction (Baker) Commentary on Mark (R. Brockhaus)  |

<a id="ga29-p492"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=494 printed_page=492 -->

Dr. Bryan Chapell  Holiness by Grace (Crossway)  Commentary on Titus (Crossway)
Dr. David Chapman  "Perceptions of Crucifixion Among Jews and Christians in the Ancient World" (Tyndale Bulletin)
Dr. Jack Collins  The God of Miracles (Crossway and IVP-UK) Old Testament editor for the English Standard Version of the Bible
Dr. Dan Doriani  Putting the Truth to Work (P&amp;R)
Dr. Donald Guthrie  A Life-long Call to Learn (Abingdon)
Dr. Robert Peterson  Hell Under Fire (Zondervan)  Knowing God Our Heavenly Father: What the Bible Teaches about Adoption (P&amp;R)
Dr. David Jones  "The Posture of the Theological School" in Aims and Purposes of Evangelical Theological Education (Eerdmans)
Dr. Richard Winter  "A Biblical and Theological Perspective on Grief and Bereavement" (Journal of Psychology and Christianity)
Dr. Robert Vasholz  Commentary on Leviticus (Christian Focus)

In addition to writing pursuits and their full-time teaching duties our professors remain engaged in many avenues of ministry. For example:

Dr. Phil Douglass (Church Planting and Renewal Professor) continues to pastor Emmanuel Presbyterian Church in a renewal ministry that uses many seminary interns, and serves as a church planting consultant to various churches.
Dr. Nelson Jennings (World Mission Professor) continues to conduct mission trips for seminary students and others (countries visited this past year include Ghana and Peru).
Dr. Hans Bayer (New Testament Professor) will minister in Germany, the Ukraine, and Hungary this year.
Dr. Dan Zink (Biblical Counseling Professor) directs the New Hope Counseling Services of Chesterfield Presbyterian Church.

## Facilities:

The most visible fruit of the $16 Million "Jewel of Grace, Fire for Ministry" capital campaign is the completed construction and tripled size of Covenant's J. Oliver Buswell, Jr. Library. We are reveling in the expansive space for books, the beautiful surroundings for student study, the vastly upgraded technology resources, multiple conference centers, and a new home for faculty offices.

We had a glorious day of dismissed classes as students and professors joined to move books and offices to the new facility. We were pleased that supervisors of the professional library moving company (that helps hundreds of libraries with such transitions) said that they had never seen the kind of student and faculty support that we had involved in this library move.

<a id="ga29-p493"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=495 printed_page=493 -->

The Library expansion also provided additional space for the PCA Historical Center Archives, and an internet-enhanced Resource and Information Center that pastors and alums can use from remote locations.

## Administrative Blessings:

In light of the growing technology pressures and opportunities in higher education we have added a Chief Technology Officer to our President’s Cabinet. Dr. Brad Hough, from our Nashville PCA church family, came this past fall to lead our technology efforts. He received a Ph.D. in Instructional Technology from Vanderbilt University, and also holds a Master’s Degree in School Administration and a Bachelor’s in Interactive Media Design. Dr. Hough is an author and speaker on the use of technology in education and has developed and provided custom technology training for a number of universities and schools.

Mr. Dave Wicker will serve as Covenant’s new Vice President for Seminary Advancement. This is a new position for the Seminary that includes both the traditional fundraising responsibilities of our chief development officer, as well as the new responsibilities of coordinating all of the Seminary’s church relations, public relations, media and publications. Mr. Wicker comes well prepared for these responsibilities having spent 25 years in marketing and management for some of our nation’s leading retailers and client relations firms.

## CONCLUSION

On March 3, 2001 the Rev. Dr. Donald J. MacNair went to join his Maker and Savior after a lifetime of faithful service to the Church of Jesus Christ. Don MacNair was one of the chief architects of the Joining and Receiving of the RPCES and the PCA. He remained one of the staunchest and most articulate advocates of bringing together these like-minded bodies committed to biblical Presbyterianism even when he knew that the consolidation would require that he leave his position as head of his denomination’s church planting arm. Dr. MacNair was far more concerned for the advancement of the Kingdom than he was for his own personal advantage.

Don MacNair’s commitment to Kingdom purposes was displayed over and over as this visionary and humble man met with a few other pastors and layman at his desk 45 years ago to plan the organization of Covenant College and Seminary. He even helped string the land survey chains when the boundaries of the original campuses were marked. He led the construction effort of the flagship Covenant Presbyterian Church in St. Louis, and also was the visionary leader behind the now-large, Christian retirement community known as Friendship Village. An author of seven books on the care and feeding of the local church, Don MacNair poured his last years into church consultation through Churches Vitalized, an organization he designed to help congregations identify their sources of hurting and their paths to healing according to biblical principles of leadership and organization. These principles Don MacNair also taught to a generation of young men at Covenant Theological Seminary as a wise and thoughtful father in the faith.

Donald J. MacNair’s entry into glory is accompanied this year by the news of the pressures of age upon several of the men and spouses of that first generation of

<a id="ga29-p494"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=496 printed_page=494 -->

Covenant Seminary professors. Specifically I covet your prayers for Dr. R. Laird
Harris, Dr. Wilbur B.W allis, and Dr. W. Harold Mare - my own dear and honored
professors. These are the men who, though no longer teaching at Covenant, we
continue to honor with emeritus status for their noteworthy sacrifices made in the cause
of defending the truth of scripture and the historic christian faith. When reputations,
pensions and salaries had to be jeopardized to establish a school faithful to the truth of
Scripture, they did not hesitate. echoing the response of isaiah they said simply, “Here
am I, send m e.”
Now God is calling these who have run the race with such integrity and courage
close to home, and they are passing the baton to us. I have never felt that baton so
weighty in my hand as in this past year, and I have never felt more thankful to these
men - my fathers in the faith - for running the race so well. May God give us grace to
do the same as we trust his Word, seek his face, and proclaim his Son for this
generation and the next.

Respectfully Submitted,

Bryan Chapell, President
Covenant Theological Seminary

recommendations
1. that the General Assembly give thanks to god for the ministry of covenant
Theological Seminary, for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the Reformed
faith, and the Great Commission, for its students and graduates, its faculty
and staff, its Trustees, and for those who support the Seminary through their
prayers and gifts.
2. that the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of covenant theological
Seminary through their prayers and gifts, and by recommending the
Seminary to prospective students.
3. that the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of covenant theological
Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by the General Assembly.
4. that the minutes of the meetings of the board of trustees of covenant
Theological Seminary for May 19-20, 2000, September 22-23, 2000, and
January 26-27, 2001 be approved; and that the minutes of the meeting of the
executive committee of the board of trustees for december 1, 2000,
January 26, 2001, and March 2, 2001 be approved.
5. That the financial audit for Covenant Theological Seminary for the fiscal
year ending June 30, 2000 by Humes and Barrington, CPA be received.
6. That the proposed 2001-2002 budget of covenant theological seminary be
approved.
7. that the assembly pray for the ministry of covenant theological Seminary.

<a id="ga29-p495"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=497 printed_page=495 -->

## REPORT OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF FUNDS TO THE TWENTY-NINTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

### Statement of Purpose

The purpose of the Board of Trustees of Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God through serving His servants who minister in the Church by offering financial products to provide for the health and welfare, retirement and relief needs of the denomination’s ministers, missionaries, lay church workers and their families, and to serve the churches, committees and agencies of the PCA and related organizations by sponsoring and endorsing a denominational property and liability insurance program.

### Summary

Year 2000 came and went with little, if any, of the dreaded predictions of Y2K computer problems that had consumed so much energy and time for several prior years. Your Board and Staff are happy to report another successful year for the PCA benefit plans and ministerial relief. We began two new programs the first of the year--long-term care insurance and a property and liability insurance plan.

The PCA retirement plan continued its growth pattern in both number of participants and assets. Our Ministerial Relief ministry continued to touch the lives of a growing number of retired and disabled pastors, missionaries and lay employees and their surviving widows and dependent children.

Total assets under management increased, but at a slower pace than in recent years, primarily because of the faltering stock market, which finally cooled and declined after the first quarter. Most benchmark indexes, including the Dow Jones Industrials, the S&amp;P 500 and the Nasdaq, reflected negative returns for the year. At year end, assets stood at $176.7 million, compared with $172.1 million for 1999, an increase of 2.7%

Following are some of the initiatives pursued during 2000.

- Implemented a new long-term care insurance program for PCA ministers, missionaries, lay employees, retirees and their families through CNA Insurance Company.

<a id="ga29-p496"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=498 printed_page=496 -->

• Implemented a property and liability insurance program for PCA churches and other
entities through Aon Religious Institutions Alliance. For 2001 we changed carriers
to GuideOne Insurance Company.
• Reduced premiums for the Long-Term Disability and Term Life Insurance Plans for
2001 .
• added chicago capital management as our fixed income manager for retirement,
insurance and relief fund portfolios.
• Changed custodial banks from W achovia to First Union.
• began the process of changing insurance administrators, which was completed on
April 1,2001.

Please refer to Appendix One —PROGRAM SUMMARY —for comparative statistical
information on the PCA benefit plans for the past three years.

Retirement Plans

New enrollments in the PCA Tax Sheltered Annuity Plan were slightly over 300. Total
enrollment, including retirees on benefit, surviving spouses and those with frozen
accounts, at year end stood at 4,328. Retirement plan assets were $171.2 million,
compared to $167.5 million for 1999, a 2.2% increase. Net returns for the retirement
funds are shown in the chart below.

1999 2000
PCA Equity Fund 15.2% 2.2%
PCA Balanced Fund 1.1% -2.1%
PCA Income Fund 3.2% 8.2%
Aggressive Growth Fund 44.4% -10.3%
Growth Fund 4.0% -20.3%
Growth and Income Fund 14.7% 13.6%
Stock Index Fund 20.7% -9.2%
Foreign Fund 39.0% -3.7%
Bond Fund 3.1% 8.9%
Money Market 4.6% 6.0%

It is significant that, in a year in which the Dow Jones (-4.7%), S&P 500 (-9.1% ) and
Nasdaq (-39.3%) were all negative, two of our equity funds (PCA Equity and Growth
and Income) produced positive gains. Overall, five of the ten funds were up for the
year. Bonds generally out-performed equities, as reflected in the returns of the PCA
Income Fund and the Bond Fund versus all the equity funds, except the Growth and
Income Fund. The Money Market Fund produced its best ever gain.

<a id="ga29-p497"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=499 printed_page=497 -->

## Insurance Plans

### Health Plans

The two health plans basically held their own for the year—beginning the year with total enrollments of 805 and ending the year with 800. The Pacific Life plan had favorable claims experience, resulting in an overall premium increase of only 4.8% for 2001, compared to a 14% increase for 2000. The Blue Choice HMO/Point of Service Plans for the Atlanta area had increases of 17-20%, effective August 1. Benefit changes also increased copays for prescription drugs and emergency room visits. We provide annual open enrollment for both plans, in December and July, respectively.

### Long-Term Disability Plan

Standard Insurance of Oregon became our LTD carrier January 1, 2000, under a contract which, in most respects, duplicated the benefits under our prior contract with UNUM. Enrollment in the plan continued to increase. Based on good experience we were able to reduce premiums for 2001 from .75% to .65% of compensation for those covered under Social Security and from 1.00% to .90% for those not under Social Security.

### Life Insurance Plans

Continued good claims experience and other favorable factors enabled the Trustees to approve a premium reduction for 2001 for the PCA Term Life Insurance Plan from $23 to $20 per month. This term plan maintains a single premium for all participants, but reduces the amount of coverage in five year age brackets. By contrast, the Optional Life Insurance Plan provides for participant flexibility as to amount of coverage—from $20,000 to $250,000—with age based premiums in five year brackets. It also provides for spouse and dependent children coverages, at the option of the participant.

### Ministerial Relief

During 2000, 7 ministers, 1 minister’s wife, 1 missionary’s wife, 2 ministers’ widows, 4 lay employees, and 1 lay employee’s widow died. Mrs. Ann Llewelyn continues to minister to a growing number of ministry widows by telephone counseling, letters and personal visits. In addition we supply regular monthly financial assistance to needy retirees, disabled, widows and dependents. The Relief Fund also provides periodic payments on a case-by-case basis to help pay medical and dental bills for eligible recipients. We also pay health insurance premiums for limited time periods for PCA ministers between calls and for some disabled ministers and lay workers.

The annual Christmas Offering provides primary funding for Relief activities. Through March 20, 2001, the offering for Christmas 2000 was over $392,993.55. Many thanks to the churches and individuals who gave.

<a id="ga29-p498"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=500 printed_page=498 -->

Legislative Changes

Social Security

The United States Congress passed legislation allowing ministers who had previously
opted out of social security to opt back into the system during 2000 or 2001.
Information about the time frames and procedures was provided in the Fall 2000 Benefit
Bulletin, our semi-annual newsletter, and in the 2001 Ministers Tax Guide, distributed
free to all PCA ministers. This information can also be found on our Web site at
www.pcanet. org.

Congress also passed legislation during 2000 dropping the earnings limits for retirees
receiving Social Security for those age 65-69 who continue to work. This is good news
for PCA ministers and lay employees who would like to draw Social Security benefits
while continuing to work beyond age 65. As normal retirement age for full benefits
creeps upward for those born after 1937, so will the effects of this change. The earnings
limits still apply to those who begin receiving benefits between ages 62-64. Those age
70 and above have never had an earnings limit.

Housing Allowance

(The following three paragraphs are excerpted from a memo dated October 25, 2000,
from attorney David A. starr of williams and Jensen, aw ashington, DC law firm
representing the Church Alliance.)

A U.S. Tax Court case in 2000, Warren v. Commissioner, has called into question the
scope of clergy housing allowance. In this case the Tax Court determined that the
amount of income that can be excluded as housing allowance is not limited to the fair
market rental value of a home. This decision is currently being appealed by the IRS to a
federal appeals court. A final judicial determination may be many months away. In the
mean time, theirs is not obligated to alter its long standing interpretation of the
housing allowance, and, as indicated by its appeal, continues to take the position that
housing allowance is limited to fair market rental value.

Churches and clergy should act with extreme caution in reiving on the Tax Court
decision until the courts have made a final determination. In addition, the housing
allowance is a creature of statute, and Congress can always modify the special tax
treatment accorded the housing allowance. The legislative risk increases if Congress
believes that the courts have expanded the scope of the housing allowance to create
unintended results.
* * *

In the event that the Tax Court decision is reversed on appeal, tax payers who either
amend their prior tax returns or prospectively exclude more than the fair market rental
value may be subject to deficiency determinations by the IRS. The IRS could assess tax

<a id="ga29-p499"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=501 printed_page=499 -->

penalties and interest. Increased housing allowance deductions can also impact the
amount of other allowable tax deductions that are based on taxable income levels, such
as deductible IRA contributions. (Or contributions to the PCA Tax-Sheltered Annuity
Plan.)

his three-year-old brother. We applaud her decision and wish her and husband
Tom best wishes and God’s blessing.

THIS AMENDMENT to the Presbyterian Church in America Money Purchase Pension
Plan (hereinafter referred to as the “Plan”), made this 2 nd day o fm arch, 2001, by
the board of trustees of the Insurance, annuity and relief funds of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereinafter referred to as the “Trustees”), to be effective as noted
below;

WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Presbyterian Church in America sponsors and maintains the
Plan as a qualified plan under Section 401(a) of the internal revenue code of 1986, as
amended (“Code”), and a church plan under Section 414(e) of such Code; and

WHEREAS, Section 11.01 of the plan reserves to the trustees the right to alter
or amend the plan subject to the approval of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the trustees to amend the plan to adopt the new
required minimum distribution rules under Section 401(a)(9) of the internal revenue
Code proposed by the Internal Revenue Service in January, 2001;

in Exhibit A; and it is

funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is hereby authorized to take any and all
actions necessary and desirable to carry out this and the foregoing resolution, including
making any modifications to the Plan required by the Internal Revenue Service.

Amendment to be executed on the day and year first above written.

The Benefits Trust is hereby amended as follows:
A rticle I, Section 1.01, last paragraph is amended by inserting a new sentence
after the first to read as follows, “The foregoing does not restrict the Trustees’
authority to re-allocate funds among various accounts in accordance with this
Trust Agreem ent.”

A rticle III, Section 3.01 is amended by splitting it into three sections, and
renumbering the remaining sections of article iii accordingly, as follows. The
section through item (p) remains Section 3.01. The paragraph starting after item
(p) and running through the first item (c) becomes “Section 3.02 Arms-Length
Requirem ent.” The paragraph beginning with “For purposes of the preceding
sentence the term “party in interest” becomes “Section 3.03 Party in Interest.”

A rticle III, Section 3.01, the second paragraph is amended by inserting in the
first sentence, after “exercised by the absolute owner thereof,” the phrase
“without report to or notification of any court or other governmental agency or
authority”.

A rticle III, New Section 3.03 is amended by modifying indented item (b) to
read as follows: “Employers participating in the Plans and their Employees,
excluding the Presbyterian Church in America, Insurance, Annuities & Relief,
and any other agency of the Church, and excluding employees of the foregoing;
or” .

A rticle III, New Section 3.04 (old 3.02) is amended to read as follows:

Notwithstanding the fact that distributees may be parties in interest as
defined in the foregoing section, the Trustees shall from time to time in
accordance with the provisions of the plans make distributions from the
Trust to such persons, in such manner, in such amounts and for such
purposes as provided in such Plans, including hardship relief
distributions to needy ministers, lay Church workers, and the surviving
spouses and dependent children of ministers and lay church workers.

A rticle III, New Section 3.06 (old 3.04) is amended by modifying item (i) to
read “solely in the interests of participants and Beneficiaries, and needy
ministers, lay Church workers, and the surviving spouses and dependent
children of ministers and lay church workers, and for the exclusive purpose of
providing benefits to those persons and defraying reasonable expenses of
administration;”.

<a id="ga29-p508"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=510 printed_page=508 -->

Article V, Section 5.01 is amended by modifying the first sentence to read, “It is the intention of the Presbyterian Church in America that this Trust and the various Plans that are funded through this Trust will be a permanent plan of benefits administered for the exclusive benefit of Participants and their Beneficiaries, needy ministers, lay Church workers, and the surviving spouses and dependent children of ministers and lay Church workers.”

Article V, Section 5.01 is amended by modifying the third sentence to read, “Upon the termination, the Trust shall be applied by the Trustees to provide additional life, sick, accident or other welfare benefits, or hardship relief, and in no event shall any assets of the Trust Fund revert to any Employer that has participated in the Plans.

Article VI, Section 6.01 is amended by modifying the last sentence and adding two new sentences to the end, to read as follows, “Furthermore, such amendment shall not operate to cause any part of the Trust Fund, other than such part as is required to pay taxes and administration expenses, to be used for, or diverted to, purposes other than for the exclusive benefit of the Employers’ Participants and their Beneficiaries, needy ministers, lay Church workers, and the surviving spouses and dependent children of ministers and lay Church workers.

Article IX, Section 9.01 is amended to read as follows:

**Diversion.** No part of the corpus or income of the Trust Fund shall be used for, or diverted to, purposes other than for the exclusive benefit of participants or their Beneficiaries, needy ministers, lay Church workers, or the surviving spouses and dependent children of ministers and lay Church workers prior to the satisfaction of all liabilities with respect to those persons under the Trust.
