Interchurch Relations Committee Report to
The Twenty-Ninth General Assembly, 2001

membership

TE Derek W. H. Thomas RE Robert Ashlock, Secretary
TE R. Irfon Hughes, Chairman RE R. Meade Guy,
TE Bruce Howes RE David R. Lambert
TE M. Christopher Ehlers, Alternate RE Jack Merry, Alternate
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk (RAO III, 3-2, j., ex officio)
TE Ken Thompson (MTW Consultant, Advisory Member)

II. Meetings

September 19, 2000 - Atlanta
December 11, 2000 - Conference call subsequent to NAP ARC fall meeting
March 12, 2001 - Conference call subsequent to NAE meeting

III. Inform ation Items
A. The Twenty-eighth Assembly referred three matters to the IRC for action
and report.
1. the national association of Evangelicals’ revising its rules so as to
allow denominations that a remembers of the national council of
Churches, and are willing to endorse the doctrinal statement of the NAE,
to hold membership in both bodies simultaneously (Min ute soft he
Twenty-eighth General Assembly, p.63).
2. Communication with the Evangelical Presbyterian Church regarding
their receiving a minister under the discipline of the PCA (Min ute soft he
Twenty-eighth General Assembly, p.63-64).
3. clarification of the PCA ’s relationship with the world fellowship of
Reformed Churches (Minutes of the Twenty-eighth General Assembly,
p.64).

B. The committee met via conference call at its September 19, 2000 meeting
with Dr. Kevin Mannoia, president of the NAE, Dr. Edward Foggs,
chairperson of the board of na E, and Mrs. Dianne Knippers, Chairperson
of the membership Committee. the committee discussed the issue of the
national association of Evangelical’s revising Bylaw B-7 as follows:

<a id="ga29-p511"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=513 printed_page=511 -->

B-7 Dual Membership. Although denominations shall not hold dual membership in the National Association of Evangelicals and any other organization at a comparable ecclesiastical level, such as the National Council of Churches of Christ in America, the World Council of Churches, and the American Council of Christian Churches, local churches and organizations whose denominations who belong to other such bodies but who desire membership in the National Association of Evangelicals may be received—provided they fulfill membership requirements. Denominations may hold dual membership in the National Association of Evangelicals and other organizations at a comparable ecclesiastical level. Denominations holding membership in other such organizations may be received into the NAE provided they fulfill the membership requirements.

The committee discussed the matter with the NAE representatives for about an hour. It was explained to the committee that the Reformed Church in America had initiated the request for the rule change so that the entire RCA, not just the Western Synod of the RCA could become a member of the NAE. The committee advised the NAE representatives of the concern the PCA has over the rule change and requested that it be changed. After the meeting the PCA Stated Clerk wrote the President of the NAE and formally requested that the rule change regarding “B-7 Dual Membership” be rescinded. The national convention of the NAE met March 5-7, 2001 in Dallas, Texas. TE Irfon Hughes, TE Bruce Howes and TE L. Roy Taylor attended, representing the General PCA IRC (See Attachment A). At the meeting of the Board of Directors the PCA Stated Clerk made the motion that the rule change regarding “dual membership” be rescinded and spoke to the issue. The motion was referred to the Bylaws Committee for study and report to the 2002 Convention. The PCA delegation believed that the PCA motion was well received by the general membership at the Board meeting. The Board also passed an resolution reaffirming the NAE’s doctrinal commitment and resolved “not to seek out or respond to any overture of any body that would seek to subsume the NAE into the contemporary ecumenical movement, while continuing to partner with groups, as appropriate, to advance singular issues.” Subsequent to the convention Dr. Mannoia issued a statement that the restudy [of Rule B-7] will occur to honor one of our own [the PCA] but that he does not expect it will result in the deletion of the bylaw from the NAE’s books and that the chances are not great that it would be altered (“NAE Washington Insight” Church Edition, April, 2001). It is the intention of the IRC to seek to persuade the NAE Board of Directors, at the 2002 convention, to rescind Rule change B-7 and then to report the outcome to the 2002 General Assembly.

<a id="ga29-p512"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=514 printed_page=512 -->

C. The Committee Chairman and the Stated Clerk met via conference call December 19, 2000 with the retiring Stated Clerk Dr. Ed Davis, the newly elected Stated Clerk Dr. Michael Glodo, and the Chairman of the Fraternal Relations Committee of the Evangelical Presbyterian Church, Mr. Paul Heidebrecht, to discuss with them the PCA’s concern over their receiving into the EPC a PCA minister who was under discipline. (The Clerk had previously written the EPC Clerk advising him of the PCA Assembly’s actions.) A procedure was agreed upon whereby the minister in question and representatives of the EPC Presbytery of the Mid-America would appear before Illiana Presbytery at their January 13, 2001 meeting to seek to effect reconciliation. When it was learned that there would be a proposal to proceed to depose and excommunicate TE David Baer at the January 13 meeting, the EPC delegation decided it would not be fruitful to attend. Illiana Presbytery did, in fact, depose and excommunicate Mr. Baer. The Presbytery subsequently adopted a motion that a committee be appointed to confer with the EPC Presbytery of the Mid-America to discuss reconciliation.

D. The World Fellowship of Reformed Churches was formed in 1992 by the PCA, the National Presbyterian Church of Mexico, and the Presbyterian Church of Brazil under the World Evangelical Fellowship through our membership in the NAE, under the authorization of the Nineteenth General Assembly. (See Minutes of the Nineteenth General Assembly, 1991, II, Appendix B, p. 67; Minutes of the Twenty-second General Assembly, 1994 pp. 198, 521-522; Minutes of the Twenty-third General Assembly, 1995, pp. 166, 382-384). The WFRC only meets every three or four years, which is why there has not been a report in recent Assemblies. The WRFC Bylaws stipulate that officers are elected to serve a full term, which is why some PCA ministers who are no longer on the IRC have been serving as officers (TE Eric Perrin, TE Paul Gilchrist). The WFRC initiated contacts with the International Reformed Fellowship, founded in 1994, which is primarily composed of Churches in Asia. Representatives of the two groups met in Orlando, FL October 24-27, 2000 to discuss a possible merger of the two fellowships. A “joining and receiving” took place whereby the IRC was assimilated into the WRFC and the name of the organization was changed to “The International Reformed Fellowship.” (See Attachment B). The doctrinal basis of the World Reformed Fellowship is the inspiration and inerrancy of Holy Scripture, the ancient universal creeds (Apostles, Nicene, Chalcedon) and adherence to one or more of the historic expressions of the Reformed Faith. The PCA representatives were TE Bruce Howes and TE L. Roy Taylor. The PCA representatives proposed a bicameral arrangement whereby denominations would vote on major policy decisions and institutions, individual members, and para-church ministry members would vote in a separate group, with agreement of both bodies being required and a weighed voting system to give larger denominations, such as the PCA, a

<a id="ga29-p513"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=515 printed_page=513 -->

greater voice. Bylaws were adopted along with the name change (see Attachment C). Of interest to the Assembly is that the World Reformed Fellowship as presently constituted is different in several respects from the World Fellowship of Reformed Churches: (1) The WRF is truly international and worldwide, whereas its precursor, the WFRC, was, to a great degree, inter-American (North, Central and South America), though there were some member Churches in India and East Africa, (2) The membership of the WFRC was limited to denominations, whereas WRF membership is open to denominations, institutions, particular congregations, mission agencies, and individuals, (3) The voting procedure is different as noted above, and (4) The membership of WFRC was limited to denominations holding to Reformed-covenantal Theology and Presbyterian polity. The WRF membership is open to conservative-evangelical denominations, institutions, particular congregations, mission agencies, and individuals, from the entirety of the Reformed Tradition as long as they held to the doctrinal basis of the Fellowship as specified in the Bylaws. For example, Reformed Episcopalians, Reformed Baptists, and Reformed Congregationalists would be eligible for membership. This recognizes the resurgence of the Reformed Faith in its various expressions around the world. *Significant also is that, for perhaps the first time in history, a fellowship of the conservative, evangelical, Reformed Community has been formed that encompasses the various sub-divisions of the Reformed Tradition.*

E. The PCA is a member of the North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council (NAPARC). IRC chairman, TE R. Irfon Hughes, TE Bruce Howes, and the PCA Stated Clerk attended the NAPARC meeting, November 14-15, 2000 in Los Angeles, California. The PCA Stated clerk was re-elected chairman. The two main items of discussion were the similarities and differences among the member denominations with a view toward possible Church union(s) and the status of the Christian Reformed Church, now a suspended member.

1. Each denomination presented its distinctives. The present member Churches of NAPARC are: Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church, Korean American Presbyterian Church, Orthodox Presbyterian Church, Presbyterian Church in America, Reformed Church in the United States, Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America, and Christian Reformed Church in North America (suspended). TE Hughes was appointed to a committee to collate the reports and report to the 2001 meeting. The IRC will report in 2002 on the progress of those continued discussions regarding possible Church union(s).

2. The Christian Reformed Church of North America reconsidered and reaffirmed its stand on the ordination of women at its General Synod 2000, i.e. they reaffirmed that the portion of their *Church Order* (Article 3-a, the word “male”) limiting the offices of minister, elder, and

<a id="ga29-p514"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=516 printed_page=514 -->

evangelist to men be declared “inoperative” so that the offices of elder, minister, and evangelist are open to women as well as men. A local option provision was approved for local churches and classes that differ with the General Synod’s decision. The CRC delegation argued that *The Belgic Confession*, in both its French and Latin versions, in Article 30, uses a generic term, translated into English as “men,” or “persons” when speaking of “The Government and Offices in the Church.”¹ The CRC delegation argued, that the matter of women’s ordination is extra-confessional as far as the CRC is concerned and that the basis of NAPARC is the confessions of the member denominations. Others argued that; (1) The Belgic Confession specifically makes mention of “the rule prescribed by St. Paul in his epistle to Timothy,” (2) The Basis of NAPARC is first of all the Bible and secondarily the Reformed Confessions,² (3) the continental Reformed Churches in the Reformation era did not ordain women to ecclesiastical leadership, (4) for the General Synod to declare a portion of its *Church Order* “inoperative” without going through the process of actually amending the constitution circumvents that constitution and deprives the respective classes from voting as classes for an actual constitutional change.³ The PCA delegation made a motion that the CRC be expelled from NAPARC on the grounds of their action on women’s ordination. A substitute motion passed that the matter to delayed until next year at which time more detailed specifications be presented. It is the intention of the IRC to ask again, at the 2001 NAPARC meeting, that the CRC be expelled from NAPARC on the ground of their action on women’s ordination. We regard this as a matter of constitutional integrity.

F. Communications from the United Reformed Churches have been received indicating their desire for pursuing further relations with the PCA. The matter will be discussed at the fall meeting (2001) of the IRC.

---

¹ The sentence in question in the *Belgic Confession* Article 30 is, “By these means everything will be carried on in the Church with good order and decency, when faithful men [emphasis added] are chosen, according to the rule prescribed by St. Paul in his epistle to Timothy.” I Timothy 3:1 and Titus 1:5 are cited as proof texts.

² The Constitution of NAPARC states in Article II, the “BASIS OF THE COUNCIL. Confessing Jesus Christ as only Savior and Sovereign Lord over all life, we affirm the basis of the fellowship of Presbyterian and Reformed Churches to be full commitment to the Bible in its entirety as the Word of God written, without error in all its parts and to its teachings as set forth in the Heidelberg Catechism, the Belgic Confession, the Canons of Dort, the Westminster Confession of Faith, and the Westminster Larger and Shorter Catechisms. That the adopted basis of fellowship be regarded as warrant for the establishment of a formal relationship in the nature of a council, that is, a fellowship that enables the constituent churches to advise, counsel, and cooperate in various matters with one another and hold out before each other the desirability and need for organic union of Churches that are of like faith and practice.”

³ A survey conducted by the General Synod of the CRC indicated that only 15% of the churches have female elders and 2% have female ministers; 37% of the classes have declared the word “male” inoperative in Article 3-a of the *CRC Church Order*.

ATTACHMENT A

National Association of Evangelicals (Reprinted from PCANews.com)

The National Association of Evangelicals (NAE) held its annual convention in Dallas on March 5-7, 2001. The Presbyterian Church in America, which is a member of the NAE, was represented by TE Irfon Hughes, Chairman of the PCA Interchurch Relations Committee (IRC), TE Bruce Howes, a member of the IRC, and TE Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk of the PCA.

One of the issues debated at the meeting was qualifications for NAE membership. At its meeting last year, the NAE changed its membership rules to allow denominations who were members of the National Council of Churches (NCC) to be members of NAE as well. This change was made at the request of the Reformed Church in America, a member of the NCC.

The 28th General Assembly of the PCA (2000) expressed its concern over this rule change and instructed the IRC to raise this matter with the NAE leadership. This past fall the IRC met with representatives of NAE to discuss the PCA’s concerns.

Another Christian organization, the National Religious Broadcasters, had already voted, 80-0 on Feb. 9, to formally end its relationship with the NAE because of this rule change. The conservative Christian broadcasters group had been affiliated with the NAE since 1944.

At the meeting of the NAE Board of Directors on March 5, Stated Clerk Taylor formally requested that the NAE rescind the membership rule change and return to its original policy. That original policy stated that denominations may be members of either the NAE or the NCC, but not both simultaneously.

Dr. Taylor offered five reasons for the motion to rescind:

1. Allowing dual membership in the NAE and the NCC is contrary to the original purpose for the founding of the NAE. The NAE was founded as an evangelical alternative to the theologically diverse Federal Council of Churches/NCC on the theological left and the fundamentalist American Council of Churches on the theological right.

2. Allowing dual membership is insensitive to the suffering some NAE-related evangelicals endured in their former connections with NCC-related denominations. For example, many of these ministers were denied opportunities

<a id="ga29-p517"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=519 printed_page=517 -->

for pastoral ministry and churches lost their buildings because of their
evangelical commitments.

3. allowing dual membership raises a question of maintaining doctrinal integrity.
a number of NCC-related denominations use orthodox creeds in worship (e.g.,
the A postles’ Creed and the Nicene Creed) and have historic confessions of faith
that are evangelical, yet ministers and church officers are not consistently
required to adhere faithfully to and minister in accordance with these creeds and
confessions. if doctrinal laxity is allowed regarding the historic creeds and
confessions of the Church, could then ae reasonably expect commitment to its
doctrinal statement?

4. Allowing dual membership could prove to be a Phyrric victory. The NAE may
gain some members of n cc denominations, but lose some denominations and
organizations concerned about the membership rule change. The National
Religious Broadcasters’ recent action to withdraw its membership from NAE
was cited as an example.

5. Allowing dual membership sends the wrong signal to our post-modern culture in
which truth is regarded as relative. The NAE needs to take care that it not
unwittingly communicate to NCC-related denominations that the bedrock truths
of evangelical is mare negotiable.

After extended discussion, the motion was referred to the Bylaws Committee to study
and report to next year’s convention. Later in the day, the board of directors approved
a Sense-of-the-Association resolution. This resolution recognized that the changes in
membership requirements “have caused some concern and m isunderstanding.” It also
indicated that the bylaws would no tallow the rescinding of last year’s action at the
present meeting.

The resolution also stated that, “We affirm that the NAE holds as firmly as ever to our
sixty-year-old statement of faith that has defined our evangelical movement from its
founding.” It also affirmed “our desire to define evangelicalism by inclusion and
affirmation rather than by exclusion.”

The resolution concluded, “We affirm that our purpose in all matters related to the
identity of then ae is to advance the cause of christ and to position then ae to speak
effectively as a voice for all Christians who will embrace our historic Christian faith
expressed in our statement of Faith.”

<a id="ga29-p518"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=520 printed_page=518 -->

ATTACHMENT B

world fellowship of reformed churches has “Jo in ing andre ce iv in g” with the
international reformed fellowship to become
the world reformed fellowship
(R e printed from pc anew s.com )

the world fellowship of reformed churches (W FRC) and the International Reformed
Fellowship (IRF) joined together October 24-26 a ta meeting in Orlando, Florida, to
create a worldwide fellowship among evangelical Presbyterian and Reformed
Christians. the new name of the expanded organization is the world reformed
Fellowship (WRF).

the meeting took place on the campus of reformed theological seminary and the
canterbury retreat center and included leaders from five continents and a number of
countries which included: the USA, France, Scotland, Ghana, Nigeria, Argentina,
Romania. Brazil. South Africa, Puerto Rico, Ecuador, the Philippines, Taiwan, Mexico,
Peru, Honduras, Korea, Kenya, Uganda, Hungary, and Myanmar (Burma) attended the
meeting.

PCA Stated Clerk Roy Taylor and Rev. Bruce Howes represented the PC A ’s
Interchurch Relations Committee.

The World Reformed Fellow ship’s purpose is to encourage understanding and
cooperation among evangelical Presbyterian and Reformed denominations and
institutions, and links those having ministry' resources with those possessing vision but
few resources.

Also, the group promotes Reformed thinking and a Reformed world and life view, and
fosters evangelism, missions and church planting, theological education and
international communication.

wrf grew out of two fellowships established in the early 1990s. The World
fellowship of reformed churches (W FRC) was formed in 1992 by the Presbyterian Church in America, the national presbyterian church o fm exico, and the Presbyterian
church of brazil and connected evangelical denominations from most of the countries
of latin America, plus churches from India, East Africa, and the United States.

The International Reformed Fellowship (IRF) was organized in 1994 to join evangelical
Reformed leaders from across Asia. The IRF, which has held its meetings in Asia,
included leaders from Korea, Indonesia, Taiwan, and Japan.

<a id="ga29-p519"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=521 printed_page=519 -->

Both organizations possessed similar visions to provide fellowship and opportunities for interaction that would encompass the entire world. In 1999, leaders of the WFRC and IRF discussed the possibility of the two fellowships coming together.

At the October meeting, the WFRC received the IRF into the WFRC by a “Joining and Receiving” to form a truly worldwide, conservative, evangelical Reformed fellowship and the name was changed to the World Reformed Fellowship (WRF).

The doctrinal standards for the newly-merged WRF were specified as the Scots Confession of 1560, the Gallican Confession, the Heidelberg Catechism, the Belgic Confession, the Canons of Dort, the Thirty-nine Articles of Religion, the Westminster Confession of Faith, the Westminster Catechisms, the Savoy Declaration, the Second Helvetic Confession, and the London Confession of 1689, as well as the Apostles’ Creed, the Nicene Creed, and the Chalcedonian Definition.

Membership eligibility was expanded to include not only denominations but also local churches, educational institutions, ministry agencies and individuals that would affirm the commitment to:

- The full inspiration and inerrancy of Scripture
- The ancient universal creeds (Apostle’s, Nicene, Chalcedon)
- Adherence to one or more of the historic expressions of the Reformed faith

On this basis virtually all Evangelical and Reformed branches of the Church could participate, that is, Presbyterian, Continental Reformed, Congregational, Reformed Baptist and Reformed Episcopal.

The WRF is a fellowship, not a council. A council issues position documents, certifies member church actions, and aims at eventual organic union of member churches.

A fellowship provides a context in which churches, ministries, and institutions may develop friendships with each other leading to more extensive and effective ministry, influencing each other toward greater consistency in biblical faith and witness.

The purposes of WRF are to:

- Promote global evangelization on the basis of Reformed Theology
- Promote Reformed thinking and world-and-life view
- Inform and encourage churches and people who embrace the Reformed Faith
- Provide a network for communication and sharing of ministry
- Provide a forum for dialogue on current issues
- Be able to offer direction to the Evangelical and Reformed Communities
- Promote diaconal mercy ministries

<a id="ga29-p520"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=522 printed_page=520 -->

Reformed fellowships will be formed in Europe, Asia, Latin America, North America, and Africa as well as in specific countries.

A number of PCA people were involved in the meeting and several were elected to leadership positions.

- Dr. K. Eric Perrin - Chairman
- Dr. Paul Gilchrist - Executive Secretary
- Dr. Luder Whitlock - Executive Committee member
- Dr. L. Roy Taylor - Governing Board member
- Dr. Woody Lajara - Treasurer

WRF has a 26-member Governing Board representing 18 nations.

One of WRF’s goals of providing an information network will be enhanced by the development of a website. Face-to-face communication at the meeting produced one immediate discovery.

In an educational group meeting a Korean delegate remarked that several dozen ministers with PhD or ThD degrees were available in Korea but there were no seminary teaching positions open there now. A Mexican delegate responded, “Send us five professors. We need them right away.”

It is hoped that WRF will be a vehicle for bringing opportunities, resources and people together throughout the worldwide Reformed family.

## ATTACHMENT C

## BYLAWS
WORLD REFORMED FELLOWSHIP

Approved October 26, 2000
Corrected April 2001

## PREAMBLE

With gratitude to God, the International Reformed Fellowship joined the World Fellowship of Reformed Churches in October 2000, which then changed the name to the World Reformed Fellowship. Both fellowships were formed in the early Nineteen Nineties to seek to promote the Reformed faith as a witness to the world of the Sovereignty of God in the redemption of his people through the Lord Jesus Christ. This people of God, with diversified cultural and ethnic backgrounds, are one in Christ. God has enabled us to express the unity of the Church in the bonds of peace through

<a id="ga29-p521"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=523 printed_page=521 -->

cooperative agreements, having the same biblical, evangelical and missionary vision.
Therefore, believing that these a rebut expressions of a well thought out reformed
perspective, we would invite other Reformed Churches, agencies, institutions and
individuals to join with us under the following principles.

world reformed fellowship makes the distinction of being a fellowship, not a council.
World Reformed Fellow’ship recognizes that barriers of distance, culture and language
often make it difficult fora church to develop and maintain binding ties with a church
or churches of another country, which is essential to the conciliar model. A fellowship
provides the context in which Churches, ministries and institutions may become
acquainted with each other, and where their leaders may develop friendships and
trusting relationships leading to closer, mutually beneficial cooperation. members of a
fellowship are not obligated formally to be responsible for one another’s positions or
actions, but in an atmosphere of free association, may lovingly influence each other
toward greater consistency in Biblical faith and witness.

ARTICLE 1. NAME

the name of this organizations hall be world reformed Fellowship, hereafter WRF.

ARTICLE 2. AFFILIATION

WRF shall function in association with the World Evangelical Fellowship.

ARTICLE 3. PURPOSE

the purpose of wrf shall be to promote understanding and cooperation in the
following ways:

1. To promote Reformed thinking and a Reformed world and life view,
2. To inform and encourage Churches and people who em brace the
Reformed Faith,
3. to provide a network for communication and the sharing of ministry
resources,
4. To provide a forum for dialogue on current issues,
5. To be able to offer direction to the evangelical Reformed Community.
6. To promote evangelization in the Reformed Tradition.
7. To maintain, strengthen and defend the sound doctrines and biblical-
theological tenets that distinguish us as Reformed Christians.

WRF will promote its ministry with creativity, flexibility and efficiency. Our mission
goal should drive our structure and administration, as we adapt to the opportunity and
needs of the hour. We should proactively seek out individuals who can help WRF
attain its objectives.

<a id="ga29-p522"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=524 printed_page=522 -->

## ARTICLE 4. DOCTRINAL BASIS

1. We affirm the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments as the authoritative, God-breathed, and inerrant Word of God.

2. We stand in the mainstream of the historic Christian Faith in affirming the following catholic creeds of the Early Church: *The Apostle’s Creed*, the Nicene Creed, and the Chalcedonian Definition.

3. More specifically, every voting member affirms one of the following historic expressions of the Reformed Faith: *The Gallican Confession*, *The Scots Confession*, *The Belgic Confession*, *The Heidelberg Catechism*, *The Thirty-Nine Articles*, *The Second Helvetic Confession*, *The Canons of Dort*, *The Westminster Confession of Faith*, *The London Confession of 1689* and *The Savoy Declaration*.

## ARTICLE 5. MEMBERSHIP AND VOTING

1. The members of WRF shall include the following:

a. Church denominations. Denominations may be represented in General Assembly by no more than the eligible votes which is as follows: Denominations with fewer than 10,000 members shall have one vote. Denominations of 10,001 to 50,000 members shall have two votes. Denominations of 50,001 to 250,000 members shall have three votes. Denominations of 250,001 to 1 million members shall have four votes. Those above 1 million members shall have five votes. However, all denominations may have two representatives, but limited to the votes as noted.

b. Particular churches of non-member denominations may be represented at the General Assembly with one vote. Particular churches of member denominations may join WRF as non-voting members.

c. Educational institutions and mission agencies which have no denominational affiliation may be represented and have one vote. Denominational institutions may join as non-voting members.

d. Individuals who may not otherwise be a representative of any of the above organizations may join as members.

e. Voting will be in two classes: (1) Denominations and (2) Single congregations, agencies and institutions, and individual members. Approval of both classes will be necessary for a matter to be adopted.

f. Observers and non-voting members may participate but not have the privilege of voting.

<a id="ga29-p523"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=525 printed_page=523 -->

2. Prospective members shall make application to the Executive Committee. Applicants must meet the doctrinal criteria. The Executive Committee, after consulting with members in the local region, may vote to enroll the applicant as members.

3. Members shall be expected to contribute financially on an annual basis as determined by the Advisory Committee.

4. Members who no longer adhere to the purposes and doctrinal positions of WRF may be suspended from membership by the Executive Committee, but may be removed from membership only by action of the General Assembly.

## ARTICLE 6. GOVERNING BOARD

1. There shall be a Governing Board of twenty-six members of which eleven shall comprise the Executive Committee.

2. The officers of WRF shall be the Chairman, the first Vice Chairman, the second Vice Chairman, the first Secretary, the second Secretary and the Treasurer. In addition, these officers together with five members-at-large shall serve as the Executive Committee. The members-at-large shall be representatives from regional areas not otherwise represented by the officers.

3. The quorum for the Executive Committee shall be the majority of its members who participate in a duly called meeting. Stated meetings and special meetings with a thirty-day notice shall be considered duly called meetings.

4. The Executive Committee shall meet at least once every year.

5. In addition, the Executive Committee shall be responsible for the following functions: Programs, Planning, Budget and Finance, and Personnel. It shall also be responsible for processing applications for membership of prospective members. The Executive Committee shall establish subcommittees as needed to carry out the business of the WRF. The Executive Committee, on behalf of the General Assembly, may convene a major congress for Reformed thinking and theology. It shall be responsible for hiring staff to accomplish the administrative work of the organization.

6. The Executive Committee shall serve as a Nominating Committee for the WRF.

## ARTICLE 7. MEETINGS

1. A General Assembly of the WRF shall ordinarily meet at least once every four years. The General Assembly of WRF shall be called by the Executive

<a id="ga29-p524"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=526 printed_page=524 -->

Committee with a six-month written notice. a special called meeting of the
General Assembly may be called with a three-month written notice. Since the
World Evangelical Fellowship meets approximately every six years, the
executive committee should plan to schedule the meetings oft hew rf to
coincide with that of we f when possible.

2. the governing boards hall meet at the time of the General Assembly of the
WRF.

3. The matters to be voted on by the General Assembly will include the following:
a. Policy issues.
b. Budget and financial matters,
c. election of officers and committee members,
d. Membership applications.
e. Bylaws,
f. Meeting dates.

ARTICLE 8. COMMITTEES

In addition to the Governing Board, the WRF may erect the following or similar
committees to serve until replaced by the next General Assembly:

1. Contemporary Issues Committee: This committee shall seek to address critical
and emerging issues facing Christianity both globally and regionally.

2. Missions Committee: This committee shall seek to address strategic, cultural
and theological issues facing the Church in seeking to obey the Great
Commission.

3. Education Committee: This committee shall seek to address problems and issues
relating to all levels of education: local churches, elementary and secondary
education, college and university education, as well as theological seminary
education.

4. Communications Committee: This committee shall serve as public relations
committee and publications for WRF.

5. Diaconal Ministries Committee: this committees hall address issues of mercy
ministries such as orphanages, hospitals, medical clinics, etc.

6. Nominating Committee: The Executive Committee shall serve as the
nominating committee of the WRF.
a. the General Assembly shall elect officers and members of the advisory
Committee from a slate provided by the Nominating Committee.

<a id="ga29-p525"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=527 printed_page=525 -->

Nominations may be made from the floor to run against any nomination
made by the committee.
b. The term for election shall be for four years or until replaced by the next
General Assembly of WRF.

- **c.** Denominations may certify their representatives to the General Assembly of WRF. These representatives, if elected to serve a san officer or member-at-large shall serve to the end of the term of office.
- **d.** if an officer or committee member resigns or dies, or otherwise is not eligible for service, the Executive Committee may present a name in nomination on a ballot to the Advisory Committee by mail or e-mail for election to complete the term of service vacated.

ARTICLE 9. BOARD OF TRUSTEES

the executive committees hall serve as the board of trustees of WRF.

ARTICLE 10. REGIONAL FELLOW SHIPS

t hew rf shall assist and oversee in the formation of national and regional fellowships
to carryout the stated purposes oft hew rf as seems good to the members of that
respective area. the bylaws of the regional fellowships shall not be in conflict with the
bylaws of WRF.

ARTICLE 11. DISSOLUTION

in case of dissolution, the assets of wrf shall be distributed to some existing
evangelical and Reformed fellowship which is a qualified 501(c)(3) organization [under
the United States Tax Code].

ARTICLE 12. AMENDMENT OR SUSPENSION OF BYLAW S

these bylaws oft hew rf maybe amended or suspended only by a two-thirds vote of
those members present and voting at a duly called meeting. Amendments may only be
made when there has been at least a one-month prior notice to the membership.

<a id="ga29-p526"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=528 printed_page=526 -->

# THE REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA TO THE 29TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

## I. INTRODUCTION

### Advancing the Gospel through Renewing the Church

The Spirit of the Sovereign Lord is on me, because the Lord has anointed me to preach good news to the poor. He has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim freedom for the captives and release from darkness for the prisoners, to proclaim the year of the Lord’s favor and the day of vengeance of our God, to comfort all who mourn, and provide for those who grieve in Zion – to bestow on them a crown of beauty instead of ashes, the oil of gladness instead of mourning, and a garment of praise instead of a spirit of despair. They will be called oaks of righteousness, a planting of the Lord for the display of his splendor.

Isaiah 61:1-3

North America today is experiencing a rapidly growing state of urgency or even emergency when it comes to our spiritual condition. This needs no documentation to those committed to biblical distinctives in the way we are in the PCA. Some recent illustrations:

- Portland, Oregon, is a city in North America that has never been reached with the Gospel. One of the biggest “churches” in Portland, Oregon, is a congregation of several thousand of the New Age “faith.” By God’s grace, Charles Garland began a church plant in the city of Portland early in 2001, joining other churches in the suburban and greater Portland area pastored by Nathan Lewis, Jeff Doerr, Stephen Lewis, Jim Bordwine and Ted Shin. The population of the state of Oregon is estimated to be well under 5% evangelical.

- Clear across the continent, Portland, Maine, represents a similar population. While the light of the Gospel was once strong in most of New England, and while in New England as a whole the Gospel now shines much more dimly, the state of Maine has never been a Gospel stronghold. Estimates here also are that less than 5% of the population is evangelical.

<a id="ga29-p527"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=529 printed_page=527 -->

- Moving into the heart of what once was the strongest center of evangelical faith in America, Christ the King Presbyterian in Cambridge, Massachusetts, purchased a vacant church building for $275,000 with the capacity to serve morning and evening congregations of over 300 attending each (in fact, the property would have been worth more as a vacant lot than it was worth on the market as a church building). Similarly, Exeter Presbyterian purchased a building that will serve 200 near the town square for $165,000. Such is the state of the evangelical church in New England – church buildings that once seemed with people now sit vacant.

While many North Americans profess the Christian faith and attend worship each Lord’s own, the loss of influence of the Christian faith and moral values is apparent on every Lord. Missiologists tell us that the majority of missionaries in the world now are coming into the two-thirds world, rather than the United States.

While we grieve at the trends from one perspective, these challenging circumstances present not just a great opportunity but also a call to action. How will the PCA address these great challenges? From the perspective of Mission to North America, we believe that we are called to seek the Lord for:

- A radically greater commitment to the planting of new churches.
- New churches reach more people with the Gospel than do established churches.
- New churches enlarge the sending base for ministries in North America and missionaries throughout the world.

- In order to accomplish this purpose, there will have to be a much greater commitment to the revitalization of existing PCA churches:
- A greater commitment to internal spiritual health in our congregations – seeking the Lord for His calling to us in the midst of such a society of need as this.
- A greater commitment to evangelism and the kinds of word/deed works of mercy in our communities that will not only proclaim the Good News of God’s grace to sinners, but also demonstrate in action the loving kindness and tender mercies of our God.
- A greater commitment to church planting throughout North America and to world missions.

*In the PCA Committee on Mission to North America, we call upon the church to undertake a renewed commitment to the vision of reaching North America.*

## Advancing the Gospel through Renewing the Church

In 1973, we believed that the Lord founded us to be faithful to the Scriptures, the informed faith, and the Great Commission of Jesus Christ. Our assessment is that while the advancement in the Gospel may be strong in comparison with others in our land,

<a id="ga29-p528"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=530 printed_page=528 -->

even that strong advancement – at its current level – will accomplish little more than maintaining the PCA in its current strength.

If we are to wait upon the Lord for a greater work – for a renewal of His work through us – what are the essential elements of this renewal?

A renewed realization that the church is built by our Lord: “I will build my church,” (Matthew 16:18), by the power of His Spirit – “But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on you; and you will be my witnesses...to the ends of the earth.”(Acts 1:8). We call upon the church to a renewal of expectation that the church will be built by God’s power – that we will trust God for a bold advancement that exceeds the humanly predictable. Particularly as we venture into church planting in our cities with all of their great challenges, or in our smaller non-growing communities, with their sense that “everyone has already decided,” we will see success in the advancement of the Gospel only by the power of God’s Spirit.

A renewed commitment to prayer: We talk much of prayer in the PCA, but at least in our worship services and stated meetings, we devote little time to prayer. In Acts 6:4, the apostles recognized the necessity of commitment to prayer (mentioned first in the text) and to the ministry of the Word. We call upon the church to a renewed commitment to directed prayer beginning with our pastors, ruling elders and other key leaders that the Lord will build His church and that the PCA will grow substantially in reaching people with the Gospel.

A renewed commitment to unity in the church: We have our debates on issues of biblical direction, confessional standards and procedures, many of which are good and necessary. But when the votes are taken, we call upon the church to be subject to one another in the Lord, moving ahead together in a united effort to advance the Gospel throughout North America – for the sake of presenting a united testimony to the watching world and for the effective use of the abundant resources with which the Lord has blessed us. Whatever may divide us internally, we are called to a united effort in the Great Commission.

A renewed commitment to reaching the many cultures, ethnic/racial groups and socio-economic groups in North America: The Gospel is for the nations. North America, as it always has been, is increasingly a land in which the nations seek refuge and a new beginning. Our calling is to reach these people with the Gospel – in all the variety of their needs, with all the challenges with which that calling presents us.

A renewed commitment to mercy ministries – preaching the Gospel in Word and showing the mercies of Christ in deed: While we live in a land of great affluence, we also live in a land with great socio-economic, emotional, educational and medical needs. The abundance of resources with which the Lord has blessed us represents a great opportunity to show the mercy of our Lord to those in need – and even to bring real solutions to the needs experienced by many.

<a id="ga29-p529"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=531 printed_page=529 -->

A renewed commitment to the kind of stewardship of the Lord's resources that will give greater priority to the advancement of the Gospel: The PCA is a denomination greatly blessed by the Lord with an abundance of time, talent and treasure. For the church to advance, a greater stewardship of these resources is essential.

- Current Partnership Share giving to Mission to North America and the other committees and agencies of the PCA is sufficient for maintenance only. If the Gospel is to be advanced, members and churches of the PCA must devote greater resources to provide the necessary leadership and training for those who will lead in planting new churches and in the revitalization of our existing congregations. For an increase in giving of around $150,000 per year, GA/MNA could put in place the staff resources that could potentially double the number of church planters placed annually.

- Project support for church planters, for Church Vitality, for Chaplain Ministries and for Urban and Mercy Ministries (Thanksgiving Offering) must see major increases if the church is to advance.

A greater commitment to functional teamwork: With great joy, at GA/MNA we have witnessed a great increase in commitment to church planting and revitalization at the "grassroots" level over the past several years, among our churches and presbyteries. Without any question, PCA commitment to church planting and revitalization is at an all-time high. But the field is white for harvest and the laborers are few (Matthew 9:37). We are gratified that the team effort is growing, and we call upon the church to take steps for even greater commitment.

## How shall we respond to these challenges?

Specifically, we propose the following:

1. That the General Assembly pause now for a 15 minute season of prayer for the advancement of the Gospel in North America – praying for the “renewed commitments” included above.

2. That the General Assembly call upon our presbyteries to set aside a significant portion of their meetings to pray for the advancement of Gospel through the revitalization of our existing churches and through the planting of new churches – throughout North America and throughout the world.

3. That the General Assembly encourage each congregation and presbytery of the General Assembly to seek with a greater zeal the Lord’s calling for them in the advancement of the Gospel. I Corinthians 12:7 and Ephesians 4:7 promise that the Spirit of God has given each a gift for the common good and the building of the church. Just as there is a calling to every local church in reaching the local community and the world with the Gospel, so there is a calling for every congregation in church planting – reaching our “Judea and Samaria,” as well as our “Jerusalem” and the “uttermost parts.”

<a id="ga29-p530"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=532 printed_page=530 -->

This is a bold calling. It is bold because the needs are great. GA/MNA is grateful for the zeal for the Gospel that has marked the PCA from her inception. We are committed to the Scriptures, the reformed faith and the Great Commission. But let us call upon the Lord for a greater commitment to the Gospel and the renewal of the church. If these grow markedly, then North America will be reached for Christ – and North America will continue as a major sending church for missions throughout the nations.

The Lord will build His Church – and His promise to advance His Kingdom indeed will be renewed and fulfilled in each generation. The question before us is, will the PCA continue to be a part of that blessing? May we as a church be able to say with the Apostle Paul.

I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith. Now there is in store for me the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous Judge, will award to me on that day--and not only to me, but also to all who have longed for His appearing.

2 Timothy 4:7-8

## II. MINISTRIES

## A. Church Planting

GA/MNA Church Planting’s purpose is to serve the churches and presbyteries of the PCA in selecting, funding, placing, coaching and equipping church planters. It is with a very strong sense of call by God that Mission to North America labors for the expansion of the Lord’s Church - Advancing the Gospel through Renewing the Church. We count it a privilege to labor in this commitment and call, given to us by the General Assembly, and pray that the entire PCA will become a Church Multiplication Movement.

While we rejoice that the PCA is among the fastest growing denominations in North America, listed below are some of the sobering realities of the spiritual condition of the United States and Canada that the PCA is faced with as we consider our mission:

- The percentage of adults in the United States who attend church (across denominational lines) is decreasing.
- The church in the United States is not growing enough to keep pace with the population.¹
- Today a smaller percentage of the population go to church than in 1995², and almost 10% fewer than in 1991.³

¹ Eileen Lindner, editor, Yearbook of American &amp; Canadian Churches 2000 (Nashville, TN: Abingdon Press, 2000, 10-12.

² Population estimates from the U.S. Census Bureau at www.census.gov/population/estimates/nation/intfile2-1.txt, then presented in “Lost in America” by Tom Clegg &amp; Warren Bird

<a id="ga29-p531"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=533 printed_page=531 -->

- To make church attendance statistics worse... other research indicates that only about half as many people actually go to church as say they do.⁴
- Roughly half of all churches in America did not add one new person through conversion growth last year.⁵
- The unchurched population in the United States is the largest mission field in the English-speaking world and the fifth largest globally.⁶
- Here on American soil, every other religion is gaining converts, while Christianity is losing them.⁷
- The number of atheists have grown from 2,000 in 1900 to 1.4 million today, and the non-religious have grown from 1 million in 1900 to 26 million today.⁸
- As a group, statistics show that Atheists are less likely to become divorced than are Christians.⁹
- Between 1989 and 1998, the Muslim population in the United States grew by 25 percent; to an estimated 4 to 6 million representing what some call “the most serious threat to the church in America.” Islam is growing so fast in the United States that it is estimated that Islam has superseded Judaism as the second largest religious group in America.¹⁰
- In Canada, the state of the Christian Church is even worse than the United States with church participation being down sharply since the late 1940’s yet while receptivity to spirituality is extensive.¹¹
- Religion a’ la carte is rampant. External authority is out; personal authority is in.

The reality of the spiritually declining condition of the broader church in the United States and Canada is staggering. These realities also present the PCA with a great

3 Church attendance statistics from a Barna Research Group press release, “The State of the Church, 2000” (March 21,2000), www.barna.org, then presented in “Lost in America” by Tom Clegg &amp; Warren Bird.

4 The Empty Church: The suicide of Liberal Christianity (New York, NY: The Free Press, 1996), 62-63. And C. Kirk Hadaway, Penny Long Marler, and Mark Chaves, “What the Polls Don’t Show: A Closer Look at U.S. Church Attendance,” American Sociological Review (vol. 58, no. 6, December 1993), 741-752, and presented in Lost In America, by Tom Clegg &amp; Warren Bird, Group Publishing, Inc., 2001, Loveland, Colorado.

5 Charles Arn, “A Response to Dr. Rainer,” Journal of the American Society for Church Growth (vol. 6, 1995), 74. Warren Bird reviewed the 1999 yearbooks from a number of denominations to confirm the ongoing validity of this claim.

6 Tom Clegg &amp; Warren Bird, “Lost In America, How You and Your Church Can Impact the World Nest Door” (Loveland, CO: Group Publishing, Inc., 2001), 30.

7 Kent R. Hunter, Move Your Church to Action (Nashville, TN: Abingdon Press, 2000), 12.

8 Justin D. Long, “North America: Decline and Fall of World Religions, 1900-2025,” from the Global Evangelization Movement’s Monday Morning Reality Check (no. 5 1998) at www.gem-werc.org.

9 Barna Research Group press release, “Christians Are More Likely to Experience Divorce Than Non-Christians” (December 21, 1999), www.barna.org.

10 Wendy Murray Zoba, “Islam, USA,” Christianity Today (April 3, 2000), 40.

11 Reginald Bibby, “The Ten Key Findings,” in Transforming Our Nation: Empowering the Canadian Church for a Greater Harvest, edited by Murray Moerman (Richmond, BC. Outreach Canada Ministries, 1998), 269-332 (Dr. Reginald Bibby is a researcher in the sociology of religion at the University of Lethbridge, in Alberta, Canada.)

<a id="ga29-p532"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=534 printed_page=532 -->

opportunity to respond with effort and resources committed to the missionary work of
the church in North America.

for the PCA to truly become a church multiplication movement, the following eight key
result areas are essential. GA/M NA focuses its resources on serving PCA churches and
presbyteries in these activities.

• Concerted Prayer
• Recruiting Church Planters
• Assessing Potential Church Planters
• Training Church Planters and Key Church Leaders
• Assisting Church Planters in Fund Raising
• Placing Church Planters on the Field
• Coaching Church Planters in the Process
• building a denominational team of church planting leadership

Concerted Prayer: prayer is the foundation of all christian endeavor. It is through
prayer that God works to advance His Kingdom. It is significant that our Lord’s
command is to pray that He will send the laborers. We call upon the General Assembly
to join in focused, concerted prayer for the advancement of the kingdom through
church planting. only if the spirit of god is at work through our efforts will our efforts
be effective in advancing the Kingdom.

By God's power, lives are transformed by the Gospel as we wait upon Him in prayer.
GA/M NA comm unication pieces. Multiply and the web site (pcanet.org), give a high
priority to prayer by suggesting weekly prayer requests on a regional basis for the
advancement of the church. The Intercessor for all regions may be accessed on the web
site, offering greater opportunity for intercession for ministry across North America.

Recruiting Church Planters: In 2000, a record number of assessed and qualified
church planters were presented to the GA/M NA Committee for approval. The
GA/M NA staff is giving high priority to recruiting during 2001 in order to increase men
available for assessment and approval for church planting. as churches increase their
Partnership Share/Askings giving, more resources will be devoted to recruiting church
planters.

Assessing Potential Church Planters: GA/M NA’s Church Planting A ssessm ent is
highly effective and now developed as one of the finest assessments in America. In
2001 GA/M na will seek to expand the number of assessment events that a reproduced
to accommodate a higher level of recruitment. the primary focus of these assessments
is the evaluation for competencies that are particular to church planting. Among these
competencies are character qualities such as integrity, spiritual vitality, prayer,
humility, and godliness in family leadership; ministry competencies such as evangelism ,
leadership, preaching, and leadership development; personal competencies such as

<a id="ga29-p533"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=535 printed_page=533 -->

flexibility, emotional stability, sensitivity to people, dynamism, likeability, and the
church planter and his family’s calling to this task.

Placing Church Planters on the Field: the placement process begins with a church
planter committing to a set of biblical co revalues which god has put on his heart.
these values form the foundation of a vision that will shape the church. After the vision
is solidified and documented, the location that will best facilitate this vision is then
determined by a sense of the Lord's call to the church planter, and mutual agreement
with the presbytery, in consultation with the GA/MNA church planting staff GA/M NA
staff then offer assistance to the presbytery and the church planter in the many practical
steps and administrative details that are necessary to begin the work.

Men are being assessed, qualified, and placed in many different dem ographic situations.
Some in places where there are core groups who wish to have a new church, others
where there is no core group but still great need. Also men are being place in many
different demographic profiles. Some areas are large cities, others are smaller
communities, both with a sensitivity to recognize where there is need and where God is
at work.

Training Church Planters: GA/M na offers five different types of church planter
training, each addressing different needs. A ll PCA church planters, whether or not they
participated in the GA/MNA assessment process, and regardless of their funding
sources or sources of oversight, are invited to take advantage of these training
resources.

1.T he national church planter training conference — In 2000 this
conference was held A ugust 7-10, and in 2001 will be held A ugust 13-16 in the
Atlanta area for all PCA church planters, their wives, leaders in church planting
churches, presbytery GA/M NA chairmen and committee members, and church
planting network leadership. This training is “curriculum driven” which means
that the various seminars and workshops are designed to respond to the different
phases of the life of a church plant (start-up phase, ministry building phase,
organization phase, and the reproduction phase).
2. regional training conferences — The curriculum is complementary to the
national training. However, by taking advantage of the smaller numbers than
attend the national conference, and by addressing issues unique to each region,
these events provide a more specific curriculum, a more personal level of
interaction, and more individualized coaching.
3. boot camp — ga /M na ’s church planter boot camps are workshop oriented,
practical, two-day training experiences, which are now offered directly
following each church planting assessment. These boot camps are designed to
provide the working environment and coaching to assist the church planter in the
development of working plans such as establishing core values, formulating
vision, philosophy of ministry, fundraising, developing learning contracts, and

<a id="ga29-p534"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=536 printed_page=534 -->

church planting strategy for the new church start. This training focuses in greater detail on the initial stages of the church plant.

4. Coaching Retreats — These retreats are designed with the individualized needs of the church planter in mind. The group is small, and the ratio of church planters to coaches as small as possible (2 to 1). The coaches are experienced church planters who are skilled in the process of assisting the church planter in working through the unique needs of his particular church planting experience.

5. Church Planting Apprenticeships — The GA/MNA Church Planting Apprenticeship is designed for men whom GA/MNA recognizes are quality church planting candidates but lack the kind of experience that will equip the man to plant a church. The apprenticeship focuses on the development of the church planter in the specific areas of concern found by the assessment and offers church planting candidates the opportunity to develop with a mentor in preparation for starting a new church, often as a project sponsored by the mentoring church. In 2000 GA/MNA invested $154,500 in 12 men through the Timothy Fund to assist such apprenticeships.

Coaching Church Planters in the Process: In addition to the coaching retreats mentioned above, GA/MNA offers the church planter direct consultation by staff, and assistance in establishing mentoring relationships for each church planter. Regional coordinators, movement leaders, Presbytery MNA Chairmen, and church planting network leaders also play a major role in the coaching process, or in identifying others who may assist the church planter.

Building a Denominational Team of Church Planting Leadership: As the vision for church planting continues to grow among particular churches, presbyteries, and among church planting networks (which include a wide variety of formal and informal structures), GA/MNA fulfills a growing role of serving, coordinating and taking the initiative to develop the resources necessary for the vision for an advancing church to be fulfilled. GA/MNA brings this leadership through several avenues:

Regional Coordinators: GA/MNA Regional Coordinators serve churches that want to reproduce themselves with daughter churches, and presbyteries that are proactive in multiplying churches. They assist launch teams (core groups) and presbytery MNA chairmen in recruiting church planters. Regional Coordinators assist in the assessment process as assessors. They assist the church planters in the development of their strategic plan to move to the field, help them develop learning contracts, assist church planters with fundraising, and provide valuable counsel regarding choosing the location for the church. They provide valuable coaching to the church planters through the process of starting the church, and serve the presbytery MNA committees in their geographic areas by helping to identify areas where new churches are needed when there is no launch team. All regional coordinators serve on a limited part-time or volunteer basis.

<a id="ga29-p535"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=537 printed_page=535 -->

*Presbytery MNA Chairmen:* GA/MNA works closely with many presbytery MNA chairmen as they provide church planting leadership in their presbyteries. On November 6-7, 2000, GA/MNA hosted what will become an annual *National Conference for Presbytery MNA Chairmen*. The 2001 Conference will be held in Atlanta on October 29-31. The goal of this conference is to offer seminars and workshops to presbytery MNA committee chairmen and members, seeking to equip them as a primary force in church multiplication in their presbyteries.

*Church Planting Networks:* GA/MNA is active in the process of encouraging the development of church planting networks. A church planting network is simply a group of churches that combine time, talent and treasure to plant churches more effectively together than they could do separately. Some are highly structured and defined, while others are more informal. GA/MNA encourages all such efforts that enhance the ability of particular churches and presbyteries to fulfill their calling to build church planting movements.
