B. multicultural ministries

state of the task

During 2000, we continued to witness a growing interest and commitment in the PCA
to communicate the Gospel among ethnic groups that are rapidly growing in North
America. Several states are already at least 51% non-white in composition, or are very
close to it. Around the year 2050 the growth of ethnic population groups will be such
that over 50% of the total population of the united states will be non-white. Some time
before the year of 2004, there will be more Hispanics in the United States than there are
African Americans. The PCA, historically motivated and recognized for its Matthew 28
Great Commission zeal and efforts, now has an unprecedented cross-cultural
evangelistic opportunity and challenge right at its own front door. These growing
groups are our “Sam aritan” mission field (Acts 1:8-9).

The growing interest in multicultural ministries and church planting is observed in part
in the following ways:
• Church and presbytery leaders communicate frequently with GA/M NA
personnel seeking help in evangelizing ethnic groups as part of the changing
demographics surrounding their churches;
• an increasing number of second-generation leaders from various ethnic
backgrounds are entering reformed seminaries and non-traditional presbytery
sponsored leadership training programs;
• Presbyteries are designing and implementing non-traditional ways to train and
credential Teaching Elders (in accordance with PCA standards) from within the
ethnic groups in which churches need to be planted.
• Articles in Multiply and Intercessor have highlighted multicultural church
planting and have generated much interest and response.
• There is an increasing interest by some presbyteries, churches, and church
planters to start churches that are intentional in reaching the ethnic populations
in their geographical areas.

<a id="ga29-p538"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=540 printed_page=538 -->

- A few PCA churches, instead of selling their property and moving, have made an intentional decision to change in order to continue to reach out to the changing cultural and demographic changes around them.

The purpose of GA/MNA Multicultural Ministries is to encourage and initiate reformed church planting among ethnic people that results in Gospel movements that saturate these people groups.

The term *multicultural* is used by GA/MNA in two ways. (1) To describe the work of ethnic-specific church planting through which congregations are formed that are largely homogeneous and non-white; (2) To describe specific churches who include leaders and members from a variety of ethnic backgrounds, worshipping and serving God together.

## Advances in 2000

- Ethnic and multicultural church planters continue to be assessed, placed, and coached in church planting. Some of GA/MNA’s assessment centers have seen over 50% of the candidates be multicultural in focus.

- There are now around 20 African American teaching elders in the PCA and another five men in seminaries. TE Wy Plummer is MNA African American Movement Leader.

- We are actively preparing for the assessment of new ethnic church planters by trained leaders from within their own ethnic communities.

- There continues to be major Korean first-generation participation in the PCA with TE Henry Koh providing leadership as MNA Korean Ministries Coordinator. Many second-generation/English speaking Koreans are enrolled in reformed seminaries and are anxious to begin multicultural English speaking congregations around the PCA. The first ever Korean EM (English Ministry) conference was held in January of 2000.

- MNA Brazilian Movement Leader TE Osni Ferreira, now counts 13 Brazilian congregations who together have committed $30,000 towards a new PCA Brazilian church plant somewhere in the United States.

- The PCA now counts four Haitian churches and church plants in the United States with a fifth plant planned for 2001. Several Haitian leaders are in seminary. A PCA presbytery on the island of Haiti is being discussed and planned by MNA, MTW, and members of Southern Florida Presbytery. Dony St. Germain is MNA Haitian Movement Leader.

- The most significant advance in terms of addressing the need for more ethnic church planters is the focus on training ethnic leaders through the use of church planting

<a id="ga29-p539"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=541 printed_page=539 -->

apprenticeship programs, through which men who appear to have the gifts and skills for church planting work under the mentoring leadership of a church planting pastor, prior to their taking the lead in planting a church on their own.

## Korean Ministries

*Reaching “First Generation” Koreans* (those who were born in Korea, and for whom Korean is their primary language for worship, as well as for everyday discourse within their communities):

There are over 180 PCA Korean language churches which are members of seven Korean language presbyteries. TE Henry Koh, GA/MNA Korean Ministries Coordinator, serves these churches with a particular focus on encouraging their participation in the fellowship and financial support of the PCA, and in working with the Korean pastors in their commitment to the reformed faith.

The annual conference of the Coalition of Korean Churches in PCA met at Korean First Presbyterian Church, in Tucker, GA, from February 5-7, 2001 with the conference theme of “The Churches that are Advancing Ahead”. The purpose of the Coalition is to maintain Christian fellowship and close relationships among Korean churches in the PCA and to discuss and carry out ministry activities in cooperation with one another.

Pastor Dong Ho Kim of Dong-An Presbyterian Church, in Seoul, Korea was the main speaker of the event. MNA Coordinator TE James Bland also gave an encouraging greeting at the dinner meeting on the opening night. New president and officers of the Coalition were chosen during this annual event. The Korean Women in the Church (WIC) also conducted their own seminars, elected a new president and discussed their annual project. WIC has finished the translation and the publication of the book, *Leadership for Women in the Church*, by Susan Hunt. Nearly 2000 copies have been distributed to Korean churches in the PCA.

All of the brochures in the IAR are now translated into the Korean language. The Korean Ministries Coordinator, TE Henry Koh, is encouraging Korean churches to participate especially in the Tax Sheltered Annuity plan for their pastors. Many churches have responded positively but it is yet far from the goal.

*Reaching “Second Generation” Koreans* (those of Korean family background who were born in the United States, and whose primary language is English):

Organized by TE Henry Koh, the second MNA Korean English Ministry Pastors’ Conference met January 29-31, 2001, at Korean Central Presbyterian Church, Vienna, VA, in the Washington, DC area. The conference theme was *Equipping English Ministry (EM) Pastors to Reach the Next Generation*. About 80 participated. TE Randy Pope, senior pastor of Perimeter Church in Atlanta, gave most challenging messages as the keynote speaker of the conference.

<a id="ga29-p540"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=542 printed_page=540 -->

Through the conference, a number of potential second generation church planters were identified. The prayers of the General Assembly are requested as men train further for this crucial ministry, with its great challenge of reaching people with the Gospel, even as they experience great transition in their own lives and the life of their families and churches. A variety of approaches to church planting is emerging, as first and second generation church planters take up this challenge. As the first and second generations work together, the Gospel will be advanced through the planting of churches that are able to reach and retain second and succeeding generations of Koreans living in North America.

## C. Thanksgiving Ministries — Urban and Mercy

The Spirit of the Sovereign Lord is on me, because the Lord has anointed me to preach good news to the poor. He has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim freedom for the captives and release from darkness for the prisoners...
Isaiah 61:1

This keynote text for the GA/MNA report is especially applicable to the very important work of MNA Urban and Mercy Ministries. The PCA urban and mercy ministry movement is a growing movement. The Spirit is at work, convincing us of His call to show the mercies of our Lord, even as we preach the Gospel in some of the most challenging communities in North America. And the Lord is blessing, bringing response to the Gospel by the power of His Spirit, along with addressing personal and community needs at a significant level.

A major highlight in the growth of mercy ministries in the PCA was the jointly sponsored MNA/CE&amp;P conference entitled, "The Call of the Gospel: Sharing Christ, Showing Mercy," meeting March 2-3, 2001, in Atlanta. Well attended, this time of worship and training was well received by those wanting to learn from the experience of others how to develop mercy ministries through the local church.

At its March 2001 meeting, the GA/MNA Committee approved the establishing of an Urban and Mercy Ministries endowment fund, to be managed by the PCA Foundation. The purpose of the fund is to build a larger support base for urban and mercy ministries in the PCA, to support both leadership training and direct assistance to people in need.

## GA/MNA Urban and Mercy Ministries continues its ongoing efforts from the past:

- Building the network of communication and resources to encourage urban and mercy ministries, and making them visible to the church and the watching world.
- The annual Christian Community Development Association (CCDA) meeting is a gathering point for PCA-affiliated urban and mercy ministries. In 2000, at least 150

<a id="ga29-p541"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=543 printed_page=541 -->

from PCA-affiliated ministries attended the CCDA conference in New York. This
conference remains the best single place to draw from the expertise, experience and
biblical perspective of others engaged in such ministries. In 2001, CCDA will meet
in Dallas, November 28 - December 2.

• At each General Assembly, GA/M NA sponsors a dinner, at which time the work of
one or more urban and mercy ministries is presented. The dinner is open by
advance reservation to all who wish to attend.

• a directory of PCA-affiliated urban and mercy ministries is available from
GA/M NA on request. we invite the churches of the PCA to visit our web site.
Since the people and organizations listed in the directory welcome opportunities to
encourage and provide consultation in forming new ministries, this directory is a
valuable resource for any PCA church who is involved in urban and mercy
ministries, or who wishes to begin such ministries in their community. PCA
churches and presbyteries are encouraged to avail themselves of the great wealth of
experience that already exists in the PCA, as they seek to develop or increase
ministry in their own communities.

• Leadership development: The Thanksgiving Offering continues to be a resource for
encouraging the development of urban and mercy leadership in the PCA. Primarily,
the funds are used to sponsor internships for new staff members, seminary students
or college students working in urban and mercy ministries. by the end of february
2001, more than $57,000 was received in response to the 2000 Thanksgiving
Offering appeal.

beginning with t hell lh General Assembly in 1983, Bethany Christian Services has
been endorsed by the PCA, upon GA /M na ’s annual review and recommendation. We
note with gratitude the services of bethany to thousands of christian families
throughout its 56 years of service, and commend Bethany to the PCA for continued
involvement and support. their report is found as attachment A.

D. church vitality

the mission of GA/MNA Church Vitality is to encourage and equip PCA churches to
watch and pray for revival as we work and pray for more vital churches that advance the
Gospel.

Watching and Praving for Revival
The Kingdom Campaign, a strategy based on Acts 1:14-15 to build Kingdom
intercessors, enjoyed a healthy start in the year 2000. Local church training events were
held in nine cities and presbytery-level training was accomplished in four PCA
presbyteries. Plans for 2001 thus far include nine presbytery events and up to ten local
church events.

<a id="ga29-p542"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=544 printed_page=542 -->

Apprentice training for The Kingdom Campaign has begun, with eight men currently in
training. In addition, twelve more men have expressed interest in this ministry, and will
be scheduled for training as soon as possible.

key covenant fellowships a redesigned to help participants in their study of revival, as
they continue to watch and pray for it. A text containing discussion guides, called The
spirit of Revival, sold over 10,000 copies in 2000.

In October/Novem ber 2000, the third annual Convocation on Revival and Reformation
was held at Trinity Presbyterian Church, Jackson, MS. Over 75 pastors and ruling
elders studied the five pillars of Ministry. Rev. Harry Reeder filled in for James
Montgomery Boice who was to have been the plenary speaker. The 2001 Conference,
set for November 6-8 is on the theme of "Revival and reformation in the book of
Acts. " J. Eric Alexander will be the plenary speaker.

Working and Praying for More Vital Churches
The Embers to a Flame Conference at Briarwood Presbyterian Church in Birmingham,
AL, provided training, ideas and encouragement to a record number of pastors and
ruling elders, some from as far away as Australia. this conference is the main source of
revitalization teaching for churches on a statistical plateau or in decline. An exciting
development in this ministry was the translation of the materials into the korean
language. briar wood held a korean embers conference march 5-8, 2001.

Development work on The Barnabas Institute was put on hold while funding was
developed. Once secured, this assessment center for revitalization pastors could be
offered in summer 2001.

materials for days of prayer and fasting continue to be developed and made available
to presbyteries, churches and other groups who want to hold this type of event. They
are available free upon request.

Pastors-at-Large and Consulting Services are other ministries available to churches and
pastors, in order to provide counsel, advice, encouragement and further training in
church vitality.

E. campus ministries

Reformed University Ministries

reformed university ministries of mission to north america has a goal of building the
church now and for the future. This is accomplished by: “Reaching students for Christ
and equipping students to serve.” that phrases peaks eloquently of our commitment to
our covenant children and our obedience to the Great Commission. College years are a
time of great opportunity for reaching students with the Gospel. So many testify in later
life that their college years were the time when they seriously considered God’s truth.

<a id="ga29-p543"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=545 printed_page=543 -->

Reformed University Fellowship (RUF) offers the truth of God’s Word to students who are searching. By working within the context of the church, the Body of Christ, we follow His leadership as He builds His Kingdom on earth. Students are instructed in Evangelism and Missions, Growth in Grace, Fellowship and Service, and a Biblical World-and-Life View. We are strengthening the church by ministering to covenant children and by evangelizing the lost on campuses across this nation. An ordained PCA minister leads each RUF while actively working to accomplish goals in these four major areas.

Reformed University Fellowship International (RUFI) is reaching out to “all nations,” sharing the Gospel of Christ and training Christian internationals to serve Him all over the globe. The hundreds of students involved in RUFI are from at least 40 different countries. Most of these countries are restricted or closed, prohibiting missionaries to serve there. Many of the students return to their own countries to build the Church at home. Our three RUFI missionaries are privileged to introduce internationals to Christ. One of their goals is to help Christian internationals in their struggle to live for Christ in their own cultures.

Campus interns allow a much broader outreach on the campuses. In this learning and ministering position, young men and women, all recent college graduates, work directly with a campus minister, receiving on-the-job-training in evangelism, small group leadership, and one-to-one ministry. A planned program of biblical and theological training accompanies this practical service. After their internship with Reformed University Ministries, interns disperse into the mission field of life, carrying to their future vocations and churches their deepened understanding of God’s Word and experience in His service. Truly, campus interns are learning to be “an instrument for noble purposes, made holy, useful to the Master and prepared to do any good work” (II Timothy 2:21 NIV).

Weekly large group, small groups, and one-to-one staff/student meetings provide the structure for campus ministry. Each is essential in ministering to college students. The weekly large group meeting teaches the Word in truth by Bible exposition, examination, and discussion of Scripture. Small groups focus on study, prayer, and fellowship. One-to-one meetings between students and staff members offer in-depth discipling of Christians, evangelistic encounters and the building of trust-confidence relationships. Each of these three parts supports and strengthens the others. One of the most distinctive things about the ministry of RUF is its emphasis upon the development of a biblical world-and-life view. Only as we see through God’s perspective can we understand the truth of any situation. Only as students learn to think biblically will it make a real difference in the Church and the world.

Summer Conference 2000 provided solid Bible exposition for equipping students to better understand and live the Christian life; with teaching, training and equipping in skills related to reaching others for Christ; and fellowship and fun in beautiful

<a id="ga29-p544"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=546 printed_page=544 -->

surroundings among Christians from over 48 college and university campuses. It was held May 15-20, 2000 and was extremely well attended. Well over 500 students and 100 staff were able to leave the routine of books and schedules to reflect on life, deal with questions, and be taught from God's Word. The theme of the conference was Sanctification and the Reverend George Robertson from Covenant Presbyterian Church in St. Louis, Missouri expounded the scriptures. Students were eager to receive God's Word. Because of the love of God and the fellowship among other Christians, many new friendships were made. These friendships were used by God to bring students closer to Himself. The 2001 conference, which will address the topic The Sufficiency of Scripture, will be held May 14-19, 2001. Our speakers will be the Reverend Ray Cortese from Seven Rivers Presbyterian Church in Lecanto, Florida, and Dr. Richard Pratt, professor of Old Testament at Reformed Theological Seminary in Orlando, FL.

Conferences provide activities and a relaxing atmosphere as well as a concentrated time for biblical teaching that is new to many students. This teaching is done through elective workshops which the student may choose (two per day), small group study, prayer, and large group teaching each evening. One campus minister rejoiced that, "Many students are understanding the Gospel for the first time and many Christians are being encouraged and strengthened in their walk with God." In addition to Summer Conference, regional Fall, Winter, and Spring conferences are held throughout the year.

In 2000, there were many weeks of training for the field staff. This in-depth training is a distinctive of the ministry and provides philosophical, practical, and reflective instruction to RUF campus ministers, interns, and staff. A Foundations of Campus Ministry Conference was held in Atlanta, Georgia, during August for those interested in learning how Reformed University Ministries does its work, whether church staff, missionary or lay person. This year the conference will be held August 6-9, 2001 at Simpsonwood Conference Center in Atlanta, Georgia.

The year 2000 was a year of record growth as ten new campus ministries were started. The new ministries are: Anderson College, Emory University, Furman University, Millersville University, North Carolina State University, University of Arizona, University of Maryland-College Park, University of Nebraska, University of Virginia, and Yale University. Fill in campus workers were placed at New York University and the University of Southern Mississippi. This growth placed RUF on 56 campuses nationwide, with a hope of reaching 62 total campuses by 2001. Also, we have our largest group of interns yet with 41 in 2000 and 47 expected for 2001. This immense increase in ministry opportunity has greatly challenged our Atlanta staff. Restructuring and changes in personnel have occurred throughout 2000. The beginning of 2001 finds us in possibly our best position in history as far as human resources and the great evidence of God's material blessing to Reformed University Ministries. Reformed University Ministries continues to grow with as many as six new ministries scheduled for 2001.

<a id="ga29-p545"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=547 printed_page=545 -->

What is the reason for this growth and excitement? One campus minister told us, “God’s Word is alive and active. The Gospel is powerful. That is why RUF is growing . . . . RUF seeks to be faithful to God’s Word and to biblically minister to students.”

In June, 2000, the 28th General Assembly voted overwhelmingly in favor of making Reformed University Ministries a permanent committee of the Presbyterian Church in America. We anticipate the transition to full committee status to be finalized at the 29th General Assembly in June, 2001, with a majority of the presbyteries giving their approval. We are humbled by the confidence placed in us. Along with this trust comes a sense of added responsibility to proclaim the Gospel on college campuses across the country.

Only God could bring together students and ministers from so many different walks of life to accomplish His purposes. Each person that is touched by Reformed University Ministries will, in turn, touch many other people in the course of his or her life. One campus intern noted that, “A true measure of success for RUF . . . may only be seen in the light of eternity.” How true! And how glorious!

Refer to Attachment B for a listing of presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff, location of ministries and the 2002 budget for the entire ministry including affiliated committees for your information.

F. Chaplain Ministries

Just before His ascension, the Lord Jesus Christ commissioned his apostles to go into the entire world and proclaim the Good News. After the resurrection and ascension, the apostles zealously committed themselves to preach the Good News throughout the known world: Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth. The goal was to ensure that all people, Jew and Gentile, had the opportunity to hear the Gospel and respond to the saving grace of Jesus Christ. Quite often they came under persecution and were banished from living in certain areas of the empire; however, regardless of the cost, they continued on with the mission of permeating the known world with the Gospel of grace. Since the age of the apostles, pastors, evangelists, teachers, missionaries and church members have been commissioned to continue the mission of proclaiming the Gospel to all people throughout the world. Today, among the people who are to be reached with the Good News are those who serve in our government organizations, our military, our hospitals, our prisons and Veteran Affairs.

Although the religious climate has dramatically changed in the post-modern era, still we continue to experience God’s blessing upon the ministry of our PCA chaplains and to enjoy the privilege of sending our chaplains into our government institutions. There is still an open door to teach and minister in accordance with the doctrinal teachings of the PCA.

<a id="ga29-p546"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=548 printed_page=546 -->

In an article entitled, *Missions Today*, Bassam Madany writes: “The pulpit may no longer assume that the average western man or woman accepts the uniqueness of the Christian message. We must give biblical reasons for our belief in the uniqueness and finality of the Christian faith.”

This seems especially true within the society in which our chaplains are called to minister. By the grace of God, more than 70 military active duty chaplains – which represents a significant increase from year 1999 – 50 military reserve or National Guard, 12 Veteran Affairs (full-time and part-time) and approximately 35 prison, hospital, retirement center, Civil Air Patrol and hospice chaplains are communicating the Gospel in their unique settings and giving “biblical reasons for belief in the uniqueness and finality of the Christian faith.” In many cases they are communicating the Gospel, pastoring Christians and discipling people who otherwise would not have the opportunity to hear the Word of God, worship in a formal setting or participate in spiritual growth opportunities.

*The Primary Mission of GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries* remains constant. On behalf of the PCA, the Chaplain Ministries Coordinator is to recruit, send, encourage and oversee chaplains who will be used as God’s instruments in faithfully proclaiming the hope of the Gospel to personnel (and their family members) working in our government institutions, hospitals, retirement centers, prisons and various other organizations employing chaplains.

*One of the major tasks for the Coordinator is to recruit additional chaplains for all government institutions (military, veteran affairs, prisons). During the year 2000, 12 additional chaplains were recruited and are now serving at their respective locations. Although specific numbers have not been given, the Presbyterian Church in America has been asked to send more.*

*The Congregational Sponsorship Program* greatly assists in meeting one of the primary needs of our chaplains: prayer support. In most cases, military active duty, veteran affairs and prison chaplains do not have the privilege of being immediately involved with a PCA congregation. Consequently, prayer support is limited. Therefore, we continue to enlist sponsoring congregations who will commit to pray for a specific chaplain. Approximately 50% of our chaplains are now sponsored by one or more PCA congregations. The chaplain, in turn, commits to sending a quarterly update on his ministry with specific prayer requests. Congregations that may be interested in supporting this ministry in such a manner may contact the Chaplain Ministries Coordinator.

*Keeping in touch with our congregations:* The Chaplain Ministries Coordinator and the associate coordinators would enjoy the privilege of informing PCA Congregations of the challenges and joys of Chaplain Ministry. This could be done during a Missions Conference or other special functions sponsored by the Church. Also, the Chaplain Ministries Video is available. There is a fifteen-minute and a five-minute version. Please contact Mission to North America (MNA) if you would like a copy.

<a id="ga29-p547"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=549 printed_page=547 -->

The Guardian Prayer Calendar continues to be published quarterly. In addition to specific prayer requests, chaplains provide a short overview of their ministry. Approximately 3000 PCA churches and individuals are on the mailing list. In addition to providing specific chaplain prayer requests, the publication increases the knowledge and awareness of chaplains and their ministry challenges and opportunities.

Financial Support: The annual budget for Chaplain Ministries is $150,000. Expenditures include salary, travel, printing, office support and administration. We are very thankful to God that year 2000 for Chaplain Ministries ended in the black. During year 2001 we are praying for an additional 50 congregations who will include Chaplain Ministries in their annual budget.

GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries Volunteers: The Congregational Sponsorship program of Chaplains has become increasingly organized because of the volunteer service of Climie Hewitson, a retired missionary and now member of Good Shepherd Presbyterian Church, Minneapolis, MN. The sponsorship program not only results in prayer for individual chaplains, it also increases the sense of connectionism between the chaplain and his sponsoring church. Associate Coordinators, CAPT. Stanley J. Beach, USN, RET. and Chaplain Beryl T. Hubbard, COL, RET, USAF provide an immeasurable contribution to this ministry. Their pastoral care of chaplains and their family members, communication with members of our denomination, and assistance with recruiting has reaped significant results and blessing.

The Lord is at work: GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries enjoyed abundant blessing during 2000. The PCA enjoys the privilege of sending a significant number of very gifted, highly motivated and spiritually growing chaplains. Several hundred people have come to a saving knowledge of Jesus Christ as a result of God using them as His instruments. Hundreds of others have been discipled and pastored. The impact chaplains have within their units, hospitals, retirement centers and prisons cannot be measured, but surely it is very significant. In addition to serving on numerous installations in the United States, our chaplains are serving throughout the world, in such places as Kosovo, Bosnia, Germany, Italy, Japan, Korea and Okinawa. Others are serving on ships that are deployed.

One of the great needs in the post-modern era is to faithfully communicate a Christian worldview to the members of the government institutions in which they serve. In almost all cases our chaplains do this well. We thank God for them and for the privilege of working with them and in this ministry.

Refer to Attachment C for the Chaplain Roster.

<a id="ga29-p548"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=550 printed_page=548 -->

G. ga /M na stewardship

As we trust the Lord to advance the Gospel through Renewing His Church, the Lord
calls us to comm it our time, talent and treasure to that advancement. GA/M NA seeks to
exercise a careful stewardship of all the resources the PCA entrusts to her, and at the
same time to encourage the churches and members of the PCA to invest their resources
in building the Kingdom. GA/M NA ministries offer the opportunity for the Lord's
people to use their resources in this way. From the Apostle Paul, “I thank my God
every time I remember you. in all my prayers for all of you, I always pray with joy
because of your partnership in the gospel from the first day until now.
as churches and individuals give of their time, talent and treasure, they enter
into a partnership which provides the base for sending the Lord's servants into the
harvest field.

comm u nications:
• multiply offers quarterly communication of what the lord is doing through
various ministries of the PCA that advance the Gospel. Weekly content for
intercessory prayer with a regional focus is included in each issue.
• The GA/MNA web site (www.pcanet.org/M NA/) is in the process of being
substantially upgraded, and will be built over time into a major resource for all
kinds of information and referral sources for ministries related to building the
church. The intercessor content for all regions is included on the web site.

partnerships hare/A sk ings giving:
In 2000, church giving to GA/M NA increased 6% over 1999. While GA/M NA
expresses gratitude to God for this increase, it must be recognized that such increases
provide for a “m aintenance” level of ministry at be stand do not equip us to advance the
Gospel in greater measure. GA/M NA encourages the churches of the PCA to make this
giving to all committees and agencies a high priority. The Lord is at work through the
PCA in advancing His Kingdom. In order for the PCA to grow stronger over the years,
the member churches must be committed to denominational support, just as a particular
church can be strong financially only if her members give faithfully. GA/M NA is
seeking to build endowment funds, in order to provide greater resources for annual
operations and program support.

church planting project Funding:
• All church planters are supported by gifts designated for their particular
projects. GA/M NA invites churches and individuals to consider joining as
partners with a church planter, enlisting him as a missionary sent and
supported by their church. There are no administrative transfer fees taken
from this project support. every dollar given to a designated ministry or
project that is related to GA/M NA is used directly and fully for that ministry
or project.
• Ethnic or multicultural church planters and movement leaders require a
special priority for project support. Often the churches they lead in planting

<a id="ga29-p549"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=551 printed_page=549 -->

take longer to reach self-support, yet these men often lack the strong networks of family and friends in the PCA who will commit to their support.

- GA/MNA seeks to establish endowment funds for the support of multicultural church planting, for annual operations support, and for urban and mercy ministries, in order to increase the number of church planters and others who can respond to the call to minister to those in need.

- The Timothy Fund for Church Planting Apprentices is a special fund to supplement the support of men who have been graduated from seminary, but who need further training before they begin to lead a church plant. Thus far, gifts totaling $462,550 have been contributed to this fund, substantially assisting many apprentices.

**Church Vitality and Chaplain Ministries:** These two ministries are supported almost entirely by designated gifts, since Partnership Share/Askings giving is insufficient to sustain them. We are grateful to the Lord that both ministries received full support at their current operating level in 2000. However, these ministries fulfill a vital role, and the potential for expansion of ministry is great, as additional resources are provided.

**Estate Design and Endowment Services:** GA/MNA utilizes and recommends the services of the PCA Foundation in assisting individuals in their stewardship. In addition to the regular PCA Foundation programs, GA/MNA Advise and Consult Funds are also available, managed by the PCA Foundation.

**Five Million Fund for Church Buildings:** Providing interest-free loans of up to $80,000, this fund continues to be a helpful source for churches as they put together funding packages for their building programs. Though the funds are limited, they often make a difference in the church's ability to qualify for a loan, or they provide additional working cash that is very necessary. This fund is supported by the return payments of the churches to whom loans are made, as well as by donations.

## III. RECOMMENDATIONS

**Mission to North America recommends the following:**

1. That having reviewed the work of the GA/MNA Coordinator during 2000 according to the General Assembly guidelines, the GA/MNA Committee commends TE James C. Bland, III for his excellent leadership, with thanks to the Lord for the good results in GA/MNA ministry during 2000, and recommends his re-election as GA/MNA Coordinator for another year.

2. That upon it’s constituting Reformed Univeristy Ministries as a separate permanent committee, the General Assembly elect TE Rod S. Mays as Coordinator of Reformed University Ministries for the 2001/2002 term. Under the new permanent committee, RUM will establish a formal evaluation procedure of the Coordinator in time for the 2002/2003 re-election.

<a id="ga29-p550"></a>

4. That the General Assembly thank God for the **increasing church planting** that is occurring under local church and presbytery initiatives; thank God for the labor in the Gospel of all PCA church planters, multicultural church planters and missionaries, chaplains, Reformed University Ministries campus ministers, staff and interns, and Reformed University Fellowship International campus ministers, staff and interns. Further, that the General Assembly encourage faithful prayer that the Lord will send additional laborers through GA/MNA and Reformed University Ministries into the fields that are ripe for harvest in North America.

5. That **PCA churches** be reminded of their **covenant obligation** to give high priority to giving GA/MNA and all other PCA Committees and Agencies the Partnership Share/Askings, so that the Gospel is advanced in greater measure.

6. That the General Assembly express its gratitude to the Lord for a **Thanksgiving Offering** of more than $57,000, and commend to the churches of the PCA the opportunity to support Urban and Mercy Ministries, preferably through an offering taken during the Thanksgiving season; and further, that churches consider how they may directly participate in greater measure in Urban and Mercy Ministries.

7. That the General Assembly express thanks to God for the long and effective ministry of **Bethany Christian Services** in the area of pregnancy counseling and adoption, encourage continued support by churches and presbyteries, and invite the Bethany representative to speak to the Assembly for five minutes at this, or a more opportune time.

8. That the General Assembly commend to the churches these **resources and opportunities** for ministry available through GA/MNA:

- The opportunity to seek the Lord’s leading in church planting, campus ministry or the chaplaincy.
- The opportunity to participate in prayer and financial support (designated support in addition to Partnership Share/Asking giving) for:
- Church Planting among the many different regions, cultures and ethnic groups in North America;
- Church Planting regional coordinators and movement leaders;
- Church Planting apprenticeships (through the Timothy Fund);
- Campus Ministries;
- Church Vitality;
- Chaplain Ministries (especially through The Chaplain Guardian Corps).

<a id="ga29-p552"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=554 printed_page=552 -->

- The opportunity to build relationships of fellowship and ministry with Korean churches and other ethnic groups within the PCA, as well as with other true believers in our local communities.
- Utilize the resources of Church Vitality materials and consultation services.
- Recommend to church members that they subscribe (at no cost) to GA/MNA Multiply/Intercessor.

9. That Overture 5 from Heartland Presbytery requesting to be divided so that the State of Iowa would become a separate Presbytery of the PCA effective January 1, 2002, be answered in the affirmative.

10. That Overture 12 from Central Carolina Presbytery requesting to remove the eleven county area known as the “Piedmont Triad” of North Carolina from the Central Carolina Presbytery to form a new, Triad Presbytery, be answered in the affirmative.

11. That Overture 22 from Great Lakes Presbytery requesting to divide Great Lakes Presbytery and form Ohio Valley Presbytery, be answered in the affirmative.

12. That the General Assembly approve the 2002 budget of Reformed University Ministries, and note the budget including the affiliated campus ministries is provided to the General Assembly for its information; and further, that the Assembly note with thanksgiving the opportunities and challenges represented by the entire 2002 budget.

13. That the General Assembly re-elect TE Kennedy Smartt and TE Tom Cheeley to another term on the Presbyterian and Reformed Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRJC) as members of the Class of 2005.

14. That the General Assembly adopt the 2002 GA/MNA budget and commend it to the churches for their support.

15. That the General Assembly pause now for a 15 minute season of prayer for the advancement of the Gospel in North America – praying for the following “renewed commitments”:

- A renewed realization that the church is built by our Lord;
- A renewed commitment to prayer;
- A renewed commitment to unity in the church;
- A renewed commitment to reaching the many cultures, ethnic/racial groups and socio-economic groups in North America;
- A renewed commitment to mercy ministries – preaching the Gospel in Word and showing the mercies of Christ in deed;

<a id="ga29-p553"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=555 printed_page=553 -->

- A renewed commitment to the kind of stewardship of the Lord’s resources that will give greater priority to the advancement of the Gospel;
- A greater commitment to functional teamwork.

16. That the General Assembly call upon our presbyteries to set aside a significant portion of their meetings to pray for the advancement of Gospel through the revitalization of our existing churches and through the planting of new churches – throughout North America and throughout the world.

17. That the General Assembly encourage each congregation and presbytery of the General Assembly to seek the Lord’s calling for them in the advancement of the Gospel. I Corinthians 12:7 and Ephesians 4:7 promise that the Spirit of God has given each a gift for the common good and the building of the church. Just as there is a calling to every local church in reaching the local community and the world with the Gospel, so there is a calling for every congregation in church planting – reaching our “Judea and Samaria,” as well as our “Jerusalem” and the “uttermost parts.”

Respectfully Submitted,

*Committee on Mission to North America*

# SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA TO THE 29TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PCA

The Committee on Mission to North America submits the following recommendation as a Supplement to the Report of the Committee on Mission to North America to the 29th General Assembly:

## RECOMMENDATIONS:

13. That the General Assembly elect TE D. Charles (Chuck) Frost to the Presbyterian and Reformed Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRJC) as a member of the Class of 2005, replacing TE Tom Cheely, elected at the March 2001 GA/MNA Committee meeting. Further, that the MNA Report, Recommendation 13 be amended for this change. (Amended)

18. That Overture 26 from the New York State Presbytery be answered in the affirmative.

19. That Overture 29 From Northern California Presbytery be answered in the negative on the following grounds:

1. Regarding Items 1-4, the overture presents no reasons for requesting this review. There is no self-evident rationale for requiring this review. There are no allegations of impropriety or misuse of funds. Therefore, even the overture itself presents no reasons to proceed with an action that would require a significant expenditure of staff time.

2. Item 4 calls for an adjustment of 1990 - 1997 church planting budgets, based on a standard put in place in 1997. It is not common practice to apply a new standard to previous actions in matters such as this, and there is no rationale offered for doing so in this case.

3. This overture and its requested actions were not supported by Northern California Presbytery, the court to which they were originally presented.

4. Effective January 1, 2000, MNA discontinued the practice of assessing fees on project funds. Since that date, it has been the practice to apply 100% of every designated dollar to the project for which it is designated.

5. Regarding membership in ECFA, the MNA Committee believes that current accountability structures are sufficient to sustain the credibility of the ministry. Its annual audit by an independent accounting firm is reviewed by the MNA Committee and the General Assembly. All aspects of MNA's work are subject to the review of the General Assembly annually. MNA believes that any value gained from ECFA accreditation is more than offset by the time and expense required to maintain such accreditation.

<a id="ga29-p555"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=557 printed_page=555 -->

20. That Overture 32 from the Mid-America Presbytery be answered in the affirmative.

21. That Overture 34 from the Westminster Presbytery be answered to the affirmative.

22. That Overture 36 from the Central Carolina Presbytery be answered in the affirmative.

23. That Overture 37 from the Potomac Presbytery be answered in the affirmative.

24. That Overture 38 from the Westminster Presbytery be answered in the affirmative; and further,

That the 29th General Assembly declare that the New Presbytery described in the Overture 38 be constituted as follows:

1. Its geographical boundaries shall be the same as those of the current Westminster Presbytery.

2. The organizing meeting of the new Presbytery shall be held on the date of the January stated meeting of the current Westminster Presbytery, in the year of 2002. The Stated Clerk of the current Presbytery shall notify all churches in the current presbytery of the time and location of that meeting.

3. The name of the New Presbytery, election of the committees, standing rules and all other operational procedures of the New Presbytery shall be established by the presbytery at the January 2002 meeting, or at later more opportune times, as determined by the New Presbytery at its January 2002 meeting.

4. All those churches and teaching elders who are currently member of the Westminster Presbytery shall be enrolled as members of the New Presbytery, unless they have certified to the Stated Clerk of the current Westminster Presbytery that they wish to remain in Westminster Presbytery.

5. The current Stated Clerk of Westminster Presbytery, TE Steve Meyerhoff, shall convene the January 2002 meeting of the New Presbytery, and preside until the election of a Moderator.

25. That Communication 5 from the Westminster Presbytery be received by the 29th General Assembly as supporting information to Overture 38, and that, in order to honor the original conditions under which Westminster Presbytery joined the PCA, the communication be spread upon the minutes of the 29th General Assembly.

<a id="ga29-p556"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=558 printed_page=556 -->

ATTACHMENT A

Bethany Christian Services

Report to the 29th General Assembly

We are spending a significant amount of time at Bethany clarifying and planning our ministry vision for the future. In the course of our discussions, we have frequently reflected on the fact that the vision we are planning is not really ours; rather, we are implementing a vision that the Lord has already planned for us.

With that in mind, and however that final vision is articulated, it will inevitably concentrate on fulfilling the Lords’ mandate that Christians protect the Sanctity of Human Life and that we minister to orphans, children and families who are hurting.

You may have seen recent reports that the number of children lost to abortion is decreasing. It may in fact be the case that the actual number of abortions occurring in a given year has decreased slightly. However, one life lost is one too many. And, the million and one half that continue to lose their lives every year is stunning. Christians taking responsibility for proclaiming the pro-life message continues to be essential. And, Bethany remains on the front lines of providing compassionate counseling that urges young people to choose life-giving alternatives.

Often times a young person’s initial contact with Bethany comes through Bethany Lifeline, our toll-free telephone counseling service (staffed entirely by trained volunteers.) This past year, Lifeline received over 11,000 calls. Here are just a few examples of calls received:

A woman called immediately prior to leaving for the hospital to deliver her baby.

She was terrified because she knew she was not able to be a good mother to her child. One of Bethany’s workers immediately went to be with her. Very soon, thereafter, this birth mother chose adoptive planning for her child.

A young woman 8 months pregnant and homeless called Bethany Lifeline. One of Bethany’s offices found a home for her with one of our foster families. We continue to counsel with her about plans for her and her child.

A 22-year-old who is 3 months pregnant called. Her mother told the woman she must get out of the house as soon as her pregnancy started to show. She received Bethany’s telephone number from Planned Parenthood. She wonders if we can help her. Sure we can and we will!

A 19-year-old called. She is 8 weeks pregnant and just had an abortion several months ago. She is struggling with the guilt she feels over the abortion of her

<a id="ga29-p557"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=559 printed_page=557 -->

first child but is still considering aborting her current pregnancy. Our volunteer
guided her into counseling at Bethany. She has subsequently chosen life for her
child. In counseling sessions, we are also trying to help her see that the Lord
will forgive if she asks his forgiveness.

Isn 't it interesting to note how Jesus committed Himself to healing while He lived
among us? many of those to whom he ministered and their situations were not
pleasant. Likewise, many of the lives bethany touches are not very easy or pleasant. It
takes the forgiving compassion of christ to care about a young person who has had
multiple abortions only to find herself pregnant once again. It takes God’s grace and
patience to love someone who has put the life of her unborn child at risk by living ina
drug culture. It takes incredible love given only by God to care for a 14-year-old who
insists she is capable of being a mother all the while trying to help her see and
understand the beauty of adoption.

Our counselors daily ask for the Lord’s blessing as they deal with these situations. We
humbly ask for your continued prayers for those we serve and for those who serve.

But, Bethany’s ministry does not stop when the child is bom. To who does the gospel
of james refer when it challenges us to care for orphans? We could use a narrow
definition and look only to children without parents. And, yes, Bethany does find
homes for well over 1,400 such children from around the world each year.

But, how about children who are being physically abused or neglected by their parents?
how about those who spirits have been broken because of emotional abuse? Or, how
about children living with physical or emotional challenges; who live in temporary
family foster care or in institutions wondering if anyone will ever care enough about
them to adopt them?

Indeed, the challenge is not conquered when the gift of life is assured. Children whose
lives are broken and whose spirits and emotions are shattered are our continuing
challenge. So ... Bethany works together with fellow Christians to minister to these
children. In many instances our ultimate goal is finding them permanent Christian
families as well.

Bethany has often referred to our relationship with the PCA as a partnership. In reality,
though, it is so much more than a partnership. Together we are priests, implementing
our Lord’s mercy to those who need our care. In some ways it might be easier to use
public resources to accomplish that care. Or, it may even be easier to commission
Christian ministries like Bethany to answer the call. And, surely much has been gained
from developing these resources. In fact, if used very carefully, government funding
can frequently be used by christian ministries to help them reach out inc hrist’s name.
However, removing the opportunity of ministry from each individual christian moves
too far in that direction. By doing so, we rob our christian community of the privilege,

<a id="ga29-p558"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=560 printed_page=558 -->

the joy and of the responsibility of caring for the needs of people. Bethany increasingly sees our mission as that of helping each of you actuate your gifts of caring.

Your congregations and members of your congregations supported Bethany with gifts totaling $1,600,000 this past year. Thank you for that wonderful outpouring of love. We pray and we trust that your support will continue. You also serve on our Boards. We use your facilities. Your open your homes to children. And, most importantly, you pray. Thank you!

As we anticipate the year ahead, we know that many challenges and opportunities will present themselves. Some of the most significant challenges we face include:

1. Sharing the message with many more pregnant young people that adoption is a beautiful alternative:

Unfortunately, less than 1% of those who become pregnant choose the adoption option. And, tragically abortion is the most frequent choice. We need to continue our mission of changing the perception of adoption so that more will choose that plan when it is obviously best for them and their child.

2. Finding Christian homes for children with special life challenges:

Thousands of these children wait for a family. If a member or members of your congregation have love and patience to give such a child, please urge them to call the Bethany office closest to their home.

3. Finding adoptive families for the hundreds of thousands of children living in orphanages around the world:

These are children who cry out for a family to love them. Unfortunately, bureaucratic obstacles often stand in the way of Bethany doing so.

Yes, indeed, we are more than partners. We are fellow Christians. You are Bethany. We are you. We look forward to a year during which we, together, continue our work of extending the love of Christ to children and families in need.

<a id="ga29-p559"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=561 printed_page=559 -->

# ATTACHMENT B

# REFORMED UNIVERSITY MINISTRIES

The Committee on Mission to North America provides support services through Reformed University Ministries to presbyteries whose campus ministries are affiliated with Reformed University Ministries. The presbyteries receiving services make a contribution toward their cost. Presbyteries and their affiliated committees are completely responsible for the funding of ministries within their area and for determining the budget for such ministries. Reformed University Ministries receives and disburses funds only as directed by the presbyteries and their affiliated committees.

From January 1, 2000 to December 31, 2000, Reformed University Ministries received $3,827,773 and disbursed $3,605,727 as directed by presbyteries and their affiliated committees. The funds are received for particular ministries, which are the responsibility of a presbytery as noted below. The responsible body receives an audit report of its funds. The following list gives the presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff, and location of ministries receiving support services—accounting through Reformed University Ministries, and other ministries and staff affiliated with Reformed University Ministries.

THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO THE WORLD TO THE TWENTY-NINTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

The motto of the Presbyterian Church is “Faithful to the scriptures, true to the Reformed faith, and obedient to the Great Commission.” We believe it is this two-fold emphasis that has steered our denomination to the place we find ourselves today. It is, of course, a two-fold and not a three-fold emphasis because the scriptures and the Reformed faith are the same thing.

The truths of scripture and missions have been the two pillars on which the Presbyterian Church in America has been built. Consider these facts which have come from the Stated Clerk of our denomination. The PCA grew at an annual growth rate of 3.16% in 1999. A review of the yearbook of American and Canadian churches over the past ten years indicates that the PCA has frequently been the fastest growing denomination in North America.

In 1973, the year in which the PCA began, we had 260 churches all in the southeastern USA. Today we have 1,415 churches in 49 states and 6 provinces of Canada. We had 196 ministers; today we have 2,873 ministers. We had 40,614 members; in 1999 we had 299,055 members. Total giving to missions in 1973 was $961,835; in 1999 it had grown to $41,500,000.

The actual budget expenses for MTW have grown in the last seven years from $16,684,113 to $32,937,516. According to the Stated Clerk’s office, MTW is the largest missionary force of any Presbyterian denomination in history, over 800 missionaries (519 career, 337 two-year term or interns) in 47 countries.

We do not have to argue concerning the purpose of the church. Our Reformed tradition has affirmed for hundreds of years that the sole purpose of the church is to glorify God. The debate always centers around the means by which the church fulfills this purpose. You will find that churches that are growing and vibrant are most often churches that have claimed Acts 1:8 as their own commission from God: “But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on you; and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.”

In 1996, we instituted a new strategic plan entitled New Horizon. The missionary force of MTW concluded in the fall of 1999 that we needed to change some of the structures of MTW to better implement New Horizon. This past year we retooled our operations in three ways. First, we made changes in the way we conduct business in seven different areas:

<a id="ga29-p570"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=572 printed_page=570 -->

1. Financial customer care - expeditious resolution of missionary financial
problems
2. People sourcing - identification of missionary candidates
3. Expense reimbursement - expediting of missionary ministry expenses
4. Candidating - approval of missionary candidates
5. Impact Two-W eek - sending of two-week church teams
6. People development - development of missionaries
7. Marketing - communication of MTW ’s ministry to key stakeholders

Secondly, we are going to review all the policies we have developed over the last 28
years. this will be done by a committee of field personnel who will attempt to
eliminate some policies that have outlived their usefulness, and clarify and simplify the
rest.

Finally, we have reorganized field operations to include five international directors
instead of the present two whose job will be to:
1. Develop and pastor regional team
2. Develop and promote new initiatives
3. Insure performance accountability
4. Coordinate, allocate, and distribute resources
5. Develop strategic partnerships, both with local PCA churches and with
national churches

Mission to the World continues to be committed to church planting as the biblical
means by which God moves His kingdom work forward. is new horizon working?
Consider the results we have seen in the Philippines. The goal was to plant 25 churches
over a six-year period in Manila and the surrounding provinces. In fact, on February
15, 2000 the MTW church-planting team started their 50th church-planting project.
eight of those have not survived, but 42 of these church plants have national pastors and
each of those men has received training fromm tw -Philippines in one form or another.
We have extensive training activities, consisting of one-on-one mentoring, a large
group training, and the church-planting institute conducted by M TW every six months.
These national pastors have also been required to take 24 units of special study at the
Presbyterian Theological Seminary. the largest of these churches has approximately
300 in attendance, while the smallest ones are still church-planting works in their
evangelism and people-finding stages. of the 42, 28 are the original m other churches,
11 are daughter churches and 3 are granddaughter churches. The strategic plan for the
Philippines was clearly based on Acts 1:8. It began in the cities, moved to the
provinces, and ultimately to the world. We are delighted to tell you that the churchplanting work in the Philippines has sent its first missionary to Japan to work with our
church-planting efforts there. We truly have gone from Jerusalem to the uttermost parts
of the world.

we are preparing fora capital campaign fora ministry to street children in the
Philippines. The ministry will revolve around five churches which will be planted with

<a id="ga29-p571"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=573 printed_page=571 -->

this specific need as an integral part of their ministry. These five churches will, in turn, grow up around the nine feeding centers that we now have in operation. The goal is for each of these small church-based residential centers to be reaching 150 children at any one time. That is a total of 750 children. We probably will not get the capital campaign off the ground until late summer. Please be in prayer about this.

The street children work, one of our most exciting new ministries at Mission to the World, is a ministry reaching out to street children around the world. The first model for this work is moving forward in Lima, Peru. There we have missionaries and nationals working both with younger children and older teens. We also have works in Kenya and Romania. We are especially thankful for Opal Hardgrove and the way in which God has used her to open up these works. She is especially gifted by God through his Holy Spirit. We are also very thankful to Tom Stewart, a physician who has given up his practice to oversee this work. We don't know how big this ministry is going to grow or what places God is going to send us, but we truly believe there will be significant churches growing up around street children. We also believe that new dynamic leadership, both for the church of Jesus Christ and for the communities in which they live are going to come from these ministries. What the gospel does in the lives of these children who have suffered is going to be a great strength and motivation for the church at large.

We also started a medical ministry a year and a half ago for physicians, nurses and other medical professionals. In the year 2000 we sent out 239 medical personnel and pastors in this short-term ministry. This is a growing and exciting work. In November we held a conference in Atlanta for medical personnel, and over 420 attended that conference. If you would like a copy of the manual for medical ministries produced by our MTW medical directors, Drs. Ted and Sharon Kuhn, you can order one from our Atlanta office.

We have also reached out to needy people in emergency situations in such places as Honduras, Turkey, and Belize. Even as this report is being written, we are working in India with those whose lives have been devastated by the terrible earthquake. We have a team there now and are preparing to send others, both medical and construction teams. God is using these opportunities to open doors for us to bring the love of Christ to places where it might not otherwise be seen. It also gives the PCA great opportunity to reach out to people in need as our Lord Jesus Christ would do.

In 2000, there were 519 career missionaries, 143 two-year Impact missionaries and 194 intern applicants for a total mission force around the world of 856 missionaries. As well, there were 5,835 short-term summer missionaries. The breakdown of the career missionaries is as follows:

1. CHURCH PLANTING
MTW-Direct
