## 6. NATIONAL CHURCH PLANTERS 133

# ASIA/EUROPE HIGHLIGHTS

## ASIA

### Australia

The Australia University Ministry Team moved to two new levels this year. Their first Australian couple is being trained as national workers, and they took their first group of university students on a missions outreach to Thailand.

### Philippines

We began a street children’s ministry connected with the local churches. In addition, the church in the Philippines is sending their first missionary, a two-year, short-term missionary to Japan.

<a id="ga29-p573"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=575 printed_page=573 -->

Taiwan
This year Christ's college has the largest number of students ever enrolled. In addition,
they began building a new girls' dormitory.

thailand
the team leader an done other member of the team have arrived in country. The team
leader is involved in language study. other team members a rein various stages of
getting ready to head to the field.

Japan
We have had a plan to grow men for ministry and leadership from the university
ministry. This year, the first six young men have moved in that direction. one of them
is actually going into church planting. five of the men will return to university work
fora period of time, growing and maturing. This is exciting because university ministry
is where we expect leadership for the church in Japan to emerge.

central asia
Two major developments occurred in Central Asia. One, the team has gotten a firm
foundation in both language and culture and is beginning to spread out to reach other
Central Asian countries and Central Asians in Europe. The second significant
development is that a group from the national presbyterian church of mexico visited
the work in central asia with the idea of eventually sending missionaries from mexico
to work there. It is encouraging to see the people we are working with catch a vision
for reaching out to others, not only in their own country, but also around the world.

EUROPE
ukraine
The government gave us title to a 150-year-old church building in Odessa. Prior to the
Bolshevik Revolution, this building belonged to the Reformed church, and the present
fledgling denomination was deemed its rightful heir. We undertook a capital campaign
to rebuild that church, both because of the legitimacy it offers with the government and
the Ukrainian culture. We believe it will provide a home base for ministry throughout
Ukraine.

Bulgaria
The first Bulgarian trained to be a teaching elder graduated from seminary and began
the first church plant with Craig Sheppard, M TW team leader. They have laid a strong
foundation for the church with good publication materials, training, and Bible studies.

Germany
Will Traub's work with conservative evangelicals from the state church is helping to
produce a new "confessing" denomination. The first church is up and running with a
pastor. six more churches have asked to be apart of the group. Only a few actually
have pastors, though, so that is a subject of prayer. In addition, they have begun the
first Reformed seminary in the German speaking world in many years.

<a id="ga29-p574"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=576 printed_page=574 -->

Italy
We were well into the Vanguard capital campaign project when we realized that there
were other significant events and relationship problems that needed to be dealt with.
We have stopped the project at present, but are continuing to be involved in the country.

France
The Christian school connected with the church plant in Lyon, after getting off to a
rocky start, seems to be settling well into its second year. It has done exceptionally well
in providing good public relations for our team among the local people.

Spain
Besides the work going in Madrid, we have started a second work in the area around
Malaga. Tito Padilla has joined a Spaniard there who has been working at planting
Reformed churches.

Portugal
The team held its first church-planting seminar inviting missionaries and pastors from
around the country. More than 20 missionaries and pastors attended, strengthening and
encouraging one another, as well as learning more about church planting. In addition
they had the largest summer short-term group they’ve had in many years. Thirty-five
came from the United States as well as eight from South Africa.

Romania
Though we still have not found a team leader for Romania, IM PACT has begun
working there with a Korean missionary. The teams are working with street children
and working alongside Romanians who are being trained for various elder roles.

England
two welsh congregations have asked to be apart of the evangelical presbyterian
church of england and Wales. In addition, our second church planter was accepted.
We have also begun working among Central Asians in London.

Scotland
we have begun a partnership to helpmeet the needs of the evangelical conservative
churches that are trying to regain a foothold in Scotland. Right now, as in many
countries, church membership and attendance is falling off. However, there are several
churches that are moving contrary to this stream. We hope to provide assistance in
continuing to see other churches move against that flow.

<a id="ga29-p575"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=577 printed_page=575 -->

Sw eden
The team has entered the country now, getting ready for full-time service. They had a
strong summer outreach again and are no win the process of selecting the area in which
they will work.

africa /L a tin america highlights

africa /M i dd le east
Senegal
will become the provisional team leader of the senegal church-planting
team this year. Frank replaces who is spending more time with the MTW
Global Training and Development training efforts. Jeff will be focusing on
Francophone training which will keep him involved in Senegal, but also extend his
outreach into other areas in Africa and Europe. The work in Senegal continues to grow
with three current mission churches and a fourth effort ina relatively un reached part of
the city. We are actively seeking others with a call to join the team in order to expand
the work.

Cote d'Ivoire
Cote d'Ivoire has continued to suffer political upheaval throughout the year. Despite
these matters, the work has continued to progress - though we did see two key national
leaders and one mission congregation decide that the Lord was leading them to work
elsewhere. Our new church-planting effort has kept the number of developing churches
at three, one with its own national pastor and another one owning its own building. The
newest church plant is looking fora larger place to meet since the living room of the
Ivoirian who had the vision to start it can no longer handle the number coming.
is involved in a new ministry on the university campus which involves a number
of muslim students and converts from Islam.

tanzania
the team in dares salaam now consists only of but they are
actively recruiting people with a variety of skills who can contribute to their effort to
plant churches among the muslims of the city. They are also involved in leadership in
haven of peace Academy, a school started by several missions, which has a majority of
students who are not "missionary kids", including many Tanzanian children and
children from Muslim and Hindu families. They have also been able to help local
churches move away from a "works" approach to the Christian life.

Sen sitive
in one country our people have been involved in the production and airing of a video
drama on national television which portrayed the problem of guilt and need fora
solution. Viewers who respond in writing receive a scripture passage in calligraphy and
a regular newsletter. From those who continue to respond, believers will be able to
pursue opportunities to present the gospel.

<a id="ga29-p576"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=578 printed_page=576 -->

MTW has its first Arab context team that is not in cooperation with a more experienced agency in Muslim ministry. They have been encouraged with a growing group of new believers, men and women, young adults and more mature, who are meeting together regularly for worship and growth. They are part of a committee, including others from Presbyterian perspective, who are planning a presbytery and the training of leadership. Another team, in a more restricted Arab country, had a leader among their small group of potential elders and house church leaders leave the country due to the pressure of his family. Recently the remaining men have started meeting again, and their growth and commitment is encouraging.

MTW has personnel who are training national churches and leadership in facing ministry challenges. We have missionaries ministering to the Fulani of Nigeria, doing Bible translation, medical work, and using other key means to get the gospel where it is least heard. It is encouraging to watch the emerging local churches become lights in these dark places.

## Southern Africa

Southern Africa is a large and diverse region of the world with many needs and a rich history of church and mission. Until very recently our efforts have focused on the country of South Africa where we are working with national partners in university ministry, theological education and community health. However, new doors of opportunity are opening in Zimbabwe and Zambia as well. We need additional educators, church planting assistants, Muslim ministry workers and administrative support.

## AIDS Ministry

Mark Mollenkof, MTW missionary in South Africa, reports that he recently had the opportunity to meet with a pastor who is chairman of the governing board of the Missionvale School. The chairman wants Mark to begin an HIV/AIDS awareness program among students and speak to other church leaders in the community. In addition he will train member of the local congregations in counseling and caring for people with AIDS. This is an exciting breakthrough as Mark has tried for the last year to get into the churches in Missionvale to discuss how they can use HIV/AIDS education and counseling as a ministry tool.

## LATIN AMERICA/CARIBBEAN

### Chile

A leadership-training program has begun in Santiago, Chile, with missionaries and national leaders leading a core group in the context of planting daughter churches. The program provides practical training in church planting, mentoring, and pastoring. There are three full-time candidates, one in Santiago, one in Osorno, and one in Renaca. There is the possibility of having several more in the program soon. The entire training program is designed to prepare men to plant and pastor churches.

<a id="ga29-p577"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=579 printed_page=577 -->

## Mexico

Mission to the World/Mexico is privileged to work alongside the National Presbyterian Church of Mexico (NPC) as it mobilizes its denomination to do church planting both throughout the Republic of Mexico and to other countries. Presently we are working in partnership with over 39 church-planting projects in 19 cities in Mexico. Some leaders of the NPC recently returned from an investigative trip to Turkey as they begin to consider where they will send their first missionaries. To undergird this effort the NPC has formed Movipres, an administrative team to promote Presbyterian church-planting movements. MTW missionaries and PCA churches are working together with Movipres. Over 26 PCA churches have partnered with NPC local churches to support Mexican church planters. In March 2001 the first Church Planters Congress was held in Mexico City to unite and train all the church planters from throughout Mexico. Church-planting leadership institutes now functioning will begin extension programs in other parts of Mexico. The training of Mexican church-planting mentors is beginning to lend assistance to the church planters. The NPC believes the Lord for 20 new church-planting projects in 2001.

The year 2001 will be one of leadership transition in Mexico. Jayson and Maureen Kyle, after fourteen years of service in Mexico, will be leaving Mexico City and moving into a ministry to global cities. Maureen is training a missionary to take over her role as the MTW Financial Director for Mexico. Jayson has been serving as MTW's Director for Mexico. We would ask for your prayers as we work through the inevitable changes that come with leadership shifts. Also please pray for the Lord's continued direction and care of the NPC/MTW church-planting partnership and for the church planters and their families.

## Haiti

Charles Amicy, the national worker in Haiti has planted four churches, each with a trained, Reformed pastor. The fifth church plant should be started in the next few months. Charles is currently training twenty men from three of the four churches to become either elders or future pastors. He is hopeful that a denomination will be formed by the end of 2001. Medical teams from the United States have been doing outreaches about four times a year. A site and design for a medical clinic have been chosen, and a Haitian doctor has enthusiastically agreed to be a part of this when it is established.

## Hinterland

The Cusco Presbyterian Church of San Jeronimo met March 4, 2001 to organize as an official church of the Reformed Presbyterian Church of Peru Denomination. They have a Peruvian session of one teaching elder and two ruling elders as well as a communicant membership of approximately 40. They have 10 people presently in training as church leaders as well as two pastoral candidates in training. Perhaps the most interesting thing about this group is their involvement in about 10 church-planting efforts in essentially unreached towns and villages.

<a id="ga29-p578"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=580 printed_page=578 -->

## Belize

There are currently two career units and three two-year units ministering in Belize. There are also two units currently raising support to go to Belize. There were 16 interns in Belize who worked in a variety of ministries: medical, education, children’s ministry, youth ministry, and ministry support. In addition there were 630 one-and two-week people who worked on 12 different construction sites, conducted 16 Vacation Bible Schools, six youth outreaches, and three adult training seminars. A two-week team also held an Evangelism Explosion Clinic at one of the Belizean churches.

MTW has also been participating with the Belize Presbytery in providing theological education materials from Covenant Theological Seminary. The Belize City Church has completed two courses: Life and Teaching of Jesus and Man, Christ and Redemption. The church in San Narciso has complete one course, Biblical Theology.

## Honduras

In the spring of 2000, Honduras was approved as a new field for MTW. There are three units preparing to go to Honduras for at least two years, and they began cross-cultural training in March of 2001. The focus of ministry has been to the island of Guanaja. We have been doing relief work, evangelism and Bible studies. There were seven interns and 101 one- and two-week teams that went to Honduras in 2000.

## RECOMMENDATIONS

The Committee on Mission to the World makes the following recommendations to the Twenty-Ninth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America:

1. That the month of October 2001, be set aside as a month of prayer for the “10/40 Window” and world evangelization, and that God would send many more laborers into His harvest field.
2. That the General Assembly urge the churches to set aside a portion of their giving for the suffering peoples of the world; to that end be it recommended that a special offering for relief and mercy (Compassion Ministry) be taken during 2002 to be distributed by MTW.
3. That the General Assembly set aside Sunday, November 11, 2001, as a day of prayer and fasting for the persecuted church worldwide.
4. With gratitude to God for the life, ministry, and service of Paul D. Kooistra, the Committee on Mission to the World enthusiastically recommends that Dr. Kooistra be re-elected as Coordinator of Mission to the World.
5. That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the Administrative Committee, be approved.
6. That the minutes of the meeting of March 8-10, 2000, be accepted.
7. That the minutes of the meeting of November 1-3, 2000, be accepted.

Respectfully submitted,
/s/ L. B. Austin III, Chairman
Committee on Mission to the World

# MINUTES OF THE NOMINATING COMMITTEE OF THE TWENTY-NINTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

The General Assembly's Nominating Committee convened in Atlanta, Georgia, at the Holiday Inn Select Hotel on Saturday, March 24, 2001. Chairman RE David Moon called the meeting to order at 9 AM and TE Dan Barr opened with prayer.

The roll call was made by the Secretary. Thirty-six were present, including TE L. Roy Taylor, PCA Stated Clerk, as a visitor.

|  Ascension | RE William Mitchell (2003)  |
| --- | --- |
|  Calvary | TE Richard Thomas (2003)  |
|  Central Carolina | RE Harold Jones (2002)  |
|  Central Georgia | TE John C. Jagoditsch (2002)  |
|  Eastern Carolina | TE Ben Inman (2002)  |
|  Evangel | TE Frank Barker (2001)  |
|  Gulf Coast | TE Bruce Sinclair (2003)  |
|  Heritage | TE Jim Brown (2003)  |
|  James River | RE Clyde Vick (2003)  |
|  Korean Southeastern | TE Young Pal Cho (2001)  |
|  Louisiana | TE Paul Lipe (2001)  |
|  Metropolitan NY | RE Clayton Prugh (2002)  |
|  New Jersey | RE Dan Barr (2001)  |
|  New River | RE Harry Musser (2001)  |
|  North Florida | TE William Lamkin (2001)  |
|  North Georgia | RE John Rollo (2001)  |
|  North Texas | RE M. C. Culbertson (2001)  |
|  Pacific | TE J. Phillip Clark (2002)  |
|  Pacific Northwest | RE Don Robertson (2002)  |
|  Palmetto | TE P. Cameron Kirker (2003)  |
|  Philadelphia | TE Erwin Morrison (2001)  |
|  Pittsburgh | TE Harold Kelley (2001)  |
|  Potomac | TE Tom Osterhaus (2001)  |
|  Rocky Mountain | TE Steve Reese (2003)  |
|  South Texas | RE Claude Roberts (2002)  |
|  Southeast Alabama | TE Stephen Estock (2002)  |
|  Southeast Louisiana | RE Edwin M. Hackenberg (2001)  |
|  Southern Florida | TE R. Lynn Downing (2001)  |
|  Southern New England | TE Rick Downs (2002)  |
|  Southwest | RE David Moon (2001)  |
|  Southwest Florida | RE Henry Whitmore (2003)  |
|  Susquehanna Valley | RE W. Lee Troup (2003)  |
|  Warrior | RE James Albritton (2003)  |

<a id="ga29-p585"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=587 printed_page=585 -->

Western Carolina RE Ed Robeson (2002)
Westminster RE Joe Reynolds (2002)

Stated Clerk, Roy Taylor, informed the Committee that TE Frederic M au had resigned
from the Administration Committee, class of '04, and that TE Michael Milton had
replaced him.

Matters related to the nomination process for the PCA Foundation with respect to
proposed by-law changes to be considered at this year's General Assembly were
referred to a Nominating Committee sub-committee.

Chairman Moon read and commented on Acts 1:21-26. a season of prayer followed
which was begun by Roy Taylor and concluded by Mr. Moon.

Subcommittee assignments were made and the main meeting divided into the
subcommittees for deliberation. The sub-committees concluded their work and were
dismissed for lunch around noon.

The Nominating Committee, as a whole, reconvened from lunch at 1 PM and was led in
prayer by Chairman Moon. reports of the subcommittees we represented in order to
finalize the nominees to be presented to General Assembly.

The Committee elected TE Jack Jagoditsch, Central Georgia Presbytery, Chairman and
RE William Mitchell. Ascension, secretary of the 2002 GA Nominating Committee.

the next meeting of the nominating committee will beat General Assembly on
Wednesday, time and place to be announced.

Next year’s Spring meeting will be Saturday, March 23, 2002. The meeting was closed
in prayer by Chairman Moon at 3:15 p.m.

Resp sub

RE David Moon, Chrm
TE Jack Jagoditsch, Clerk

D. B iograp hical Sketches:
TE Timothy Diehl: Heartland. pastor of faith pc in Ackley, Iowa. Has served on the
Presbytery-level Admin. Co. and the Nominating Co. Has already served a 4
year term on the AC in the past.
RE Pat Hodge: Calvary. owner of hodge frozen food Center. Has served in the
military, is active in local church, and is involved in the local community. Has
serve dona number of presbytery level committees, including moderator of
Calvary Presbytery. Has served on Co. of commissioners for AC and MNA.

<a id="ga29-p587"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=589 printed_page=587 -->

RE William Joseph, Jr.: SE Alabama. served as moderator of presbytery twice,
moderator of the 7th GA, delegate to the first GA in 1973; was member of the
first GA level Admin. Co. and has served several times since. Operated an
Architect and Engineering business for 45 years and has served as Montgomery
County Commissioner for 35 years. Has served on the AC this past year as an
alternate.
TE William Joseph, III: Warrior. Campus minister since 1980. Has served on
numerous presbytery committees and as moderator of warrior Presbytery. Has
served on numerous committees of commissioners (twice on AC) and GA
Nominating Co. [He is son of bill Joseph, Jr., bio listed above.]

## C. Nominations:

Class of 2005

D. Marion Clark, No. Florida
Willard (Will) G. LaRose, Potomac
Steve Fox, SE Alabama

Alternates

Dave Matthews, Evangel
Ross Cook, No. Georgia

## D. Biographical Sketches:

TE D. Marion Clark: North Florida. Pastor of Faith PC in Gainesville, FL. Has served as chairman of Candidates and Internship Co. of Philadelphia Presbytery. Has served as Executive Minister of Tenth PC, Philadelphia, with responsibilities including reviewing and editing public materials and writing, developing curriculum for new members classes, teaching classes, and setting direction for the church’s ministry. Has also served 5 yrs as principal of a college-preparatory high school and has taught English literature.

RE Ross Cook: North Georgia. Ruling Elder at Covenant PC, Fayetteville, GA. Has served on Presbytery-level CE committees, served 10 yrs as Business Administrator for AC of the PCA, and has served on Covenant College Board of Trustees. Served in the military, graduated from U.S. Naval Academy; Has held VP positions at several businesses, currently retired.

RE Steve Fox: SE Alabama. President of printing and publishing company dealing with national ministries. Has served previously on GA CE Co. – 1 year as V-Chrm and 2 yrs as Chrm; served one term on MNA Co., currently serving on GCP Board. Served as Moderator on the Presbytery level, and, for 10 years, on Presbytery CE Co.

TE Willard (Will) G. LaRose: Potomac. Assoc. Pastor at Grace Evangelical PC in Edgewater, MD. Has served on both CE and Missions Co. on the Presbytery-level. Has served on staff for the GA CE Co. and continues to serve as regional youth trainer for the PCA. Has served in the military – Lt. Colonel, retired.

TE Dave Matthews: Evangel. Assoc. Pastor of Briarwood PC in Birmingham, AL. Has served 5 yrs as Director of Children’s Ministries at Briarwood, has served on CE Committees in several Presbyteries, member of Sunday School Convention Board in Dallas, TX, and in Charlotte, NC, and 10 years involved in youth work and 5 yrs of Children’s Ministry.

D. B iograp hical Sketches:
RE Harvey L. Anderson: North Georgia. owner of a construction contracting firm
before becoming the urban ministry director of perimeter ministries Int’l
(PMI). he has an active teaching ministry on the issues of mercy ministry in
churches all over Atlanta. Has felt the need to be more closely connected to
those he ministers to and has moved into one of the inner city communities he
was seeking to reach. Has served on the Presbytery-level MNA Committee.
TE Frank M. Barker, Jr.: Evangel. Pastor Emeritus at Briarwood PCA in Birmingham,
AL. Has served on Presbytery-level M NA Co. and has served previously on GA
MNA Co.

<a id="ga29-p590"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=592 printed_page=590 -->

TE Osni Ferreira: Metro NY. director of redeemer urban church planting Center,
Redeemer PCA in New York City. has served as senior pastor of a couple of
churches in Brazil as well as here in PCA churches in the US. In Brazil he
served in Presbyteries as Moderator and on different committees on all levels -
presbytery, synod and GA. he was vp of the board of lon dr ina hospital and
board of the evangelical college of Londrina; founder and president of
M eprovi, an Evangelical pro-life ministry.
RE E. J. Nusbaum: Rocky Mountain. insurance agent and member of villages even
PCA in Colorado Springs, CO. Has served in the military. Has served on the
MNA Co. of Presbytery, several committees of commissioners at ga and
served on the GAAC (host committee) for the 27th GA.
RE Michael A. Russell: Evangel. Has served 26 yrs in the printing business as
president and owner. Has been an RE in church plants in the Birmingham area
for over 22 years and has served on Presbytery MNA Committee for 2 terms and
on Evangel’s Examinations Co. Has served as alternate this past year on GA
M NA Committee.

## B. To be Elected:

## C. Nominations:

Class of 2005
Shelton P. Sanford, Fellowship
D. Clair Davis, Philadelphia
Edward J. Lang, Potomac
Whaley (Bill) S. Barton, Palmetto
Alternates
John Vanderveld, No. Texas

## D. Biographical Sketches:

TE Whaley (Bill) S. Barton: Palmetto. Pastor of St. Andrews PC in Irmo, SC. Has a love of missions, has made missions trips, and has served previously on the GA MNA Co. Has also served on Covenant College Board. On Presbytery-level has served as chairman of the Shepherding Co., Chairman of MNA Co., Chairman of Judicial Co. and as Moderator. Has served in local community as a police chaplain for crisis intervention. Has served in an advisory capacity with RTS and Columbia Int'l University, and has taught a couple of courses in the seminary division of Columbia Int'l U.

TE D. Clair Davis: Philadelphia. Seminary Professor in Church History at WTS and Associate Pastor of New Life PC in Fort Washington, PA. Has previously served on GA MTW Co. Has been active in speaking and teaching in Bonn and Hamburg, Germany; in Hungary and Transylvania, Romania; and in the Ukraine.

RE Edward J. Lang: Potomac. Work experience has been in administration and human resources, more recently began working part-time at Chesapeake Seminary where they have developed seminary training programs to assist missionaries in the development of seminaries in other countries. Has supported worldwide missions and missionaries for many years, has seen his children go on short term missions trips. During 1999, served as an advisory member of MTW.

TE Shelton P. Sanford: Fellowship. Senior pastor of Westminster PC in Rock Hill, SC. Has been active on the Presbytery-level for many years and currently is the Chairman of the Admin. Co., Member of Carolina Area Joint Committee on Campus Ministry (RUF). Has visited numerous mission fields around the world and attends 2 meetings a year of Christ's College Board in Taipei, Taiwan. He is deeply committed to world missions, prioritizes support for MTW missionaries, has hosted several training sessions for church leaders on planning missions conferences.

<a id="ga29-p592"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=594 printed_page=592 -->

RE John Vanderveld: North Texas. Self-employed providing advisory services in corporate strategic planning, development and finance with extensive international experience. Is a Ruling Elder at Park Cities PC in Dallas, TX. Was one of the early founders of Browning Ferris Industries, has served on advisory Board of Southwest Legal Foundation, on the Board of Directors amd executive committee of Living Bibles Int'l and Int'l Bible Society, Chairman of Board of Directors of Global Action. Has served on GA Co. of Commissioners for MTW.

## D. Biographical Sketches:

TE W. Wilson Benton: Missouri. Pastor of Kirk of the Hills PC in St. Louis, MO. Served on Presbytery-level RUM Co. and as Moderator; GA Moderator in 1992, GA MNA and on Transition Co. – RUM to Permanent Co.

<a id="ga29-p593"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=595 printed_page=593 -->

RE Bradford L. Bradley: North Texas. Executive Director of Southwest Church Planting Network and comes with a broad business background in the software industry serving health and life insurance companies. Current position as Exec. Director of SW Church Planting assists the 3 Presbyteries in the states of TX, NM, AZ and part of OK in planting new churches and providing initial funding for new RUF campus ministries. RE at Park Cities PC in Dallas, TX, and active in local church and presbytery. Has personally been involved in the establishment of new RUF campus ministries at Baylor, TCU, Rice and U. of AZ.

RE Elwood (Woody) Camp: Nashville. Real estate lawyer. RE at Christ PC in Nashville, TN. Has served on GA Nominating Co. His 3 children have all participated in ministry of RUF and seen first hand the impact of this ministry in the lives of college students. Has been involved in work of a number of RUF chapters.

RE Robert M. Danner, Jr.: Central Georgia. District Agent for Northwestern Mutual Financial Network and RE at First PC in Macon, GA, and serving on church MNA Co. Daughter has served as RUF Intern for 3 years.

TE William E. Dempsey: Missouri. Pastor at New Port PC in Washington, MO. Has served in various capacities with RUM from 1982-1997. Has served on Presbytery-level MNA Co. RUM Task Force and its study committees in MS Valley Presbytery; currently serving on Missouri Presbytery MNA Co., Missouri Co. on Campus Work, and GA MNA Sub-Committee on Campus Ministry.

TE Sam Downing: Rocky Mountain. Church Planter at City Church of Denver, CO. Has been involved with RUF at every level – as student, intern and campus minister and attended RUF Staff Training as both an intern and campus minister.

TE Lee Ferguson: Palmetto. Pastor of Covenant PC in Columbia, SC. Was one of the pioneering RUM campus ministers, serving U of SC from 1981-88, and is very familiar with staff training and intern program. On Presbytery-level has served on MNA, Candidates and Credentials Committee. On GA-level has served on SJC and Ad Interim Co. on Judicial Procedures.

TE Jonathan Inman: Western Carolina. Organizing pastor of Grace &amp; Peace PC in Asheville, NC. Serves on Candidates, Examinations &amp; Education Co. on Presbytery-level. Has served on Committees of Commissioners at GA. Has also served as evangelist and Director of Student Services for Harvest USA, developing ministry for junior high, high school and college students.

RE John M. Kimbrough, III: MS Valley. Farmer and Agricultural Consultant. RE at First PC in Lexington, MS. While in college helped to start Bible study groups and with others worked toward having a campus ministry move onto MS State U campus. Served on Presbytery-level Credentials Co. Has participated in short term missions trip and active on Missions Co. and serves as Chairman of Faith Promise Missions Co. at church.

<a id="ga29-p594"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=596 printed_page=594 -->

TE Robert Sehwanebaek: MS Valley. director of pastoral care and church relations
at Palmer Home for Children in Columbus, MS. Has served on MS Joint Co. on
Campus Work for over 15 yrs, served on RUM /M NA Task Force, and
RUM /M NA Study Co. been active in work of presbytery and served on several
committees of commissioners on GA-level.
TE Gary B. Spooner: SE Alabama. pastor of covenant pc in Auburn, AL. Has
served on alabama joint Co. on Campus Work, GA M NA Sub-committee on
Campus Ministry, Presbytery-level M NA Co.
TE William Sutherland: Potomac. pastor of wallace pc in Hyattsville, MD. Has been
serving on Special Ministries Co. of presbytery since 1996, overseeing campus
ministry a swell as urban and mercy concerns of the presbytery. Was involved
in a leadership role to bring about another campus group.
RE Frank C. Young, Jr.: SE Alabama. Retired ophthalmologist. RE at trinity pc in
Montgomery, AL. Has served on Presbytery-level Examination & Credentials
Co. and GA CCB. Has served as School Board Chairman, president of local
medical society and local hospital’s medical staff, retired vp of local healthcare
system.

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Joel Belz: W. Carolina. Magazine publisher (World Magazine) and member of Covenant Reformed PC in Asheville, NC. Has served more than 20 yrs on Covenant College Board, 12 years as Chairman. He attended Covenant College and all 4 of his children have attended. Has continued to recruit students and do fund raising for the college, whether on the board or not. He is totally committed to the philosophy of Christ-centered education as espoused by Covenant and is zealous to recruit faculty who are committed to that point of view.
RE Charles R. Cox: SW Florida. Retired business executive with a great deal of experience in large, complex organizations. Has previously served on this Board. Is a Covenant grad, as are his wife and 2 daughters. When not on the Board, served on the Co. of Commissioners for Covenant College.
RE John B. Harley, III: Philadelphia. Division Manager of Culligan Water Conditioning, RE at Lehigh Valley PC in Allentown, PA. Son of evangelical missionaries, grew up in Venezuela, and later returned to teach English as a second language at the Universidad de Oriente. An active member of Presbytery, including Moderator one year, 4 yrs on MNA Co., 12 yrs on Ordination Co., 8 yrs on Judicial Business Co., 5 yrs as Recording Clerk. Has served on a variety of GA level committees, including Nominating Co., Theological Examining Co., and Co. of Commissioners for the college and for IRC. All of his studies have been in Christian Schools. Has served the last 2 years on the Covenant College Board and feels he is just getting up to speed and reaching a comfort level with the work of the Board and wants to continue to serve in this capacity.

<a id="ga29-p596"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=598 printed_page=596 -->

RE G. Richard Hostetter: TN Valley. Attorney and RE at Lookout Mountain PC in Lookout Mt., TN. Currently serving on the Boards of Ligonier Ministries, Serve Int'l, The Foundation for Reformation (publisher of the New Geneva Study Bible), the Bible League, and Trustee on the Maclellan Foundation. Has served as Moderator at the 21st GA.

TE Michael Malone: Central Florida. Senior Pastor at St. Paul's PC in Winter Park, FL. Has served on numerous Presbytery-level committees, on a number of GA Committees of Commissioners and as a Trustee on the CTS Board. Has a daughter currently at Covenant as a student and 2 other daughters hoping to also attend.

TE Marvin Padgett: Nashville. Editorial VP of Crossway Books and attends Faith PC in Goodlettsville, TN. Has served on GA CE&amp;P Co., on GCP Board (including President), Coordinator of Campus Ministries, and previously on Covenant College Board. Has been active on Presbytery-level, including Moderator of Presbytery twice.

TE Rodney D. Stortz: Missouri. Senior Pastor of Twin Oaks PC in Ballwin, MO. Has served on CTS Board, Presbytery-level MNA Co. and is presently serving on the Trans World Radio Board. Currently completing first term on CC Board. Is deeply committed to the Reformed World and Life View that is the core of the philosophy of Covenant College.

D. Biographical Sketches:

RE Robert E. Hamby, Jr.: Calvary. Retired CPA/CFO and a RE at Mitchell Road PC in Greenville, SC. Is serving as a volunteer consultant to non-profit organizations, principally faith-based Christian organizations, providing advice in the areas of administration and finance. Has served on CTS Board, been an executive committee member, chairman of finance committee and a consultant for special administrative and finance projects. Currently serving on the board of directors of Focus on the Family, Young Life of Greenville, and other community organizations.

RE John H. Kramer: Missouri. Chairman and CEO of Cambridge Engineering, Inc., a manufacturer of nationally marketed industrial space heating systems and RE at Chesterfield PC in Chesterfield, MO. Completing first term on CTS Board, presently on the Development Committee where his experience and background in business have been beneficial. Is teaching leader and area advisor for Bible Study Fellowship, president of the board of Westminster Christian Academy of St. Louis.

RE Lanny W. Moore: SW Florida. President of Suncoast Contractors' Supply, a family owned-and-operated lumber and building supply company and RE at Evangelical PC of Cape Coral, FL. Has served as director on the Board of God's World Publications, and on the Board of Precious Life Ministries; was elected to the Lee County Public School Board and instrumental in getting Bible instruction into the school system. Has previously served on the CTS Board or been an advisor Board continually since 1972.

<a id="ga29-p598"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=600 printed_page=598 -->

RE Rudolph E. Schmidt: TN Valley. Retired. Is a RE at New City Fellowship in Chattanooga, TN. Has served as an administrator of both Covenant College and Seminary when the 2 schools shared an identity and then continued with the college when it moved to Lookout Mountain. Has served on CTS Board for a number of years and currently serves on the seminary's board of advisors. Has served on several Presbytery-level committees and is Moderator-elect of TN Valley Presbytery for 2001.

TE H. Andrew Silman: Grace. Senior pastor at First PC in Hattiesburg, MS. Is completing a term on the CTS Board and eligible for re-election. Has served on the Development Co. of the Board and the CTS rep. on the GA AC. Has been involved in church planting and church revitalization. Has served on GA MNA Co. and their rep on the GA AC.

TE William Spink, Jr.: Covenant. Pastor of Riveroaks Reformed PC in Germantown, TN. Has served previously on the CTS Board, has served on the Student Affairs Co. which maintains contact with student leaders and brings the concerns of the students to the Board, also works with the Student Services Office which handles student placement after graduation. He maintains a keen interest in CTS, the importance of training the ministers who serve our denomination, and the commitment to biblical authority and doctrinal purity.

## D. Biographical Sketches:

**TE** David Anderegg: Gulf Coast. Pastor of First PC in Panama City, FL. Has an AB in History and MBA with major emphasis in Finance. Non-ministry experience includes serving as adjunct professor at Belhaven College, President-Certified Appraiser/Consultant, Financial Planner, Assistant Acquisitions Officer and Financial Analyst with Commodore Realty Investments.

**RE** Earle E. Morris, Jr.: Palmetto. Chairman of the Board of Carolina Investors, Inc. and RE at Rose Hill PC in Columbia, SC. Retired after 48 years of public service in SC including, 4 yrs in SC House of Representatives, 16 yrs in SC Senate, 4 yrs as Lieutenant Governor of SC, and 20 yrs as Comptroller General of SC. Retired Brigadier General of SC Army National Guard. Has previously served 2 terms on IAR Board.

**RE** J. Allen Wright: North Georgia. Investment Consultant Financial Advisor and Senior VP at Paine Webber; was also previously Account Executive with Kidder, Peabody &amp; Co. Has worked primarily with Retirement Plans, Non-Profits, Financial Institutions and Family Offices, Investment Manager select and performance measurement. Is a RE at Perimeter Church in Duluth, GA. Has previously served as Chairman of the Board and Chairman of the Investment Co. for PCA IAR.

## D. Biographical Sketches:

RE John Albritton, Jr.: SE Alabama. Retired Attorney; RE at Trinity PC in Montgomery, AL. Has served previously on PCA Foundation Board, has gifts and experience in establishing financial trusts and estate plans; organizational management involvement for a number of firms and organizations; attorney and banking officer; strong abilities to advise in financial and legal concerns. Has also served on GA Nominating Co.

RE William F. Farrell: North Texas. Life Insurance Agent – Chartered Financial Consultant with expertise in the field of Estate Planning. Has served in the military. Is an RE at Park Cities PC in Dallas, TX. Has been active in the community, serving on the Board of the Dallas Housing Authority and Founder/Chairman of the Board of “I Have A Dream” Foundation. Has previously served as an Advisory Member of the PCA Foundation Board.

RE Charles W. Francis, Jr.: North Georgia. Commercial Real Estate. RE at ChristChurch PC in Atlanta, GA. Experienced in managing negotiations, personnel, budgets and marketing plans; has working knowledge of investment concepts as it relates to office development investments.

TE Steven D. Froehlich: New York State. Pastor of New Life PC in Ithaca, NY. Has served on a variety of Presbytery-level committees and is on the Board for Chesterton House: A Centre for Christian Studies at Cornell U.

RE Ronald A. Kohlin: Gulf Coast. Retired businessman. RE at First PC in Niceville, FL, and active in local church. Served on Co. of Commissioners for PCA Foundation in Tampa. Has served in the military.

RE George D. Sinclair: Philadelphia. President and owner of Keystone Appraisal Co. in Philadelphia, PA, and member of Proclamation PC in Bryn Mawr, PA. Has served on Board of Trustees of Westminster Seminary and secretary for over 30 years, working with the Finance Co. and staff on WTS’s investment program, setting investment goals and acting as an adviser on all of the WTS investments. At Keystone, deals with investments and investment planning, providing financial counseling to clients in the field of real estate, buying and selling of large industrial and commercial facilities. Has served as a RE in OPC and CRC previously.

D. Biographical Sketches:
TE Michael D. Bolus: Fellowship. Pastor of Hopewell PC in Rock Hill, SC. Founding father of the PCA, has served on numerous Presbytery-level committees, and been active on GA-level, including serving on SJC. Has assisted the GA in examining candidates who presented themselves to go abroad as a part of our joint venture in the Co-Mission, especially to the Ukraine.
TE Stephen Clark: Potomac. Pastor of Wallace PC in Hyattsville, MD. Has been a lecturer in Historical Theology at RTS and Chesapeake Seminary, and Practical Theology at WTS and Jamaica Theological Seminary. Has previously served on the GA Theological Examining Co. and brings a pastor's heart as well as background in historical theology.

<a id="ga29-p604"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=606 printed_page=604 -->

RE Royce C. Seifert: Philadelphia. Teacher and businessman. Has taught at Lehigh
Valley Christian High School, served as vp and general manager of federation
of dairy Cooperatives, senior accountant with Peat, Marwick, Mitchell & Co.
(CPA firm), served as consultant to Cum berland Valley Milk Producers, done
income tax preparation since 1975, served on Board and as Treasurer at
Bethlehem Christian School and at Lehigh Valley Christian High School. Has
served as Presbytery Moderator and Treasurer and on several Presbytery-level
committees; has served on GA Co. of commissioners for CE&P.
RE Robert T. Thetford: SE Alabama. Retired FBI agent and Attorney, primarily in the
mediation field, and has taught criminal justice at a local university. RE at
Trinity PC in Montgomery, AL, and active in local church.

## D. Biographical Sketches:

TE William S. Barker: Philadelphia. Professor Emeritus of Church History, having taught many years at WTS. Has served as Moderator of GA (1994), on 25th Anniversary Co., Creation Study Co., and on the Board for Covenant College. Has contacts with most of the NAPARC denominations from working at the Presbyterian Journal and at WTS; has represented the PCA at NAE.

TE R. Irfon Hughes: Ascension. Pastor of Hillcrest PC in Volant, PA. Served as pastor previously in Wales so has a wide experience in church life both in the UK and in the US and has seen first hand many of the relevant issues and needs for good church relations. Has previously served on GA IRC.

RE John L. Marshall: Philadelphia. RE at Calvary PC in Willow Grove, PA and Director of Development for the mid-Atlantic region and director of alumni relations for WTS. Serves as church bulletin editor for GCP, editing old and writing new devotional pieces that appear on the back of church bulletins produced by GCP.

RE Jack D. Merry: New York State. Retired engineer. Has been a member of CRC, RPCNA, OPC as well as PCA churches over many years. Has served on GA level Nominating Co. and previously on PCA IRC, serving as alternate this past year.

B. to be Elected:
class of 2005
Three TE Primaries AND Three RE Primaries

C. N om inations:
class of 2005
Dominic A. Aquila, So. Florida Bruce Gunther, New Jersey
Robert Ferguson, So. Texas M. Dale Peacock, Louisiana
Robert D. Stuart, Rocky Mountain (to be filled by Floor Nomination)

D. biographical Sketches:
TE Dominic A. Aquila: So. Florida. Currently Editor for PCANEW S.COM . Has
previously served on the SJC and been an officer for SJC. He knows the Book of Church Order and understands the polity and history of the PCA.
TE Robert Ferguson: So. Texas. pastor of spring cypress pc in Spring, TX. Has
served as director of churches vitalized and school serve and a sate in the
reformed churches of Australia. Has been active on Presbytery-level. Has
served GA as Parliamentarian, on CCB, and various committees of
Commissioners. Has previously served on SJC.
RE Bruce Gunther: New Jersey. Attorney specializing in wills and estates, real estate
and arbitration/mediation; is a court appointed arbitrator in all of the southern
counties in New Jersey, arbitrating approximately 500 civil cases a year. Has
been active in local church and on Presbytery-level.
RE Dale Peacock: Louisiana. Attorney with extensive experience both in civil and
church courts. Has served 2 terms on SJC before, including during the time the
original Manual for the SJC was drafted. Has served on numerous committees
and commissions on both the Presbytery and General Assembly level.
TE Robert D. Stuart: Rocky Mountain. pastor of christ covenant church in colorado
Springs, CO. On Presbytery-level, has served on MNA Co., as Moderator,
Credentials, Officers and Churches and CE. On GA-level, has served on Ad
Hoc Co. on Divorce & Remarriage, and previously on SJC.

minutes of the nominating committee
■TH
of the 2 9 'H General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America

The General Assembly’s Nominating Committee convened in Dallas, Texas, at the
Adams Mark Hotel on June 20, 2001. chairman RE david moon called the meeting to
order and led in prayer. The roll was taken by the Secretary and the Committee
members with their Presbytery as follows:

<a id="ga29-p608"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=610 printed_page=608 -->

Ascension RE william mitchell
Central Carolina RE Harold Jones
Central Georgia TE John C. Jagoditsch
Covenant RE Jim Alinder
Evangel TE Frank Barker
Great Lakes RE William Billings
Gulf Coast TE Bruce Sinclair
James River RE Clyde Vick
Louisiana TE Paul Lipe
Nashville TE J. Arch Warren
New Jersey TE Dan Barr
North Florida TE William Lamkin
North Georgia RE John Rollo
North Texas RE M. C. Culbertson
Philadelphia TE Erwin Morrison
Potomac TE Tom Osterhaus
South Texas RE Claude Roberts
Southeast Alabama TE Stephen Estock
Southeast Louisiana RE Ed Hackenberg
Southern Florida TE Lynn Downing
Southern New England TE Rick Downs
Southwest RE David Moon
Southwest Florida RE Henry W hitmore
Susquehanna Valley RE Lee Troup
Warrior RE James Albritton

the purpose of the meeting was to consider eligibility of nominees received from the
floor. All floor nominees were found to be eligible. The following is the committees
report:

committee on mission to north america
for class of 2005 Alternate

nominating committee nominee floor nominee
TE Osni Ferreira, Metro New York TE Philip Douglass, Missouri

TE Osni Ferreira: Metro NY. director of redeemer urban church planting Center,
Redeemer PCA in New York City. has served as senior pastor of churches in
Brazil as well as PCA churches in the US. In Brazil he served in Presbyteries as
Moderator and on different committees on all levels. he was vp of the board of
lon dr ina hospital and board of the evangelical college of Londrina; founder and
president of Meprovi, an evangelical pro-life ministry.

<a id="ga29-p609"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=611 printed_page=609 -->

TE Philip Douglas: Missouri. Professor at Covenant Theological Seminary. Church
planter for 5 PCA churches. Has taught Church Planting, Growth, and Renewal at
the Seminary for 15 yrs. He would provide vast experience and vision to the
ministries of MNA.

committee on reformed university ministries
class of 2002

nominating committee nominees floor nominees
Vacant RE John Marshall, Central Georgia
Vacant RE James M. (Jim) Sutton, Sr., C.Carolina
RE J. David Woodard, Calvary
RE Philip W. “Tom ” Clark, Fellowship

RE John Marshall: C. Georgia. elder at trinity pc in Statesboro, GA since 1987.
member of the C. georgia MNA subcommittee on campus ministries since 1989,
and has served as Chairman since 1993. The RUM Transition Committee requests
that he be elected to serve on this Committee. owner o fm arshall’s Farm & Garden
Center.
RE James M. (Jim) Sutton, Sr.: C. Carolina. Elder at Christ Covenant in Matthews, NC.
owner of sutton insurance Services. has served on various committees of
Commissioners. His son was involved in starting RUF at NC State.
RE J. David Woodard: Calvary. Elder at Clem son PC in Clemson, SC. Faculty Advisor
for RUF at Clem son University since 1984. Occupation: J. Strom Thurmond Chair
of government in the department of political science at clemson University. The
RUM Transition Committee requests that he be elected to serve on this Committee.
RE Philip W. “Tom” Clark: Fellowship. Retired from sales thereby giving him time to
serve. He has served on the GA Nominating Committee, CE/P, and the PCA
Foundation. Has served as Moderator for both Calvary and Fellowship Presbyteries,
and treasurer of fellowship Presbytery. RE clark has been a faithful servant of the
Lord Jesus Christ through the local church, Presbytery, and General Assembly.

Alternate

Vacant RE David Brown, Great Lakes

RE David Brown: Great Lakes. Elder at Christ Covenant Reform ed Church, Columbus,
OH since 1995. member of great lakes MNA subcommittee on campus
Ministries, having also served as Chairman. The RUM Transition Committee
requests that he be elected to serve on this Committee. Occupation, U.S. Postal
Service.

<a id="ga29-p610"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=612 printed_page=610 -->

# BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF COVENANT COLLEGE
Class of 2005

## Nominating Committee Nominees
TE Michael Malone, Central Florida
RE John B. Harley, III, Philadelphia

## Floor Nominees
TE Jim Garretson, Pacific
RE Dr. Charles Carraher, Southern Florida

TE Michael Malone: Central Florida. Senior Pastor at St. Paul’s PC in Winter Park, FL. Has served on numerous Presbytery-level committees, on a number of GA Committees of Commissioners and as a Trustee on the CTS Board. Has a daughter currently at Covenant as a student and 2 other daughters also hoping to attend.

TE Jim Garretson: Pacific. 1981 graduate of Covenant College. Serves as a board member for a large Christian school in CA and the Covenant College Board of Trustees. As a Trustee, he has served on the Executive Committee and as the Chairman of the Institutional Effectiveness Committee. Possesses a strong background in Reformed educational philosophy.

RE John B. Harley, III: Philadelphia. Division Manager of Culligan Water Conditioning, RE at Lehigh Valley PC in Allentown, PA. An active member of Presbytery, including Moderator one year, 4 yrs. on MNA Co., 12 yrs. on Ordination Co., 8 yrs on Judicial Business Co., 5 yrs. as Recording Clerk. Has served on a variety of GA level committees. Has served the last 2 years on the Covenant College Board.

RE Charles Carraher: S. Florida. Professor of Chemistry and Biochemistry at Florida Atlantic University. He has served in numerous academic and administrative positions. He served two terms as a Board member for Covenant College beginning in 1989. He has authored/edited numerous books and academic articles. He is listed in numerous Who’s Who collections, including Who’s Who in America.

# Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity, and Relief Funds
Class of 2005

## Nominating Committee Nominee
TE David Anderegg, Gulf Coast
RE Earle E. Morris, Jr., Palmetto

## Floor Nominee
TE David H. Jussely, Grace
RE John Mardirosian, New Jersey

TE David Anderegg: Gulf Coast. Pastor of First PC in Panama City, FL. Has an AB in History and MBA with major emphasis in Finance. Non-ministry experience includes serving as adjunct professor at Belhaven College, President-Certified Appraiser/Consultant for Anderegg Appraisals, Financial Planner, Assistant Acquisitions Officer and Financial Analyst with Commodore Realty Investments.

TE David H. Jussely: Grace. Former pastor in MS, currently professor and Dean of Students at Reformed Theological Seminary in Jackson, MS. Has served as Moderator of Grace Presbytery three times as well as two terms on the Board of Insurance, Annuities and Relief. He is currently the Chairman of the Presbytery’s Advisory Committee and Treasurer of the Benevolence Committee.

<a id="ga29-p611"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=613 printed_page=611 -->

RE Earle E. Morris, Jr.: Palmetto. Chairman of the Board of Carolina Investors, Inc. and RE at Rose Hill PC in Columbia, SC. Retired after 48 years of public service in SC including, 4 yrs. in SC House of Representatives, 16 yrs. in SC Senate, 4 yrs. as Lt. Governor of SC, and 20 yrs. as Comptroller General of SC. Retired Brigadier General of SC Army National Guard. Has previously served 2 terms on IAR Board.

RE John Mardirosian: New Jersey. He retired from a career in the life insurance industry, ending his career as an executive with Penn Mutual Insurance. Has been an elder since 1974 and Moderator of NJ Presbytery. He served on the Board of Insurance, Annuities and Relief from 1991-1999.

## Assembly Theological Examining Committee
Class of 2004

### Nominating Committee Nominee
RE Royce C. Seifert, Philadelphia

### Floor Nominee
RE Howell Burkhalter, Central Carolina

RE Royce C. Seifert: Philadelphia. Teacher and businessman. Has taught at Lehigh Valley Christian High School, served as VP and General Manager of Federation of Dairy Cooperatives, senior accountant with Peat, Marwick, Mitchell &amp; Co. (CPA firm), served as consultant to Cumberland Valley Milk Producers. Has served as Presbytery Moderator and Treasurer and on several Presbytery-level committees; has served on GA Co. of Commissioners for CE &amp; P.

RE Howell Burkhalter: C. Carolina. Elder for 11 yrs. at Redeemer PCA in Winston-Salem, NC, where he serves in an executive pastoral role. Former Moderator of C. Carolina Presbytery and Moderator-elect for the proposed Piedmont Triad Presbytery. He has an M.A. in Biblical Studies from RTS-Charlotte and possesses a deep passion for biblical and theological studies. Licensed by C. Carolina Presbytery.

## Committee on Constitutional Business
Class of 2005

### Nominating Committee Nominee
RE Dan Carrell, James River

### Floor Nominee
RE Charles Waldron, Missouri

Vacant
Alternate TE
TE Larry C. Hoop, Heartland

Alternate RE
Vacant
RE Tom Billiodeaux, Louisiana
RE David Yates, Missouri

<a id="ga29-p612"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=614 printed_page=612 -->

RE Dan Carrell: James River. Lawyer. RE at Stoney Point Reformed PC in Richmond, VA and very active in local church and in the community. Is active on Presbytery-level and GA Committees of Commissioners. Serves as a Hearing Officer for the Commonwealth of VA and is in the process of being certified as a Mediator.

RE Charles Waldron: Missouri. Elder at Kirk of the Hills PC in St. Louis, MO. He has been a strong and active elder, especially in the area of church renewal and revitalization. He is also a leader in the church’s prayer ministry.

TE Larry C. Hoop: Heartland. Currently Senior Pastor of the Colfax Center Church in Holland, Iowa. He was a member of the organizing committee to form Heartland Presbytery and has been an active member of the Administration and MNA Committees. He has served on the GA Review of Presbytery Records Committee. Former Stated Clerk of Northern Illinois Presbytery.

RE Tom Billiodeaux: Louisiana. Has worked as a mechanical engineer for 20 yrs. He also is a notary public and the sole proprietor of a business. He also has experience in the civil judicial system. He is currently serving on the Presbytery Pastoral Care Committee and Secretary of the GA Committee on Review of Presbytery Records. He desires to see the Constitution applied fairly and accurately.

RE David Yates: Missouri. Ruling Elder at Covenant Presbyterian Church in St. Louis, MO. He is a partner in a law firm that has its principle office in St. Louis with a branch in Washington, D.C. His primary practice for 32 years has been representing management in labor relations, equal employment, and employee benefits matters. He has a particular interest in the influence of Reformed Theology on jurisprudence as part of God’s redemptive plan.

## Committee on Interchurch Relations
Class of 2004

**Nominating Committee Nominee**
TE R. Irfon Hughes, Ascension

**Floor Nominee**
TE Paul Gilchrist, Tennessee Valley

TE R. Irfon Hughes: Ascension. Pastor of Hillcrest PC in Volant, PA. Served as pastor previously in Wales so has a wide experience in church life both in the UK and in the US and has seen first hand many of the relevant issues and needs for good church relations. Has previously served on GA ICR.

TE Paul Gilchrist: TN Valley. Former Stated Clerk of the GA. Former Professor at Covenant College. He is a church consultant for several denominations both nationally and internationally. He is currently the Executive Secretary of the World Reformed Fellowship.

<a id="ga29-p613"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=615 printed_page=613 -->

## Standing Judicial Commission
Class of 2005

### Nominating Committee Nominee
RE M. Dale Peacock, Louisiana
Vacant

### Floor Nominee
RE Tom Leopard, Evangel
RE Dr. M.C. “Cub” Culbertson, N. Texas
RE Steven O’Ban, Pacific Northwest

RE M. Dale Peacock: Louisiana. Attorney with extensive experience both in civil and church courts. Has served 2 terms on SJC before, during the time the original Manual for the SJC was drafted. Has served on numerous committees and commissions on both the Presbytery and General Assembly level.

RE Tom Leopard: Evangel. Past Moderator of the GA (1999). Former Chairman of the IAR Board and the PCA Foundation. He was on the staff of Briarwood PC and Christian School for 26 yrs where he served as the Church Administrator for 23 yrs. He is past chairman of the Committee on Administration od Evangel Presbytery. He has served on the SJC for 4 yrs and has the qualities of wisdom and discernment essential for the SJC.

RE Marvin “Cub” Culbertson: N. TX. Dr. Culbertson has served the denomination for many years as the GA “resident physician”. He currently serves on the GA Nominating Committee.

RE Steven O’Ban: Pacific Northwest. Member of Faith Presbyterian, Tacoma, WA. Partner in Seattle law firm. Practices law in areas of Federal litigation, religious institutions, religious liberty and campaign finance law. Served as caucus attorney for WA House of Representatives. Active member of Christian Legal Society and Board of Covenant High School.

It was moved, seconded, and passed that the Chairman and Secretary would review the final report before its presentation to the Assembly. The meeting was closed with prayer by RE Ed Hackenberg.

Respectfully submitted

RE David Moon, Chairman
TE Jack Jagoditsch, Secretary (Clerk)

of the 29™ General Assembly of the

The General A ssem bly’s Nominating Committee convened in Dallas, Texas, at the
Adams Mark Hotel on June 21, 2001 at 7:45 AM. Chairman RE David Moon called
the meeting to order and led in prayer. The roll was taken by the Secretary and the
Committee members with their Presbytery as follows:

Ascension RE William Mitchell
Central Georgia TE John C. Jagoditsch
Palmetto TE Cameron Kirker
Philadelphia TE Erwin Morrison
Potomac TE Tom Osterhaus
Southern New England TE Rick Downs
Southwest RE David Moon
Southwest Florida RE Henry W hitmore
Susquehanna Valley RE Lee Troup
Westminster RE Joe Reynolds

the purpose of the meeting was to deal wi than irregular lit y not identified previously.
The irregularity involved one person receiving duplicate nominations for the same
position. It was moved, seconded and passed by the Committee to waive the
irregularity. The meeting was closed with prayer by TE Rick Downs at 8:00 AM.

Respectfully submitted

RE David Moon, Chairman

<a id="ga29-p615"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=617 printed_page=615 -->

PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
FOUNDATION, INC.
REPORT TO THE 29th GENERAL ASSEMBLY

The PCA Foundation is pleased to report that it has just completed 2000 on a self-supported
basis. This has been a goal that we have been striving toward for three years. The Lord
continues to bless the work of the Foundation.

There were several other noteworthy events in 2000. The Foundation reached new record
levels for gifts, distributions to ministry, and total assets. Also, we took a big step forward
by hiring a Vice President - Development to help continue with our growth and
effectiveness.

Total assets as of December 31, 2000 reached $25.0 million - a new record. This is an
increase of $2.6 million, or 12% from the 1999 year-end amount of $22.4 million. It is
significant to note that, while growth in total assets is important, it does not tell the whole
story about what the Foundation does and will accomplish in its ministry. So much of what
we do results in gifts coming into the Foundation and going right back out as distributions
to ministries within a relatively short period of tim e- often within the same year.
Therefore, the Foundation realizes significant amounts as both gifts and distributions in a
given year, yet may not experience a substantial change in total assets.

The most significant contribution to the increase in total assets was the continued growth of
the Foundation’s Advise and Consult Fund, a donor advised fund, which increased to $7.4
million as of 12/31/00 from $5.5 million at the end of 1999, an increase of $1.9 million or
35%. This $1.9 million net increase resulted from contributions to the Advise and Consult
Fund of $7.0 million, less distributions to Christian ministries totaling approximately $5.1
million.

Total gifts to the Foundation during 2000 also reached a new milestone - $9.2 million. This
compares to $5.8 million in 1999, an increase of $3.4 million or 59%. As mentioned above,
$7.0 million of this amount consisted of contributions to the Advise & Consult Fund, and
the remainder represents contributions by individuals to Endowment Funds, Charitable
Remainder Trusts, Designated Funds and other miscellaneous contributions.

Distributions, or grants to ministry, also reached an all time high during 2000 — $6.1
million. This compares to $3.5 million in 1999, an increase of $2.6 million or 74%. The

<a id="ga29-p616"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=618 printed_page=616 -->

increase in distributions was primarily to PCA churches: $3.8 million in 2000 compared to
$1.5 million in 1999 - an increase of $2.3 million or 153%.

The Foundation’s total 2000 fund raising, management and general expenses as a
percentage of all expenses and grants was only 8%, well below the industry average of
16%, as compiled by Capin Crouse, LLP, the Foundation’s accountants.

The 2000 distributions (grants) to ministry by the Foundation were as follows:

Mission to the World $188,000
Mission to North America 171,000
Christian Education and Publications 15,000
Administrative Committee 15,000
lAR-Ministerial Relief 13,000
Reformed University Ministries 34,000
Covenant College 64,000
Covenant Seminary 332,000
PCA Foundation 143,000
Ridge Haven 13.000
Total Committees & Agencies 988,000

PCA Churches 3,847,000
Other Christian Ministries 1.301.000

Total $6.136.000

It has been exciting to see how the Lord used the Foundation during 2000 in Kingdom
ministry.

During 2000, we began aggressively marketing Designated Funds for churches,
presbyteries, and other ministries. This service has been well received, and we believe that
it will be used by more churches, presbyteries, and ministries as we continue to market this
service to them. By setting up a Designated Fund with the Foundation, a church, presbytery
or ministry specifies the intended use of the Fund, and controls the distributions from it.
The Foundation invests and administrates the Fund, and can process various types of gifts,
including appreciated assets.

The Foundation plans to continue vigorously promoting and providing its services to
individuals and families, churches, presbyteries and ministries, as well as provide services
to PCA Committees and Agencies whenever possible.

Because the focus of the Foundation is not on raising funds for its own operations, it has a
unique opportunity and niche. Our ministry is providing services and financial vehicles to
help Christians to carry out their stewardship responsibilities, as well as offering services
and financial vehicles to churches, presbyteries and other ministries. The Foundation is

<a id="ga29-p617"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=619 printed_page=617 -->

“Kingdom focused” and “donor driven,” which means that we work on the donor’s agenda,
not our own. We provide charitable services to individuals without pressuring them to give
to the PCA Foundation, or any other ministry. The result is more money for ministry.

Now that the Foundation has successfully achieved self-supporting status and no longer
receives General Assembly mandated support from Committees and Agencies, our Board is
recommending that the Board itself be re-constituted so that all ten of its members are
elected at large by the General Assembly, rather than have four of its members appointed
from the Boards and Committees of other PCA Committees and Agencies. The Foundation
Board recognizes the necessity of this in order to enable the Foundation to better represent
and serve the Church at large. The Foundation will continue to invite representatives from
all Committees and Agencies to attend its Board meetings.

the foundation will also continue to strive to effectively meet the needs of its present and
future donors, as well as the PCA: its churches, presbyteries, Committees, and Agencies.

By God's grace, the result of our work will be a continued increase in the gifts to the
Foundation, distributions to ministry from the Foundation, and growth of the Foundation’s
total assets.

We ask that you continue to pray for the Board and staff of the Foundation as they seek to
continue leading the Foundation successfully into the future.

THE PCA FOUNDATION’S RECOM M ENDATIONS ARE:

1. That the first and second sentences of ARTICLE 2. DIRECTORS. Section 2.2,
Number and Qualifications, and the first, third and fourth sentences in Section 2.3,
Election and Term, of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED BYLAWS
OF PERSBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC. be amended
so that all ten members of the PCA Foundation’s Board are elected at large by the
General Assembly, and to allow an individual who has served on the Board of
another Committee or Agency to serve on the PCAF Board without any lapse in
time, as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 2.2 “The Board shall consist of ten (10) members {: (a) six-(6)
members) in classes elected through the standard nomination and election
procedures by the General Assembly of Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation ) {(the "At Large Members"); and-(b) ene (l-)-member each of the
following permanent committees or board of trustees of the agencies: (1) Covenant
College; (2) Covenant Theological Seminar)'; (3) Mission to the World; and (4)
Mission to North America (the "Appointed Members'1)-. The-C h ai man of the board
shall be an At Large Member ) .”

<a id="ga29-p618"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=620 printed_page=618 -->

Section 2.3. “The {At-Large ■Members) [Directors) shall serve for four (4)
year terms, and shall be divided into four (4) classes, each class being as nearly
equal in number as possible, so that the terms of approximately one-fourth of the
{At Large Members) [Directors] shall expire each year. Each Director elected
shall hold office until his successor shall be elected and shall qualify, or until his
earlier resignation, death or removal. Directors shall be eligible for (re election}
[reelection] to a second four (4) year term, after which there must be a one (1) year
interval before (re election) [reelection. The restrictions set forth in the first
sentence of rule 4-5 of the Rules of Assembly Operations of Presbyterian Church in America shall not apply to the C orporation].”

2. That the last sentence of ARTICLE 2. DIRECTORS. Section 2.2 Election and Term
of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED BYLAWS OF
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC., be amended so
that Deacons may be elected to the PCA Foundation’s Board, as follows:
(Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 2.2 “Directors need not be residents of th estate of Georgia. All of
the members of the Board shall be either Teaching Elders {«}-[>] Ruling Elders [or
Deacons] in the Presbyterian Church in America, and at least one-half of such
members shall be Ruling Elders [and Deacons].”

3. That the first and second sentences of ARTICLE 4. OFFICERS. Section 4.4
Compensation and the first, second and third sentences of section 4.5 Term of the
Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED BYLAWS OF PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC., be amended so that these sections
are updated and properly state the compensation policies and procedures and term of
office as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 4.4. “The (salary) [compensation] of the president of the
Corporation shall be (recommended by--the board to the General Assembly fe f
approval. No other Officer shall receive compensation for his services as an
Officer) [determined by the Board].”

Section 4.5. “Each Officer (of the Corporation, except for the President)
[who is also a member of the Board (other than the President)], shall hold office
for a term of one year and until his successor is chosen or until his earlier
resignation, death or removal. The President (of the Corporation) [and all Officers
that are not members of the Board] shall hold office at the pleasure of the Board
(of Directors). No Officer other than the President may serve in the same office for
more than two (2) consecutive years."

4. That the second and third sentences of ARTICLE 4. OFFICERS. Section 4.12
Contracts (presented in its entirety) of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND

<a id="ga29-p619"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=621 printed_page=619 -->

RESTATED BYLAWS OF PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
FOUNDATION, INC., be amended raising the dollar limitation by which an officer,
employee or agent may bind the Foundation without Board authorization, as
follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold added.)

Section 4.12 “Unless otherwise restricted in a particular instance by the
Board by a resolution, the Officers of the Corporation shall always have the
authority to (a) bind the Corporation by any contract or (b) render the Corporation
liable pecuniarily, in connection with the performance by the Corporation of its
exempt activities and other activities in the ordinary course of its business,
including, but not limited to, the execution, delivery and performance by the
Corporation of annuity contracts, trust agreements, advise and consult fund
agreements, and all expenses incurred by the Corporation related thereto.
Notwithstanding the above, unless authorized in a particular instance by the Board
by resolution, no Officer, employee or agent shall have the authority to bind the
Corporation by any contract, to pledge its assets or credit, or to render it liable
pecuniarily, respecting or in any manner related to any unbudgeted operating
expense of the Corporation, if the amount involved in such contract, pledge or
obligation exceeds {$1,000} [$10,000]. If so authorized or if less than ($1,000)
[$10,000], any of the foregoing Officers singly may execute contracts or deliver
instruments on behalf of the Corporation, pledge its assets or credit, or render it
liable pecuniarily, with respect to such unbudgeted operating expenses.”

5. That ARTICLE 5. INDEMNIFICATION. Sections 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 and 5.6
(presented in their entirety) of the Foundation’s AMENDED AND RESTATED
BYLAWS OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION,
INC., be amended in order to reflect certain changes in the State of Georgia Code of
Indemnification, as follows: (Strikethroughs to be deleted and bracketed/in bold
added.)

Section 5.1. Conditional Indemnification. “Under the circumstances
prescribed in this Section 5.1, the Corporation shall indemnify and hold harmless
any ftndwidual) [person who was or is a party or is threatened to be] made a
party to any threatened, pending or completed action, suit or proceeding, whether
civil, criminal, administrative-fyf or investigative, and whether formal or informal (a
"Proceeding"), (because the individual) [by reason of the fact that he or she] is or
was a Director, Officer, employee or agent of the Corporation, or, while a Director,
Officer, employee or agent of the Corporation, is or was serving at the request of the
Corporation as a director, officer, partner, [joint venturer,] trustee, employee or
agent of another foreign or domestic (business or nonprofit) corporation,
partnership, joint venture, trust, employee benefit plan or other enterprise, against
(any) [the] obligation to pay a judgment, settlement, penalty, fine or reasonable
expenses (including (counsel} [attorneys'] fees) actually [and reasonably]

<a id="ga29-p620"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=29 pdf_page=622 printed_page=620 -->

incurred by him [or her in connection] with {respect to} such Proceeding (collectively a "Liability"), but only if {the individual acted in a manner he believed in good faith to be in or not opposed to} [he or she conducted himself or herself in good faith, and reasonably believed: (1) with respect to conduct in his or her "official capacity" (as that term is defined in Section 14-3-850 of the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, as amended), that such conduct was in] the best interests of the Corporation{, and, in the case of }[; (2) with respect to all other cases, only if that conduct was at least not opposed to the best interests of the Corporation; or (3) with respect to] any criminal Proceeding, {the individual} [that he or she] had no reasonable cause to believe his [or her] conduct was unlawful. Notwithstanding the above, the indemnification permitted hereunder in connection with a Proceeding by or in the right of the Corporation is limited to reasonable expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) incurred in connection with {the Proceeding} [a Proceeding in which it is determined that such person has met the standard of conduct required by this Section 5.1]. The termination of {a} [any] Proceeding by judgment, order, settlement, conviction[,] or upon a plea of nolo contendere or its equivalent shall not, of itself, {be determinative} [create a presumption] that the {individual} [person] did not meet the standard of conduct set forth in this Section 5.1. Notwithstanding the {above} [foregoing], the Corporation shall not indemnify any Director, Officer, employee or agent in connection with any Proceeding {(i) by or in the right of the Corporation in which the individual was adjudged liable to the Corporation, or (ii) in which he} [with respect to conduct for which he or she] was adjudged liable on the basis that personal benefit was improperly received by him [or her].

"Section 5.2. Mandatory Indemnification. {To the extent that a} [If any] Director, Officer, employee or agent has been [wholly] successful, on the merits or otherwise, in the defense of any Proceeding to which he {was a party, or in the defense of any claim, issue or matter therein, because he} [or she was a party because he or she] is or was a Director, Officer, employee or agent of the Corporation, the Corporation shall indemnify him [or her] against reasonable expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) {actually} incurred by him [or her] in connection therewith.

"Section 5.3. Advance or Reimbursement of Litigation Expenses. {The Corporation shall pay for or reimburse the reasonable} [Reasonable] expenses (including {counsel} [attorneys'] fees) incurred by a Director, Officer, employee or agent who is a party to a Proceeding [shall be paid by the Corporation] in advance of [the] final disposition of {the Proceeding if: (i) the Director, Officer, employee or agent furnishes the Corporation a written affirmation of his good faith belief that he has met the standard of conduct described in Section 5.1 above; and (ii) the Director, Officer, employee or agent furnishes the Corporation a written undertaking, executed personally or on his behalf, to repay the advance if it is ultimately determined that he is not entitled to indemnification under this Article 5}
